time series] PS3 --> PR4 style IN1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style IN2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style OUT1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style PR1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style PR2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style PR3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style PR4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style PS1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style PS2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style PS3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` --- # ­¤ōÖ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** **Define a hyetograph. How is it different from a hydrograph?** **Model Answer:** A **hyetograph** is a graphical plot of **rainfall intensity (mm/hr)** versus **time** during a storm. It is a bar/step chart that represents the temporal distribution of rainfall. **Difference from Hydrograph:** | Feature | Hyetograph | Hydrograph | |---|---|---| | Variable Plotted | Rainfall **intensity** | Streamflow **discharge** | | Y-axis Unit | mm/hr | m┬│/s or cumecs | | Shape | Bars (step) | Smooth continuous curve | | Source | Rain gauge data | River/stream gauging | | Purpose | Input to runoff model | Output of catchment response | *(3 marks ŌĆö full marks if all three distinctions are tabulated correctly)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** **Explain the procedure to construct a hyetograph from a mass curve of rainfall.** **Model Answer:** 1. Obtain **cumulative rainfall depths** $P(t)$ at successive time instants from an autographic rain gauge. *(1 mark)* 2. Compute **incremental rainfall** $\Delta P_j = P(t_j) - P(t_{j-1})$ for each interval. *(1 mark)* 3. Calculate **intensity** $i_j = \Delta P_j / \Delta t$ for each interval. *(0.5 marks)* 4. Plot a **bar chart** of $i_j$ (Y-axis) against time intervals (X-axis) ŌåÆ this is the hyetograph. *(0.5 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each) ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]** **(a)** A storm produced the following cumulative rainfall depths as recorded by a self-recording rain gauge. Construct the **hyetograph** for the storm and compute the **average rainfall intensity** over the entire duration. *(7 marks)* | Time (hr) | 0 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 | 3.5 | 4.0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Cumulative Rainfall (mm) | 0 | 1.5 | 5.0 | 8.0 | 14.0 | 17.5 | 19.0 | 19.8 | 20.0 | **(b)** A **triangular design hyetograph** is to be constructed for a 3-hour storm with a total depth of 60 mm. Compute the **peak intensity** if the peak occurs at the **center** of the storm. Compare the peak intensity with the **average intensity**. *(7 marks)* ### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compute incremental depths } \Delta P_j = P(t_j) - P(t_{j-1}): \\ & \Delta P_1 = 1.5, \;\; \Delta P_2 = 3.5, \;\; \Delta P_3 = 3.0, \;\; \Delta P_4 = 6.0 \\ & \Delta P_5 = 3.5, \;\; \Delta P_6 = 1.5, \;\; \Delta P_7 = 0.8, \;\; \Delta P_8 = 0.2 \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Verify total depth: } P = 1.5+3.5+3.0+6.0+3.5+1.5+0.8+0.2 = 20.0 \text{ mm} \;\checkmark \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Compute intensities with } \Delta t = 0.5 \text{ hr}: \\ & i_1 = 3.0, \; i_2 = 7.0, \; i_3 = 6.0, \; i_4 = 12.0 \text{ (peak)}, \\ & i_5 = 7.0, \; i_6 = 3.0, \; i_7 = 1.6, \; i_8 = 0.4 \text{ mm/hr} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Average intensity: } \bar{i} = \dfrac{20.0 \text{ mm}}{4.0 \text{ hr}} = \mathbf{5.0 \text{ mm/hr}} \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Plot bar chart with } i_4 = 12.0 \text{ mm/hr as the tallest bar (peak).} \end{aligned}$$ ### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Given: Total depth } P = 60 \text{ mm}, \; T = 3 \text{ hr}, \; t_p = T/2 = 1.5 \text{ hr}. \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{For a triangular hyetograph, total area} = P: \\ & \frac{1}{2} \cdot T \cdot i_p = P \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & i_p = \dfrac{2P}{T} = \dfrac{2 \times 60}{3} = \mathbf{40 \text{ mm/hr}} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Average intensity: } \bar{i} = \dfrac{P}{T} = \dfrac{60}{3} = 20 \text{ mm/hr} \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Comparison: } \dfrac{i_p}{\bar{i}} = \dfrac{40}{20} = 2.0 \\ & \text{The peak intensity is } \mathbf{2.0 \text{ times the average intensity}}. \end{aligned}$$ --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]** **(a)** Define the **$\phi$-index** and the **$W$-index**. A storm of total depth 80 mm over a duration of 6 hours produced a runoff depth of 32 mm. If the **effective rainfall** occurs only during the 4 hours when the rainfall intensity exceeds the $\phi$-index, determine the **$\phi$-index** value. *(7 marks)* **(b)** The **Intensity-Duration-Frequency (IDF)** relationship for a city is given by: $$i = \dfrac{800}{(t + 20)^{0.75}}$$ where $i$ is in **mm/hr** and $t$ is in **minutes**. Determine: (i) the **2-hour, 50-year** storm intensity, and (ii) the **total rainfall depth** for a 30-min, 100-year storm if the 100-year intensity at 30 min is 1.2 times the 50-year value. *(7 marks)* ### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \phi\text{-index: average infiltration rate during effective rainfall period.} \\ & W\text{-index: average loss rate over entire storm duration (includes interception, depression storage).} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Given: } P = 80 \text{ mm}, \; T = 6 \text{ hr}, \; R = 32 \text{ mm}, \; t_e = 4 \text{ hr}. \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Apply } \phi\text{-index formula:} \\ & R = P_{\text{eff}} = \sum (i_j \cdot \Delta t) - \phi \cdot t_e \\ & \text{But } \sum (i_j \cdot \Delta t) = P - \text{(losses during non-effective time)} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Standard formula: } R = P - \phi \cdot t_e \quad \text{(assuming total losses = }\phi \cdot t_e\text{)} \\ & 32 = 80 - \phi \times 4 \\ & 4\phi = 48 \\ & \boxed{\phi = 12 \text{ mm/hr}} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note for student:** The problem's wording implies that **all storm depth minus infiltration** equals runoff. If we interpret losses only during the effective period: $R = P_{\text{total effective}} - \phi \cdot t_e$. Here $P_{\text{eff}} = R = 32$ mm, so $\phi \cdot t_e = P_{\text{eff}} - R$? No ŌĆö actually for this simplified KTU problem, the correct interpretation is: > > $$\text{Losses} = P - R = 80 - 32 = 48 \text{ mm}; \quad \phi = \dfrac{48}{4} = 12 \text{ mm/hr} \;\checkmark$$ ### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Part (i):} \quad & t = 2 \text{ hr} = 120 \text{ min} \\ & i = \dfrac{800}{(120 + 20)^{0.75}} = \dfrac{800}{(140)^{0.75}} \\ & 140^{0.75} = 140^{3/4} = (140^{1/4})^3 \approx (3.446)^3 \approx 40.92 \\ & i = \dfrac{800}{40.92} \approx \mathbf{19.55 \text{ mm/hr}} \\ \text{Part (ii):} \quad & t = 30 \text{ min}; \quad i_{100} = 1.2 \times i_{50} \\ & i_{50}(30) = \dfrac{800}{(30+20)^{0.75}} = \dfrac{800}{50^{0.75}} = \dfrac{800}{18.80} \approx 42.55 \text{ mm/hr} \\ & i_{100}(30) = 1.2 \times 42.55 = 51.06 \text{ mm/hr} \\ & P_{100}(30 \text{ min}) = i_{100} \times t = 51.06 \times 0.5 \text{ hr} = \mathbf{25.53 \text{ mm}} \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **­¤ö┤ COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch in Intensity Calculation:** Students often forget to convert minutes ŌåÆ hours when computing $i = \Delta P / \Delta t$. Always check: depth in mm, time in **hr** ŌåÆ intensity in **mm/hr**. *(Lose 1-2 marks easily)* > > 2. **Confusing Hyetograph with Mass Curve:** Hyetograph = Intensity vs. Time (Bar Chart). Mass Curve = Cumulative Depth vs. Time (Increasing Curve). Examiners specifically test this distinction. *(Lose 1 mark)* > > 3. **Forgetting to Verify Total Area:** When constructing a hyetograph from data, always verify that the **sum of (intensity ├Ś ╬öt)** equals the **total reported rainfall depth**. This is a self-check that examiners love to test. *(Lose 1 mark if missing)* > > 4. **Using $T$ instead of $t_e$ in $\phi$-index Formula:** The $\phi$-index uses the **effective duration** $t_e$ (time during which $i > \phi$), NOT the total storm duration $T$. This is a classic KTU trap. *(Lose 2-3 marks)* > > 5. **Triangular Hyetograph Peak Calculation:** The factor is **2P/T**, not P/T. Many students write $i_p = P/T$, which is the *average* intensity, not the peak. *(Lose 1 mark)* > > 6. **Skipping the Units in the Final Answer:** Always write units (mm/hr, mm, hr) in the boxed final answer. KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for unit-less answers. --- ## 5.4 ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY ŌĆö REVISE BEFORE KTU EXAM** - Ō£ģ **Hyetograph** = bar chart of **Rainfall Intensity (mm/hr) vs. Time (hr)**. - Ō£ģ Total depth $P$ = **area under the hyetograph** = $\sum i_j \cdot \Delta t$. - Ō£ģ Average intensity $\bar{i} = P / T$ (over total duration). - Ō£ģ Hyetograph is **derived from** the **mass curve of rainfall** (cumulative depth curve). - Ō£ģ **Three types:** Actual (from data), Average (from multiple storms), Synthetic/Design (for engineering design). - Ō£ģ Common synthetic patterns: **Uniform, Triangular, SCS Type II, Chicago, Huff, Pilgrim & Cordery**. - Ō£ģ **Triangular hyetograph peak** intensity: $i_p = 2P / T$ (symmetric case). - Ō£ģ **$\phi$-index formula:** $\phi = (P - R) / t_e$ where $t_e$ is effective duration (when $i > \phi$). - Ō£ģ **$W$-index formula:** $W = (P - R - S) / t_e$ where $S$ is surface storage + interception. - Ō£ģ **IDF relationship** gives intensity as a function of duration and return period. - Ō£ģ Hyetograph is the **INPUT** to the rainfall-runoff transformation (via unit hydrograph). - Ō£ģ Units: Intensity in **mm/hr**, Depth in **mm**, Time in **hr or min** (be consistent!). - Ō£ģ Construction steps: (1) Read cumulative depths, (2) Compute $\Delta P$, (3) Compute $i = \Delta P / \Delta t$, (4) Plot bar chart, (5) Verify total area. - Ō£ģ **Common confusion points:** Hyetograph ŌēĀ Hydrograph; Hyetograph ŌēĀ Mass Curve; $T$ (total) vs. $t_e$ (effective). --- > **END OF NOTES ŌĆö HYETOGRAPH | MODULE 1 | PBAGT304** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.*

topics.title: Depth-Area-Duration Curves and Intensity-Duration-Frequency Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Depth-Area-Duration Curves and Intensity-Duration-Frequency Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Depth-Area-Duration (DAD) Curves

### Formal Definition

A **Depth-Area-Duration (DAD) curve** is a hydrological tool that graphically represents the maximum average rainfall depth occurring over a specified area of a watershed for a given duration of storm. It encapsulates the simultaneous relationship between three fundamental storm parameters ŌĆö **depth (P)**, **area (A)**, and **duration (D)** ŌĆö for a single storm event.

Mathematically, the DAD relationship can be expressed as:

$$\bar{P} = f(A, D)$$

where $\bar{P}$ is the average precipitation depth over area $A$ during duration $D$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DAD analysis is an *event-based* analysis. It does **not** represent long-term probability ŌĆö for that, you must use the **IDF relationship**. DAD is extracted from a single historical storm.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant rectangular sponge dropped onto a wet, irregular puddle on a table:
- The **size of the sponge** = the **area** over which rainfall is averaged.
- The **time you press down** = the **duration** of rainfall considered.
- The **water absorbed** = the **average depth of rainfall**.

A DAD curve tells you: *"If I look at the wettest central 50 km┬▓ of the storm for the wettest 6-hour period, how much rain fell on average across that region?"* The answer changes with the area and the time-window chosen ŌĆö and that is exactly what the DAD curve captures.

### Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Average Precipitation Depth | $\bar{P}$ | **mm** or **cm** |
| Area | $A$ | **km┬▓** |
| Duration | $D$ or $t$ | **hours** or **minutes** |
| Storm Return Period | $T$ | **years** |

---

## 1.2 Intensity-Duration-Frequency (IDF) Curves

### Formal Definition

The **Intensity-Duration-Frequency (IDF) curve** is a graphical or mathematical relationship expressing the rainfall intensity as a function of rainfall duration for a specified return period (or frequency of occurrence). It is a *probabilistic* relationship derived from long-term statistical analysis of rainfall records.

Mathematically:

$$i = f(D, T)$$

where $i$ is the average rainfall intensity, $D$ is duration, and $T$ is return period (in years).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Difference from DAD:**
> - **DAD** ŌåÆ *Single storm* ŌåÆ Spatial-temporal averaging.
> - **IDF** ŌåÆ *Many storms* ŌåÆ Statistical probability of exceedance.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of IDF as a "**rainfall strength chart for design engineers**":
- Imagine you are designing a stormwater drain for a city in Kerala.
- You ask: *"What is the heaviest rain my drain must handle, and how often can such a heavy rain be expected?"*
- The IDF curve answers this by saying: *"In any given year, there is a 1-in-50 chance (2% probability) that rain will fall at more than **X mm/hour** for **30 minutes straight**."*

A **higher return period (T)** ŌåÆ a **rarer, more intense event** ŌåÆ IDF curve sits **higher**.

### Common IDF Empirical Forms

| Form | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sherman (general) | $i = \dfrac{k}{(t+c)^n}$ | Most common |
| With frequency | $i = \dfrac{k T^x}{(t+c)^n}$ | KTU-preferred form |
| Simplified power | $i = \dfrac{a}{t^n}$ | Used when $c \approx 0$ |

Where $k, c, n, x, a$ are **regional constants** obtained from rainfall data fitting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DAD Analysis ŌĆö Theoretical Framework

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Storm Selection & Mass Curve Construction:** Choose the storm and plot the cumulative rainfall mass curve (hyetograph) for each raingauge station in and around the basin.
2. **Isohyetal Map Preparation:** For a given time interval (e.g., 1-hour increments), plot isohyets ŌĆö lines of equal rainfall depth ŌĆö using data from all gauges.
3. **Area Averaging:** For each isohyet interval, calculate the incremental area between successive isohyets. Compute the average rainfall over the watershed using the isohyetal method:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( \frac{P_{j-1} + P_{j}}{2} \right) A_{j}}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} A_{j}}$$

where $P_{j-1}$ and $P_{j}$ are successive isohyet values and $A_{j}$ is the area between them.

4. **Depth-Duration Curves:** For each chosen duration (e.g., 6 h, 12 h, 24 h), compute the average depth over the *entire* basin from the storm mass curve.
5. **Maximum Depth Envelope:** Repeat the averaging by restricting to the *wettest sub-area* (using isohyets as boundaries) ŌĆö the result is the **maximum average depth** for that area-duration pair.
6. **Plotting:** Plot depth (ordinate) vs. area (abscissa), with a family of curves ŌĆö one curve per duration. This is the **DAD diagram**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> DAD curves are critical for:
> - Designing **spillways of dams** (PMF estimation).
> - Designing **flood control reservoirs** and **urban stormwater systems**.
> - Estimating **Probable Maximum Precipitation (PMP)** for a basin.

---

## 2.2 IDF Analysis ŌĆö Theoretical Framework

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Data Compilation:** Collect annual maximum rainfall depths for various standard durations (e.g., 15 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h) over a long period (typically $\geq$ 30 years).
2. **Frequency Analysis:** Fit a probability distribution (commonly **Gumbel** or **Log-Pearson Type III**) to each duration series to estimate rainfall depth for return periods $T = 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100$ years.
3. **Intensity Computation:** Convert each depth to intensity: $i = P / t$ (where $t$ is in hours, $i$ in mm/h).
4. **Curve Fitting:** Plot $i$ vs. $t$ on log-log paper. For each return period, fit a curve of the form:

$$i = \frac{k}{(t+c)^n}$$

5. **Family of Curves:** One curve per return period. The curve **shifts upward** as $T$ increases.

### Why IDF Curves Are Important

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 examinations and real engineering practice, the IDF relationship is used to:
> - Derive **design storm hyetographs** for rational method runoff estimation.
> - Compute **time of concentration ($t_c$)**-based peak flow.
> - Size **storm sewers, culverts, and urban drainage networks**.
> - Apply the **Rational Formula**: $Q = C i A$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{\sum_{j} \left( \dfrac{P_{j-1}+P_{j}}{2} \right) A_{j}}{\sum_{j} A_{j}}$ | Isohyetal mean rainfall | **mm** |
| 2 | $\bar{P}_A = \dfrac{1}{A} \int_A P \, dA$ | Continuous form of area-averaging | **mm** |
| 3 | $i = \dfrac{P}{t}$ | Rainfall intensity definition | **mm/h** |
| 4 | $i = \dfrac{k}{(t+c)^n}$ | Sherman IDF general form | **mm/h** |
| 5 | $i = \dfrac{k T^x}{(t+c)^n}$ | IDF with frequency (KTU-preferred) | **mm/h** |
| 6 | $i = \dfrac{a}{t^n}$ | Simplified IDF (when $c=0$) | **mm/h** |
| 7 | $T = \dfrac{1}{P(\text{exceedance})}$ | Return period from exceedance prob. | **years** |
| 8 | $P(\text{exceedance}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda}$ | Poisson exceedance prob. | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta P = P \, \eta \, \Delta t$ | Storm depth-increment (incremental hyetograph) | **mm** |
| 10 | $Q = C i A$ | Rational formula (uses IDF intensity) | **m┬│/s** |

---

## 2.4 Physical Interpretation of Constants

- **$k$**: Controls magnitude ŌĆö higher $k$ means a wetter (or rarer-event) region.
- **$c$**: Time offset ŌĆö represents the minimum realistic storm duration; typically small (a few minutes).
- **$n$**: Decay exponent ŌĆö represents how rapidly intensity drops with duration. For Indian conditions, $n$ lies between **0.5 and 0.8**.
- **$x$**: Frequency exponent ŌĆö how sensitive intensity is to return period. Typical range: **0.1 ŌĆō 0.3**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Average Depth from Isohyetal Map

Consider a watershed of total area $A_T$ covered by isohyets $P_0 = 0, P_1, P_2, \dots, P_m$ (with $P_0 = 0$ being the basin boundary). Between consecutive isohyets, the area is $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_m$.

**Step 1:** The mean rainfall between isohyets $P_{j-1}$ and $P_j$ is taken as their arithmetic mean (since the rainfall is assumed to vary linearly between isohyets):

$$\bar{P}_{j} = \frac{P_{j-1} + P_{j}}{2}$$

**Step 2:** The total volume of rainfall over the area between these two isohyets is:

$$V_j = \bar{P}_j \cdot A_j = \left(\frac{P_{j-1}+P_j}{2}\right) A_j$$

**Step 3:** The total volume over the entire basin is the sum over all strips:

$$V_T = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left(\frac{P_{j-1}+P_j}{2}\right) A_j$$

**Step 4:** The average rainfall depth over the whole basin is total volume divided by total area:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{V_T}{A_T} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \left(\dfrac{P_{j-1}+P_j}{2}\right) A_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} A_j}$$

**Step 5:** This is the standard isohyetal mean. For a continuous distribution of rainfall over area $A$, the integral form follows by letting the strip width $\Delta A \to 0$:

$$\bar{P}_A = \frac{1}{A} \int_A P \, dA$$

where $P$ is the local rainfall at infinitesimal area $dA$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Depth-Duration Curve from Mass Curve

A **mass curve of rainfall** is a plot of cumulative precipitation $P(t)$ vs. time $t$. The depth over any interval $[t_1, t_2]$ is:

$$\Delta P_{t_1 \to t_2} = P(t_2) - P(t_1)$$

For a given storm duration $D = t_2 - t_1$, the **maximum depth** is found by sliding the interval along the mass curve:

$$P_{\max}(D) = \max_{t_1} \left[ P(t_1 + D) - P(t_1) \right]$$

By computing $P_{\max}(D)$ for several values of $D$, we obtain the **depth-duration curve**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of IDF Relationship ŌĆö Sherman Form

Starting from physical observation that:
- Intensity **decreases** as duration **increases** (since deeper storms last longer but dilute intensity).
- Intensity **increases** as return period **increases** (rarer events are more extreme).

Assume a power-type relationship between intensity and duration:

$$i \cdot (t+c)^n = \text{constant} = k'$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\log i = \log k' - n \log(t+c)$$

Plotting $\log i$ vs. $\log(t+c)$ should give a straight line with slope $-n$ and intercept $\log k'$. This is the basis of curve fitting.

To incorporate return period $T$, an additional frequency factor $T^x$ is introduced:

$$i = \frac{k T^x}{(t+c)^n}$$

In logarithmic form (used for fitting by graphical or regression methods):

$$\log i = \log k + x \log T - n \log(t+c)$$

For a fixed $T$, $\log i$ vs. $\log(t+c)$ is linear with slope $-n$ and intercept $(\log k + x \log T)$.

---

## 3.4 Frequency Factor Approach for IDF

For **Gumbel distribution** (most common in KTU textbooks for extreme rainfall):

$$x_T = \bar{x} + K \cdot \sigma$$

where $K$ is the frequency factor:

$$K = -\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi}\left[0.5772 + \ln\left(\ln\frac{T}{T-1}\right)\right]$$

For a chosen duration, the rainfall depth corresponding to return period $T$ is:

$$P_T = \bar{P} + K_T \cdot \sigma_P$$

Converting to intensity:

$$i_T = \frac{P_T}{t}$$

Tabulated values of $K_T$ (Gumbel):

| $T$ (years) | 2 | 5 | 10 | 25 | 50 | 100 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $K_T$ | -0.164 | 0.719 | 1.305 | 2.044 | 2.592 | 3.137 |

---

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example ŌĆö IDF Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** An IDF equation for a region is $i = \dfrac{900 \, T^{0.15}}{(t + 0.30)^{0.80}}$, where $i$ is in **mm/h**, $t$ in **hours**, $T$ in **years**.

**(a) Find the 50-year, 2-hour design rainfall intensity.**

**Step 1:** Substitute $T = 50$, $t = 2$:

$$i = \frac{900 \times (50)^{0.15}}{(2 + 0.30)^{0.80}}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the numerator:

$$(50)^{0.15} = e^{0.15 \ln 50} = e^{0.15 \times 3.912} = e^{0.5868} = 1.798$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 900 \times 1.798 = 1618.2$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator:

$$(2.30)^{0.80} = e^{0.80 \ln 2.30} = e^{0.80 \times 0.8329} = e^{0.6663} = 1.947$$

**Step 4:** Final result:

$$i = \frac{1618.2}{1.947} = 831.2 \, \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{h}} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{check units}}$$

*(Correction ŌĆö units are dimensionless factors. The coefficient 900 already carries the mm/h unit.)*

$$i \approx 831.2 \, \text{mm/h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality check:** Such extreme intensity (831 mm/h) is unusually high. In a real problem, the coefficient would be much smaller (e.g., $k = 50$ŌĆō$200$ mm/h). This example is purely numerical to demonstrate the procedure.

**(b) Find the duration at which the 10-year intensity equals 50 mm/h.**

**Step 1:** Substitute $T = 10$, $i = 50$:

$$50 = \frac{900 \times (10)^{0.15}}{(t + 0.30)^{0.80}}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate $900 \times (10)^{0.15}$:

$$(10)^{0.15} = 1.4125$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 900 \times 1.4125 = 1271.25$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$(t + 0.30)^{0.80} = \frac{1271.25}{50} = 25.425$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $t$:

$$t + 0.30 = (25.425)^{1/0.80} = (25.425)^{1.25}$$

$$(25.425)^{1.25} = e^{1.25 \ln 25.425} = e^{1.25 \times 3.2354} = e^{4.0443} = 57.10$$

$$t = 57.10 - 0.30 = 56.80 \, \text{h}$$

This extremely long duration is unrealistic ŌĆö confirming our earlier note that the coefficient is illustrative, not realistic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DAD Curve Construction Procedure ŌĆö Block Architecture Flow

The following diagram maps the full procedure for constructing DAD curves from a single historical storm:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
