## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*List any three types of recording rain gauges and state the principle of operation of a tipping-bucket rain gauge.*

**Model Answer:**
The three recording rain gauges are:
1. **Tipping-bucket gauge** ŌĆö Rain collected by a funnel falls into a small twin-bucket mechanism. Each bucket holds an equivalent of 0.25 mm (or 0.5 mm) of rain; when full, it tips, emptying the water out and bringing the second bucket under the funnel. Each tip produces an electrical pulse, which is recorded on a data logger.
2. **Weighing-bucket gauge** ŌĆö Rain is collected in a bucket resting on a weighing mechanism (spring or strain gauge). The continuous record of cumulative weight vs. time is plotted on a chart.
3. **Float / Syphon (Natural Syphon) gauge** ŌĆö Rain enters a float chamber; a float rises with the water level. When the chamber fills to 10 mm, a siphon automatically empties it, and the cycle repeats, providing a continuous record.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**
*Define the Thornthwaite Precipitation Effectiveness Index (PŌĆōE Index) and state its climatic classification ranges.*

**Model Answer:**
The **PŌĆōE Index** proposed by C. W. Thornthwaite (1931) is a climatic index that combines precipitation and temperature to classify climate. For each month $i$ where monthly rainfall $P_i > 0.5 T_i$ (with $T_i$ in ┬░C), the term $12 P_i / T_i$ is summed over 12 months:

$$
P\text{-}E = \sum_{i=1}^{12} \frac{12 P_i}{T_i} \quad \text{(only for months with } P_i > 0.5 T_i \text{)}
$$

**Climatic Classification:**

| PŌĆōE Index Range | Climate Type |
|---|---|
| $> 128$ | Rainforest (A) |
| $64$ to $127$ | Forest (B) |
| $32$ to $63$ | Grassland (C) |
| $16$ to $31$ | Steppe (D) |
| $< 16$ | Desert (E) |

---

### PART B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **QUESTION A ŌĆö Thiessen Polygon (Module Internal Choice A)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**
A river basin of area **520 km┬▓** has **four rain gauge stations** with the following readings and corresponding Thiessen polygon areas (km┬▓):

| Station | P (mm) | Area (km┬▓) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 85 | 130 |
| B | 70 | 150 |
| C | 95 | 140 |
| D | 60 | 100 |

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the mean areal rainfall of the basin using the Thiessen polygon method. Verify the total area.

**(b) [7 Marks]** State the assumptions of the Thiessen polygon method. Compare it with the arithmetic mean method and the isohyetal method, listing two advantages and two limitations of each.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Computation of Mean Areal Rainfall [7 marks]**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify total area**

$$
A = 130 + 150 + 140 + 100 = 520 \text{ km}^2 \quad \text{(matches given)}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $P_i \times A_i$ for each station**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A \cdot A_A &= 85 \times 130 = 11050 \text{ mm┬Ękm}^2 \\
P_B \cdot A_B &= 70 \times 150 = 10500 \text{ mm┬Ękm}^2 \\
P_C \cdot A_C &= 95 \times 140 = 13300 \text{ mm┬Ękm}^2 \\
P_D \cdot A_D &= 60 \times 100 = 6000 \text{ mm┬Ękm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sum**

$$
\sum P_i A_i = 11050 + 10500 + 13300 + 6000 = 40850 \text{ mm┬Ękm}^2
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Mean rainfall**

$$
\bar{P} = \frac{\sum P_i A_i}{\sum A_i} = \frac{40850}{520} = 78.56 \text{ mm}
$$

**Answer:** Mean areal rainfall of the basin = **$78.56 \text{ mm}$** (Ōēł 78.6 mm).

**(b) Assumptions and Comparison [7 marks]**

**Assumptions of Thiessen Polygon Method:**
1. Rainfall is assumed **uniform** within each polygon (constant at the gauge value).
2. Each station's reading is **representative** of its entire polygon area.
3. The polygon boundary is determined **purely on geometry** (perpendicular bisectors) and does not account for topography.

**Comparison Table:**

| Method | Assumes | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic Mean | Gauges uniformly distributed; flat terrain | Simple, fast | Inaccurate for irregular networks or mountainous basins |
| Thiessen Polygon | Geometric Voronoi zones; uniform within polygon | Accounts for non-uniform gauge density; rigorous geometry | Ignores topography; laborious to construct manually |
| Isohyetal | Spatial pattern; topographic influence | Most accurate, accounts for terrain | Requires experienced analyst; subjective isohyet drawing |

**Conclusion:** The **Isohyetal Method is most accurate**, the **Thiessen Polygon Method is the engineering standard** (best balance of accuracy and effort), and the **Arithmetic Mean** is suitable only for very flat, well-instrumented basins.

---

#### **QUESTION B ŌĆö Climate Indices (Module Internal Choice B)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, Sample Paper 2024]**
A watershed in central Kerala has the following data:
- Annual precipitation, $P = 2400$ mm
- Annual potential evapotranspiration, $PET = 1800$ mm

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compute the **Thornthwaite Aridity Index** and **Moisture Index** for the region. Classify its climate.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **UNEP Aridity Index** and the **Coefficient of Variation $C_v$** given that the standard deviation of annual rainfall is 480 mm. State the practical significance of $C_v$ in the design of water-resource structures.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Thornthwaite Aridity & Moisture Index [7 marks]**

**Thornthwaite Aridity Index:**

$$
I_a = 100 \times \frac{P - PET}{PET} = 100 \times \frac{2400 - 1800}{1800} = 100 \times 0.3333 = 33.33
$$

**Thornthwaite Moisture Index:**
For an annual assessment, the moisture index is calculated from the difference between precipitation and PET:

$$
I_m = 100 \times \frac{P - PET}{PET} = 33.33 \text{ (same form for annual basis)}
$$

**Climatic Classification:**

| $I_m$ Range | Climate Class |
|---|---|
| $> 100$ | Per-humid (A) |
| $20$ to $100$ | Humid (B) |
| $0$ to $20$ | Sub-humid (CŌéé) |
| $-33.3$ to $0$ | Semi-arid (D) |
| $-66.7$ to $-33.3$ | Arid (E) |

Since $I_m = 33.33$, the watershed lies in the **Humid (B) climate** zone ŌĆö consistent with Kerala's tropical monsoon climate.

**(b) UNEP Aridity Index and Coefficient of Variation [7 marks]**

**UNEP Aridity Index:**

$$
AI = \frac{P}{PET} = \frac{2400}{1800} = 1.333
$$

**Interpretation (UNEP / Middleton & Thomas 1997):**

| AI | Climate Class |
|---|---|
| $< 0.03$ | Hyper-arid |
| $0.03$ to $0.20$ | Arid |
| $0.20$ to $0.50$ | Semi-arid |
| $0.50$ to $0.65$ | Dry sub-humid |
| $> 0.65$ | Humid |

Since $AI = 1.333 > 0.65$, the region is **Humid**.

**Coefficient of Variation:**

$$
C_v = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{P}} = \frac{480}{2400} = 0.20 = 20\%
$$

**Practical Significance of $C_v$:**
- $C_v$ quantifies the **inter-annual variability** of rainfall.
- A **low $C_v$** (as in Kerala, ~0.20) means rainfall is fairly consistent, so reservoirs can be designed with **low buffer storage**.
- A **high $C_v$** (e.g., 0.5ŌĆō1.0 in arid Rajasthan) means highly erratic rainfall, requiring **oversized reservoirs, drip irrigation, and contingency drought plans**.
- In **flood estimation**, $C_v$ is the primary input to the **Log-Pearson Type III** distribution used by the USGS and Central Water Commission (CWC), India.
- For **irrigation design**, $C_v$ governs the **dependable rainfall** (rainfall exceeded in 75% or 80% of years) used to size canals and crop-water budgets.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls (Measurement & Indices):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Thiessen problems:** A frequent error is mixing up $\text{mm┬Ękm}^2$ and $\text{mm┬Ęm}^2$. Always keep area and rainfall in compatible units; the ratio is dimensionless depth, but the intermediate product is not.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify $\sum A_i =$ Total basin area.** Examiners often deduct 1 mark if this sanity-check is missing.
> 3. **Confusing $PET$ and $PE$:** They are conceptually similar but used in different index formulas (Humidity Index uses $PE$; Aridity Index uses $PET$). Read the formula table carefully.
> 4. **PŌĆōE Index months:** Many students apply the formula to all 12 months, ignoring the $P_i > 0.5 T_i$ condition. This yields wrong climatic class.
> 5. **Arithmetic Mean ŌēĀ Thiessen:** When the problem says "Thiessen polygon", do **not** simply average the $P_i$ values. The polygon areas are essential and they are tested deliberately.

---

### ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Precipitation is the master input** to the hydrological cycle; measured via rain gauges (non-recording: Symons; recording: tipping-bucket, weighing, float).
- **Symons gauge** has a 127 mm orifice and is the IMD standard in India.
- **Spatial estimation methods (ascending accuracy):** Arithmetic Mean ŌåÆ Thiessen Polygon ŌåÆ Isohyetal.
- **Thiessen polygon construction:** Delaunay triangulation ŌåÆ perpendicular bisectors ŌåÆ Voronoi cells ŌåÆ area weighting.
- **Thiessen formula:** $\bar{P} = \dfrac{\sum P_i A_i}{\sum A_i}$
- **Thornthwaite Aridity Index:** $I_a = 100 \cdot \dfrac{P - PET}{PET}$
- **UNEP Aridity Index:** $AI = \dfrac{P}{PET}$
- **PŌĆōE Index:** $P\text{-}E = \sum \dfrac{12 P_i}{T_i}$ (only for months with $P_i > 0.5 T_i$)
- **Coefficient of Variation:** $C_v = \sigma / \bar{P}$ ŌĆö measures rainfall reliability.
- **$C_v$ is a direct input to Log-Pearson Type III** flood-frequency analysis.
- **WMO / IMD recommended gauge density** in India: 1 station per 520 km┬▓ (plains), 1 per 130ŌĆō390 km┬▓ (mountainous).
- **Kerala** typically has $AI > 1$ (Humid) and $C_v \approx 0.2$ (low variability).
- **Topography matters:** Always prefer **Isohyetal Method** for mountainous basins (Western Ghats).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporation - Estimation and Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaporation - Estimation and Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In engineering hydrology, **Evaporation (E)** is defined as the physical process by which water is transformed from its liquid state into a gaseous state (water vapor) and is subsequently transferred from the evaporating surface to the atmosphere. For the purpose of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, evaporation is classified as one of the principal *abstractions* in the hydrological cycle, representing a continuous loss of moisture from free water surfaces (lakes, reservoirs, rivers), soil, and vegetation-covered land surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *"Evaporation is the process in which water changes from the liquid to the gaseous state at a temperature below the boiling point and is transferred from the evaporating surface to the atmosphere."*

Mathematically, the rate of evaporation is expressed as **depth of water lost per unit time** (mm/day or mm/month) and depends fundamentally on the vapor pressure gradient between the water surface and the overlying air.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a wet towel hanging on a clothesline on a hot, windy day in Kerala. The towel dries quickly because:
- **Sun (heat)** provides energy to break water molecules free from the surface.
- **Wind (air movement)** sweeps the moist air away, making room for dry air to absorb more vapor.
- **Low humidity (dry air)** creates a "thirst" in the atmosphere for more moisture.

Evaporation from a reservoir works **exactly the same way**:
- The **reservoir is the wet towel** (water source).
- The **sun** is the energy input (latent heat of vaporization).
- The **wind** is the turbulent transport mechanism.
- The **humidity deficit** is the driving force (vapor pressure difference).

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Intuition Check:** If you cover the towel with a plastic sheet (no wind, saturated air just above), it stays wet for a long time. This is why **shallow, sheltered ponds** in Kerala lose far less water to evaporation than large, exposed reservoirs.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and units are **frequently tested** in KTU examinations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of vaporization (at 20┬░C) | $\lambda$ | **2.45 ├Ś 10ŌüČ** | J/kg |
| Density of water | $\rho_w$ | **1000** | kg/m┬│ |
| Specific heat of water | $C_w$ | **4.186 ├Ś 10┬│** | J/(kg┬ĘK) |
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüĖ** | W/(m┬▓┬ĘKŌü┤) |
| Psychrometric constant | $\gamma$ | **0.66** | mbar/┬░C |
| Standard pan area (Class A) | $A_p$ | **1.21** | m┬▓ (approx.) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always remember the latent heat value $\lambda = 2.45$ MJ/kg ŌĆö it appears in **almost every numerical problem** related to energy budget and Penman equations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factors Affecting Evaporation

The rate of evaporation from a water body is governed by three broad categories of factors. Understanding these is critical for the **7-mark short essay questions** that frequently appear in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).

**A. Meteorological Factors (Atmospheric drivers):**
- **Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD):** The difference between saturation vapor pressure ($e_s$) at water-surface temperature and actual vapor pressure ($e_a$) of overlying air. This is the **primary driving force**.
- **Air Temperature:** Higher temperatures increase $e_s$ exponentially (Clausius-Clapeyron relation), thereby increasing the deficit.
- **Solar Radiation (Net Radiation):** Provides the **latent heat** required for phase change. Without it, evaporation would stop.
- **Wind Speed:** Removes the saturated boundary layer of air just above the water surface, maintaining the vapor pressure gradient.
- **Atmospheric Pressure:** Lower pressure at high altitudes facilitates easier molecular escape (e.g., Munnar hill stations).

**B. Surface/Water Body Factors:**
- **Water Quality:** Dissolved salts reduce evaporation (sea water evaporates ~2-3% slower than fresh water).
- **Size of Water Body:** Larger bodies develop internal wind patterns and have lower "shelter effect."
- **Depth:** Shallow lakes heat up faster ŌåÆ more evaporation.
- **Surface Roughness:** Rippled surfaces increase surface area exposed.

**C. Geographic/Topographic Factors:**
- Latitude, altitude, surrounding vegetation, exposure to sun.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' ŌĆö Operational Logic

The evaporation process can be conceptualized as a three-step chain:
1. **Energy Supply:** Net radiation $R_n$ delivers the energy required to overcome latent heat of vaporization $\lambda$.
2. **Vapor Pressure Gradient:** The difference $(e_s - e_a)$ acts as the "thermodynamic pull" driving water molecules upward.
3. **Turbulent Transport:** Wind and atmospheric turbulence physically carry the vapor away from the boundary layer.

If **any one** of these three conditions is restricted, evaporation slows down ŌĆö this is the foundation of all estimation equations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam essential**. Memorize these for both Part A and Part B questions.

| Method | Core Formula | Key Variables | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dalton's Law** | $E = C(e_s - e_a)$ | $C$ = empirical constant, $e_s$, $e_a$ = vapor pressures (mbar) | Basic mass-transfer estimation |
| **Meyer's Formula** | $E = C(e_s - e_a)\left(1 + \dfrac{V}{16}\right)$ | $V$ = wind speed in km/h at 9 m height | Most common in KTU numericals |
| **Penman's Equation** | $E = \dfrac{\Delta}{\Delta + \gamma} \cdot \dfrac{R_n}{\lambda} + \dfrac{\gamma}{\Delta + \gamma} \cdot f(V)(e_s - e_a)$ | $\Delta$ = slope of SVP curve, $\gamma$ = psychrometric constant | Combined energy-aerodynamic method |
| **Water Balance** | $E = P - R - S_g - \Delta S$ | $P$ = precipitation, $R$ = runoff, $S_g$ = seepage, $\Delta S$ = storage change | For a known reservoir over a period |
| **Energy Budget** | $R_n = E\lambda + H + G$ | $H$ = sensible heat, $G$ = heat to ground | Bowen ratio approach |
| **Pan Coefficient** | $E_{lake} = C_p \times E_{pan}$ | $C_p$ = pan coefficient (0.7ŌĆō0.8 for Class A) | Convert pan reading to lake evaporation |
| **Bowen's Ratio** | $\beta = \dfrac{H}{E\lambda} = 0.61 \cdot \dfrac{(T_s - T_a)}{(e_s - e_a)} \cdot \dfrac{P}{1000}$ | $T_s, T_a$ = surface and air temps (┬░C) | Partition net radiation |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Units Convention in KTU:** Wind speed $V$ in Meyer's formula **must be in km/h at 9 m height**. If given in m/s or at a different height, use the **1/7th power law** to convert. Many students lose 2 marks for unit mistakes!

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Evaporation estimation is not merely academic ŌĆö it directly impacts:
- **Reservoir Design (Idukki, Mullaperiyar):** Accurate evaporation loss estimation is critical for dead-storage and yield calculations.
- **Irrigation Scheduling (Kuttanad, Palakkad):** Paddy fields lose 5ŌĆō10 mm/day; canal design must account for conveyance + evaporation losses.
- **Water Supply Schemes:** Drinking water reservoirs in Kerala lose ~1500ŌĆō2000 mm/year to evaporation.
- **Climate Studies:** Evaporation trends help model drought conditions in the Western Ghats.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Dalton's Law of Evaporation

Dalton's law forms the **foundation** of all mass-transfer evaporation equations. Let us derive it step-by-step from first principles.

**Starting Physical Principle:**
The rate of evaporation is directly proportional to the difference between the saturation vapor pressure at the water-surface temperature ($e_s$) and the actual vapor pressure of the overlying air ($e_a$).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the proportionality:**

$$E \propto (e_s - e_a)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Introduce a proportionality constant $C$ (which is NOT universal; it depends on local conditions like wind, turbulence, and atmospheric pressure):**

$$E = C \cdot (e_s - e_a)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognize limitations. Dalton's constant $C$ was found to vary with barometric pressure $P$ (in mbar). For a more general form, we write:**

$$E = \dfrac{C'}{P} \cdot (e_s - e_a)$$

where $C'$ is a regional constant (often back-calculated from pan data) and $P$ is atmospheric pressure in mbar. This is **Dalton's Law** in its complete form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by P?** At higher altitudes, lower atmospheric pressure allows water molecules to escape more easily, so $C$ effectively increases. The division by $P$ normalizes this.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Meyer's Modification (1886):** Recognizing that wind speed $V$ significantly enhances evaporation, Meyer proposed:

$$E = C(e_s - e_a)\left(1 + \dfrac{V}{16}\right)$$

where $V$ is the mean wind speed in **km/h measured at 9 m height** and $C$ is a coefficient depending on the size of the water body:
- For **large deep water**: $C = 0.36$ (for $E$ in mm/day, $e$ in mbar)
- For **small shallow water**: $C = 0.50$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Wind Speed Conversion (1/7th Power Law)

Wind speeds measured at heights other than 9 m must be corrected using the **1/7th power law** (also called the Prandtl velocity profile).

**Starting Relation:**

$$\dfrac{V_z}{V_9} = \left(\dfrac{z}{9}\right)^{1/7}$$

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express wind speed at desired height $z$ in terms of reference height 9 m:**

$$V_9 = \dfrac{V_z}{\left(\dfrac{z}{9}\right)^{1/7}} = V_z \cdot \left(\dfrac{9}{z}\right)^{1/7}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into Meyer's Equation:**

$$E = C(e_s - e_a)\left[1 + \dfrac{V_z}{16}\left(\dfrac{9}{z}\right)^{1/7}\right]$$

**Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style):**
Given: $V_2 = 10$ km/h (measured at 2 m), $e_s = 30$ mbar, $e_a = 20$ mbar, $C = 0.36$ (large water body). Find $E$ in mm/day.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert $V_2$ to $V_9$:**

$$V_9 = 10 \times \left(\dfrac{9}{2}\right)^{1/7} = 10 \times (4.5)^{0.1429}$$

$$V_9 = 10 \times 1.236 = 12.36 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Meyer's Equation:**

$$E = 0.36 \times (30 - 20) \times \left(1 + \dfrac{12.36}{16}\right)$$

$$E = 0.36 \times 10 \times (1 + 0.7725)$$

$$E = 0.36 \times 10 \times 1.7725$$

$$E = 6.381 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sanity check:** A value of 6.4 mm/day is realistic for tropical Indian conditions (Kerala average: 4ŌĆō6 mm/day). Ō£ō

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pan Evaporimeter Data Reduction

| Pan Type | Diameter / Area | Pan Coefficient ($C_p$) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class A Pan (USWB) | 1.21 m dia, 25.5 cm deep | **0.70 ŌĆō 0.80** | Standard reference, white painted |
| ISI Standard Pan | 1.22 m dia, 25.5 cm deep | **0.80** | Used in India, similar to Class A |
| Colorado Sunken Pan | 0.92 m dia, 46 cm deep | **0.78 ŌĆō 0.85** | Insulated, sunken to reduce radiation error |
| USSR GGI-3000 Pan | 61.8 cm dia, 60 cm deep, sunken | **0.81 ŌĆō 0.87** | Cylindrical, buried in ground |

**Conversion Formula:**

$$E_{lake} = C_p \times E_{pan}$$

where $C_p$ is dimensionless. The lake evaporation is **always less** than pan evaporation because pans have higher side-wall exposure and radiation absorption.

### 3.4 Derivative: Evaporation Energy Budget

The **energy balance** for a water body over a time period $\Delta t$ is:

$$R_n = E\lambda + H + G + \Delta Q$$

**Where:**
- $R_n$ = net radiation absorbed (MJ/m┬▓)
- $E\lambda$ = energy used for evaporation (latent heat)
- $H$ = sensible heat transfer to air
- $G$ = heat conducted to/from the water body bottom
- $\Delta Q$ = change in stored energy

**Bowen's Ratio** partitions $H$ and $E\lambda$:

$$\beta = \dfrac{H}{E\lambda} = 0.61 \cdot \dfrac{P}{1000} \cdot \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{e_s - e_a}$$

**Then:**

$$E = \dfrac{R_n - G - \Delta Q}{\lambda(1 + \beta)}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **most precise** field method. KTU often asks: "If $R_n = 15$ MJ/m┬▓/day, $\beta = 0.2$, $G + \Delta Q = 1$ MJ/m┬▓/day, find $E$." Practice this format.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Hydrological Cycle with Evaporation Component

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of evaporation within the broader hydrological cycle, as commonly tested in KTU descriptive questions.

For a physical cross-section drawing, we use a **block-level functional architecture** representation, since Mermaid cannot render true physical drawings.


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] Define evaporation. List the meteorological factors affecting the rate of evaporation.**

**Model Answer:**

Evaporation is the process by which water is converted from its liquid state to vapor state at a temperature below the boiling point and is transferred from the evaporating surface to the atmosphere. The principal meteorological factors affecting evaporation are:

1. **Vapor Pressure Deficit** $(e_s - e_a)$ ŌĆö the difference between saturation and actual vapor pressures.
2. **Air and Water-Surface Temperature** ŌĆö higher temperatures increase the saturation vapor pressure.
3. **Wind Speed** ŌĆö removes the saturated boundary layer and maintains the vapor pressure gradient.
4. **Atmospheric Pressure** ŌĆö lower pressure enhances evaporation.
5. **Solar Radiation** ŌĆö supplies the latent heat of vaporization required for phase change.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] What is meant by the 'Pan Coefficient'? State the typical value of the pan coefficient for a Class A Pan.**

**Model Answer:**

The pan coefficient $C_p$ is a dimensionless factor that relates the measured pan evaporation to the actual evaporation from a large natural water body (lake/reservoir). It is defined as:

$$C_p = \dfrac{E_{lake}}{E_{pan}}$$

For a **Class A Pan** (US Weather Bureau standard), the typical value of the pan coefficient ranges from **0.70 to 0.80**, with a commonly adopted design value of **0.75** in Indian conditions. The lake evaporation is always less than the pan evaporation because the pan has higher side-wall radiation absorption and greater exposure.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain Dalton's Law of evaporation. State the limitations of the law and show how Meyer's formula overcomes these limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Dalton's Law (1802) states that the rate of evaporation from a water surface is directly proportional to the difference between the saturation vapor pressure at the water-surface temperature ($e_s$) and the actual vapor pressure of the overlying air ($e_a$). The mathematical form is:

$$E = C(e_s - e_a)$$

where $C$ is an empirical constant that depends on local conditions.

**Limitations of Dalton's Law:**
1. It does not explicitly account for **wind speed**, which is one of the most influential factors in field conditions.
2. The constant $C$ is **not universal** and must be determined for each region.
3. It neglects the effect of **atmospheric pressure** variation with altitude.
4. It assumes a **steady-state** condition, which is rarely the case in nature.

**Meyer's Modification (1886):**
Meyer introduced a wind-speed correction term, leading to:

$$E = C(e_s - e_a)\left(1 + \dfrac{V}{16}\right)$$

where $V$ is the wind speed in km/h at 9 m height, and $C$ takes values:
- $C = 0.36$ for large deep water bodies
- $C = 0.50$ for small shallow water bodies

The factor $\left(1 + \dfrac{V}{16}\right)$ directly addresses limitation (1) by incorporating wind effects, and the dual values of $C$ partially address limitation (2) by providing a size-based calibration.

---

**Q. (b)** A Class A pan has the following observations over a 7-day period. The pan water level dropped by 28 mm, and rainfall recorded during the period was 15 mm. Estimate the lake evaporation using a pan coefficient of 0.78. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Drop in pan water level = 28 mm (over 7 days)
- Rainfall = 15 mm
- Pan coefficient $C_p = 0.78$
- Period = 7 days

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate Pan Evaporation (Observed):**
Pan evaporation accounts only for the actual water loss, not the rainfall input. The observed drop in water level includes both evaporation and rainfall effect. Therefore:

$$E_{pan, \text{observed}} = \text{Water level drop} - \text{Rainfall}$$

$$E_{pan, \text{observed}} = 28 - 15 = 13 \text{ mm over 7 days}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert to Daily Pan Evaporation:**

$$E_{pan, \text{daily}} = \dfrac{13}{7} = 1.857 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Pan Coefficient to Get Lake Evaporation:**

$$E_{lake, \text{daily}} = C_p \times E_{pan, \text{daily}} = 0.78 \times 1.857$$

$$E_{lake, \text{daily}} = 1.449 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Express the Final Answer:**

$$E_{lake, \text{daily}} \approx 1.45 \text{ mm/day} \quad \text{or} \quad 10.14 \text{ mm/week}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Supplementary 2023]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the components and working of a Class A Evaporation Pan. What are the advantages and disadvantages of using a sunken pan over an exposed pan? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Class A Pan ŌĆö Components:**
A Class A Pan is a cylindrical tank of galvanized iron, **121 cm (4 ft) in diameter** and **25.5 cm (10 in) deep**, mounted on a wooden frame **10 cm above ground level** to allow air circulation beneath. It is painted white externally to reflect radiation. The water depth is maintained between **5 cm and 20 cm**.

**Working Principle:**
1. The pan is filled with water to a standard level.
2. A **stilling well with a hook gauge** is placed inside to measure the water level accurately without disturbance.
3. Daily observations are taken at a fixed time (typically 08:30 IST).
4. The change in water level over 24 hours, corrected for any rainfall (measured using a nearby rain gauge), gives the daily pan evaporation.
5. Formula used: $E_{pan} = P + \Delta h$ where $P$ = precipitation, $\Delta h$ = observed drop in level.

**Advantages of Sunken Pans (e.g., Colorado Pan):**
1. The pan rim is at ground level, **minimizing heat exchange** through the side walls ŌĆö this gives readings closer to actual lake evaporation.
2. **Reduced radiation error** because the side walls are shielded by the surrounding soil.
3. **Lower pan coefficient variation** ŌĆö readings are more representative of the natural water body.
4. **Better simulation** of actual lake thermal and aerodynamic conditions.

**Disadvantages of Sunken Pans:**
1. **Installation is costly** ŌĆö requires excavation and insulation.
2. **Water level measurement is harder** since the pan is at ground level.
3. **Difficult to clean and maintain.**
4. **Splashing and inflow** from surrounding ground can introduce errors.

---

**Q. (b)** The following data are obtained from a meteorological station near a reservoir:

- Mean wind speed at 2 m height: $V_2 = 12$ km/h
- Saturation vapor pressure at water surface: $e_s = 32.4$ mbar
- Actual vapor pressure of air: $e_a = 21.8$ mbar
- Water body type: large deep reservoir

Estimate the daily evaporation using Meyer's formula. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $V_2 = 12$ km/h (at 2 m height)
- $e_s = 32.4$ mbar
- $e_a = 21.8$ mbar
- $C = 0.36$ (large deep water body)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert Wind Speed to 9 m Reference Height using 1/7th Power Law:**

$$V_9 = V_2 \times \left(\dfrac{9}{2}\right)^{1/7}$$

$$V_9 = 12 \times (4.5)^{0.1429}$$

$$(4.5)^{0.1429} = e^{0.1429 \times \ln(4.5)} = e^{0.1429 \times 1.5041} = e^{0.2149} = 1.2397$$

$$V_9 = 12 \times 1.2397 = 14.876 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Vapor Pressure Deficit:**

$$\Delta e = e_s - e_a = 32.4 - 21.8 = 10.6 \text{ mbar}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Meyer's Formula:**

$$E = C \cdot (e_s - e_a) \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{V_9}{16}\right)$$

$$E = 0.36 \times 10.6 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{14.876}{16}\right)$$

$$E = 0.36 \times 10.6 \times (1 + 0.9298)$$

$$E = 0.36 \times 10.6 \times 1.9298$$

$$E = 7.364 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{E \approx 7.36 \text{ mm/day}}$$

**Sanity Check:** This value is realistic for a tropical, windy reservoir location in India. The result is consistent with the typical 4ŌĆō8 mm/day range observed in Indian water bodies.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit of Wind Speed:** Forgetting to convert m/s to km/h OR not converting height to 9 m. **Penalty: 1ŌĆō2 marks.**
> 2. **Sign Convention in Water Balance:** Evaporation appears as a negative term in inflow-outflow analysis. Students often forget the negative sign. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Pan Coefficient Direction:** Students multiply $E_{lake}$ by $C_p$ to get $E_{pan}$, which is **backwards**. Always: $E_{lake} = C_p \times E_{pan}$ where $C_p < 1$. **Penalty: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Confusing $e_s$ and $e_a$:** Saturation vapor pressure $e_s$ is at the **water surface temperature**, while $e_a$ is at the **air (dew point) temperature**. Swapping them gives negative answers. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 5. **In Meyer's formula, omitting the "+1":** Some students write $E = C(e_s - e_a) \cdot \dfrac{V}{16}$ and lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Not using SI units in Energy Budget:** Mixing cal/cm┬▓ and MJ/m┬▓ in the same equation. **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Memorize):**

- **Evaporation** is a *vaporization* process from a free water surface at sub-boiling temperatures.
- **Three requirements** for evaporation: (i) Energy supply, (ii) Vapor pressure gradient $(e_s - e_a) > 0$, (iii) Wind/turbulence to remove vapor.
- **Dalton's Law:** $E = C(e_s - e_a)$ ŌĆö simplest, ignores wind.
- **Meyer's Formula:** $E = C(e_s - e_a)\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{9m}}{16}\right)$ ŌĆö includes wind correction. **MOST TESTED** in KTU.
- **Wind conversion:** Use 1/7th power law: $V_9 = V_z \left(\dfrac{9}{z}\right)^{1/7}$.
- **Meyer's C-values:** $C = 0.36$ (large deep) or $C = 0.50$ (small shallow).
- **Pan Coefficient:** $C_p = E_{lake}/E_{pan}$, with $C_p = 0.70$ to $0.80$ for Class A Pan. **Always less than 1.**
- **Class A Pan specs:** 121 cm dia, 25.5 cm deep, white painted, mounted on wooden frame 10 cm above ground.
- **Penman Equation:** Combines energy budget + mass transfer; uses $\Delta$, $\gamma$, $R_n$, $f(V)$.
- **Latent heat of vaporization:** $\lambda = 2.45 \times 10^6$ J/kg (memorize!).
- **Water Balance:** $E = P - R - S_g - \Delta S$ (applied over a chosen period).
- **Bowen's Ratio:** $\beta = 0.61 \cdot (P/1000) \cdot (T_s - T_a)/(e_s - e_a)$ ŌĆö used in energy budget method.
- **Units of E in KTU:** Always **mm/day** unless stated otherwise.
- **Reduction methods:** Wind barriers, surface films (cetyl/stearyl alcohol), shading, floating covers.
- **Piche Evaporimeter:** Measures evaporation from a porous paper disc, not a free water surface ŌĆö used for relative comparison.
- **Kerala-specific data:** Average annual lake evaporation in Kerala Ōēł 1500ŌĆō1800 mm; peak in MarchŌĆōMay.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Runoff - Factors Affecting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Runoff - Factors Affecting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Runoff

> [!NOTE]
> **RUNOFF (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> Runoff is the portion of precipitation (rainfall or snowmelt) that flows over the land surface through surface and sub-surface pathways and ultimately reaches a stream, river, lake, reservoir, or ocean. It is the residual water left after accounting for evaporation, transpiration, infiltration, surface detention, and depression storage in the hydrological cycle.

Mathematically, the **Water Balance Equation** governing runoff over a watershed for a given time period is expressed as:

$$P = R + E + T + I + \Delta S$$

Where:
- $P$ = Total Precipitation (mm)
- $R$ = Total Runoff (mm) ŌĆö the **direct response** we wish to quantify
- $E$ = Evaporation losses (mm)
- $T$ = Transpiration losses (mm)
- $I$ = Infiltration losses (mm)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in watershed storage (mm)

The total runoff $R$ is often split into three components:

$$R = R_{surface} + R_{subsurface} + R_{baseflow}$$

Where:
- $R_{surface}$ = Overland flow (Hortonian flow / Saturation excess flow)
- $R_{subsurface}$ = Interflow (throughflow in the unsaturated zone)
- $R_{baseflow}$ = Groundwater contribution to streams during dry periods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU BOARD EXAMINATION FOCUS:** Direct definition of runoff and identification of factors affecting runoff is a guaranteed **2-mark short answer** and forms the base of every long-answer numerical/descriptive question in Module 2.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Leaky Bathtub"

Imagine a giant **bathtub** (your watershed) standing in an open field during a rainstorm (precipitation). Several things happen simultaneously:

- **Water splashes out the top** ŌåÆ This is **surface runoff** (Hortonian flow).
- **Water seeps through the bathtub floor** ŌåÆ This is **infiltration** into the soil.
- **Water evaporates into the air** ŌåÆ This is **evapotranspiration** loss.
- **Water slowly trickles out of a small crack at the bottom** ŌåÆ This is **baseflow** (groundwater contribution).
- **Some water remains stuck in the corners and rim** ŌåÆ This is **depression storage and detention**.

The water that **reaches the stream** at the end of the storm is the **runoff** ŌĆö the bathtub's "spillway" discharge. The shape, material, and condition of the bathtub (watershed characteristics) and the storm's intensity (climatic factors) determine exactly how much water spills out.

### 1.3 Classification of Runoff by Pathway

| Runoff Type | Pathway | Travel Time | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface Runoff (Overland Flow)** | Flows over ground surface | Minutes to hours | Largest contribution to flood peaks |
| **Interflow (Throughflow)** | Lateral flow through unsaturated soil | Hours to days | Significant in humid watersheds |
| **Baseflow (Groundwater Runoff)** | Emerges from saturated aquifers | Days to years | Maintains streamflow in dry periods |
| **Direct Runoff** | Surface + quick interflow | Storm duration | Used in unit hydrograph analysis |
| **Total Runoff** | All components | Variable | Used in water balance studies |

> [!NOTE]
> **STANDARD METRICS** ŌĆö Runoff is universally expressed in:
> - **Depth units:** millimetres (mm) or centimetres (cm) over the watershed area
> - **Volume units:** cubic metres (m┬│) or Million Cubic Metres (MCM)
> - **Rate units:** cumecs (m┬│/s) or litres per second
>
> Conversion: **Runoff volume = Runoff depth ├Ś Watershed area**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Framework ŌĆö The Four Pillars Governing Runoff

Every factor influencing runoff at any watershed can be grouped into **four fundamental pillars** (the KTU examiner's favourite classification):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE 4 PILLARS OF RUNOFF FACTORS (KTU Mnemonic ŌĆö "C-W-S-V")**
> 1. **C** ŌĆö Climatic (Atmospheric) Factors
> 2. **W** ŌĆö Watershed (Physiographic) Factors
> 3. **S** ŌĆö Soil & Geological Factors
> 4. **V** ŌĆö Vegetation & Land Use Factors

### 2.2 PILLAR 1 ŌĆö Climatic Factors (Atmospheric)

These are the **input drivers** of the runoff process. They govern the *quantity* and *intensity* of water supplied to the watershed.

#### 2.2.1 Type of Precipitation
- **Rainfall** contributes almost immediately to runoff.
- **Snow** is stored temporarily ŌĆö runoff occurs during snowmelt (lag effect).
- **Hail/Sleet** behaves like snow; contribution is delayed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KEY RULE:** A rainfall intensity exceeding the soil's infiltration capacity produces **Hortonian overland flow**.

#### 2.2.2 Intensity of Precipitation
- High intensity ŌåÆ exceeds infiltration rate ŌåÆ **more surface runoff**.
- Low intensity ŌåÆ fully infiltrates ŌåÆ **negligible surface runoff**.

#### 2.2.3 Duration of Precipitation
- Longer duration on a saturated watershed ŌåÆ greater runoff depth.
- If duration > **time of concentration** ($t_c$), entire watershed contributes ŌåÆ **peak runoff**.

#### 2.2.4 Areal Distribution of Rainfall
- Uniform rainfall over the whole watershed produces **higher peak runoff** than an equal amount concentrated over part of it.

#### 2.2.5 Direction of Storm Movement
- Storm moving **downstream (with flow direction)** ŌåÆ **higher peak**, sharper hydrograph.
- Storm moving **upstream (against flow direction)** ŌåÆ **lower, delayed peak**, flatter hydrograph.

#### 2.2.6 Antecedent Precipitation Index (API)
$$API_t = API_0 \cdot k^t + P_t$$

Where $k$ = recession constant (typically 0.85ŌĆō0.95) and $t$ = time in days. High API ŌåÆ wet watershed ŌåÆ **more runoff** from a new storm.

#### 2.2.7 Temperature, Humidity, Wind
- **High temperature** ŌåÆ more evaporation ŌåÆ less runoff.
- **Low humidity + high wind** ŌåÆ more ET losses ŌåÆ reduced runoff.
- **Temperature > 0┬░C** triggers snowmelt, generating significant seasonal runoff.

---

### 2.3 PILLAR 2 ŌĆö Watershed (Physiographic) Factors

These are the **basin geometry** characteristics ŌĆö they determine *how fast* runoff concentrates at the outlet.

#### 2.3.1 Size (Area) of Watershed
- **Small watershed** ŌåÆ quick response, sharp peak, short lag time.
- **Large watershed** ŌåÆ delayed response, attenuated peak, longer lag time.

#### 2.3.2 Shape of Watershed
| Shape | Flow Concentration | Peak Discharge |
|---|---|---|
| **Fan-shaped (Radial)** | Fast | **High** |
| **Elongated (Fern-shaped)** | Slow | **Low** |

#### 2.3.3 Slope
- **Steep slopes** ŌåÆ high overland flow velocity ŌåÆ **less infiltration time** ŌåÆ more runoff, faster arrival at outlet.
- **Gentle/flat slopes** ŌåÆ more ponding and infiltration ŌåÆ reduced runoff.

#### 2.3.4 Orientation / Aspect
- South-facing slopes (in Northern Hemisphere) receive more solar radiation ŌåÆ higher ET ŌåÆ slightly less runoff.

#### 2.3.5 Drainage Density ($D_d$)
$$D_d = \frac{\sum L_i}{A}$$

Where $\sum L_i$ = total length of all streams (km), $A$ = watershed area (km┬▓). **Higher $D_d$ ŌåÆ more efficient drainage ŌåÆ higher peak runoff.**

---

### 2.4 PILLAR 3 ŌĆö Soil & Geological Factors

These control the **infiltration capacity**, which is the *key determinant* of how much precipitation becomes runoff.

#### 2.4.1 Soil Texture & Structure
- **Sandy soils** (high permeability) ŌåÆ high infiltration ŌåÆ **low runoff**.
- **Clayey soils** (low permeability) ŌåÆ low infiltration ŌåÆ **high runoff**.

#### 2.4.2 Soil Depth
- Deep soils ŌåÆ larger storage capacity ŌåÆ reduced runoff.
- Shallow soils over bedrock ŌåÆ rapid saturation ŌåÆ high runoff.

#### 2.4.3 Permeability and Porosity
- High permeability ŌåÆ water moves downward ŌåÆ less surface runoff.
- Impermeable layers (e.g., hard rock) ŌåÆ water flows laterally ŌåÆ more interflow and surface runoff.

#### 2.4.4 Initial Moisture Content
- **Dry soil** ŌåÆ high infiltration capacity ŌåÆ low initial runoff.
- **Wet/saturated soil** ŌåÆ low infiltration capacity ŌåÆ high runoff (this is the **antecedent moisture condition**).

#### 2.4.5 Geological Formations
- Karst terrain (limestone) ŌåÆ high infiltration via sinkholes ŌåÆ minimal surface runoff.
- Igneous/metamorphic bedrock ŌåÆ low infiltration ŌåÆ high runoff.

---

### 2.5 PILLAR 4 ŌĆö Vegetation & Land Use Factors

These are the **modifiable** factors ŌĆö they are the primary tools of watershed management.

#### 2.5.1 Vegetation Cover
- **Forests:** Intercept rainfall, promote infiltration via root channels, extract moisture via transpiration ŌåÆ **reduces peak runoff by 10ŌĆō30%**.
- **Grasslands:** Moderate interception, moderate infiltration.
- **Bare soil:** Minimal interception, low infiltration ŌåÆ **maximum surface runoff**.

#### 2.5.2 Land Use Patterns
- **Urban areas** (impervious surfaces: roads, buildings) ŌåÆ near-zero infiltration ŌåÆ **dramatic increase in runoff volume (2├Ś to 5├Ś) and peak**.
- **Agricultural land** (ploughed fields) ŌåÆ reduced infiltration vs forest ŌåÆ moderate runoff.
- **Wetlands/Lakes** ŌåÆ act as natural reservoirs ŌåÆ **attenuate floods**.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table ŌĆö these equations are the most-asked in KTU University Exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = P - (E + T + I + \Delta S)$ | Water balance runoff | Direct definition problems |
| 2 | $R = C \cdot P$ | Rational Method (runoff depth) | $C$ = runoff coefficient |
| 3 | $Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | Rational Method (peak flow) | $I$ = rainfall intensity, $A$ = area |
| 4 | $f = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)e^{-kt}$ | **Horton's infiltration equation** | Infiltration capacity decay |
| 5 | $F(t) = f_c \cdot t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}(1 - e^{-kt})$ | Cumulative infiltration (Horton) | Total infiltration depth |
| 6 | $\phi_{index} = \frac{P_{eff} - R}{t_e}$ | **$\phi$-index method** | Average infiltration rate |
| 7 | $W_{index} = \frac{P - R - S}{t_r}$ | **$W$-index method** | Includes surface detention $S$ |
| 8 | $S = \frac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | **SCS-CN method** potential retention | $S$ in mm, $CN$ = curve number |
| 9 | $R = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$ for $P \geq 0.2S$ | **SCS-CN runoff depth** | Most-used KTU runoff formula |
| 10 | $API_t = API_0 \cdot k^t + P_t$ | Antecedent Precipitation Index | Climatic factor analysis |
| 11 | $D_d = \frac{\sum L_i}{A}$ | Drainage density | Physiographic factor analysis |
| 12 | $R_{volume} = R_{depth} \times A$ | Volume conversion | Unit conversion problems |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility of Runoff Analysis

| Application | Use of Runoff Data |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Structure Design** | Sizing of dams, spillways, culverts, bridges |
| **Flood Forecasting** | Real-time flood warning systems |
| **Urban Stormwater Design** | Storm sewer network design |
| **Irrigation Planning** | Water availability at command areas |
| **Water Supply** | Reservoir yield estimation |
| **Watershed Management** | Evaluating effects of afforestation/deforestation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Horton's Infiltration Equation (Exponential Decay Model)

Horton (1933) observed that infiltration capacity $f$ **decreases exponentially** with time $t$ as the soil becomes wetter.

**Step 1:** State the basic differential relationship.

The rate of change of infiltration capacity is proportional to the difference between current capacity and the final (steady) capacity:

$$\frac{df}{dt} = -k(f - f_c)$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables to set up integration.

$$df / (f - f_c) = -k \cdot dt$$

**Step 3:** Apply initial condition. At $t = 0$, $f = f_0$ (initial infiltration capacity).

Integrating from $f_0$ to $f$ on LHS, and from $0$ to $t$ on RHS:

$$\int_{f_0}^{f} \frac{df}{f - f_c} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integrals.

$$\left[\ln(f - f_c)\right]_{f_0}^{f} = -k \cdot t$$

$$\ln(f - f_c) - \ln(f_0 - f_c) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 5:** Combine logarithms.

$$\ln\left(\frac{f - f_c}{f_0 - f_c}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate both sides.

$$\frac{f - f_c}{f_0 - f_c} = e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $f$ ŌĆö the **Horton's Infiltration Capacity Equation**:

$$\boxed{f = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}}$$

**Step 8:** Derive the cumulative infiltration $F(t)$ by integrating capacity over time.

$$F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f \cdot dt = \int_{0}^{t} \left[ f_c + (f_0 - f_c)e^{-k \cdot t} \right] dt$$

**Step 9:** Perform the integration term by term.

$$F(t) = f_c \cdot t + (f_0 - f_c) \left[ \frac{-e^{-k \cdot t}}{k} \right]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 10:** Apply the limits and simplify.

$$F(t) = f_c \cdot t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k} \cdot (1 - e^{-k \cdot t})$$

**This is the total depth of water infiltrated in time $t$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world application:** Horton's equation is used in the KTU syllabus to compute rainfall excess. When actual rainfall intensity $i > f$, the excess $i - f$ becomes surface runoff.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö SCS-CN Method (Soil Conservation Service Curve Number)

Developed by USDA-SCS (1972). It is the **single most-asked runoff formula** in KTU exams.

**Step 1:** Start with the basic proportional equality (the SCS hypothesis).

The actual retention $F$ is proportional to the potential maximum retention $S$:

$$\frac{F}{S} = \frac{Q}{P - I_a}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = direct runoff (mm) [what we want]
- $P$ = total precipitation (mm)
- $I_a$ = initial abstraction (mm)
- $F$ = actual retention = $P - Q - I_a$ (mm)
- $S$ = potential maximum retention (mm)

**Step 2:** Substitute $F = P - Q - I_a$ into the proportional equation.

$$\frac{P - Q - I_a}{S} = \frac{Q}{P - I_a}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply.

$$(P - Q - I_a)(P - I_a) = Q \cdot S$$

**Step 4:** Expand the left side.

$$(P - I_a)^2 - Q(P - I_a) = Q \cdot S$$

**Step 5:** Group all $Q$ terms on one side.

$$(P - I_a)^2 = Q \cdot S + Q(P - I_a) = Q[S + (P - I_a)]$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $Q$.

$$Q = \frac{(P - I_a)^2}{S + (P - I_a)}$$

**Step 7:** Apply the empirical simplification. From analysis of many watersheds, SCS found that $I_a \approx 0.2S$.

Substitute $I_a = 0.2S$:

$$Q = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{S + (P - 0.2S)} = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$$

**Step 8:** Define $S$ in terms of the **Curve Number (CN)**:

$$S = \frac{25400}{CN} - 254 \quad \text{(S in mm)}$$

**Step 9:** Final consolidated formula ŌĆö **The SCS-CN Equation**:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S} \quad \text{valid for } P \geq 0.2S}$$

> If $P < 0.2S$, then $Q = 0$ (insufficient rainfall to produce direct runoff).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CN TABLE (KTU Quick Reference):**
> - CN = 100 ŌåÆ Completely impervious (concrete, water body)
> - CN = 75ŌĆō95 ŌåÆ Urban / paved areas
> - CN = 60ŌĆō75 ŌåÆ Agricultural / pasture land
> - CN = 40ŌĆō60 ŌåÆ Forest cover
> - CN = 30ŌĆō40 ŌåÆ Sandy / highly permeable soils

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö $\phi$-Index Method (Simplified Runoff Calculation)

The $\phi$-index is the **average rainfall intensity** above which all rainfall becomes runoff.

**Step 1:** Write the runoff equation. Only rainfall intensity $i > \phi$ contributes to runoff.

$$R = \sum (i - \phi) \cdot \Delta t \quad \text{for } i > \phi$$

**Step 2:** For continuous rainfall with uniform intensity $i$ over duration $t_e$:

$$R = (i - \phi) \cdot t_e$$

**Step 3:** Apply water balance: $P = R + \text{(infiltration + losses)}$

If infiltration occurs at rate $\phi$ for the **effective duration** $t_e$ (rainfall duration producing runoff):

$$\phi = \frac{P - R}{t_e}$$

Where:
- $P$ = total storm rainfall (mm)
- $R$ = total runoff (mm) = direct runoff depth
- $t_e$ = effective duration (hours)

**Step 4:** Rearrange to solve for runoff:

$$R = P - \phi \cdot t_e$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference between $\phi$-index and $W$-index:**
> The $W$-index also includes surface detention $S$ as a loss, while $\phi$-index considers only infiltration losses.
> $$W = \frac{P - R - S}{t_r}$$

---

### 3.4 Solved KTU Numerical Example

> **Example Problem:** A watershed of area 12 km┬▓ receives a uniform rainfall of 80 mm over 6 hours. The measured runoff at the outlet is 0.36 MCM. Calculate the **$\phi$-index** and the **W-index** (if surface detention is 10 mm).

**Given:**
- Watershed area $A = 12 \text{ km}^2 = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- Precipitation $P = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$
- Duration $t_r = 6 \text{ hours}$
- Runoff volume $V = 0.36 \text{ MCM} = 360,000 \text{ m}^3$
- Surface detention $S = 10 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1: Convert runoff volume to depth (mm).**

$$R = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{360,000 \text{ m}^3}{12 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2} = 0.030 \text{ m} = 30 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the $\phi$-index.**

$$\phi = \frac{P - R}{t_r} = \frac{80 - 30}{6} = \frac{50}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 8.33 \text{ mm/hr}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the W-index.**

$$W = \frac{P - R - S}{t_r} = \frac{80 - 30 - 10}{6} = \frac{40}{6}$$

$$\boxed{W = 6.67 \text{ mm/hr}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical check:** $\phi > W$ is always true because $\phi$-index excludes surface detention (it is treated as part of runoff-producing intensity).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Factor-by-Factor Impact on Runoff

| Factor | Change | Effect on Runoff Volume | Effect on Peak Runoff | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rainfall Intensity Ōåæ | Increases | ŌåæŌåæ | ŌåæŌåæŌåæ | High |
| Rainfall Duration Ōåæ | Increases | ŌåæŌåæ | Ōåæ | High |
| Watershed Area Ōåæ | Increases | ŌåæŌåæ | Ōåō (per unit area) | High |
| Slope Ōåæ | Increases | Ōåæ | ŌåæŌåæ | High |
| Drainage Density Ōåæ | Increases | ŌĆö | ŌåæŌåæ | Medium |
| Soil Permeability Ōåæ | Increases | ŌåōŌåōŌåō | ŌåōŌåō | High |
| Antecedent Moisture Ōåæ | Increases | ŌåæŌåæ | ŌåæŌåæ | High |
| Vegetation Cover Ōåæ | Decreases | ŌåōŌåō | ŌåōŌåō | High |
| Urbanization Ōåæ | Increases | ŌåæŌåæŌåæ | ŌåæŌåæŌåæ | High |
| Forest Cover Ōåæ | Decreases | Ōåō | Ōåō | Medium |
| Air Temperature Ōåæ | Variable | Ōåō (more ET) | Ōåō | Low |
| Wind Speed Ōåæ | Variable | Ōåō (more ET) | Ōåō | Low |
| Snow ŌåÆ Rain Ōåæ | Increases | Ōåæ | Ōåæ | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 ŌĆö The Hydrological Cycle & Runoff Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Precipitation P]:::inputNode --> A2[Interception by vegetation]
    A2 --> A3[Evaporation from canopy]
    A1 --> A4[Rain reaches ground surface]
    A4 --> A5{Soil Infiltration capacity check}
    A5 -->|i greater than f| A6[Surface Runoff Hortonian flow]:::runoffNode
    A5 -->|i less than f| A7[Water enters soil]
    A7 --> A8[Soil moisture storage]
    A8 --> A9[Percolation to groundwater]
    A9 --> A10[Groundwater Aquifer]:::gwNode
    A10 --> A11[Baseflow to stream]:::runoffNode
    A8 --> A12[Lateral subsurface interflow]:::runoffNode
    A6 --> A13[Channel flow]:::runoffNode
    A11 --> A13
    A12 --> A13
    A13 --> A14[Stream Outlet Total Runoff R]:::outputNode
    A4 --> A15[Depression storage]
    A15 --> A14
    A4 --> A16[Evapotranspiration losses]:::lossNode
    A7 --> A16
    A15 --> A17[Surface Detention]:::lossNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef runoffNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gwNode fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lossNode fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 ŌĆö The Four Pillars of Factors Affecting Runoff

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Climatic Factors]:::pillar1
    P2[Watershed Physiographic Factors]:::pillar2
    P3[Soil and Geological Factors]:::pillar3
    P4[Vegetation and Land Use Factors]:::pillar4
    P5[RUNOFF RESPONSE]:::output

    P1 --> P1a[Rainfall type]
```
