<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
Define runoff. List any four climatic factors affecting runoff.

**Model Answer:**

**Runoff:** Runoff is the portion of precipitation that flows over the land surface through surface and sub-surface pathways and reaches a stream, river, lake, reservoir, or ocean. It is the residual precipitation after losses due to evaporation, transpiration, and infiltration.

**Water Balance Equation:**
$$P = R + E + T + I + \Delta S$$

**Four Climatic Factors Affecting Runoff:**

1. **Type of Precipitation** ŌĆö Rain produces immediate runoff; snow produces delayed runoff during snowmelt.
2. **Intensity of Precipitation** ŌĆö High intensity rainfall exceeds infiltration capacity, producing more surface runoff.
3. **Duration of Precipitation** ŌĆö Longer duration storms over saturated soils generate greater runoff depth.
4. **Direction of Storm Movement** ŌĆö Storms moving downstream produce sharper, higher peaks than upstream-moving storms.

*(Other acceptable factors: Temperature, Humidity, Wind, Antecedent Precipitation Index.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
What is the Curve Number? State the SCS-CN equation for runoff depth.

**Model Answer:**

The **Curve Number (CN)** is a dimensionless parameter developed by the USDA Soil Conservation Service (now NRCS) that characterises the runoff potential of a watershed based on its **soil type, land use, hydrologic condition, and antecedent moisture condition**. CN ranges from **0 (infinite infiltration)** to **100 (completely impervious)**.

**SCS-CN Equation for Runoff Depth:**

$$Q = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S} \quad \text{valid for } P \geq 0.2S$$

Where $Q$ = direct runoff depth (mm), $P$ = precipitation (mm), and $S$ = potential maximum retention (mm).

**Relation between S and CN:**

$$S = \frac{25400}{CN} - 254$$

If $P < 0.2S$, then $Q = 0$ (no direct runoff produced).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **physiographic (watershed) factors** affecting runoff. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 5 km┬▓ urban catchment has a **Curve Number of 85**. A storm delivers **120 mm of rainfall** in 24 hours. Compute the **direct runoff depth** using the SCS-CN method. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The physiographic (watershed) factors affecting runoff are:

1. **Size of Watershed:** Small watersheds have a quick hydrologic response with sharp flood peaks and short lag times. Large watersheds show delayed, attenuated peaks because runoff from distant parts takes longer to reach the outlet.

2. **Shape of Watershed:** Fan-shaped (radial) watersheds concentrate flow at the outlet almost simultaneously, producing high peaks. Elongated (fern-shaped) watersheds produce lower, broader peaks because flow contributions arrive at different times.

3. **Slope:** Steep terrain increases overland flow velocity, reduces infiltration time, and produces higher, faster peaks. Flat terrain allows more infiltration and depression storage, reducing runoff volume.

4. **Orientation and Aspect:** South-facing slopes (in the Northern Hemisphere) receive more solar radiation, increasing ET losses and slightly reducing runoff. North-facing slopes are cooler and wetter, producing slightly more runoff.

5. **Drainage Density ($D_d$):** Defined as $D_d = \sum L_i / A$, it measures the total stream length per unit area. High $D_d$ implies an efficient drainage network, shorter flow paths, and higher peak discharges.

6. **Topography and Elevation:** Mountainous terrain has steep slopes and thin soils, producing rapid runoff. Low-lying areas may have higher water tables and saturated soils, increasing runoff.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $A = 5 \text{ km}^2$, $CN = 85$, $P = 120 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1:** Compute potential maximum retention $S$.

$$S = \frac{25400}{CN} - 254 = \frac{25400}{85} - 254$$

$$S = 298.82 - 254 = 44.82 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute initial abstraction $I_a$.

$$I_a = 0.2S = 0.2 \times 44.82 = 8.96 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Check the applicability condition.

$$P = 120 \text{ mm} > 0.2S = 8.96 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

The SCS-CN equation is valid.

**Step 4:** Apply the SCS-CN formula.

$$Q = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S} = \frac{(120 - 8.96)^2}{120 + 35.86}$$

$$Q = \frac{(111.04)^2}{155.86} = \frac{12329.88}{155.86}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 79.11 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5:** Express the runoff volume for completeness.

$$V = Q \times A = 0.07911 \text{ m} \times 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 = 3.96 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3 = 0.396 \text{ MCM}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the **Horton's infiltration equation** starting from the basic differential form. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **effect of vegetation and land use on runoff** with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Horton's equation describes how the infiltration capacity of a soil decreases exponentially with time as the soil becomes saturated during a storm.

**Step 1:** Basic differential form. The rate of decrease of infiltration capacity is proportional to the difference between current capacity $f$ and the steady-state capacity $f_c$:

$$\frac{df}{dt} = -k(f - f_c)$$

where $k$ is a decay constant (1/time).

**Step 2:** Separate variables:

$$\frac{df}{f - f_c} = -k \cdot dt$$

**Step 3:** Integrate with limits. At $t = 0$, $f = f_0$ (initial infiltration capacity). At time $t$, $f = f$:

$$\int_{f_0}^{f} \frac{df}{f - f_c} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$\ln\left(\frac{f - f_c}{f_0 - f_c}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $f$:

$$\boxed{f = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-k \cdot t}}$$

**Step 6:** Cumulative infiltration $F(t)$ is obtained by integrating capacity over time:

$$F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f \, dt = f_c \cdot t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k}(1 - e^{-k \cdot t})$$

**Physical interpretation:** As $t \to \infty$, $f \to f_c$ (the soil approaches its minimum steady infiltration capacity). The decay constant $k$ controls how quickly this transition occurs. For $k = 0$, $f = f_0$ (no decay).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Vegetation and land use are the **most easily modified** factors affecting runoff, making them the primary targets of watershed management.

**1. Effect of Forest Cover:**
- Forest canopy **intercepts** 10ŌĆō30% of rainfall, which is later evaporated.
- **Root systems** create macropores that enhance infiltration capacity.
- **Leaf litter** protects the soil surface from raindrop impact, reducing surface crusting.
- Forests can **reduce peak runoff by 20ŌĆō50%** and increase lag time.
- Example: Deforested watersheds in the Western Ghats have shown **2ŌĆō3 times higher peak floods** compared to forested catchments of similar area.

**2. Effect of Grasslands:**
- Moderate interception (5ŌĆō15%); grass roots improve soil structure.
- Runoff reduction is moderate ŌĆö about 10ŌĆō25% compared to bare soil.

**3. Effect of Urbanisation:**
- Construction of **impervious surfaces** (rooftops, roads, pavements) drastically reduces infiltration.
- Studies show urbanisation can **increase annual runoff volume by 2ŌĆō5 times** and **peak discharge by 3ŌĆō10 times**.
- Stormwater drainage systems deliver water to streams **much faster** than natural flow paths.
- Example: Chennai (2015 floods) ŌĆö rapid urbanisation increased peak runoff in the Adyar and Cooum basins by 40ŌĆō60% compared to pre-urbanisation conditions.

**4. Effect of Agricultural Practices:**
- Ploughed bare fields ŌåÆ maximum runoff.
- **Contour bunding, terracing, and cover crops** reduce runoff by 30ŌĆō50%.
- Strip cropping alternates dense and sparse rows, slowing overland flow.

**5. Effect of Wetlands and Water Bodies:**
- Lakes, ponds, and marshes act as **natural flood storage reservoirs**.
- They **attenuate flood peaks** and increase evaporation losses.
- A 1% increase in wetland area can reduce regional peak discharge by approximately 5ŌĆō8%.

**Summary Table:**

| Land Use Type | Runoff Coefficient $C$ | Peak Runoff Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Dense Forest | 0.10ŌĆō0.20 | Very Low |
| Pasture / Grassland | 0.20ŌĆō0.35 | Low |
| Cultivated Land | 0.30ŌĆō0.50 | Moderate |
| Suburban Residential | 0.40ŌĆō0.60 | High |
| Urban / Paved | 0.70ŌĆō0.95 | Very High |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **In SCS-CN problems, ALWAYS check $P > 0.2S$ first.** Forgetting this yields negative or zero runoff without explanation ŌåÆ loses 2 marks.
> 2. **In Horton's derivation, students often write the integrated form as the final answer without showing the initial differential form.** KTU requires at least the differential equation as the starting point.
> 3. **Mixing up $\phi$-index and W-index definitions.** Remember: $\phi$ excludes surface detention; $W$ includes it. Both use **rainfall duration** in the denominator.
> 4. **For watershed factor questions, do not just list factors ŌĆö explain the mechanism** (e.g., "steep slope ŌåÆ high velocity ŌåÆ less infiltration time ŌåÆ more runoff"). A bare list loses 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 5. **Always state units** in numerical answers (mm, mm/hr, MCM). Unit-less answers trigger mark deductions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ŌĆö RUNOFF & ITS FACTORS**

- **Runoff = P ŌłÆ (E + T + I + ╬öS)** ŌåÆ the water balance definition.
- **Runoff has 3 components:** Surface flow, Interflow, Baseflow.
- **4 Pillars of factors:** **C**limatic, **W**atershed, **S**oil/Geology, **V**egetation.
- **Steep slope + low permeability + bare soil + high intensity = maximum runoff.**
- **Horton's equation:** $f = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) e^{-k t}$ ŌĆö capacity decays exponentially.
- **$\phi$-index:** $\phi = (P - R)/t_e$ ŌĆö average infiltration rate.
- **$W$-index:** $W = (P - R - S)/t_r$ ŌĆö includes surface detention $S$.
- **SCS-CN equation:** $Q = (P - 0.2S)^2 / (P + 0.8S)$ ŌĆö most-used in KTU problems.
- **S-CN relation:** $S = 25400/CN - 254$ (S in mm).
- **CN range:** 30 (sandy/permeable) to 100 (concrete/impervious).
- **Always check** $P \geq 0.2S$ before applying SCS-CN; otherwise $Q = 0$.
- **Drainage density** $D_d = \sum L_i / A$ ŌĆö higher $D_d$ ŌåÆ sharper peak.
- **Urbanisation increases runoff 2ŌĆō5├Ś** and peak discharge 3ŌĆō10├Ś.
- **Forest cover reduces runoff 20ŌĆō50%** through interception and infiltration enhancement.
- **Fan-shaped watershed** ŌåÆ high peak; **Elongated watershed** ŌåÆ low, broad peak.
- **Storm direction:** Downstream movement = higher peak; Upstream = delayed, lower peak.
- **Conversion:** Runoff volume (m┬│) = Runoff depth (m) ├Ś Watershed area (m┬▓).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Measurement in Hydrology ŌĆö The Foundation of Water Resources

**Hydrological Measurement** is the systematic, quantitative observation and recording of various components of the hydrological cycle (precipitation, evaporation, infiltration, streamflow, groundwater levels, etc.) using standardized instruments and techniques. It forms the empirical backbone of all hydrological analysis, design of hydraulic structures, flood forecasting, drought assessment, and water-resources planning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Measurement of precipitation, evaporation, transpiration, and infiltration forms the core of this module. Mastery of instrument types, observational networks, and averaging techniques is mandatory for ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

---

## 1.2 Why "Measurement" is a Critical Engineering Topic

In surface and groundwater hydrology, every design decision ŌĆö be it a spillway of a dam, a stormwater drain in a city, an irrigation canal, or a watershed management plan ŌĆö depends on **measured hydrological data**. Without reliable measurements, hydrology would collapse into pure guesswork.

**Real-World Analogy:**
Imagine you are a doctor trying to diagnose a patient. Just as you cannot prescribe medicine without measuring the patient's temperature, blood pressure, or sugar level, a hydrologist cannot design a dam or predict a flood without measuring rainfall, runoff, evaporation, and groundwater. The rain gauge is to a hydrologist what a thermometer is to a doctor ŌĆö a fundamental diagnostic instrument.

---

## 1.3 Major Hydrological Parameters Measured

| **Parameter** | **Primary Instrument Class** | **Standard Metric Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Precipitation (Rainfall) | Rain gauges (non-recording & recording) | **millimeters (mm)** of rain depth |
| Evaporation | Evaporation pans, atmometers | **mm/day** |
| Transpiration | Lysimeters, potometers | **mm/day** |
| Infiltration | Double-ring infiltrometer, rainfall simulators | **mm/hr** or **cm/hr** |
| Streamflow / Discharge | Current meters, weirs, flumes | **cubic meters per second (m┬│/s)** |
| Groundwater Level | Peizometers, observation wells | **meters below ground level (mbgl)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition ŌĆö Rainfall Depth (mm):**
> A rainfall of "1 mm" means 1 litre of water has fallen over an area of 1 m┬▓. This depth is the universal unit so that rainfall can be compared regardless of the catchment area.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Measurement of Precipitation (Rainfall)

Precipitation is measured in terms of **depth** (vertical height of water accumulated on a flat, impervious surface), assuming no loss due to runoff, evaporation, or infiltration. This standardized definition allows uniform comparison across the globe.

### 2.1.1 Non-Recording Rain Gauge (Symons' Gauge)

The **Symons' rain gauge** is the standard non-recording gauge used widely by the India Meteorological Department (IMD).

**Components:**
- A **circular collecting funnel** with a sharp rim of diameter **127 mm (5 inches)**.
- A **collecting bottle** placed inside a metallic cylinder to store the collected rainwater.
- An **outer casing** to protect the inner assembly.

**Working Principle:**
Rainwater falls on the funnel rim ŌåÆ gets magnified **11 times** (because the cross-sectional area of the receiving rim is 11├Ś the cross-sectional area of the measuring cylinder) ŌåÆ collects in the measuring cylinder ŌåÆ measured manually every 24 hours (typically at **08:30 IST** in India by IMD).

**Limitation:** Cannot capture intensity variations within the day.

### 2.1.2 Recording Rain Gauges

These provide a **continuous graphical trace** of rainfall against time, allowing determination of rainfall intensity, duration, and cumulative depth.

| **Type** | **Working Principle** | **KTU Note** |
|---|---|---|
| **Tipping-Bucket Type** | Rainwater fills a small calibrated bucket; when full (e.g., 0.25 mm rainfall), it tips and empties, sending a pulse to a recorder. | Most common **automatic weather station** gauge. |
| **Weighing-Bucket Type** | A weighing mechanism continuously records the cumulative mass of rainfall collected. | Suitable for all precipitation types (rain + snow). |
| **Float (Siphon) Type** | Rainwater enters a float chamber; as the water level rises, a float drives a pen on a rotating drum. A siphon empties the chamber at every **10 mm** of rainfall. | Standard recording gauge in IMD observatories. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Tipping-Bucket is High-Yield for KTU:**
> The bucket size is calibrated to give a precise rainfall increment per tip. If a bucket tips for every 0.25 mm of rainfall and there are 80 tips in an hour, the rainfall intensity = $80 \times 0.25 = 20 \text{ mm/hr}$. This type of numerical is **frequently asked** in ESE.

### 2.1.3 Network Design of Rain Gauges

Adequate density of rain-gauge stations is essential. The **World Meteorological Organization (WMO)** recommends:

| **Region Type** | **Minimum Gauge Density (per 1000 km┬▓)** |
|---|---|
| Flat, temperate, humid | 1 station |
| Mountainous, arid, polar | 1 station per 100ŌĆō250 km┬▓ |

For **India (IMD norms)** in plains, minimum 1 station per **520 km┬▓**.

---

## 2.2 Measurement of Evaporation

### 2.2.1 Class-A Evaporation Pan (USA Standard)

The **Class-A pan** is a circular pan, **1210 mm diameter**, **255 mm deep**, made of galvanized iron, placed on a wooden platform exposed to weather.

**Operational Procedure:**
- Water is filled to a standard level (5 cm below the rim).
- Daily observation: water level is measured using a **hook gauge** or **point gauge** in a **still-well**.
- Evaporation = (Initial water level + Rainfall) ŌłÆ Final water level.

### 2.2.2 Evaporation Pan Coefficient ($K_p$)

A pan does not represent a natural lake/reservoir. Therefore, a **coefficient ($K_p$)** is applied:

$$E_{\text{lake}} = K_p \times E_{\text{pan}}$$

For **Class-A pan**: $K_p$ typically ranges from **0.70 to 0.80** (commonly taken as 0.77).

### 2.2.3 Other Evaporation Measurement Methods

- **ISI Standard Pan (modified Class-A)** ŌĆö used in India; $K_p \approx 0.80$.
- **Colorado Sunken Pan** ŌĆö sunken into the ground; $K_p$ closer to 1.0.
- **Atmometers** (e.g., Piche atmometer) ŌĆö measure evaporative power of air using a porous porcelain disc.

---

## 2.3 Measurement of Infiltration

### 2.3.1 Double-Ring Infiltrometer

A pair of concentric rings is driven into the soil:
- **Outer ring**: 30 cm diameter (or larger).
- **Inner ring**: 15 cm diameter.

Water is ponded in both rings. The **outer ring** acts as a buffer to prevent lateral spreading of water from the inner ring. The drop in water level in the **inner ring** gives the **vertical infiltration rate**.

### 2.3.2 Single-Ring and Rainfall-Simulator Methods

- **Single-Ring Infiltrometer**: Simpler but less accurate due to lateral flow.
- **Rainfall Simulators**: Apply simulated rainfall of known intensity on a plot and measure runoff + soil moisture to derive infiltration.

---

## 2.4 Measurement of Streamflow (Discharge)

The **discharge ($Q$)** of a stream is the volume of water flowing per unit time, expressed in **m┬│/s**.

$$Q = A \times \bar{V}$$

Where $A$ = cross-sectional area, $\bar{V}$ = mean velocity.

### 2.4.1 Area-Velocity Method (Current Meter Method)

- The stream cross-section is divided into vertical strips.
- Velocity is measured at each strip using a **current meter** (Price-type or cup-type) at depths $0.2d$ and $0.8d$ (where $d$ = depth at that vertical).
- Mean velocity at a vertical: $\bar{V} = \dfrac{V_{0.2} + V_{0.8}}{2}$ (two-point method).

### 2.4.2 Weir Method

A **weir** is a barrier across a stream with a known geometric notch. Discharge is computed from the head over the weir using a standard formula:

**Rectangular Weir:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \, C_d \, L \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$

Where:
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (typically 0.61ŌĆō0.66)
- $L$ = length of the weir crest
- $H$ = head over the weir crest
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s┬▓)

**Triangular (V-notch) Weir:**

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$

Where $\theta$ is the notch angle (commonly 90┬░, giving $\tan(45┬░) = 1$).

### 2.4.3 Stage-Discharge Curve (Rating Curve)

A plot of **stage (water level)** vs **discharge** at a gauging station. Once established, only stage needs to be measured continuously using an **automatic stage recorder (limnigraph or pressure transducer)**.

---

## 2.5 Measurement of Groundwater

- **Observation Wells / Peizometers:** Cased boreholes tapping an aquifer; water level is measured using a **dipper / contact gauge**.
- **Water Table Indicator:** An electric circuit closes (buzzer/light) when the probe touches water ŌĆö depth is read off a graduated cable.
- **Pumping Tests:** Step-drawdown and recovery tests for aquifer parameters (transmissivity, storativity).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variable Meaning** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rainfall Depth | $P = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | V = volume, A = area | mm |
| Arithmetic Mean Rainfall | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i}{n}$ | $P_i$ = station rainfall | mm |
| Thiessen Mean Rainfall | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{\sum P_i A_i}{\sum A_i}$ | $A_i$ = polygon area | mm |
| Isohyetal Mean Rainfall | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{\sum P_i A_i}{\sum A_i}$ | $A_i$ = isohyet-bounded area | mm |
| Pan to Lake Evaporation | $E_{\text{lake}} = K_p \cdot E_{\text{pan}}$ | $K_p$ = pan coefficient | mm/day |
| Tipping-Bucket Intensity | $i = N \times \Delta P / \Delta t$ | N = tips, $\Delta P$ = tip increment | mm/hr |
| Discharge (Continuity) | $Q = A \times \bar{V}$ | A = area, V = mean velocity | m┬│/s |
| Mean Velocity (2-point) | $\bar{V} = \dfrac{V_{0.2} + V_{0.8}}{2}$ | Subscripts = depth fractions | m/s |
| Rectangular Weir | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ | H = head over crest | m┬│/s |
| V-Notch Weir | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$ | ╬Ė = notch angle | m┬│/s |
| Rational Formula | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | C = runoff coeff., I = intensity | m┬│/s |
| Infiltration Capacity (Horton) | $f_t = f_c + (f_0 - f_c)e^{-kt}$ | $f_0$ = initial, $f_c$ = final | mm/hr |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean Precipitation over a Catchment

### 3.1.1 Arithmetic Mean Method (AMM)

The simplest technique, valid only when:
- Rain gauges are **uniformly distributed** over the catchment.
- The **terrain is flat**.
- The rainfall variation is **small**.

**Derivation:**

Let there be $n$ rain-gauge stations within a catchment, with measured rainfall values $P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n$.

The arithmetic mean rainfall is defined as the **simple unweighted average**:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \ldots + P_n}{n}$$

Using the summation notation:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

> **Assumption in AMM:** Each station is assumed to represent an equal area. This is the **major limitation** of AMM in mountainous or non-uniform catchments.

---

### 3.1.2 Thiessen Polygon Method (TPM)

**Step-by-step construction:**

1. Plot all rain-gauge stations on a map of the catchment.
2. Connect each station to its **neighbouring stations** using straight lines, forming a triangulated network.
3. Draw the **perpendicular bisector** of each connecting line. The bisectors intersect to form closed polygons ŌĆö these are **Thiessen polygons**.
4. The **entire catchment is thus divided into polygons**, each polygon containing exactly one rain-gauge station whose rainfall is assumed to apply to the entire polygon.
5. Compute the area of each polygon ($A_i$).
6. The weighted mean rainfall is then:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i A_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i} = \frac{P_1 A_1 + P_2 A_2 + \ldots + P_n A_n}{A_1 + A_2 + \ldots + A_n}$$

**Derivation of Weights:**
For polygon $i$, the **weight** $w_i$ is the fraction of catchment area it represents:

$$w_i = \frac{A_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} A_j}$$

Hence:

$$\bar{P} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{A_i}{\sum A_j} P_i = \frac{\sum P_i A_i}{\sum A_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **TPM Limitation:** It does not account for **orographic (terrain) effects**. Isohyetal method is preferred for mountainous catchments.

---

### 3.1.3 Isohyetal Method (IM)

**Procedure:**

1. Plot the rain-gauge stations on a catchment map with their respective $P_i$ values.
2. Draw **isohyets** (lines of equal rainfall) by interpolation, similar to drawing contour lines.
3. The catchment is now divided into **strips** between consecutive isohyets.
4. Assign each strip a representative rainfall = **arithmetic mean of the bounding isohyet values**.
5. Measure the area of each strip.
6. Compute the weighted mean:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} \bar{P}_i \, A_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} A_i}$$

Where $\bar{P}_i$ is the mean of the $i$-th strip's bounding isohyets and $A_i$ is the strip area.

> **Why IM is the Most Accurate:** It honours actual spatial rainfall patterns and terrain influences.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Arithmetic vs Thiessen Method

**Problem:**
A catchment of area **1000 km┬▓** has **4 rain-gauge stations** with the following data. Compute mean rainfall by (a) Arithmetic Mean, (b) Thiessen Polygon (areas given).

| **Station** | **Rainfall $P_i$ (mm)** | **Thiessen Area $A_i$ (km┬▓)** |
|---|---|---|
| A | 80 | 250 |
| B | 60 | 300 |
| C | 90 | 200 |
| D | 70 | 250 |

### (a) Arithmetic Mean Method

$$\bar{P} = \frac{80 + 60 + 90 + 70}{4} = \frac{300}{4} = 75.00 \text{ mm}$$

### (b) Thiessen Polygon Method

**Step 1:** Verify that polygon areas sum to the catchment area.

$$\sum A_i = 250 + 300 + 200 + 250 = 1000 \text{ km}^{2} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\sum P_i A_i$:

$$\sum P_i A_i = (80)(250) + (60)(300) + (90)(200) + (70)(250)$$

$$= 20000 + 18000 + 18000 + 17500 = 73500 \text{ mm}\cdot\text{km}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute mean rainfall:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{73500}{1000} = 73.50 \text{ mm}$$

> **Conclusion:** The two methods give different results (75 mm vs 73.5 mm). The Thiessen method, being area-weighted, is **more accurate** and is preferred in KTU board examinations.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example: Discharge by Velocity-Area Method

A stream is gauged at a section divided into **3 segments**. Data:

| **Segment** | **Width $b$ (m)** | **Depth $d$ (m)** | $V_{0.2}$ (m/s) | $V_{0.8}$ (m/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 2.0 | 0.50 | 0.40 |
| 2 | 5 | 3.0 | 0.70 | 0.55 |
| 3 | 4 | 1.5 | 0.40 | 0.30 |

**Step 1: Mean velocity at each vertical**

$$\bar{V}_1 = \frac{0.50 + 0.40}{2} = 0.45 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\bar{V}_2 = \frac{0.70 + 0.55}{2} = 0.625 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\bar{V}_3 = \frac{0.40 + 0.30}{2} = 0.35 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Discharge in each segment** ($q_i = b_i \cdot d_i \cdot \bar{V}_i$):

$$q_1 = 4 \times 2.0 \times 0.45 = 3.60 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$q_2 = 5 \times 3.0 \times 0.625 = 9.375 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$q_3 = 4 \times 1.5 \times 0.35 = 2.10 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

**Step 3: Total discharge**

$$Q = q_1 + q_2 + q_3 = 3.60 + 9.375 + 2.10 = 15.075 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Rectangular Weir Discharge

A **rectangular weir** of crest length $L = 0.90$ m has a head $H = 0.20$ m. Take $C_d = 0.62$. Compute the discharge.

**Step 1:** Standard formula:

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $H^{3/2}$:

$$H^{3/2} = (0.20)^{3/2} = (0.20)^{1.5} = 0.20 \times \sqrt{0.20} = 0.20 \times 0.4472 = 0.08944 \text{ m}^{1.5}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\sqrt{2g}$:

$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429 \text{ m}^{0.5}\text{/s}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.90 \times 4.429 \times 0.08944$$

$$Q = 0.4133 \times 4.429 \times 0.08944$$

$$Q = 1.8305 \times 0.08944 = 0.1637 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$Q \approx 163.7 \text{ L/s}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Tipping-Bucket Gauge Intensity

A tipping-bucket rain gauge has a calibration of **0.2 mm per tip**. In 30 minutes, the recorder logs **48 tips**. Find the rainfall intensity.

**Step 1:** Total rainfall depth:

$$P = N \times \Delta P = 48 \times 0.2 = 9.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Duration in hours:

$$\Delta t = 30 \text{ min} = 0.5 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3:** Rainfall intensity:

$$i = \frac{P}{\Delta t} = \frac{9.6 \text{ mm}}{0.5 \text{ hr}} = 19.2 \text{ mm/hr}$$

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example: Evaporation Pan to Lake Conversion

A Class-A pan records an average daily evaporation of **6.5 mm/day**. Take $K_p = 0.77$. Find the **lake evaporation** and the **monthly evaporation depth**.

**Step 1:** Lake evaporation:

$$E_{\text{lake}} = 0.77 \times 6.5 = 5.005 \text{ mm/day}$$

**Step 2:** Monthly (30-day) evaporation:

$$E_{\text{monthly}} = 5.005 \times 30 = 150.15 \text{ mm}$$

> **Engineering Note:** A small change in $K_p$ (e.g., from 0.75 to 0.80) can alter the estimated **reservoir evaporation loss** by several percentage points ŌĆö critical in water-balance studies of dams.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hydrological Measurement Workflow

This block diagram illustrates the **process chain of hydrological measurement** ŌĆö from field observation to data interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrological Parameter E.g. Rainfall Evaporation Streamflow] --> B[Selection of Instrument]
    B --> C[Site Installation and Calibration]
    C --> D[Field Observation or Continuous Recording]
    D --> E[Data Logging Manual or Automatic]
    E --> F[Quality Control and Validation]
    F --> G[Data Analysis and Averaging]
    G --> H[Engineering Application Design Flood Drought Water Balance]
    H --> I[Decision Support Dam Design Irrigation Planning]
    I --> J[Feedback Loop Instrument Recalibration Network Redesign]
    J --> C
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow is cyclic ŌĆö the results of engineering applications feed back into network redesign, ensuring that the measurement system evolves to meet project needs.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Rainfall Measurement ŌĆö Sub-Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAIN_MEASUREMENT["Rainfall Measurement System"]
        direction TB
        A1[Atmospheric Precipitation] --> A2{Type of Gauge}
        A2 -->|Manual Daily| A3[Non Recording Symons Gauge]
        A2 -->|Continuous Record| A4[Recording Gauge]
        A4 --> A41[Tipping Bucket]
        A4 --> A42[Weighing Bucket]
        A4 --> A43[Float Siphon]
        A3 --> A5[Daily Reading in mm]
        A41 --> A51[Pulses per Tip]
        A42 --> A52[Mass vs Time Trace]
        A43 --> A53[Stage vs Time Trace]
        A5 --> A6[Mean Rainfall Computation]
        A51 --> A6
        A52 --> A6
        A53 --> A6
        A6 --> A61[Arithmetic Mean]
        A6 --> A62[Thiessen Polygon]
        A6 --> A63[Isohyetal Method]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Streamflow Measurement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Stream Channel] --> Q{Cross Section Selection}
    Q --> R[Divide Section into Verticals]
    R --> S[Measure Depth at each Vertical]
    S --> T{Velocity Measurement Method}
    T -->|Direct| U[Current Meter at 0.2d and 0.8d]
    T -->|Indirect| V[Weir or Flume Installation]
    U --> W[Compute Mean Velocity per Vertical]
    W --> X[Compute Segment Discharge q = b x d x Vmean]
    X --> Y[Sum to get Total Discharge Q]
    V --> Z[Apply Weir Formula Q = Function of H]
    Y --> AA[Establish Stage Discharge Rating Curve]
    Z --> AA
    AA --> AB[Continuous Stage Measurement via Limnigraph]
    AB --> AC[Daily or Hourly Discharge Record]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Evaporation Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[Water Body Lake Reservoir] --> F[Choose Measurement Method]
    F --> G[Class A Pan]
    F --> H[ISI Pan]
    F --> I[Colorado Sunken Pan]
    F --> J[Piche Atmometer]
    G --> K[Daily Hook Gauge Reading]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compute Pan Evaporation Epan]
    L --> M[Apply Pan Coefficient Kp]
    M --> N[Lake Evaporation Elake = Kp x Epan]
    N --> O[Use in Water Balance and Reservoir Yield Studies]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**List any three advantages of a recording rain gauge over a non-recording rain gauge. Mention one example of a recording type.**

**Model Answer:**
Recording rain gauges provide a **continuous rainfall record** versus time, whereas non-recording gauges give only a daily total.

**Advantages:**
1. They provide **rainfall intensity (mm/hr)**, essential for design storms and runoff analysis.
2. They capture the **distribution of rainfall within a day** ŌĆö start time, end time, peaks, and dry intervals.
3. They are **labour-saving** as they record automatically, reducing observer errors.
4. They are essential for **flood forecasting** in telemetered/storm-warning networks.

**Example:** Tipping-bucket type (or weighing-bucket / float-siphon type).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**What is a "pan coefficient"? Why is it used in evaporation measurement?**

**Model Answer:**
The **pan coefficient ($K_p$)** is the ratio of lake evaporation to pan evaporation:

$$K_p = \frac{E_{\text{lake}}}{E_{\text{pan}}}$$

It is used because the **evaporation from a small pan is NOT equal to that from a large natural water body** (lake/reservoir) due to differences in:
- **Heat storage capacity** of the water mass.
- **Pan exposure** (edge effects, wind, splashing).
- **Reflection, radiation exchange**, and pan colour/material.

For a **Class-A pan**, $K_p$ typically ranges from **0.70 to 0.80** (commonly taken as 0.77), meaning pan evaporation is **higher** than lake evaporation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö ESE Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **Symons' non-recording rain gauge** with a neat sketch. Explain its working and state any two limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A catchment of area **1200 km┬▓** has **5 rain-gauge stations** with the following readings. Compute the **mean precipitation** over the catchment using:
(i) the **Arithmetic Mean Method**, and
(ii) the **Thiessen Polygon Method**. **(7 Marks)**

| **Station** | **Rainfall (mm)** | **Thiessen Polygon Area (km┬▓)** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 85 | 280 |
| 2 | 70 | 220 |
| 3 | 95 | 250 |
| 4 | 80 | 200 |
| 5 | 75 | 250 |

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)**

**Symons' Non-Recording Rain Gauge (IMD Standard):**

**Components:**
1. A **circular collecting funnel** of diameter **127 mm (5 inches)** with a sharp, bevelled brass rim to avoid splash-out errors.
2. An **outer galvanized iron cylinder** (collector body), about 30 cm diameter, that houses the funnel and the storage bottle.
3. An **inner glass/plastic collecting bottle** that fits inside the cylinder below the funnel stem.
4. A **graduated measuring cylinder** of smaller cross-section (calibrated to give a magnified reading ŌĆö magnification factor Ōēł 11├Ś).

**Working:**
- Rainwater falling on the funnel rim drains through the stem into the collecting bottle.
- The **magnification factor** ($= \dfrac{\text{Area of funnel rim}}{\text{Area of measuring cylinder}}$) enlarges the measured depth, improving reading accuracy.
- Every morning at **08:30 IST** (standard IMD observation time), the observer:
  1. Removes the collecting bottle and measures the collected water in the graduated cylinder.
  2. Replaces the bottle with an empty one.
- The reading (in mm) is recorded as the previous 24-hour rainfall.

**Limitations:**
1. **No intensity information** ŌĆö only the total daily rainfall is obtained, so the engineer cannot determine how the rain was distributed during the day.
2. **Observer error** ŌĆö depends on the punctuality, accuracy, and honesty of the human observer; readings may be missed on holidays.
3. **Splash-in / splash-out errors** at the rim in heavy rain or high winds.
4. **Evaporation losses** from the bottle if not measured promptly in hot weather.
5. **Not suitable for snowfall** measurement.

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)**

**(i) Arithmetic Mean Method**

$$\bar{P} = \frac{85 + 70 + 95 + 80 + 75}{5} = \frac{405}{5} = 81.00 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Thiessen Polygon Method**

**Step 1:** Verify total area:

$$\sum A_i = 280 + 220 + 250 + 200 + 250 = 1200 \text{ km}^{2} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\sum P_i A_i$:

$$\sum P_i A_i = (85)(280) + (70)(220) + (95)(250) + (80)(200) + (75)(250)$$

$$= 23800 + 15400 + 23750 + 16000 + 18750$$

$$= 97700 \text{ mm┬Ękm}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute mean rainfall:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{97700}{1200} = 81.42 \text{ mm}$$

> **Answer:** Arithmetic mean = **81.00 mm**, Thiessen mean = **81.42 mm**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle and working of a tipping-bucket rain gauge** with a sketch. Derive the expression for **rainfall intensity** in terms of bucket calibration. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **rectangular weir** of crest length **L = 1.2 m** has a measured head of **H = 0.18 m**. Take $C_d = 0.62$. Calculate the **discharge** over the weir. If the same head were measured over a **90┬░ V-notch weir** ($C_d = 0.58$), find the **discharge**. Compare the two values. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)**

**Tipping-Bucket Rain Gauge ŌĆö Principle and Working:**

**Principle:** Rainwater is funneled into a small two-compartment **"see-saw" bucket**. When one compartment fills with a calibrated volume of water (corresponding to a fixed rainfall depth, e.g., 0.25 mm), the bucket **tips** under its own weight, emptying the water out and bringing the other compartment under the funnel. Each tip generates a **pulse** (electrical or mechanical) that is counted or recorded.

**Components:**
1. A **circular collector funnel** (e.g., 200 mm diameter) directing water to the bucket.
2. A **tipping-bucket assembly** ŌĆö two small buckets pivoted on a horizontal axis.
3. A **calibration adjuster** that sets the rainfall increment per tip.
4. A **reed switch / magnetic sensor** for electrical pulse generation.
5. A **data logger** that counts pulses and computes intensity in real time.

**Derivation of Intensity Expression:**
Let the bucket be calibrated to tip for a rainfall increment of $\Delta P$ (mm). Suppose the bucket tips $N$ times in a time interval $\Delta t$ (hours).

Total rainfall depth in time $\Delta t$:

$$P = N \times \Delta P \quad \text{(mm)}$$

Rainfall intensity (mm/hr):

$$i = \frac{P}{\Delta t} = \frac{N \cdot \Delta P}{\Delta t}$$

**Worked example:** If $\Delta P = 0.25$ mm, $N = 60$ tips, $\Delta t = 30$ min = 0.5 hr:

$$i = \frac{60 \times 0.25}{0.5} = 30 \text{ mm/hr}$$

**Advantages:** Real-time data, suitable for telemetry, low maintenance.
**Disadvantages:** Errors at very high intensities (bucket may tip while still receiving water) and under-recording in light drizzle.

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)**

**(i) Rectangular Weir Discharge**

Formula:

$$Q_{\text{rect}} = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $H^{3/2}$:

$$H^{3/2} = (0.18)^{1.5} = 0.18 \times \sqrt{0.18} = 0.18 \times 0.4243 = 0.07637 \text{ m}^{1.5}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294 \text{ m}^{0.5}\text{/s}$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$Q_{\text{rect}} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 1.2 \times 4.4294 \times 0.07637$$

$$Q_{\text{rect}} = 0.496 \times 4.4294 \times 0.07637$$

$$Q_{\text{rect}} = 2.197 \times 0.07637 = 0.1678 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{rect}} \approx 0.168 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}}$$

**(ii) V-Notch (90┬░) Weir Discharge**

Formula with $\theta = 90┬░$:

$$Q_V = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$

Since $\tan(45┬░) = 1$:

$$Q_V = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.58 \times 1 \times 4.4294 \times H^{5/2}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $H^{5/2}$:

$$H^{5/2} = (0.18)^{2.5} = 0.18^2 \times \sqrt{0.18} = 0.0324 \times 0.4243 = 0.01375 \text{ m}^{2.5}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$Q_V = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.58 \times 4.4294 \times 0.01375$$

$$Q_V = 0.5333 \times 0.58 \times 4.4294 \times 0.01375$$

$$Q_V = 0.3093 \times 4.4294 \times 0.01375$$

$$Q_V = 1.3702 \times 0.01375 = 0.01884 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_V \approx 0.0188 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\frac{Q_{\text{rect}}}{Q_V} = \frac{0.1678}{0.01884} \approx 8.91$$

The rectangular weir discharges nearly **9 times** more water than the V-notch for the same head. Hence, **V-notch weirs are preferred for small discharges** (laboratory, small streams) where higher accuracy is needed, while **rectangular weirs are used for larger flows**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** ŌĆö If $L$ and $H$ are in metres and $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓, the result $Q$ comes in m┬│/s. Mixing mm and m loses full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the magnification factor** in Symons' gauge description is a common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Thiessen polygon areas MUST sum to the catchment area** ŌĆö always verify before calculation. If they don't, you have constructed the polygons incorrectly.
> 4. **V-notch formula:** Students frequently write $\tan(\theta)$ instead of $\tan(\theta/2)$ ŌĆö this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Pan coefficient direction:** $E_{\text{lake}} < E_{\text{pan}}$, so $K_p$ is always **less than 1.0** for the Class-A pan. Writing $K_p > 1$ is a conceptual error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Measurement" (Module 2)**

- **Symons' gauge**: 127 mm funnel rim; 11├Ś magnification; 08:30 IST observation; **non-recording**.
- **Tipping-bucket**: pulse per tip; intensity $i = N \cdot \Delta P / \Delta t$.
- **Weighing-bucket**: works for **snow + rain**; cumulative mass trace.
- **Float-siphon**: siphons out at every 10 mm; provides stage-time chart.
- **Class-A pan**: 1210 mm dia, 255 mm deep; $K_p \approx 0.77$.
- **Double-ring infiltrometer**: outer ring = buffer, inner ring = measurement; gives vertical infiltration only.
- **Arithmetic Mean**: simplest; assumes uniform gauge distribution and flat terrain.
- **Thiessen Polygon**: area-weighted; uses perpendicular bisectors; $\bar{P} = \sum P_i A_i / \sum A_i$.
- **Isohyetal Method**: most accurate; uses interpolated lines of equal rainfall; honours orography.
- **Velocity-area method**: 2-point method uses 0.2d and 0.8d depths; $Q = \sum b_i d_i \bar{V}_i$.
- **Rectangular weir**: $Q = (2/3) C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$.
- **V-notch weir**: $Q = (8/15) C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$.
- **Rating curve**: plot of stage vs discharge; enables indirect discharge estimation from stage only.
- **Units to remember**: rainfall in **mm**, discharge in **m┬│/s**, intensity in **mm/hr**, evaporation in **mm/day**.

> **Pro-Tip for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> In Part B numericals, always **box the final answer** and **show units**. A correct numerical without units is treated as incomplete. Also, mention the **assumptions** (e.g., "Assuming uniform distribution for AMM" or "Assuming end contractions suppressed for the rectangular weir") ŌĆö examiners reward conceptual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Stage-Discharge Rating Curve** (also called a **Stage-Discharge Relation** or simply **Rating Curve**) is a functional graphical or mathematical relationship established between the **stage (water-surface elevation)** measured at a stream gauging station and the corresponding **discharge (volumetric flow rate)** of the river at that station.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$Q = f(G)$$

where:
- $Q$ = Discharge at the gauging section, typically in **m┬│/s** (cumecs)
- $G$ = Gauge reading (stage height), typically in **meters (m)** measured above an arbitrary datum called the **Gauge Zero (GŌéĆ)**
- $f$ = Functional operator established empirically from concurrent measurements

In practice, the relationship is rarely linear, and is most commonly expressed as a **power function** of the form:

$$Q = C \, (G - G_0)^n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GŌéĆ (Effective Gauge Zero Offset)** is NOT the same as the Gauge Datum. It is the hypothetical gauge reading at which **discharge becomes zero**, often negative in real-world stations because the zero-discharge level lies below the established gauge datum.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Bucket and Tap" Intuition

Imagine a **bathtub** with a fixed drain hole. As you pour more water (increasing the **stage**), water rushes out the drain faster (increasing **discharge**). If you plotted the height of water in the tub against the outflow rate, you would draw a smooth curve ŌĆö this is exactly what a rating curve is for a river.

The river is your "bathtub with a shaped channel," the water level is the **stage**, and the flow rushing past the gauge is the **discharge**. Every river behaves like this ŌĆö though the shape of the "bathtub" (channel geometry, slope, roughness) dictates the curve's mathematical shape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called a "Rating Curve"?**
> Because the curve essentially *rates* or *quantifies* the river's ability to carry flow at any observed water level ŌĆö turning a simple staff gauge reading into an actual volumetric flow rate.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard Unit |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| Discharge ($Q$) | **m┬│/s** (cumecs) or **L/s** for small streams |
| Stage ($G$) | **meters (m)** above gauge datum |
| Exponent ($n$) | **1.5 to 3.0** (Ōēł **5/3 Ōēł 1.667** for wide rectangular channels) |
| Coefficient ($C$) | Site-specific, dimensionless or with units |
| Slope ($S$) | Bed slope of channel at gauge, **m/m** |
| Manning's $n$ | **0.025 ŌĆō 0.075** for natural channels |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Principles Behind the Rating Curve

The existence of a stable, single-valued relationship between $Q$ and $G$ rests on three hydraulic assumptions:

1. **Steady Flow Assumption** ŌĆö At any instant, the discharge is uniquely determined by the stage (no loop/hysteresis effects initially).
2. **Fixed Control Assumption** ŌĆö The downstream "control" section (which governs the stage-discharge relationship) does not change shape, slope, or roughness over time.
3. **Uniform Channel Geometry** ŌĆö The cross-section and slope at the gauge remain constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When these assumptions are violated (e.g., during floods causing scour, vegetation growth, or backwater effects), the rating curve must be **shifted** or expressed as a **family of curves** for different conditions.

### 2.2 Theoretical Basis ŌĆö From Manning's Equation to Rating Curve

The simplest rating curve form arises from the **Manning's equation** for a **wide rectangular channel** (where channel width $B \gg$ flow depth $y$):

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

For wide channels: $A = By$, $P \approx B$, so $R = A/P \approx y$.

Substituting:

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot (By) \cdot (y)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2} = \frac{B \sqrt{S}}{n} \cdot y^{5/3}$$

This yields the canonical **simple power-law rating curve**:

$$Q = C \cdot y^{5/3}$$

where $C = \dfrac{B \sqrt{S}}{n}$ is the rating constant.

### 2.3 The General Power-Law Rating Curve Form

In practice, channel sections are not infinitely wide, and the zero-discharge level often lies below the gauge datum. The general form is:

$$Q = C \cdot (G - G_0)^n$$

where:
- $G$ = Observed gauge reading (m)
- $G_0$ = Gauge reading corresponding to zero discharge (m), often negative
- $n$ = Exponent (typically 1.5 ŌĆō 2.5 for natural channels)
- $C$ = Rating coefficient

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Exam |
|---------------------|----------------|-------------------------|
| $Q = C(G - G_0)^n$ | General power-law rating curve | Standard rating curve problems |
| $Q = C \cdot y^{5/3}$ | Wide-channel simplification from Manning's | Derivation/short derivation |
| $\log Q = \log C + n \log(G - G_0)$ | Linearized form for graphical fitting | Plotting technique |
| $C = \dfrac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's equation (SI units) | Theoretical basis |
| $Q = C_0 + C_1 G + C_2 G^2 + C_3 G^3$ | Polynomial rating curve (multi-term) | Complex non-power-law sections |
| $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's velocity formula | Auxiliary for $Q = VA$ |
| $G_0 = G - (Q/C)^{1/n}$ | Gauge-zero offset back-calculation | Curve fitting problems |
| Shift correction: $Q_c = Q_s + \Delta Q$ | Shift-adjusted discharge after control change | Post-flood re-rating |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** When solving for $G_0$, students commonly use the **trial-and-error method** (described in Section 3) or the **Stevens Method** for graphical determination. Memorize both.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rating curves are the **backbone of operational hydrology** and are used for:

- **Flood Forecasting** ŌĆö Convert telemetered gauge readings (cheap, easy) into discharge values (expensive to measure directly) in real-time.
- **Water Resources Planning** ŌĆö Develop flood frequency curves, design hydraulic structures (bridges, culverts, spillways).
- **Reservoir Operation** ŌĆö Compute inflows to dams from upstream gauge stations.
- **Drought Assessment** ŌĆö Track low-flow discharges for ecological and agricultural water allocations.
- **Hydropower** ŌĆö Estimate available head and flow for turbine design.
- **Calibration of Hydrologic Models** ŌĆö Rainfall-Runoff models (HEC-HMS, SWAT) require observed discharge data.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Wide-Channel Power-Law Rating Curve from Manning's Equation

**Starting Point ŌĆö Manning's Equation (SI units):**

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the wide-rectangular channel assumption:**
For a rectangular channel of width $B$ with flow depth $y$, and when $B \gg y$ (i.e., sidewall wetted perimeter is negligible):

$$A = B \cdot y$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Simplify the hydraulic radius:**
The wetted perimeter is $P = B + 2y$. When $B \gg 2y$:

$$P \approx B$$

Therefore:

$$R = \frac{A}{P} \approx \frac{By}{B} = y$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $A$ and $R$ into Manning's Equation:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot (By) \cdot (y)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine the powers of $y$:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot B \cdot S^{1/2} \cdot y \cdot y^{2/3}$$

$$Q = \frac{B \sqrt{S}}{n} \cdot y^{1 + 2/3}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final simplified form:**

$$Q = \frac{B \sqrt{S}}{n} \cdot y^{5/3}$$

Define the rating constant:

$$C = \frac{B \sqrt{S}}{n}$$

This gives the theoretical exponent **$n = 5/3 \approx 1.667$** for an ideal wide rectangular channel. Ō£ģ

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Logarithmic Linearization for Graphical Fitting

To find $C$ and $n$ from field data, take the logarithm (base 10) of the general rating equation:

**Starting equation:**

$$Q = C \cdot (G - G_0)^n$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply $\log_{10}$ to both sides:**

$$\log_{10}(Q) = \log_{10}\left[C \cdot (G - G_0)^n\right]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the product rule of logarithms:**

$$\log_{10}(Q) = \log_{10}(C) + \log_{10}\left[(G - G_0)^n\right]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Use the power rule of logarithms:**

$$\log_{10}(Q) = \log_{10}(C) + n \cdot \log_{10}(G - G_0)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange into the standard straight-line form $Y = mX + b$:**

$$\underbrace{\log_{10}(Q)}_{Y} = \underbrace{n}_{m} \cdot \underbrace{\log_{10}(G - G_0)}_{X} + \underbrace{\log_{10}(C)}_{b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The slope of the log-log plot directly gives the exponent $n$, and the intercept (extrapolated to $X = 0$, i.e., $\log_{10}(G - G_0) = 1$) gives the value of $C$. This is a **guaranteed question type** in KTU examinations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Analytical Determination of $G_0$ (Trial-and-Error / Stevens Method)

When $G_0$ is unknown, the analyst tries successive values of $G_0$ and computes the correlation coefficient (or graphical fit quality) until the points $\left[\log_{10}(G - G_0), \, \log_{10}(Q)\right]$ form the **best straight line** (highest correlation coefficient $r$).

The **Pearson correlation coefficient** for the linearized data is:

$$r = \frac{N \sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{\sqrt{\left[N \sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2\right] \cdot \left[N \sum Y_i^2 - (\sum Y_i)^2\right]}}$$

where $X_i = \log_{10}(G_i - G_0)$ and $Y_i = \log_{10}(Q_i)$, and $N$ is the number of observations.

The value of $G_0$ that maximizes $\vert r \vert$ is the **effective gauge zero**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** During a stream gauging campaign, the following concurrent stage and discharge measurements were recorded at a gauging station:

| Observation | Stage $G$ (m) | Discharge $Q$ (m┬│/s) |
|:-----------:|:-------------:|:--------------------:|
| 1           | 1.0           | 5                    |
| 2           | 2.0           | 35                   |
| 3           | 3.0           | 120                  |
| 4           | 4.0           | 280                  |

Assume the effective gauge zero is $G_0 = 0$. Establish the rating curve equation $Q = C \cdot G^n$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $X = \log_{10}(G - G_0) = \log_{10}(G)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= \log_{10}(1.0) = 0.000 \\
X_2 &= \log_{10}(2.0) = 0.301 \\
X_3 &= \log_{10}(3.0) = 0.477 \\
X_4 &= \log_{10}(4.0) = 0.602
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $Y = \log_{10}(Q)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= \log_{10}(5) = 0.699 \\
Y_2 &= \log_{10}(35) = 1.544 \\
Y_3 &= \log_{10}(120) = 2.079 \\
Y_4 &= \log_{10}(280) = 2.447
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute regression statistics:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 4 \\
\sum X &= 0.000 + 0.301 + 0.477 + 0.602 = 1.380 \\
\sum Y &= 0.699 + 1.544 + 2.079 + 2.447 = 6.769 \\
\sum X^2 &= 0.000 + 0.091 + 0.228 + 0.362 = 0.681 \\
\sum Y^2 &= 0.489 + 2.384 + 4.322 + 5.988 = 13.183 \\
\sum XY &= (0)(0.699) + (0.301)(1.544) + (0.477)(2.079) + (0.602)(2.447) \\
&= 0.000 + 0.465 + 0.992 + 1.473 = 2.930
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the slope $n$:**

$$n = \frac{N \sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{N \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$$

$$n = \frac{(4)(2.930) - (1.380)(6.769)}{(4)(0.681) - (1.380)^2}$$

$$n = \frac{11.720 - 9.341}{2.724 - 1.904}$$

$$n = \frac{2.379}{0.820} = 2.901$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the intercept $b = \log_{10}(C)$:**

$$b = \frac{\sum Y - n \sum X}{N} = \frac{6.769 - (2.901)(1.380)}{4}$$

$$b = \frac{6.769 - 4.003}{4} = \frac{2.766}{4} = 0.692$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute $C$ from intercept:**

$$C = 10^{b} = 10^{0.692} = 4.92$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Final rating curve equation:**

$$\boxed{Q = 4.92 \cdot G^{2.90}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exponent $n = 2.90$ is higher than the theoretical $5/3 = 1.667$ for wide channels. This indicates the channel is **narrower than "wide"**, and the cross-section likely tapers or has steeper sidewalls ŌĆö typical of natural upland rivers.

---

### 3.5 Extension of Rating Curve Beyond Measured Range

When floods produce stages exceeding the maximum gauged value, **extension** is needed:

**Method 1 ŌĆö Logarithmic Extrapolation:**
Simply extrapolate the straight line in the $\log Q$ vs $\log(G - G_0)$ plot. Risky for large extrapolations.

**Method 2 ŌĆö Manning's Equation Extension:**

$$Q_{ext} = Q_{meas} \cdot \frac{A_{ext} \cdot R_{ext}^{2/3}}{A_{meas} \cdot R_{meas}^{2/3}}$$

Using the known channel cross-section geometry to extrapolate hydraulically.

**Method 3 ŌĆö Conveyance Method:**
The total channel conveyance is:

$$K = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3}$$

$$Q = K \sqrt{S}$$

Extend by computing $K$ for the higher stage using surveyed cross-section.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Rating Curve Development Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Data Collection] --> B[Concurrent Measurement of Stage G and Discharge Q]
    B --> C[Compilation of Stage-Discharge Pairs]
    C --> D{Determine Effective Gauge Zero G0}
    D --> D1[Trial and Error Method]
    D --> D2[Stevens Graphical Method]
    D --> D3[Least Squares Optimisation]
    D1 --> E[Linearize Data using Log10]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Plot log Q vs log G minus G0]
    F --> G{Best Fit Straight Line?}
    G -->|No| D
    G -->|Yes| H[Extract Slope n and Intercept log C]
    H --> I[Construct Rating Equation Q = C times G minus G0 to the power n]
    I --> J{Validate with Independent Observations}
    J -->|Fail| K[Apply Shift Correction or Refit]
    K --> I
    J -->|Pass| L[Final Rating Curve Adopted]
    L --> M[Used for Real Time Discharge Estimation]
    L --> N[Used for Flood Frequency Analysis]
    L --> O[Used for Hydrologic Model Calibration]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Schematic ŌĆö Loop Rating Curve (Hysteresis Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rising Limb of Flood Hydrograph] --> B[Higher Discharge for Given Stage]
    B --> C[Loop Curve - Upper Branch]
    C --> D[Peak Stage]
    D --> E[Falling Limb of Flood Hydrograph]
    E --> F[Lower Discharge for Same Stage]
    F --> G[Loop Curve - Lower Branch]
    G --> H[Returns to Baseflow]
    H -.Hysteresis Loop Closes.-> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The hysteresis loop occurs because during **rising stage**, water surface slope is **steeper** than the equilibrium bed slope (faster flow for given depth). During **falling stage**, the slope is **flatter** than equilibrium (slower flow for same depth). This produces a **counter-clockwise loop** in the $Q$ vs $G$ plot.

### 4.3 Schematic ŌĆö Causes of Rating Curve Shift

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Stable Rating Curve] --> S1[Channel Scour during Flood]
    R --> S2[Channel Aggradation or Sediment Deposition]
    R --> S3[Vegetation Growth on Banks]
    R --> S4[Man Made Modifications like Bridge or Weir]
    R --> S5[Seasonal Aquatic Growth]
    S1 --> T[Shift Downward More Discharge for Same Stage]
    S2 --> U[Shift Upward Less Discharge for Same Stage]
    S3 --> V[Shift Upward due to Increased Roughness]
    S4 --> W[Shift in Either Direction depending on Type]
    S5 --> X[Periodic Seasonal Shift]
    T --> Y[Re rating Required]
    U --> Y
    V --> Y
    W --> Y
    X --> Y
```
