## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023]**
*Define a stage-discharge rating curve. Why is it considered an essential tool in operational hydrology?*

**Model Answer:**

A stage-discharge rating curve is an empirical functional relationship $Q = f(G)$ established between the water surface elevation (stage $G$) observed at a stream gauging station and the corresponding volumetric discharge ($Q$) passing through the gauge section.

**Significance in operational hydrology:**

- It transforms an easily measured quantity (water level via staff gauge/pressure transducer) into a difficult-to-measure quantity (discharge), enabling continuous flow records.
- It forms the basis of flood forecasting, water budgeting, reservoir inflow estimation, and design of hydraulic structures.
- It supports real-time decision-making in flood warning systems.

**Course Outcome Mapping:** CO2 ŌĆö Understand the principles of streamflow measurement.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]**
*What is meant by the "effective gauge zero" $G_0$ in a rating curve equation? How does it differ from the gauge datum?*

**Model Answer:**

The **effective gauge zero ($G_0$)** is the hypothetical gauge reading at which the discharge becomes zero. It is a fitted parameter in the rating curve equation $Q = C(G - G_0)^n$.

**Difference from Gauge Datum:**

| Feature | Effective Gauge Zero $G_0$ | Gauge Datum |
|---------|----------------------------|-------------|
| Definition | Stage at zero discharge | Arbitrary reference plane adopted by the agency |
| Sign | Often negative | Usually zero (or positive reference) |
| Determination | From curve fitting | Arbitrary, fixed at installation |
| Hydraulic meaning | True hydraulic reference | Administrative reference |

The gauge datum is fixed by the survey agency, while $G_0$ depends on the channel hydraulics at the control section.

**Course Outcome Mapping:** CO2 ŌĆö Analyze rating curve parameters.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### ŌŁÉ Question A (Choice 1) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** *Derive the form of a simple power-law stage-discharge rating curve $Q = Cy^{5/3}$ starting from Manning's equation for a wide rectangular channel. Clearly state all assumptions used.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *At a gauging station, the following stage and discharge observations were recorded:*

| Stage $G$ (m) | Discharge $Q$ (m┬│/s) |
|:-------------:|:--------------------:|
| 0.5           | 2                    |
| 1.0           | 12                   |
| 2.0           | 78                   |
| 3.5           | 320                  |

*Taking $G_0 = 0$, develop the rating curve equation in the form $Q = C \cdot G^n$ using the least-squares method on log-transformed data.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö State Manning's equation:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the "wide rectangular channel" assumptions** ($B \gg y$, sidewall effects negligible):

$$A = By, \quad P = B + 2y \approx B, \quad R = \frac{A}{P} \approx \frac{By}{B} = y$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into Manning's equation:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} (By) (y)^{2/3} S^{1/2} = \frac{B \sqrt{S}}{n} \cdot y^{5/3}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Identify rating parameters:**

$$C = \frac{B \sqrt{S}}{n}, \quad n_{exponent} = \frac{5}{3}$$

This yields $Q = Cy^{5/3}$. **(Full marks)**

**Course Outcome Mapping:** CO2, CO3.

---

**(b) Least-Squares Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $X_i = \log_{10}(G_i)$, $Y_i = \log_{10}(Q_i)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= \log_{10}(0.5) = -0.301, & Y_1 &= \log_{10}(2) = 0.301 \\
X_2 &= \log_{10}(1.0) = 0.000, & Y_2 &= \log_{10}(12) = 1.079 \\
X_3 &= \log_{10}(2.0) = 0.301, & Y_3 &= \log_{10}(78) = 1.892 \\
X_4 &= \log_{10}(3.5) = 0.544, & Y_4 &= \log_{10}(320) = 2.505
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute required sums ($N = 4$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum X &= -0.301 + 0.000 + 0.301 + 0.544 = 0.544 \\
\sum Y &= 0.301 + 1.079 + 1.892 + 2.505 = 5.777 \\
\sum X^2 &= 0.091 + 0.000 + 0.091 + 0.296 = 0.478 \\
\sum Y^2 &= 0.091 + 1.164 + 3.580 + 6.275 = 11.110 \\
\sum XY &= (-0.301)(0.301) + (0)(1.079) + (0.301)(1.892) + (0.544)(2.505) \\
&= -0.091 + 0.000 + 0.569 + 1.363 = 1.841
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute slope $n$:**

$$n = \frac{N \sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{N \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$$

$$n = \frac{(4)(1.841) - (0.544)(5.777)}{(4)(0.478) - (0.544)^2}$$

$$n = \frac{7.364 - 3.143}{1.912 - 0.296} = \frac{4.221}{1.616} = 2.612$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute intercept $b = \log_{10}(C)$:**

$$b = \frac{\sum Y - n \sum X}{N} = \frac{5.777 - (2.612)(0.544)}{4} = \frac{5.777 - 1.421}{4} = \frac{4.356}{4} = 1.089$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $C$:**

$$C = 10^{1.089} = 12.27$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final rating curve:**

$$\boxed{Q = 12.27 \cdot G^{2.61} \;\; \text{(m}^3/\text{s)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Don't forget to take anti-log** when reporting the final $C$ value. Many students write $\log C = 1.089$ as the answer and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Round-off discipline:** Keep at least 4 significant digits in intermediate computations; only round the final answer.
> 3. **Unit consistency:** $G$ is in meters, so $C$ has units of (m┬│/s)/(m^n) ŌĆö note this when substituting back.
> 4. **Comparison check:** Always verify by plugging one observation back. For example, $Q(1.0) = 12.27 \times 1.0^{2.61} = 12.27$ m┬│/s ŌĆö matches observed 12 m┬│/s Ō£ō.

---

#### ŌŁÉ Question B (Choice 2) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**(a)** *Explain the phenomenon of the hysteresis loop in a stage-discharge rating curve. Under what flow conditions does it occur, and how is it accounted for in hydrological practice?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss the various methods available for extending a rating curve beyond the highest measured stage. Why is this problem significant in flood hydrology?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hysteresis Loop (7 marks):**

**Definition:**
A hysteresis loop in a stage-discharge rating curve refers to the phenomenon where, during the passage of a flood wave, the discharge for a given stage is **different on the rising limb** compared to the **falling limb** of the hydrograph, producing a loop-shaped curve instead of a single-valued relationship.

**Cause ŌĆö Why does it happen?**
During a flood:

- **Rising stage:** Water surface slope is **steeper** than the steady-state equilibrium slope because water is "piling up" upstream. The increased slope drives a higher velocity, hence higher discharge for the same stage.
- **Falling stage:** Water surface slope is **flatter** than equilibrium because the flood wave has passed and water is draining back. Lower slope yields lower velocity, hence lower discharge for the same stage.

**Direction of loop:** Counter-clockwise when discharge is plotted on the y-axis and stage on the x-axis (rising limb on right, falling limb on left).

**When does it occur:**
- Primarily during **unsteady flood flows**.
- Most pronounced in **flat, low-gradient rivers** (where storage effects dominate).
- Negligible in **steep, rocky mountain streams** (rapid response, near-steady flow).

**How is it handled in practice:**
- Develop **separate rating curves** for rising and falling stages (loop rating).
- Use **dynamic rating models** incorporating the Saint-Venant equations.
- Use the **simple steady-state rating** for non-flood periods.
- Apply **index-velocity methods** with continuous velocity monitoring.

**Course Outcome Mapping:** CO3 ŌĆö Apply rating curves to real-world unsteady flows.

---

**(b) Methods of Rating Curve Extension (7 marks):**

**Significance:** Flood peaks frequently exceed the maximum stage ever measured by current meters. Yet engineers need to estimate the **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** and design **flood protection structures**. This requires extending the rating curve into unmeasured territory.

**Method 1 ŌĆö Logarithmic/Graphical Extrapolation:**
Continue the straight line on the $\log Q$ vs $\log(G - G_0)$ plot. Simple but unreliable beyond ~50% extrapolation.

**Method 2 ŌĆö Manning's Equation Extension:**
Use the channel's surveyed cross-section to compute $Q = \frac{1}{n}AR^{2/3}S^{1/2}$ for higher stages, where $n$ and $S$ are calibrated using measured low-stage data.

**Method 3 ŌĆö Conveyance Method:**
Compute total channel conveyance $K = \frac{1}{n}AR^{2/3}$ for extended stages. Use $Q = K\sqrt{S}$, where $\sqrt{S}$ is calibrated from measured data.

**Method 4 ŌĆö Slope-Area Method Integration:**
Use the slope-area equation at the extended stage with surveyed high-water marks as calibration points.

**Method 5 ŌĆö Step-Backwater Modeling:**
Use HEC-RAS to compute the rating curve for higher stages numerically, validating against measured data at lower stages.

**Course Outcome Mapping:** CO4 ŌĆö Extension and uncertainty quantification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ **Rating Curve Definition:** $Q = f(G)$, empirical relationship between stage and discharge.
- Ō£ģ **Power Law Form:** $Q = C(G - G_0)^n$ ŌĆö **most common form tested in KTU exams.**
- Ō£ģ **Theoretical Exponent:** For wide rectangular channels, $n = 5/3 \approx 1.667$. Real channels deviate from this.
- Ō£ģ **Logarithmic Linearization:** $\log Q = \log C + n \log(G - G_0)$ ŌĆö enables graphical fitting and least-squares regression.
- Ō£ģ **Effective Gauge Zero $G_0$:** The fitted gauge reading at zero discharge (often negative). Differs from gauge datum.
- Ō£ģ **Hysteresis Loop:** Occurs during unsteady flood flows; rising limb gives higher $Q$ than falling limb for same $G$.
- Ō£ģ **Shift in Rating Curve:** Caused by channel scour/fill, vegetation changes, or man-made modifications. Requires periodic re-rating.
- Ō£ģ **Extension Methods:** Logarithmic extrapolation, Manning's equation extension, conveyance method, step-backwater modeling.
- Ō£ģ **Manning's Equation:** $Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ is the theoretical foundation.
- Ō£ģ **KTU Trick Question:** When asked for "advantages of rating curves," always mention **continuous flow estimation** from a single staff-gauge reading.
- Ō£ģ **Units Check:** Always state units of $C$ when deriving; for $Q$ in m┬│/s and $G$ in m, $C$ has units of m┬│/s divided by m^n.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning ŌĆö Most Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Forgetting to **take anti-log** of the intercept to find $C$ ŌĆö costs 1 mark.
> 2. Mixing up **gauge datum** and **effective gauge zero** in definitions ŌĆö costs 1 mark.
> 3. Not stating **assumptions** (wide channel, steady flow, fixed control) in derivations ŌĆö costs 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. Reporting $n$ without comparing to theoretical $5/3$ and **justifying the deviation** ŌĆö costs 1 mark in 14-mark questions.
> 5. Not drawing the **loop rating curve diagram** in hysteresis questions ŌĆö costs 2 marks. Always include a labelled sketch.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Peak Runoff Rate and Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Peak Runoff Rate and Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Peak Runoff Estimation?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peak Runoff (Q_p):** The maximum instantaneous rate of discharge (in m┬│/s or cumecs) that occurs at the outlet of a catchment during a storm event. It is the *highest* flow rate recorded on the hydrograph's rising limb or at its crest.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Peak runoff rate is the **maximum rate of flow** passing a given point of a stream or river during a specific storm event, typically corresponding to the maximum ordinate of the Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DROH). The **runoff volume** is the total quantity of water (in m┬│, ha-m, or mm depth equivalent) that flows past the outlet over the entire duration of the event.

**KTU Terminology ŌĆö Three Critical Parameters:**

| Symbol | Parameter | Definition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_p$ | Peak discharge | Maximum instantaneous runoff | m┬│/s |
| $t_p$ | Time to peak | Time from start of rainfall excess to peak | hours |
| $t_b$ | Time base | Total duration of direct runoff | hours |
| $V$ | Runoff volume | Total water volume in DROH | m┬│ or ha-m |

---

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Bathtub & Drain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a catchment as a giant sloped bathtub, and the outlet as the drain hole.**

Imagine pouring a watering can over your entire bathtub (this represents **rainfall**). Not all the water becomes "runoff" ŌĆö some soaks into the surface (infiltration), some is stored in depressions, and some evaporates. The water that actually slides down to the drain and exits is the **runoff**.

- **Peak Runoff Rate ($Q_p$):** This is the **maximum gush of water coming out of the drain** at any single instant. It depends on *how hard it's raining* and *how fast the water can travel* to the drain.
- **Runoff Volume ($V$):** This is the **total amount of water that came out of the drain** over the entire event. It depends on *how much rain fell* and *how much of it became runoff*.

A catchment is a complex system of varying slopes, vegetation, soils, and surface textures ŌĆö and **estimating** how much water will reach the outlet (and how fast) is the heart of engineering hydrology.

---

### 1.3 Why Do We Estimate Peak Runoff? (Real-World Necessity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every hydraulic structure must be designed for a specific discharge. Under-estimate ŌåÆ Structure fails. Over-estimate ŌåÆ Wasted money.**

The peak runoff value is the **direct design input** for sizing:

1. **Culverts & Bridges** ŌĆö to prevent road/railway overtopping.
2. **Spillways of Dams** ŌĆö must pass the **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** safely.
3. **Storm Sewer Networks** ŌĆö urban drainage design.
4. **Flood Plain Zoning** ŌĆö to delineate vulnerable areas.
5. **Embankments & Levees** ŌĆö height of river protection works.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Rainfall intensity** ($i$): always expressed in **mm/hr** for KTU standard practice.
- **Catchment area** ($A$): typically in **km┬▓** (SI) or **hectares** (for small urban catchments).
- **Conversion factor (metric)**: $1$ km┬▓ = **100 hectares** = **10ŌüČ m┬▓**.
- **Conversion (cfs Ōåö cumecs)**: $1$ cumec = **35.315 cfs** (cusecs).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Estimation Methods

KTU 2024 syllabus prescribes three broad families of methods for estimating peak runoff:

```
PEAK RUNOFF ESTIMATION METHODS
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ 1. RATIONAL METHOD (Small urban/rural catchments A < 50 km┬▓)
Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ 2. EMPIRICAL FORMULAE (Regional ŌĆö Indian context)
Ōöé       Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Ryves Formula
Ōöé       Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Inglis Formula
Ōöé       Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ Nawab Jung Bahadur Formula
Ōöé       ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Dickens Formula
ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ 3. UNIT HYDROGRAPH METHOD (Larger catchments, A > 50 km┬▓)
```

---

### 2.2 THE RATIONAL METHOD (Most Tested in KTU)

**Foundation Logic:**
The Rational Method is based on a deceptively simple but powerful assumption:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Assumption:** *Peak runoff occurs when the entire catchment is contributing runoff simultaneously, i.e., when the duration of rainfall equals the **time of concentration** ($t_c$).*

The Rational Formula is:

$$Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{360}$$

Where:
- $Q_p$ = peak discharge in **m┬│/s**
- $C$ = **runoff coefficient** (dimensionless, $0 \le C \le 1$)
- $i$ = rainfall intensity in **mm/hr** corresponding to duration $t_c$
- $A$ = catchment area in **hectares (ha)**

> If $A$ is given in km┬▓, the formula becomes: $Q_p = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot i \cdot A$

**Derivation of the constant $360$:**
- Rainfall $i$ (mm/hr) ├Ś Area $A$ (ha) ├Ś Runoff fraction $C$ = Runoff volume rate
- $1$ mm over $1$ ha for $1$ hour = $10{,}000$ m┬▓ ├Ś $0.001$ m = $10$ m┬│ per hour
- $= 10 / 3600$ m┬│/s = $1/360$ cumecs
- So dividing by $360$ gives the rate in m┬│/s. ŌłÄ

---

### 2.3 COMPONENTS OF THE RATIONAL METHOD (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1: Determine Catchment Area (A)**
From topographic maps, GIS, or boundary surveys. For irregular shapes, use planimeter or grid overlay method.

**Step 2: Determine Runoff Coefficient (C)**
A weighted average based on surface type. Reference KTU standard table:

| Surface Type | $C$ Value |
|---|---|
| Pavement (concrete/asphalt) | 0.70 ŌĆō 0.95 |
| Roofs | 0.75 ŌĆō 0.95 |
| Bare soil (steep) | 0.50 ŌĆō 0.70 |
| Lawns (flat, 2% slope) | 0.05 ŌĆō 0.10 |
| Lawns (steep, 7% slope) | 0.15 ŌĆō 0.20 |
| Wooded/forested | 0.10 ŌĆō 0.20 |
| Cultivated land | 0.20 ŌĆō 0.40 |
| Parks, open spaces | 0.10 ŌĆō 0.30 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For composite catchments: $C_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum C_i \cdot A_i}{\sum A_i}$ (Area-weighted mean)

**Step 3: Determine Time of Concentration ($t_c$)**
$t_c$ is the time taken for a drop of water from the **most remote point** of the catchment to reach the outlet.

**Kirpich Formula (most common, for rural catchments):**

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$$

Where $L$ = length of main channel (m), $S$ = average slope (m/m).

**Izzard Formula (for overland flow on paved surfaces):**

$$t_c = \frac{0.058 \cdot (1.1 - C) \cdot L^{0.333}}{S^{0.333} \cdot i^{0.667}}$$

**Step 4: Get Rainfall Intensity (i)**
From **Intensity-Duration-Frequency (IDF) curves** of the region, find $i$ corresponding to:
- Duration = $t_c$ (in hours/minutes)
- Return Period = design frequency (e.g., 25, 50, 100 years)

**Step 5: Compute $Q_p$**

$$Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{360}$$

---

### 2.4 EMPIRICAL FORMULAE (Regional, Indian Context)

> [!NOTE]
> These are **regionally calibrated** formulae developed by engineers from observed flood data in specific Indian regions. KTU expects familiarity with all four.

#### (A) Ryves Formula (1884)

$$Q_p = C_R \cdot A^{2/3}$$

Where $Q_p$ in **cusecs**, $A$ in **square miles**.
- $C_R = 6.8$ for areas **near the hills** (South India, hilly)
- $C_R = 8.5$ for areas **away from hills** (flat terrain)
- $C_R = 10.0$ for areas **with heavy rainfall**

#### (B) Inglis Formula (Indian application)

$$Q_p = \frac{124 \cdot A}{\sqrt{A + 10.4}}$$

Where $A$ in **sq miles**, $Q_p$ in **cusecs** (suitable for catchments in Western India with annual rainfall 100ŌĆō200 cm).

#### (C) Nawab Jung Bahadur Formula (1894)

$$Q_p = C_N \cdot A^{0.5}$$

Where $Q_p$ in **cusecs**, $A$ in **sq miles**.
- $C_N = 0.92$ for areas near the **coast** (heavy rainfall)
- $C_N = 0.36$ for areas **away from coast** (inland)

#### (D) Dickens Formula (1865, Central India)

$$Q_p = C_D \cdot D \cdot A^{3/4}$$

- $C_D = 6$ for **North India** (low rainfall, broad plains)
- $C_D = 11$ to $14$ for **South India** (heavy rainfall, hilly)
- $C_D = 9$ for **Central India**
- $D$ = density factor (related to population density, typically 1.0)

---

### 2.5 UNIT HYDROGRAPH METHOD (For Volume Estimation)

The Unit Hydrograph (UH), proposed by **Sherman (1932)**, is the foundation for converting excess rainfall into direct runoff.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** A *Unit Hydrograph* is the direct runoff hydrograph resulting from **1 cm (or 1 unit) of effective rainfall** generated uniformly over the basin at a **uniform rate** for a specified **duration (D-hr)**.

**Volume under a Unit Hydrograph:**

$$\text{Volume} = 1 \text{ cm over basin area} = \dfrac{A}{100} \text{ hectare-metres} = 0.01 \cdot A \text{ million m}^3$$

(where $A$ is in km┬▓)

**Peak Discharge from UH:** $Q_p = $ ordinate at the time of peak ($t_p$)

**Runoff Volume:**

$$V = 0.36 \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sum Q_i$$

Where $Q_i$ are ordinates of DROH in m┬│/s, $\Delta t$ in hours.

---

### 2.6 KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET (High-Yield Table)

| # | Method | Formula | Units (A, Q) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rational (ha) | $Q_p = CiA/360$ | A: ha, Q: m┬│/s | A < 50 km┬▓ |
| 2 | Rational (km┬▓) | $Q_p = 0.00278\,CiA$ | A: km┬▓, Q: m┬│/s | A < 50 km┬▓ |
| 3 | Ryves | $Q_p = C_R A^{2/3}$ | A: mi┬▓, Q: cfs | South India |
| 4 | Inglis | $Q_p = 124A/\sqrt{A+10.4}$ | A: mi┬▓, Q: cfs | Western India |
| 5 | Nawab Jung | $Q_p = C_N A^{0.5}$ | A: mi┬▓, Q: cfs | Coastal/Inland |
| 6 | Dickens | $Q_p = C_D D A^{3/4}$ | A: mi┬▓, Q: cfs | Central India |
| 7 | Kirpich $t_c$ | $0.01947 L^{0.77}/S^{0.385}$ | L: m, S: m/m | Rural catchments |
| 8 | UH Volume | $V = 0.36 \Delta t \sum Q_i$ | $\Delta t$: hr, Q: m┬│/s | All UH problems |
| 9 | Area Conversion | $1$ mi┬▓ = **2.59** km┬▓ | ŌĆö | Imperial to SI |
| 10 | Discharge Conv. | $1$ cumec = **35.315** cfs | ŌĆö | Metric to Imperial |

---

### 2.7 Engineering Utility in Real World

- **Urban drainage design** uses Rational Method because storm sewer networks respond to short-duration, high-intensity bursts.
- **Bridge/culvert design** in rural Kerala uses Ryves/Inglis (validated for local conditions).
- **Dam spillway sizing** requires Probable Maximum Flood (PMF) from extreme rainfall + UH routing.
- **Floodplain mapping** uses regional empirical formulae to get first-pass estimates for ungauged catchments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Rational Method Constant $1/360$

We want to find: $Q = C \cdot i \cdot A$ with appropriate constant $K$, so that the output is in m┬│/s.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with basic volumetric flow:**

$$\text{Volume rate} = C \cdot (\text{Rainfall rate}) \cdot (\text{Area})$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute with units:**
- Rainfall rate: $i$ mm/hr = $i/1000$ m/hr
- Area: $A$ hectares = $A \times 10000$ m┬▓
- Runoff fraction: $C$ (dimensionless)

$$\text{Volume rate} = C \cdot \frac{i}{1000} \cdot 10000 \cdot A \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{hr}]$$

$$\text{Volume rate} = 10 \cdot C \cdot i \cdot A \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{hr}]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert hours to seconds (1 hour = 3600 s):**

$$Q = \frac{10 \cdot C \cdot i \cdot A}{3600} = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{360} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final form:**

$$\boxed{Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{360}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Derivation of the $0.00278$ constant (when $A$ in km┬▓):**

$$Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A \times 100}{360} = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{3.6} = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot i \cdot A$$

This is used when $A$ is in km┬▓, $i$ in mm/hr, $Q_p$ in m┬│/s. ŌłÄ

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Kirpich's Time of Concentration

Kirpich (1940) calibrated an empirical relation from data of seven small catchments (0.005 to 0.7 km┬▓) in Tennessee, USA.

**Starting form:**

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Decompose the slope term:**
Slope $S$ is dimensionless (m/m) or can be expressed in % form.

$$S = \frac{\Delta H}{L} \quad \text{(dimensionless, m/m)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Why $0.01947$?**
This is the empirical constant derived from regression fitting. To verify, let:
- $L = 1000$ m, $S = 0.01$ (1% slope)

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot (1000)^{0.77} \cdot (0.01)^{-0.385}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute each component:**

$$(1000)^{0.77} = 10^{3 \times 0.77} = 10^{2.31} \approx 204.17$$

$$(0.01)^{-0.385} = 10^{2 \times 0.385} = 10^{0.77} \approx 5.888$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Multiply together:**

$$t_c = 0.01947 \times 204.17 \times 5.888$$

$$t_c = 0.01947 \times 1202.07$$

$$t_c \approx 23.41 \text{ minutes}$$

This is a reasonable value for a 1 km channel with 1% slope. ŌłÄ

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Conversion from Imperial to Metric in Ryves Formula

The Ryves formula natively uses **cfs (cusecs)** and **square miles**. To convert to **cumecs** and **km┬▓**, we apply unit conversion:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify given formula:**

$$Q_p = C_R \cdot A^{2/3} \quad [A \text{ in mi}^2, Q_p \text{ in cfs}]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Unit conversion factors:**
- 1 sq mile = 2.59 km┬▓
- 1 cfs = 0.0283 m┬│/s (cumecs)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Transform the area term:**

$$A_{\text{mi}^2} = \frac{A_{\text{km}^2}}{2.59}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute:**

$$Q_p = C_R \cdot \left(\frac{A_{\text{km}^2}}{2.59}\right)^{2/3}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Re-distribute the constant:**

$$Q_p = C_R \cdot \frac{(A_{\text{km}^2})^{2/3}}{(2.59)^{2/3}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Evaluate $(2.59)^{2/3}$:**

$$(2.59)^{0.667} = e^{0.667 \cdot \ln(2.59)} = e^{0.667 \cdot 0.952} = e^{0.635} \approx 1.886$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Apply discharge conversion:**

$$Q_p \, [\text{m}^3/\text{s}] = 0.0283 \cdot \frac{C_R \cdot A_{\text{km}^2}^{2/3}}{1.886}$$

$$Q_p = 0.0150 \cdot C_R \cdot A_{\text{km}^2}^{2/3}$$

**Final Metric Ryves:**

$$\boxed{Q_p = 0.0150 \cdot C_R \cdot A^{2/3} \quad [A \text{ in km}^2, Q_p \text{ in m}^3/\text{s}]}$$

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Volume Under a Hydrograph

A hydrograph ordinates $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, \ldots, Q_n$ are recorded at equal time intervals $\Delta t$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Approximate volume as sum of trapezoids:**

$$V = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(Q_i + Q_{i+1})}{2} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö For large $n$, with $\Delta t$ in hours and $Q$ in m┬│/s:**

Each trapezoid's volume = average discharge ├Ś time duration. With $Q$ in m┬│/s and $\Delta t$ in seconds, volume is in m┬│.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert $\Delta t$ from hours to seconds:** $\Delta t_{sec} = 3600 \cdot \Delta t_{hr}$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine with sum approximation:**

$$V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 3600 \cdot \Delta t_{hr} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (Q_i + Q_{i+1})$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Simplify (approximate for large $n$):**

$$V = 3600 \cdot \Delta t_{hr} \cdot \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{(correction)}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Standard KTU form:**

$$\boxed{V = 0.36 \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \quad [\text{m}^3]}$$

Where:
- $\Delta t$ in hours
- $Q_i$ in m┬│/s
- $V$ in m┬│ (├Ś 10ŌüČ for Mm┬│)

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof of $0.36$ constant:** $3600 / 10000 = 0.36$ (when converting m┬│/s ├Ś hours to m┬│ over km┬▓ area; the factor absorbs the area normalization for unit hydrograph).

---

### 3.5 ANALYTICAL MATRIX: Method Selection by Catchment Size

| Catchment Size | Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $A < 5$ km┬▓ | Rational Method (CID) | Single-peaked, short $t_c$ |
| $5 < A < 50$ km┬▓ | Rational / UH | Depends on data availability |
| $A > 50$ km┬▓ | Unit Hydrograph / Routing | Spatially variable rainfall matters |
| Ungauged (any size) | Empirical Formulae | Regional calibration only |
| Coastal Kerala | Nawab Jung / Inglis | Heavy rainfall, high $C_N$ |
| Hilly Kerala | Ryves ($C_R = 6.8$) | Hilly terrain, steep slopes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Rainfall to Peak Runoff

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Storm Rainfall P mm] --> B{Abstractions}
    B --> B1[Interception Loss]
    B --> B2[Depression Storage]
    B --> B3[Infiltration]
    B --> B4[Evaporation]
    B1 --> C[Effective Rainfall Pe = P - Losses]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Catchment Routing via UH]
    D --> E[Direct Runoff Hydrograph DROH]
    E --> F[Peak Discharge Qp]
    E --> G[Runoff Volume V]
    F --> H[Design of Hydraulic Structure]
    G --> I[Reservoir Storage / Yield]
```

---

### 4.2 Rational Method Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start]) --> S1[Identify Catchment Boundary]
    S1 --> S2[Measure Area A in hectares]
    S2 --> S3[Subdivide by Land Use]
    S3 --> S4[Assign Runoff Coefficient Ci per zone]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Area-weighted C avg]
    S5 --> S6[Find Longest Flow Path L]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Catchment Slope S]
    S7 --> S8[Apply Kirpich: compute tc]
    S8 --> S9[Use IDF Curve: get i for tc and return period]
    S9 --> S10[Compute Qp = C i A divided by 360]
    S10 --> OUT([Peak Discharge Qp in m3 per second])
```

---

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Unit Hydrograph Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT]
        R1[Effective Rainfall Hyetograph Pe of t]
        R2[Unit Hydrograph UH of D hr]
    end
    INPUT --> M[Convolution: Direct Runoff Ordinate]
    M --> DROH[Direct Runoff Hydrograph DROH]
    DROH --> PEAK[Identify Peak Qp]
    DROH --> VOL[Compute Volume V]
    VOL --> STORAGE[Reservoir Capacity Estimation]
    PEAK --> SPILLWAY[Spillway Design]
```

---

### 4.4 Comparison Block: Empirical Formulae Applicability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EMP[Empirical Formula Selection] --> Q1{Catchment in India?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Region}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Use Rational or Regional Flood Frequency]
    Q2 -->|Hilly South| RYV[Ryves: CR = 6.8]
    Q2 -->|Western India| ING[Inglis Formula]
    Q2 -->|Coastal| NJB[Nawab Jung: CN = 0.92]
    Q2 -->|Inland| NJB2[Nawab Jung: CN = 0.36]
    Q2 -->|Central India| DKN[Dickens: CD = 9]
    Q2 -->|North India| DKN2[Dickens: CD = 6]
    Q2 -->|South India| DKN3[Dickens: CD = 11 to 14]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A ŌĆö SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
*State the Rational Method for estimating peak runoff and list the factors affecting the runoff coefficient.*

**Model Answer:**

The Rational Method estimates peak runoff as:

$$Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{360}$$

where $Q_p$ is in m┬│/s, $C$ is the runoff coefficient, $i$ is rainfall intensity (mm/hr), and $A$ is the catchment area in hectares.

**Factors affecting runoff coefficient $C$:**
1. **Soil type and infiltration capacity** ŌĆö sandy soils have lower $C$, clay has higher.
2. **Vegetation cover** ŌĆö dense vegetation reduces $C$.
3. **Slope of catchment** ŌĆö steeper slopes increase $C$.
4. **Land use** ŌĆö paved surfaces have $C$ near 0.9, open fields around 0.2.
5. **Antecedent moisture conditions** ŌĆö wet soil ŌåÆ higher $C$.
6. **Intensity of rainfall** ŌĆö higher intensity ŌåÆ higher $C$.

*(3 marks ŌĆö definition + any 4 factors)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*Define Unit Hydrograph. What are its three principal assumptions?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Unit Hydrograph (Sherman, 1932):** It is the Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DROH) resulting from **1 cm (or 1 unit) of effective rainfall** generated uniformly over the basin at a **uniform rate** for a specified **duration $D$**.

**Three principal assumptions:**
1. **Time invariance** ŌĆö The UH for a given duration $D$ is constant in time (does not change between events).
2. **Linear response** ŌĆö The ordinates of DROH are directly proportional to the effective rainfall depth (superposition principle applies).
3. **Invariance of base period** ŌĆö The time base ($t_b$) of the UH remains the same for the same duration $D$ regardless of rainfall intensity.

*(3 marks ŌĆö 1 mark definition + 2 marks assumptions)*

---

### PART B ŌĆö LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks)

---

**QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A small urban catchment in Thrissur has the following sub-areas: Paved area = 8 ha ($C = 0.85$), Roof tops = 4 ha ($C = 0.90$), Lawns = 6 ha ($C = 0.15$). The rainfall intensity for a 25-year storm of 30-min duration is 50 mm/hr. Estimate the peak runoff using the Rational Method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain Kirpich's formula for time of concentration. A catchment has $L = 2.5$ km and average slope $S = 0.012$. Compute $t_c$.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total catchment area:**

$$A = 8 + 4 + 6 = 18 \text{ hectares}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Weighted runoff coefficient:**

$$C_{avg} = \frac{(0.85 \times 8) + (0.90 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 6)}{18}$$

$$C_{avg} = \frac{6.80 + 3.60 + 0.90}{18} = \frac{11.30}{18} = 0.628$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Rational formula:**

$$Q_p = \frac{C \cdot i \cdot A}{360} = \frac{0.628 \times 50 \times 18}{360}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute numerator:**

$$0.628 \times 50 \times 18 = 0.628 \times 900 = 565.2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Divide by 360:**

$$Q_p = \frac{565.2}{360} = 1.57 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

> **Final Answer: $Q_p = 1.57$ m┬│/s** Ō£ō

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Kirpich's Formula:**

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot \frac{L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$$

**Given:** $L = 2.5$ km = **2500 m**, $S = 0.012$ (m/m)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $L^{0.77}$:**

$$L^{0.77} = (2500)^{0.77} = e^{0.77 \cdot \ln(2500)} = e^{0.77 \times 7.824} = e^{6.024} \approx 412.5$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $S^{-0.385}$:**

$$S^{-0.385} = (0.012)^{-0.385} = e^{-0.385 \cdot \ln(0.012)} = e^{-0.385 \times (-4.423)} = e^{1.703} \approx 5.49$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply together:**

$$t_c = 0.01947 \times 412.5 \times 5.49$$

$$t_c = 0.01947 \times 2264.6$$

$$t_c \approx 44.1 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Final Answer: $t_c \approx 44.1$ min** (or 0.735 hours) Ō£ō

---

**QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A catchment in South India near the hills covers an area of 12 km┬▓. Estimate the peak runoff using:
(i) Ryves formula, (ii) Inglis formula, (iii) Nawab Jung Bahadur formula.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The ordinates of a 4-hr Unit Hydrograph are given below. The effective rainfall is 2 cm in 4 hours. Calculate the peak discharge and the volume of runoff.

| Time (hr) | 0 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 24 | 28 | 32 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| UH ordinate (m┬│/s) | 0 | 30 | 90 | 150 | 110 | 70 | 40 | 15 | 0 |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Given:** $A = 12$ km┬▓
**Conversion to sq miles:** $A = 12 / 2.59 = 4.633$ mi┬▓

**(i) Ryves Formula (South India, hilly ŌåÆ $C_R = 6.8$):**

$$Q_p = C_R \cdot A^{2/3} = 6.8 \times (4.633)^{2/3}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $A^{2/3}$:**

$$(4.633)^{2/3} = (4.633^{1/3})^2 = (1.668)^2 = 2.782$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multiply:**

$$Q_p = 6.8 \times 2.782 = 18.92 \text{ cfs}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to cumecs:**

$$Q_p = 18.92 \times 0.0283 = 0.535 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

> **Ryves Answer: $Q_p = 18.92$ cfs = 0.535 m┬│/s** Ō£ō

---

**(ii) Inglis Formula:**

$$Q_p = \frac{124 \cdot A}{\sqrt{A + 10.4}} = \frac{124 \times 4.633}{\sqrt{4.633 + 10.4}}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Numerator:** $124 \times 4.633 = 574.49$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Denominator:** $\sqrt{15.033} = 3.877$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Divide:**

$$Q_p = \frac{574.49}{3.877} = 148.18 \text{ cfs}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert to cumecs:**

$$Q_p = 148.18 \times 0.0283 = 4.19 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

> **Inglis Answer: $Q_p = 148.18$ cfs = 4.19 m┬│/s** Ō£ō

---

**(iii) Nawab Jung Bahadur (away from coast ŌåÆ $C_N = 0.36$):**

$$Q_p = C_N \cdot A^{0.5} = 0.36 \times (4.633)^{0.5}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute square root:**

$$\sqrt{4.633} = 2.152$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multiply:**

$$Q_p = 0.36 \times 2.152 = 0.775 \text{ cfs}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to cumecs:**

$$Q_p = 0.775 \times 0.0283 = 0.022 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

> **Nawab Jung Answer: $Q_p = 0.775$ cfs = 0.022 m┬│/s** Ō£ō

> [!NOTE]
> The wide variation in empirical estimates (0.022 to 4.19 m┬│/s) is **expected and typical**. KTU often asks for "compare and justify" ŌĆö note that Ryves and Inglis are best for South/Western India. The huge spread shows that empirical formulae must be **regionally validated** before use.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:** Effective rainfall $P_e = 2$ cm over 4 hours.

**DROH ordinates = UH ordinate ├Ś rainfall depth:**

| Time (hr) | UH (m┬│/s) | DROH (m┬│/s) = 2 ├Ś UH |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 30 | 60 |
| 8 | 90 | 180 |
| 12 | 150 | **300** ŌåÉ Peak |
| 16 | 110 | 220 |
| 20 | 70 | 140 |
| 24 | 40 | 80 |
| 28 | 15 | 30 |
| 32 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify peak discharge:**

$$\boxed{Q_p = 300 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \text{ at } t = 12 \text{ hr}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute volume using $V = 0.36 \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sum Q_i$:**

$\Delta t = 4$ hr

$$\sum Q_i = 0 + 60 + 180 + 300 + 220 + 140 + 80 + 30 + 0 = 1010 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$V = 0.36 \times 4 \times 1010 = 1454.4 \text{ m}^3 \times 10^3$$

$$V = 1.4544 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 = 1.4544 \text{ Mm}^3$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cross-check using UH principle:**

For 2 cm of effective rainfall over area $A$ (need to find or assume). Since this is a 4-hr UH, by definition the area under UH = 1 cm ├Ś basin area. Given $\sum Q_i = 1010/2 = 505$ m┬│/s, then $0.36 \times 4 \times 505 = 727.2$ m┬│ ├Ś 10┬│ = $0.7272$ Mm┬│ corresponds to 1 cm over catchment.

Thus catchment area:

$$A = \frac{0.7272 \times 10^6}{0.01} = 7.272 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^2 = 72.72 \text{ km}^2$$

> **Final Answer: $Q_p = 300$ m┬│/s; $V = 1.4544$ Mm┬│; implied $A = 72.72$ km┬▓** Ō£ō

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Rational Method** ŌĆö Most common error. Always check whether $A$ is in hectares (use $/360$) or km┬▓ (use $/3.6$). KTU explicitly penalizes unit errors with **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Confusing $C_R$ values for Ryves** ŌĆö Wrong region ŌåÆ $C_R$ wrong ŌåÆ 50% of marks lost. Remember: hills = 6.8, away from hills = 8.5.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert cfs ŌåÆ cumecs** ŌĆö Empirical formulae yield cfs. KTU expects SI units in final answer.
> 4. **Missing $\Delta t$ in volume formula** ŌĆö $V = 0.36 \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sum Q_i$, not just $0.36 \cdot \sum Q_i$. **3-mark deduction if missed.**
> 5. **Not using area-weighted $C$ for composite catchments** ŌĆö na├»ve average is wrong; always use $\sum C_i A_i / \sum A_i$.
> 6. **Ignoring return period** ŌĆö Always state which IDF curve was used (25-yr, 50-yr, 100-yr storm) in design problems.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Peak runoff** = maximum ordinate of DROH; **Volume** = area under DROH.
- [x] **Rational Method** $Q_p = CiA/360$ valid for $A < 50$ km┬▓; assumes uniform rainfall = $t_c$.
- [x] **Runoff coefficient $C$** depends on soil, slope, vegetation, land use, antecedent moisture.
- [x] **Composite $C$** is area-weighted average: $C = \sum C_i A_i / \sum A_i$.
- [x] **Kirpich $t_c$** = $0.01947 L^{0.77}/S^{0.385}$ (most common for rural catchments).
- [x] **Empirical formulae** are regionally calibrated; convert $A$ in mi┬▓ and $Q$ in cfs.
- [x] **Ryves** ŌĆö $C_R = 6.8$ (hilly), $8.5$ (flat), $10$ (heavy rainfall).
- [x] **Inglis** ŌĆö $124A/\sqrt{A+10.4}$ for Western India.
- [x] **Nawab Jung** ŌĆö $C_N A^{0.5}$; $C_N = 0.92$ (coast), $0.36$ (inland).
- [x] **Dickens** ŌĆö $C_D D A^{3/4}$; $C_D = 6$ (N. India), $11$ŌĆō$14$ (S. India).
- [x] **Unit Hydrograph** ŌĆö DROH from 1 cm uniform rain over $D$ hours; Sherman (1932).
- [x] **UH Volume** = 1 cm ├Ś basin area = $0.01 A$ Mm┬│ (A in km┬▓).
- [x] **DROH Volume** $V = 0.36 \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sum Q_i$ (in m┬│, with $\Delta t$ in hours).
- [x] **Conversions**: 1 mi┬▓ = 2.59 km┬▓; 1 cfs = 0.0283 m┬│/s.
- [x] **IDF curves** ŌĆö always needed to find $i$ for given $t_c$ and return period.
- [x] **Design storm** ŌĆö return period = 25 yr (urban drains), 50 yr (minor bridges), 100 yr (major structures), PMF (dams).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rational Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rational Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Rational Method** is a simplified, deterministic, lumped-parameter hydrological model used to estimate the **peak runoff rate** (peak discharge) from a small drainage catchment during a **design storm event**. It is fundamentally based on the principle that the peak runoff is directly proportional to the catchment area and the intensity of rainfall, with the proportionality constant being the runoff coefficient.

The classical governing equation is expressed as:

$$Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A$$

Where:
- $Q_p$ = Peak rate of runoff (m┬│/s or cumecs)
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless, $0 \le C \le 1$)
- $I$ = Average rainfall intensity (mm/hr or m/s)
- $A$ = Catchment area (hectares or km┬▓ or m┬▓)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Rational Method is the simplest and most widely taught peak runoff estimation technique, restricted to **small catchments (Ōēż 50 km┬▓)** where rainfall can be assumed to be **spatially uniform** and the **time of concentration ($t_c$)** is the dominant parameter governing peak flow.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Leaking Bucket

Imagine a rectangular bucket placed in a rainstorm. Rain falls uniformly over the bucket's opening (the catchment area $A$). However, not all rain that falls reaches the outlet hole (the basin outlet) ŌĆö some is absorbed by the bucket walls (infiltration), some evaporates, and some is stored in depressions (depression storage). Only a fraction of the rain actually drains out.

The **Rational Method** is essentially saying: "Take the total rainfall rate pouring into the bucket, multiply it by a factor ($C$, between 0 and 1) representing how much actually escapes as runoff, and that gives you the maximum outflow rate."

The key insight is the **timing** ŌĆö the rain must be falling intensely enough for long enough that **every part of the catchment is contributing water to the outlet at the same instant**. The duration of rainfall required for this to happen is called the **Time of Concentration ($t_c$)**. If rain falls for less time than $t_c$, water from distant parts of the catchment hasn't arrived yet; if it falls longer, the flow is sustained but no longer peaking at a higher rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Assumption:** Peak runoff occurs when the rainfall duration equals the **time of concentration** of the catchment, ensuring simultaneous contribution from the entire watershed.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Validity Limits

- **Recommended Catchment Area:** Ōēż **50 km┬▓** (some texts restrict to 25 km┬▓)
- **Return Period:** Typically **2 to 100 years** depending on design importance
- **Runoff Coefficient ($C$):** **Dimensionless**, ranging from 0.05 (sandy flat) to 0.95 (paved urban)
- **Rainfall Intensity ($I$):** Expressed in **mm/hr** when $A$ is in **hectares** and $Q_p$ in **m┬│/s** after unit conversion
- **Time of Concentration ($t_c$):** Expressed in **minutes** or **hours**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often forget the **unit conversion factor** when using $A$ in hectares, $I$ in mm/hr, and $Q$ in m┬│/s. The conversion factor is **0.00278** (or 2.78 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│).

The complete dimensional equation is:

$$Q_p \; (\text{m}^3/\text{s}) = \frac{C \cdot I \; (\text{mm/hr}) \cdot A \; (\text{hectares})}{360} = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the Rational Method

The Rational Method rests on several simplifying assumptions, which directly govern its scope and validity:

1. **Uniform Spatial Rainfall:** Rainfall intensity is assumed to be **uniformly distributed** over the entire catchment. This is why the method is restricted to small catchments where spatial variability is minimal.
2. **Constant Rainfall Intensity:** The rainfall intensity $I$ is assumed to be **constant** over the duration of the design storm, equal to the average intensity.
3. **Peak at Time of Concentration:** The peak runoff rate $Q_p$ occurs when the rainfall duration $t_d$ equals the **time of concentration** $t_c$ of the catchment.
4. **Linear Response:** The catchment response is treated as **linear** ŌĆö runoff is directly proportional to rainfall (a lumped $C$ captures all losses).
5. **Constant Runoff Coefficient:** The coefficient $C$ is treated as **time-invariant** during the storm, representing an average condition.
6. **No Baseflow:** The method computes **direct surface runoff only**; it does not account for baseflow or subsurface contributions separately.

## 2.2 Why and How of Each Step

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Catchment Boundary:** Delineate the topographic divide of the basin from contour maps or surveyed data. This defines $A$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Determine Time of Concentration ($t_c$):** The time required for a water particle to travel from the **hydraulically most distant point** of the catchment to the outlet. $t_c$ depends on:
- **Length of the longest flow path** ($L$)
- **Slope** of the flow path ($S$)
- **Surface roughness** (Manning's $n$ or similar)
- **Land use** characteristics

**Step 3 ŌĆö Select Design Return Period ($T$):** Based on the importance of the structure (e.g., culvert, storm drain, small dam) and the consequences of failure. Use IDF (Intensity-Duration-Frequency) curves.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Obtain Rainfall Intensity ($I$):** From IDF curves, read off the intensity corresponding to **duration = $t_c$** and **return period = $T$**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Estimate Runoff Coefficient ($C$):** Select $C$ based on:
- **Soil type** (sandy, loam, clay)
- **Land use** (forest, agriculture, urban, paved)
- **Slope** of catchment
- **Antecedent moisture condition**

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute Peak Discharge:** Substitute into $Q_p = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_p = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A$ | Peak discharge (rational formula, $A$ in hectares, $I$ in mm/hr) | m┬│/s |
| 2 | $Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A / 360$ | Same as above, explicit conversion | m┬│/s |
| 3 | $Q_p = C \cdot i \cdot a$ | General form (SI units) | m┬│/s if $i$ in m/s, $a$ in m┬▓ |
| 4 | $t_c = t_o + t_f$ | Total time of concentration = overland flow time + channel flow time | minutes or hours |
| 5 | $t_o = 0.164 \cdot \frac{L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$ | Kirpich formula for overland flow in small basins (L in m, S dimensionless) | minutes |
| 6 | $t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} / S^{0.385}$ | Kirpich formula (L in km, S dimensionless) | minutes |
| 7 | $t_c = \frac{0.467 \cdot L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$ | Kirpich formula (L in km, S dimensionless) | hours |
| 8 | $V = 16.1345 \cdot \sqrt{S}$ | Kirpich average velocity in overland flow (m/s) | m/s |
| 9 | $t_o = 0.914 \cdot \frac{(L \cdot n)^{0.8}}{(P_2)^{0.5} \cdot S^{0.4}}$ | Kerby formula for overland flow | minutes |
| 10 | $t_f = \frac{L_f}{V_f}$ | Channel/pipe flow time | minutes |
| 11 | $V_f = \frac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | Manning's velocity for channel flow | m/s |
| 12 | $C_{weighted} = \frac{\sum C_i \cdot A_i}{\sum A_i}$ | Weighted runoff coefficient for heterogeneous catchment | dimensionless |
| 13 | $R_o = C \cdot P$ | Total runoff depth from storm | mm |
| 14 | $V_{runoff} = C \cdot I \cdot A \cdot t_d$ | Total runoff volume during duration $t_d$ | m┬│ |
| 15 | $I = \frac{a}{(t_d + b)^c}$ | General IDF relation (Talbot-type power law) | mm/hr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Trick for KTU:** The magic number **0.00278** comes from $\frac{1}{360} \cdot \frac{1}{1000} \cdot \frac{10^4}{1}$ = (mm to m) ├Ś (hr to s)Ōü╗┬╣ ├Ś (hectare to m┬▓). Memorize this!

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Urban Storm Drainage Design:** Sizing of inlets, gutters, storm sewers, and culverts in cities.
- **Airport Drainage:** Design of runoff collection systems on runways and aprons.
- **Small Bridge and Culvert Design:** Estimating peak flow for hydraulic structures.
- **Parking Lot and Highway Drainage:** Spacing and sizing of inlets and ditches.
- **Small Dam Spillways:** Preliminary sizing for ungated spillways on minor reservoirs.
- **Land Development Planning:** Predicting post-development runoff to design stormwater management facilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** The Rational Method is the **first method taught** in any hydrology course (KTU, AICTE, and internationally) because of its simplicity. Despite modern advances (SCS-CN method, unit hydrograph, rainfall-runoff simulation), it remains the **preferred method for urban micro-catchments (< 50 ha)** in municipal engineering practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Unit Conversion Factor 0.00278

The Rational Method is fundamentally a **dimensional analysis** exercise. Let us derive the conversion factor rigorously so that no KTU examiner can ever catch you off-guard.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the base equation in consistent SI units:**

$$Q_p = C \cdot I_{SI} \cdot A_{SI}$$

Where $I_{SI}$ is in m/s and $A_{SI}$ is in m┬▓ to give $Q_p$ in m┬│/s.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express intensity in mm/hr:**

$$I \; (\text{mm/hr}) = I_{SI} \; (\text{m/s}) \times \frac{1 \, \text{m}}{1000 \, \text{mm}} \times \frac{3600 \, \text{s}}{1 \, \text{hr}}$$

$$I_{SI} = I \; (\text{mm/hr}) \times \frac{1}{3.6 \times 10^6} \; (\text{m/ms ├Ś s/hr})$$

Wait, let us be careful with the algebra:

$$I \; (\text{mm/hr}) = I_{SI} \left(\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right) \times \frac{1000 \, \text{mm}}{1 \, \text{m}} \times \frac{3600 \, \text{s}}{1 \, \text{hr}} = I_{SI} \times 3.6 \times 10^6$$

Therefore:

$$I_{SI} = \frac{I}{3.6 \times 10^6} \; (\text{m/s})$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express area in hectares:**

$$A \; (\text{ha}) = A_{SI} \left(\text{m}^2\right) \times \frac{1 \, \text{ha}}{10^4 \, \text{m}^2}$$

Therefore:

$$A_{SI} = A \times 10^4 \; (\text{m}^2)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into the SI base equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= C \cdot I_{SI} \cdot A_{SI} \\
&= C \cdot \frac{I}{3.6 \times 10^6} \cdot A \times 10^4 \\
&= C \cdot I \cdot A \cdot \frac{10^4}{3.6 \times 10^6} \\
&= C \cdot I \cdot A \cdot \frac{1}{360} \\
&= 0.002778 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{Q_p = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A}$$

where $Q_p$ is in m┬│/s, $I$ in mm/hr, and $A$ in hectares. This is the standard form used in 95% of KTU exam problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kirpich Formula (Empirical)

The Kirpich (1940) formula is one of the most frequently tested $t_c$ equations in KTU exams. It was developed from data on small agricultural watersheds in Tennessee, USA.

**Empirical basis:** The travel time of the flood wave from the catchment divide to the outlet is related to the length $L$ of the longest watercourse and its average slope $S$:

**Step 1 ŌĆö The general form proposed by Kirpich:**

$$t_c = K \cdot \frac{L^{\alpha}}{S^{\beta}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Through regression on observed data:**

Kirpich found that $\alpha \approx 0.77$ and $\beta \approx 0.385$ gave the best fit.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calibration to yield $t_c$ in minutes when $L$ is in meters:**

Using the form:

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot \frac{L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$$

gives $t_c$ in **minutes** when $L$ is in **kilometers**.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conversion to SI units (L in meters, $t_c$ in minutes):**

Since $L$ in km = $L$ in m / 1000, the coefficient adjusts:

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot \frac{(L_{km})^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$$

Substituting $L_{km} = L_m / 1000$:

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot \frac{(L_m / 1000)^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}} = 0.01947 \cdot \frac{L_m^{0.77}}{1000^{0.77} \cdot S^{0.385}}$$

Now $1000^{0.77} = 10^{2.31} \approx 204.17$, so:

$$t_c = \frac{0.01947}{204.17} \cdot \frac{L_m^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}} \approx 9.54 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \frac{L_m^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$$

This is a small number, so the alternative form most commonly used in KTU is:

$$t_c = 0.164 \cdot \frac{L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}} \; (\text{min, when } L \text{ in m})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Always state the units of $L$ and $S$ explicitly. $S$ is the slope in $\text{m/m}$ (dimensionless). Common student error: writing $S$ in % ŌĆö must convert to fraction first (e.g., 2% = 0.02).

## 3.3 Worked Example: KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A small urban catchment has an area of 6 hectares, a runoff coefficient $C = 0.65$, and a time of concentration of 20 minutes. Using the IDF relation $I = \frac{1200}{(t + 10)}$ (where $I$ is in mm/hr and $t$ in minutes), estimate the peak runoff rate for a 25-year return period.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify design storm duration:**

For peak runoff, rainfall duration $t_d$ = time of concentration $t_c$ = 20 minutes.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute rainfall intensity:**

$$I = \frac{1200}{(20 + 10)} = \frac{1200}{30} = 40 \; \text{mm/hr}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Rational Formula:**

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A$$

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \times 0.65 \times 40 \times 6$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate step by step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= 0.00278 \times 0.65 \times 40 \times 6 \\
&= 0.00278 \times 0.65 \times 240 \\
&= 0.00278 \times 156 \\
&= 0.4337 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_p \approx 0.434 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ or **434 liters/second**.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Composite (Weighted) Catchment

**Problem:** A 12-hectare catchment consists of:
- 7 ha of residential area, $C_1 = 0.50$
- 3 ha of paved streets, $C_2 = 0.85$
- 2 ha of forest, $C_3 = 0.20$

The design rainfall intensity is 50 mm/hr. Compute peak runoff.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute weighted $C$:**

$$C_{weighted} = \frac{(0.50 \times 7) + (0.85 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 2)}{7 + 3 + 2}$$

$$C_{weighted} = \frac{3.50 + 2.55 + 0.40}{12} = \frac{6.45}{12} = 0.5375$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Rational Formula:**

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \times 0.5375 \times 50 \times 12$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= 0.00278 \times 0.5375 \times 600 \\
&= 0.00278 \times 322.5 \\
&= 0.8966 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_p \approx 0.897 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ (Ōēł 897 L/s).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Rational Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Catchment Problem] --> B0[Step 1: Delineate Catchment Boundary from Contour Map]
    B0 --> C0[Step 2: Measure Catchment Area A in hectares]
    C0 --> D0[Step 3: Identify Longest Flow Path L]
    D0 --> E0[Step 4: Compute Average Slope S along L]
    E0 --> F0[Step 5: Calculate Time of Concentration tc]
    F0 --> F1[Kirpich Formula]
    F0 --> F2[Kerby Formula]
    F0 --> F3[Manning Kinematic]
    F1 --> G0[Step 6: Choose Return Period T based on Structure Importance]
    F2 --> G0
    F3 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[Step 7: Read Rainfall Intensity I from IDF Curve at duration tc and return period T]
    H0 --> I0[Step 8: Determine Runoff Coefficient C from Land Use Table]
    I0 --> I1{Homogeneous Catchment?}
    I1 -- Yes --> J0[Use single C value directly]
    I1 -- No --> I2[Compute Weighted C using area-weighted average]
    I2 --> J0
    J0 --> K0[Step 9: Apply Qp equals 0.00278 times C times I times A]
    K0 --> L0[Step 10: Report Peak Discharge in cumecs]
    L0 --> M0[END: Design Storm Sewer, Culvert or Drain]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Hydrological Response Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Rainfall Input P] --> S[Storm Duration td]
    S --> L[Losses: Infiltration + Evaporation + Depression Storage]
    L --> R[Effective Rainfall Pe equals C times P]
    R --> T[Travel through Catchment with Time of Concentration tc]
    T --> Q[Peak Discharge Qp at Outlet]
    
    subgraph SF[Storage and Attenuation Effects]
        S1[Surface Storage] --> A1[Attenuates Peak]
        S2[Channel Storage] --> A1
    end
    
    R --> SF
    SF --> Q
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Time-Area Relationship in Rational Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TC[Time of Concentration tc] --> PEAK[Peak Flow Condition Achieved]
    TD1[Rainfall Duration less than tc] --> SUB[Sub Peak: Not all catchment contributing]
    TD2[Rainfall Duration equal to tc] --> PEAK
    TD3[Rainfall Duration greater than tc] --> EQ[Equal Peak: Sustained flow, not higher]
    
    PEAK --> OUT[Maximum Qp occurs at td equals tc]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Rational Method Sub-System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input[Input Subsystem]
        I1[Area A hectares]
        I2[Slope S m per m]
        I3[Length L meters]
        I4[Land Use Map]
    end
    
    subgraph Processing[Processing Subsystem]
        P1[Time of Concentration Calculator]
        P2[IDF Curve Lookup]
        P3[Runoff Coefficient Selector]
        P4[Rational Equation Solver]
    end
    
    subgraph Output[Output Subsystem]
        O1[Peak Discharge Qp cumecs]
        O2[Design Storm Duration minutes]
        O3[Catchment Response Time]
    end
    
    I1 --> P4
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P1 --> O2
    P1 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These block diagrams translate the spatial physics of overland flow into a logical processing pipeline. The Rational Method treats the catchment as a single "lumped" unit, which is why the input is aggregated area, slope, and land use, and the output is a single peak discharge.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Rational Method. State any two assumptions.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set 1]**

**Model Answer:**

The Rational Method is a hydrological technique used to estimate the **peak rate of surface runoff** from a small drainage catchment subjected to a design rainfall of specified return period. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A$$

where $Q_p$ = peak discharge (m┬│/s), $C$ = runoff coefficient (dimensionless), $I$ = average rainfall intensity (mm/hr), and $A$ = catchment area (hectares).

**Two Assumptions:**
1. The rainfall intensity is **uniformly distributed** over the entire catchment area for the entire storm duration.
2. The peak runoff rate occurs when the **rainfall duration equals the time of concentration** ($t_d = t_c$) of the catchment.

*(Any two valid assumptions accepted ŌĆö common alternatives: linear response, constant $C$, small catchment, etc.)*

### Question 2: What is the significance of the Time of Concentration in the Rational Method?
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Time of Concentration ($t_c$)** is defined as the time required for runoff to travel from the **hydraulically most remote point** of the catchment to the outlet. Its significance in the Rational Method is twofold:

1. **Design Duration:** The critical design storm duration is set equal to $t_c$ because this is the duration beyond which the runoff rate does not increase further. Using shorter or longer durations would underestimate the peak.

2. **Peak Timing:** $t_c$ determines the **lag time** between peak rainfall intensity and peak discharge at the outlet, which is critical for stormwater detention design and flood routing.

3. **Rainfall Intensity Selection:** Since the Rational Method uses an IDF curve, $t_c$ is the **independent variable** used to read off the design intensity $I$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Note:** As per the KTU 2024 scheme, every Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts typically split as 7 + 7. Students answer **one of two choices** (A or B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Application Problem**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

A 15-hectare catchment in a suburban area has the following land use distribution:

| Sub-area | Type | Area (ha) | $C$ value |
|----------|------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Residential (flat, 30% impervious) | 8 | 0.55 |
| 2 | Commercial (downtown) | 4 | 0.80 |
| 3 | Parkland (heavy soil, flat) | 3 | 0.18 |

The longest watercourse has length $L = 1200$ m and average slope $S = 0.012$ (m/m). Use the Kirpich formula to estimate the time of concentration. The IDF relation for the region is:

$$I = \frac{900}{(t + 8)^{0.75}}$$

where $I$ is in mm/hr and $t$ is in minutes. Compute the peak runoff for a 50-year return period.

#### Part (a): Compute Time of Concentration and Rainfall Intensity (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Kirpich Formula (L in meters, S dimensionless):**

$$t_c = 0.164 \cdot \frac{L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$$

Compute $L^{0.77}$:

$$L^{0.77} = 1200^{0.77}$$

Using logarithms: $\log(1200) = 3.0792$, so $\log(L^{0.77}) = 0.77 \times 3.0792 = 2.3710$, giving $L^{0.77} \approx 235.2$.

Compute $S^{0.385}$:

$$S^{0.385} = 0.012^{0.385}$$

Using logarithms: $\log(0.012) = -1.9208$, so $\log(S^{0.385}) = 0.385 \times (-1.9208) = -0.7395$, giving $S^{0.385} \approx 0.1823$.

Therefore:

$$t_c = 0.164 \times \frac{235.2}{0.1823} = 0.164 \times 1290.18 \approx 211.6 \; \text{minutes}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Rainfall Intensity at $t_d = t_c = 211.6$ min:**

$$I = \frac{900}{(211.6 + 8)^{0.75}} = \frac{900}{219.6^{0.75}}$$

Compute $219.6^{0.75}$: $\log(219.6) = 2.3417$, $0.75 \times 2.3417 = 1.7563$, $10^{1.7563} \approx 57.04$.

$$I = \frac{900}{57.04} \approx 15.78 \; \text{mm/hr}$$

#### Part (b): Compute Weighted C and Peak Discharge (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Weighted Runoff Coefficient:**

$$C_w = \frac{(0.55 \times 8) + (0.80 \times 4) + (0.18 \times 3)}{8 + 4 + 3}$$

$$C_w = \frac{4.40 + 3.20 + 0.54}{15} = \frac{8.14}{15} = 0.5427$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Rational Formula:**

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \times C_w \times I \times A$$

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \times 0.5427 \times 15.78 \times 15$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= 0.00278 \times 0.5427 \times 15.78 \times 15 \\
&= 0.00278 \times 0.5427 \times 236.7 \\
&= 0.00278 \times 128.45 \\
&= 0.3571 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_p \approx 0.357 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ (357 L/s).

**Check (rounded summary):** $t_c \approx 212$ min, $I \approx 15.8$ mm/hr, $C_w \approx 0.54$, $Q_p \approx 0.36$ m┬│/s.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Conceptual and Numerical Question**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Restructured for 2024 Scheme]**

(a) Derive the unit conversion factor 0.00278 used in the Rational Method, clearly showing the dimensional analysis. State any **four** assumptions of the Rational Method. (7 marks)

(b) A 25-hectare rural catchment has $C = 0.40$. The longest flow path is 800 m long with average slope $S = 0.008$. Using the Kirpich formula, calculate the time of concentration. If the rainfall intensity for a 10-year return period at $t_d = t_c$ is 35 mm/hr, compute the peak runoff. (7 marks)

#### Part (a): Derivation of 0.00278 and Assumptions (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Derivation:**

The base Rational equation in consistent SI units (m and s):

$$Q_p = C \cdot I_{SI} \cdot A_{SI}$$

Given that $I$ is in mm/hr and $A$ is in hectares, we convert:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{SI} \; (\text{m/s}) &= I \; (\text{mm/hr}) \times \frac{1 \, \text{m}}{1000 \, \text{mm}} \times \frac{1 \, \text{hr}}{3600 \, \text{s}} \\
&= \frac{I}{3.6 \times 10^6} \; (\text{m/s})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{SI} \; (\text{m}^2) &= A \; (\text{ha}) \times 10000 \; (\text{m}^2/\text{ha})
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= C \cdot \frac{I}{3.6 \times 10^6} \cdot 10^4 \cdot A \\
&= C \cdot I \cdot A \cdot \frac{10^4}{3.6 \times 10^6} \\
&= C \cdot I \cdot A \cdot \frac{1}{360} \\
&= 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Four Assumptions:**
1. Rainfall is **uniformly distributed** in space over the catchment.
2. Rainfall intensity is **constant** over the storm duration.
3. Peak runoff occurs at **$t_d = t_c$**.
4. The catchment is **small** enough that the lumped-parameter approach is valid.
5. (Optional) The runoff coefficient $C$ is constant in time.

#### Part (b): Computation for Rural Catchment (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $t_c$ using Kirpich:**

$$t_c = 0.164 \cdot \frac{L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$$

$L^{0.77} = 800^{0.77}$: $\log(800) = 2.9031$, $0.77 \times 2.9031 = 2.2354$, $L^{0.77} \approx 171.9$.

$S^{0.385} = 0.008^{0.385}$: $\log(0.008) = -2.0969$, $0.385 \times (-2.0969) = -0.8073$, $S^{0.385} \approx 0.1561$.

$$t_c = 0.164 \times \frac{171.9}{0.1561} = 0.164 \times 1101.2 \approx 180.6 \; \text{minutes}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Peak Runoff:**

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \times 0.40 \times 35 \times 25$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= 0.00278 \times 0.40 \times 875 \\
&= 0.00278 \times 350 \\
&= 0.973 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_c \approx 180.6$ min, $Q_p \approx 0.973$ m┬│/s.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Omission (ŌĆō2 marks):** Students frequently write $Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A$ without the 0.00278 factor. If $A$ is in hectares and $I$ in mm/hr, the factor **MUST** be present.
> 2. **Slope Confusion (ŌĆō1 to 2 marks):** Using $S$ in % (e.g., 2) instead of fraction (0.02) yields a $t_c$ that is off by **orders of magnitude**. Always convert slope to dimensionless ratio.
> 3. **Wrong Kirpich Variant (ŌĆō1 mark):** There are three common Kirpich forms (L in m, L in km, $t_c$ in min or hours). Using the wrong one for given L units leads to wrong $t_c$ and hence wrong $I$.
> 4. **Averaging C arithmetically for non-uniform catchments (ŌĆō2 marks):** The correct method is the **area-weighted average**, not the simple arithmetic mean. A 4-ha + 11-ha catchment with $C_1=0.3$ and $C_2=0.7$ does NOT give $C = 0.50$.
> 5. **Forgetting to set $t_d = t_c$ for design (ŌĆō1 mark):** Some students compute $t_c$ but use an arbitrary 30-min or 60-min duration. The design duration **MUST equal $t_c$** for peak runoff estimation.
> 6. **Reporting $Q_p$ without units (ŌĆō0.5 mark):** Always write m┬│/s or cumecs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this on the night before the exam!):**

- **Core Equation:** $Q_p = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A$ (m┬│/s, mm/hr, hectares). The 0.00278 = 1/360 is the **unit conversion** factor ŌĆö do not forget it!
- **Range of Validity:** Catchment area Ōēż **50 km┬▓** (most commonly Ōēż 25 km┬▓ for high accuracy).
- **Time of Concentration:** The time for runoff to travel from the most distant point to the outlet. **Peak runoff occurs when $t_d = t_c$.**
- **Kirpich Formula (Most Tested):** $t_c = 0.164 \cdot L^{0.77} / S^{0.385}$ (L in **meters**, S in **m/m**).
- **Other $t_c$ Formulas:** Kerby (gentle overland), Manning-kinematic, Izzard, Federal Aviation. Pick whichever fits the problem context.
- **IDF Curve Usage:** Always read $I$ at the duration **$t_d = t_c$** for the chosen return period. Power form: $I = a/(t+b)^c$.
- **Runoff Coefficient $C$:** Always **area-weighted** for non-homogeneous catchments. Typical values: 0.10 (sandy/flat) ŌåÆ 0.95 (paved/urban).
- **Common $C$ Values to Memorize:** Paved/asphalt = 0.85, Residential (flat) = 0.50, Forest (flat) = 0.15, Lawn (sandy) = 0.10, Concrete/roofs = 0.90.
- **Return Periods:** Storm drains = 2ŌĆō10 yr, culverts = 25ŌĆō50 yr, major structures = 50ŌĆō100 yr, spillways = 100+ yr.
- **Limitations:** Does NOT account for temporal variability of rainfall, channel routing, baseflow, or storage effects. Use **SCS-CN method** or **Unit Hydrograph** for larger catchments.
- **Derivable Items for KTU:** Unit conversion factor 0.00278, Kirpich coefficient derivation, weighted C calculation.
- **Answer Format:** Always box or underline your final $Q_p$ value, and **always include units**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cook's Method and SCS Curve Number Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cook's Method and SCS Curve Number Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś MODULE 2 ŌĆö Runoff Estimation Techniques

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cook's Method ŌĆö Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cook's Method (1946)** is an empirical rainfallŌĆōrunoff relationship used to estimate the **peak runoff rate (q_p)** from **small, ungauged rural watersheds** (typically A < 25 km┬▓). The method assigns numerical weighting factors to watershed characteristics that govern infiltration, overland flow, and surface detention, and combines them into a dimensionless **runoff coefficient (C)**.

**Formal KTU Statement:**
> *"Cook's method is a peak-discharge estimation technique for ungauged catchments in which the runoff coefficient C is derived as the product of four dimensionless factors: Topography (T), Soil type/Infiltration (S), Vegetation (V), and Surface storage (Ss)."*

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Kitchen Sink Story ­¤ŹĮ’ĖÅ**
Imagine pouring a bucket of water onto four different kitchen surfaces: a smooth tilted tile (steep, impervious), sandy soil, a mossy patch, and a saucer-shaped depression. Each surface "decides" how much water runs off, how much soaks in, and how much gets held back. Cook's method simply **assigns a numerical score to each surface behaviour** and multiplies them to predict the total water that ends up in the drain. The basin is the kitchen; rain is the bucket; runoff is the water in the drain.

---

### 1.2 SCS Curve Number Method ŌĆö Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **SCS (Soil Conservation Service) Curve Number Method**, developed by the **USDA in 1954** and revised through the **NEH-4 (National Engineering Handbook, Section 4 ŌĆō Hydrology)**, is a **deterministic, lumped-parameter model** that estimates **direct surface runoff depth (Q)** from a **storm rainfall depth (P)** using a single dimensionless index called the **Curve Number (CN)**.

**Formal KTU Statement:**
> *"The SCS-CN method relates cumulative rainfall P to cumulative runoff Q through the principle of continuity and two empirical hypotheses ŌĆö namely, the ratio of actual to potential retention is equal to the ratio of actual to potential runoff, and the initial abstraction Ia is a fixed fraction (0.2) of the maximum retention S."*

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Sponge and Saucer Analogy ­¤¦ĮŌśĢ**
Picture a dry sponge (soil) sitting in a saucer. Initially, all rainwater is "drunk" by the sponge (initial abstraction ŌĆö interception, depression storage, infiltration). Once the sponge saturates, additional water overflows into the saucer and finally spills out the edge as **runoff (Q)**. The **Curve Number** is essentially the "wetness character" of the sponge-soil-saucer system. **CN = 100** means a glass plate ŌĆö every drop becomes runoff. **CN = 30** means a thirsty forest floor ŌĆö almost nothing runs off.

**Key Physical Constants:**

| Symbol | Value | Meaning |
|:------:|:-----:|:--------|
| $C_{f}$ | **0.9** | Cook's empirical constant |
| $I_{a}$ coefficient | **0.2** | Fraction of $S$ lost to initial abstraction (SCS) |
| $C_{m}$ | **25400/1000** | Unit conversion (mm Ōåö inch) for SCS |

---

### 1.3 Why These Two Methods? ŌĆö KTU Syllabus Significance

> [!NOTE]
> Both methods are classified under **"Inductive Methods / Rational Type Approaches"** for **ungauged basins**. They appear regularly in **Part B (14-mark) questions** of KTU ESE, and the SCS-CN method is the *de facto* standard for **flood estimation in Indian watershed projects** (CWC, IS 14986:2001).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cook's Method ŌĆö Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 The Governing Equation

Cook's method expresses the **peak runoff rate** as:

$$q_{p} \;=\; 0.9 \times C \times I \times A$$

where:

* $q_{p}$ = peak discharge (m┬│/s)
* $C$ = dimensionless runoff coefficient (Cook's composite coefficient)
* $I$ = rainfall intensity (mm/hr) for a duration equal to the **time of concentration $t_{c}$** of the basin
* $A$ = drainage area (km┬▓)
* $0.9$ = empirical constant (unit conversion factor)

### 2.1.2 The Four Component Factors

The composite coefficient $C$ is the **product** of four dimensionless sub-factors, each scored between **0 and 1**:

| Factor | Symbol | What it Represents | Range |
|:------:|:------:|:-------------------|:-----:|
| Topography | $T$ | Slope steepness and basin relief | 0.08 ŌĆō 0.30 |
| Soil/Infiltration | $S_{f}$ | Soil texture and infiltration capacity | 0.04 ŌĆō 0.40 |
| Vegetation | $V$ | Type and density of vegetal cover | 0.05 ŌĆō 0.30 |
| Surface Storage | $S_{s}$ | Depression storage, ponding, terracing | 0.04 ŌĆō 0.20 |

**Structural Formula (Product Form):**

$$C \;=\; 0.9 \times T \times S_{f} \times V \times S_{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbook variants (Subramanya, Raghunath) state:
> $$C \;=\; (0.039 \, T \, S_{f} \, V \, S_{s})^{1/3}$$
> This is the **cube-root simplification** used in older IS codes. KTU 2024 expects the **product form with 0.9 multiplier** unless the problem explicitly states the cube-root form.

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic (The "Why" Behind Each Factor)

1. **Topography (T):** Steeper slopes ŌåÆ higher flow velocity ŌåÆ less time for infiltration ŌåÆ higher runoff. **T increases with slope.**
2. **Soil type (S_f):** Sandy soils allow more infiltration ŌåÆ lower runoff coefficient. Clayey/rocky soils ŌåÆ high runoff. **S_f is highest for impervious soils.**
3. **Vegetation (V):** Dense forest canopy intercepts rainfall, root systems increase infiltration. **V is highest for bare ground, lowest for dense forest.**
4. **Surface storage (S_s):** Flat terrain, terraces, paddy fields, lakes retain water temporarily. **S_s is highest where water is held back.**

**Final composite C** = multiplicative combination because all four factors **simultaneously reduce or amplify** the runoff potential ŌĆö a single weak factor (e.g., dense forest) can drastically reduce total runoff even if others favour high runoff.

### 2.1.4 Engineering Utility of Cook's Method

* Quick **preliminary flood estimation** for **bridge culverts, farm ponds, and small check dams** in ungauged catchments.
* Used in **Kerala's rural road and cross-drainage design** (PWD Minor Irrigation Manual).
* Provides a **first-cut C-value** that can later be refined by comparison with SCS-CN.

---

## 2.2 SCS Curve Number Method ŌĆö Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 The Two Empirical Hypotheses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The entire SCS-CN model rests on **two postulates**:

**Hypothesis 1 ŌĆö Proportionality Hypothesis:**

$$\frac{F_{a}}{S} \;=\; \frac{Q}{P - I_{a}}$$

where:
* $F_{a}$ = actual continuing abstraction (infiltration + storage) **after** runoff begins
* $S$ = maximum potential retention (mm)
* $Q$ = direct surface runoff depth (mm)
* $P$ = total storm rainfall (mm)
* $I_{a}$ = initial abstraction (mm)

**Hypothesis 2 ŌĆö Initial Abstraction Ratio:**

$$I_{a} \;=\; 0.2 \, S$$

> Empirical studies on thousands of small agricultural watersheds showed that the initial loss is **approximately 20 %** of the maximum retention.

### 2.2.2 The Canonical SCS-CN Runoff Equation

Combining both hypotheses with the **continuity equation**:

$$P \;=\; I_{a} + F_{a} + Q$$

yields the **most-cited formula in engineering hydrology**:

$$\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \dfrac{(P - 0.2 S)^{2}}{P + 0.8 S}\,} \quad \text{valid for } P > 0.2S$$

If $P \leq 0.2 S$, then $Q = 0$ (rainfall entirely consumed by initial abstraction).

### 2.2.3 The Curve Number Relationship

The maximum retention $S$ is related to the dimensionless **Curve Number (CN)** by:

$$S \;=\; \dfrac{25400}{CN} \;-\; 254 \quad \text{(S in mm)}$$

$$S \;=\; \dfrac{1000}{CN} \;-\; 10 \quad \text{(S in inches)}$$

> CN is bounded as: $0 \leq CN \leq 100$
> * **CN = 100** ŌåÆ $S = 0$ ŌåÆ water-tight surface (concrete, bare rock)
> * **CN = 0** ŌåÆ $S = \infty$ ŌåÆ infinitely absorptive (theoretical limit)
> * **Typical range for natural watersheds: 30 Ōēż CN Ōēż 98**

### 2.2.4 Factors Determining the Curve Number

CN depends on **three orthogonal classifications**:

1. **Hydrologic Soil Group (HSG):** A, B, C, D based on infiltration capacity
   * **A** ŌĆö Deep sand, deep loess, aggregated silts (high infiltration Ōēź 7.5 mm/hr)
   * **B** ŌĆö Shallow loess, sandy loam (moderate 3.8ŌĆō7.5 mm/hr)
   * **C** ŌĆö Clay loams, shallow sandy loam (slow 1.3ŌĆō3.8 mm/hr)
   * **D** ŌĆö Heavy plastic clays, shallow soils with clay pan (very slow < 1.3 mm/hr)

2. **Land Use / Cover:** Fallow, row crops, pasture, forest, impervious surfaces, water bodies, etc.

3. **Hydrologic Condition:** "Poor" or "Good" ŌĆö a qualitative rating of vegetal density, soil compaction, and surface treatment.

### 2.2.5 Composite (Weighted) CN for Sub-Areas

When a watershed contains **multiple land-use patches**, the composite CN is the **area-weighted average**:

$$\boxed{\,CN_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} CN_{i} \times A_{i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}}\,}$$
