# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the SCS-CN Runoff Equation

### Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the continuity equation

$$P \;=\; I_a + F_a + Q$$

**Logic:** The total storm rainfall $P$ partitions into **three** simultaneous sinks:
* $I_a$ ŌĆö water intercepted/depressed/infiltrated *before* runoff starts
* $F_a$ ŌĆö water continuing to be lost *after* runoff starts
* $Q$ ŌĆö the direct surface runoff

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the SCS Proportionality Hypothesis

$$\frac{F_a}{S} \;=\; \frac{Q}{P - I_a}$$

**Logic:** The ratio of *actual* continuing loss to *potential* maximum retention equals the ratio of *actual* runoff to *potential* runoff ($P - I_a$). This is a self-similar scaling assumption.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $F_a$

Cross-multiplying:

$$F_a \;=\; S \cdot \frac{Q}{P - I_a}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $F_a$ into the continuity equation

$$P \;=\; I_a + S \cdot \frac{Q}{P - I_a} + Q$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Multiply both sides by $(P - I_a)$ to clear the denominator

$$P \cdot (P - I_a) \;=\; I_a (P - I_a) + S \, Q + Q (P - I_a)$$

Expanding the left side:

$$P^2 - P \, I_a \;=\; P \, I_a - I_a^2 + S \, Q + P \, Q - I_a \, Q$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Group all $Q$-terms on one side

$$P^2 - P \, I_a - P \, I_a + I_a^2 \;=\; S \, Q + P \, Q - I_a \, Q$$

$$P^2 - 2 P \, I_a + I_a^2 \;=\; Q (S + P - I_a)$$

The left side is a **perfect square**: $(P - I_a)^2$. Thus:

$$(P - I_a)^2 \;=\; Q \, (P - I_a + S)$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Solve for $Q$

$$Q \;=\; \frac{(P - I_a)^2}{P - I_a + S}$$

### Step 8 ŌĆö Apply the **Initial Abstraction Hypothesis** ($I_a = 0.2 S$)

Substituting $I_a = 0.2 S$:

$$Q \;=\; \frac{(P - 0.2 S)^2}{P - 0.2 S + S} \;=\; \frac{(P - 0.2 S)^2}{P + 0.8 S}$$

> Ō£ģ **Result recovered.** The same form valid for $P > 0.2 S$; for $P \le 0.2 S$, set $Q=0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the SCS RetentionŌĆōCN Relation

The CN method is constructed so that **CN = 100** corresponds to $S = 0$ (zero retention), and **CN = 0** corresponds to $S = \infty$.

A convenient algebraic mapping satisfying these limits is the **hyperbolic relation**:

$$S \;=\; \frac{a}{CN} - a \quad \text{where } a \text{ is a constant}$$

For $CN = 100$: $S = a/100 - a = a(0.01 - 1) = -0.99 a$. For $S = 0$, we need $a = 0$, which is degenerate ŌĆö so a different form is used.

**Standard form adopted by USDA-NEH-4:**

$$S \;=\; \frac{25400}{CN} - 254 \quad \text{(mm)} \qquad \text{or} \qquad S = \frac{1000}{CN} - 10 \quad \text{(in)}$$

**Verification at $CN=100$:**

$$S = \frac{25400}{100} - 254 = 254 - 254 = 0 \;\; \text{mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification at $CN=50$:**

$$S = \frac{25400}{50} - 254 = 508 - 254 = 254 \;\; \text{mm}$$

The constant **25400 (or 1000)** is a *curve-fitting coefficient* chosen so that the CN values listed in the **NEH-4 Table 2-2a** reproduce observed $S$ from thousands of experimental plots.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Cook's Method

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A rural catchment near Thrissur has area **A = 5 km┬▓**. The four Cook's factors are:
> * Topography: $T = 0.20$
> * Soil: $S_f = 0.30$
> * Vegetation: $V = 0.15$
> * Surface storage: $S_s = 0.10$
>
> The rainfall intensity for a duration equal to $t_c$ is **I = 60 mm/hr**. Estimate the peak runoff rate.

**Solution ŌĆö Step-by-Step**

**Step 1:** Compute composite C.

$$C = 0.9 \times T \times S_f \times V \times S_s$$

$$C = 0.9 \times 0.20 \times 0.30 \times 0.15 \times 0.10$$

$$C = 0.9 \times 0.0009 \;=\; 8.1 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Cook's peak discharge equation.

$$q_p = 0.9 \times C \times I \times A$$

$$q_p = 0.9 \times 8.1 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 5$$

$$q_p = 0.9 \times 8.1 \times 10^{-4} \times 300$$

$$q_p = 0.9 \times 0.243 \;=\; 0.2187 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\,q_p \;\approx\; 0.219 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö SCS-CN Method

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A 10 km┬▓ watershed has the following sub-area distribution:
>
> | Sub-area | Land Use | HSG | Area (km┬▓) | CN |
> |:--------:|:---------|:---:|:----------:|:--:|
> | 1 | Row crops, poor condition | C | 4 | 85 |
> | 2 | Pasture, good condition | C | 3 | 74 |
> | 3 | Forest, good condition | B | 3 | 60 |
>
> A storm delivers **P = 80 mm** of rainfall. Compute the direct runoff depth $Q$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Composite CN (area-weighted):**

$$CN_c = \frac{(85 \times 4) + (74 \times 3) + (60 \times 3)}{4 + 3 + 3}$$

$$CN_c = \frac{340 + 222 + 180}{10} = \frac{742}{10} = 74.2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Maximum retention $S$:**

$$S = \frac{25400}{CN_c} - 254 = \frac{25400}{74.2} - 254$$

$$S = 342.32 - 254 = 88.32 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Initial abstraction $I_a$:**

$$I_a = 0.2 \, S = 0.2 \times 88.32 = 17.66 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Test the runoff threshold:**

$$P = 80 \;\; \text{mm}, \quad 0.2 S = 17.66 \;\; \text{mm}$$

Since $P = 80 > 17.66$, **runoff occurs** ($P > I_a$).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the SCS-CN equation:**

$$Q = \frac{(P - 0.2 S)^2}{P + 0.8 S} = \frac{(80 - 17.66)^2}{80 + 0.8 \times 88.32}$$

$$Q = \frac{(62.34)^2}{80 + 70.66} = \frac{3886.28}{150.66}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q \;\approx\; 25.79 \;\; \text{mm}\,}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Runoff volume over the watershed:**

$$\text{Volume} = Q \times A = 25.79 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ km}^2 = 0.02579 \text{ m} \times 10 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\text{Volume} = 257{,}900 \;\; \text{m}^3 \;=\; 0.258 \;\; \text{Mm}^3$$

> Ō£ģ **Runoff coefficient (volumetric):** $C_{vol} = Q/P = 25.79/80 \approx 0.322$ ŌĆö i.e., about **32 %** of rainfall becomes direct runoff.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example ŌĆö LaTeX Multi-Step Derivation Summary

For a **conceptual recap of the SCS-CN proof**, the aligned derivation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= I_a + F_a + Q \quad &&\text{(continuity)} \\[4pt]
\frac{F_a}{S} &= \frac{Q}{P - I_a} \quad &&\text{(proportionality hypothesis)} \\[4pt]
F_a &= \frac{S \, Q}{P - I_a} \quad &&\text{(rearranging)} \\[4pt]
P - I_a &= \frac{S \, Q}{P - I_a} + Q \quad &&\text{(continuity rearranged)} \\[4pt]
(P - I_a)^2 &= Q (S + P - I_a) \quad &&\text{(multiplying through)} \\[4pt]
Q &= \frac{(P - I_a)^2}{P - I_a + S} \quad &&\text{(isolating Q)} \\[4pt]
Q &= \frac{(P - 0.2 S)^2}{P + 0.8 S} \quad &&\text{(substituting } I_a = 0.2 S\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**State and explain the two empirical hypotheses of the SCS Curve Number method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö Bloom Level: Remember/Understand):**

The SCS-CN method is based on two hypotheses:

1. **Proportionality Hypothesis:** The ratio of actual continuing abstraction $F_a$ to the potential maximum retention $S$ is equal to the ratio of actual direct runoff $Q$ to the potential maximum runoff $(P - I_a)$. Mathematically:

$$\frac{F_a}{S} = \frac{Q}{P - I_a}$$

2. **Initial Abstraction Hypothesis:** The initial abstraction $I_a$ is a fixed fraction (0.2) of the maximum retention $S$.

$$I_a = 0.2 S$$

These two hypotheses, combined with the continuity equation $P = I_a + F_a + Q$, lead to the canonical SCS-CN runoff equation $Q = (P - 0.2 S)^2 / (P + 0.8 S)$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**List the four factors used in Cook's method and state what each represents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö Bloom Level: Remember):**

Cook's method uses **four** dimensionless sub-factors to compute the composite runoff coefficient $C$:

| Factor | Symbol | Represents |
|:------:|:------:|:-----------|
| Topography | $T$ | Slope steepness, basin relief |
| Soil/Infiltration | $S_f$ | Soil texture, infiltration capacity |
| Vegetation | $V$ | Type, density, and condition of vegetal cover |
| Surface Storage | $S_s$ | Depression storage, terracing, pondage |

The composite coefficient is:

$$C = 0.9 \times T \times S_f \times V \times S_s$$

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** ŌĆö student answers either one.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Cook's Method with Peak Discharge Estimation**

**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024, Modified from Dec 2022]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the four factors involved in Cook's method for estimating the runoff coefficient of a small ungauged catchment. How is the peak runoff rate calculated once $C$ is determined?

**(b) [7 marks]** A small watershed near Palakkad has the following characteristics:

* Catchment area $A = 8 \text{ km}^2$
* Topography factor $T = 0.25$
* Soil factor $S_f = 0.30$
* Vegetation factor $V = 0.10$
* Surface storage factor $S_s = 0.15$
* Rainfall intensity at time of concentration $I = 75 \text{ mm/hr}$

Determine the **peak runoff rate** using Cook's method.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory ŌĆö 7 marks:**

Cook's method identifies **four** sub-factors that govern runoff from a small ungauged catchment:

1. **Topography factor (T):** Represents slope. Steep slopes ŌåÆ less time for infiltration ŌåÆ high T. Range: 0.08ŌĆō0.30.
2. **Soil factor (S_f):** Represents infiltration capacity. Sandy soils ŌåÆ low S_f, clayey ŌåÆ high. Range: 0.04ŌĆō0.40.
3. **Vegetation factor (V):** Represents vegetal cover density. Dense forest ŌåÆ low V, bare ground ŌåÆ high. Range: 0.05ŌĆō0.30.
4. **Surface storage factor (S_s):** Represents depression storage, terraces, lakes. Flat paddy areas ŌåÆ high. Range: 0.04ŌĆō0.20.

The composite coefficient is the **product** of all four (multiplicative because all four simultaneously affect runoff):

$$C = 0.9 \times T \times S_f \times V \times S_s$$

The peak runoff rate is then:

$$q_p = 0.9 \times C \times I \times A \;\; \text{(m}^3/\text{s)}$$

where $I$ is rainfall intensity for duration $t_c$.

**(b) Numerical ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute product of factors.

$$T \times S_f \times V \times S_s = 0.25 \times 0.30 \times 0.10 \times 0.15 = 1.125 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $C$.

$$C = 0.9 \times 1.125 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0125 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $q_p$.

$$q_p = 0.9 \times 1.0125 \times 10^{-3} \times 75 \times 8$$

$$q_p = 0.9 \times 1.0125 \times 10^{-3} \times 600 = 0.9 \times 0.6075 = 0.5468 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\,q_p \approx 0.547 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}\,}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö SCS Curve Number Method with Composite Watershed**

**`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Modified from Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the SCS-CN runoff equation starting from the continuity equation and the two empirical hypotheses. State clearly the assumptions and limitations of the method.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 15 km┬▓ watershed in Idukki consists of three sub-areas:
* Sub-area 1: Residential (30 % impervious), HSG-B, Area = 5 km┬▓
* Sub-area 2: Open space, good condition, HSG-C, Area = 6 km┬▓
* Sub-area 3: Deciduous forest, good condition, HSG-B, Area = 4 km┬▓

Take CN values from NEH-4 Table 2-2a (Residential 30% impervious on HSG-B: CN = 72; Open space good HSG-C: CN = 74; Deciduous forest good HSG-B: CN = 55). A storm of **P = 90 mm** falls on the watershed. Compute the **direct runoff depth** $Q$ and the **total runoff volume** in Mm┬│.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation ŌĆö 7 marks (see full derivation in Section 3.1):**

* Continuity: $P = I_a + F_a + Q$  (1 mark)
* Hypothesis 1: $F_a / S = Q / (P - I_a)$  (1 mark)
* Hypothesis 2: $I_a = 0.2 S$  (1 mark)
* Algebraic manipulation to isolate $Q$  (3 marks)
* Final boxed result: $Q = (P - 0.2 S)^2 / (P + 0.8 S)$  (1 mark)

**Assumptions/Limitations (mention any 2 for 0.5 marks each):**
1. Uniform rainfall over the watershed.
2. $I_a = 0.2 S$ is an empirical average; in arid regions $I_a / S$ can be 0.1 or lower.
3. The method gives only **direct runoff depth**, not the hydrograph shape.
4. CN is for AMC-II (average) condition; for wet/dry AMC, conversion factors are needed.

**(b) Numerical ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Composite CN.

$$CN_c = \frac{(72 \times 5) + (74 \times 6) + (55 \times 4)}{5 + 6 + 4}$$

$$CN_c = \frac{360 + 444 + 220}{15} = \frac{1024}{15} = 68.27$$

**Step 2:** Maximum retention.

$$S = \frac{25400}{68.27} - 254 = 372.06 - 254 = 118.06 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** Initial abstraction.

$$I_a = 0.2 \times 118.06 = 23.61 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**Step 4:** Threshold check.

$$P = 90 \;\; \text{mm} > I_a = 23.61 \;\; \text{mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{runoff occurs}$$

**Step 5:** Direct runoff depth.

$$Q = \frac{(90 - 23.61)^2}{90 + 0.8 \times 118.06} = \frac{(66.39)^2}{90 + 94.45} = \frac{4407.65}{184.45}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q \approx 23.90 \;\; \text{mm}\,}$$

**Step 6:** Runoff volume.

$$V = Q \times A = 23.90 \text{ mm} \times 15 \text{ km}^2$$

$$V = 0.02390 \text{ m} \times 15 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 = 358{,}500 \text{ m}^3 \approx 0.359 \text{ Mm}^3$$

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU ESE (and how to avoid them):**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Cook's method (ŌĆō2 marks typical):** $I$ must be in **mm/hr** and $A$ in **km┬▓**. The constant **0.9** already includes the unit conversion. Writing $I$ in m/hr or $A$ in m┬▓ is the single most common error.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $P > 0.2 S$ test in SCS-CN (ŌĆō2 marks):** Many students blindly apply the formula even when $P \le 0.2 S$. **Always state the threshold check explicitly** in your solution. If $P \le 0.2 S$, the answer is $Q = 0$ ŌĆö write this as a sentence, not silently skip it.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the Cook's cube-root and product forms (ŌĆō1 mark):** KTU 2024 expects the **product form with 0.9 multiplier**. If you use the cube-root form, you will lose 1 mark for not following the convention unless the problem explicitly says so.
>
> 4. **Area-weighting trap (ŌĆō2 marks):** In SCS-CN, students often take a simple arithmetic mean of CN values, ignoring the **area weights**. Always write $CN_c = \sum CN_i A_i / \sum A_i$ explicitly.
>
> 5. **Not writing units in the final answer (ŌĆō1 mark):** KTU examiners deduct marks if the answer has no unit. Always end with $q_p = 0.219 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ or $Q = 25.79 \text{ mm}$.
>
> 6. **Mixing $I_a$ and $S$ in the numerator (ŌĆō2 marks):** The formula is $(P - 0.2 S)^2$, **not** $(P - S)^2$ and **not** $(P - 0.2 I_a)^2$. Memorize this exactly.

---

## 5.4 ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Reference Revision Checklist (for last-night revision):**

### ­¤Ź│ Cook's Method ŌĆö Key Points
- [ ] Use for **small ungauged** watersheds (A Ōēż 25 km┬▓).
- [ ] Four factors: **T, S_f, V, S_s** ŌĆö each in range [0, 1].
- [ ] Composite: $C = 0.9 \cdot T \cdot S_f \cdot V \cdot S_s$.
- [ ] Peak discharge: $q_p = 0.9 \cdot C \cdot I \cdot A$.
- [ ] $I$ is intensity for duration = **time of concentration**.
- [ ] Units: $I$ in **mm/hr**, $A$ in **km┬▓**, $q_p$ in **m┬│/s**.
- [ ] Older IS variant uses **cube-root** ŌĆö avoid unless specified.

### ­¤ī¦’ĖÅ SCS-CN Method ŌĆö Key Points
- [ ] Two hypotheses: **proportionality** + **$I_a = 0.2 S$**.
- [ ] Final formula: $Q = (P - 0.2 S)^2 / (P + 0.8 S)$.
- [ ] Valid only for $P > 0.2 S$ else $Q = 0$.
- [ ] $S$ in **mm** = $25400 / CN - 254$.
- [ ] $S$ in **inches** = $1000 / CN - 10$.
- [ ] CN depends on **HSG (A/B/C/D)** + **land use** + **condition**.
- [ ] Composite: $CN_c = \sum (CN_i \cdot A_i) / \sum A_i$.
- [ ] CN table values are for **AMC-II** (average moisture).
- [ ] Method gives **runoff depth**, not hydrograph shape.
- [ ] Cannot be used for snowmelt or frozen-ground conditions.

### ­¤åÜ Quick Comparison
| Aspect | Cook's | SCS-CN |
|:-------|:------:|:------:|
| Output | Peak discharge (m┬│/s) | Runoff depth (mm) |
| Parameters | 4 dimensionless factors | 1 dimensionless CN |
| Best for | Peak design flood | Volume + ID curves |
| Spatial | Single sub-area | Multi sub-area weighted |
| Time | Instantaneous peak | Cumulative event depth |

### ­¤Ä» Highest-Yield Mnemonics
- **Cook's factors order:** **T**op **S**oil **V**egetation **S**torage ŌåÆ **TSVS** = *"The Soil's Vegetal Store"*
- **SCS-CN threshold:** *"P must be more than 0.2 S, else Q is zero"*
- **$I_a$:** *"Initial Abstraction = 0.2 S"*
- **Numerator:** *"(P minus 0.2 S) squared"*
- **Denominator:** *"P plus 0.8 S"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden rule for KTU ESE:** Always state the *threshold check* ($P > 0.2 S$?) **before** writing the formula. Examiners reward explicit reasoning with **+1 to +2 marks** for methodology even if the arithmetic slips.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrograph - Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydrograph - Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Hydrograph

A **hydrograph** is a continuous time-series graphical representation of the **discharge (flow rate)** of a river, stream, or watershed outlet as a function of time, plotted in response to a given rainfall event (a hyetograph). It captures how a watershed transforms an input of precipitation into an output of streamflow at a gauging station.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A hydrograph is a plot of discharge vs. time at a given point on a stream, resulting from a storm event. It is a fundamental tool in hydrology representing the integrated effect of watershed characteristics and storm input on streamflow."

The term is derived from the Greek root *hydro* (water) and *graph* (written record), and is the most fundamental output signature of watershed behavior in surface hydrology.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Bathtub and Faucet"

Imagine your watershed is a **bathtub**, and the rainfall event is a **faucet suddenly turned on at full blast**. The bathtub is a leaky one ŌĆö it has a **small permanent drain** (representing **base flow** from groundwater), and the main water body sloshes out through the **big pipe** at the bottom (representing **direct runoff** at the stream gauge).

- The moment the faucet turns on, water accumulates faster than it can drain ŌåÆ the **rising limb** climbs steeply.
- Eventually, inflow equals outflow ŌåÆ the water level peaks (the **crest/peak discharge**).
- After you turn off the faucet, water still sloshes out, draining gradually ŌåÆ the **falling limb (recession curve)**.
- Even after the tub empties, a small trickle continues from a hidden reservoir underneath ŌåÆ this is the **base flow** continuing long after the rain ends.

This bathtub perfectly mirrors how precipitation enters a watershed, becomes runoff, and exits as streamflow at the gauging station.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terminology

- **Discharge (Q):** Measured in **cubic meters per second (m┬│/s)** or **cubic feet per second (cusec/cfs)**.
- **Time (t):** Measured in **hours (h)** or **minutes (min)**.
- **Drainage Area (A):** Measured in **km┬▓** or **hectares (ha)**.
- **Time of Concentration ($t_c$):** Travel time of water from the hydraulically most distant point of the watershed to the outlet.
- **Lag Time ($t_L$):** Elapsed time between the **centroid of the rainfall hyetograph** and the **peak of the hydrograph**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In KTU examinations, you will often be asked to identify or sketch the components of a hydrograph. Always label the axes: **X-axis ŌåÆ Time (h)** and **Y-axis ŌåÆ Discharge (m┬│/s)**. A sketch without axis labels will fetch partial credit only.

## 1.4 Why Hydrograph Components Matter

Understanding hydrograph components is essential for:

- **Flood forecasting** and early warning systems.
- Designing **hydraulic structures** (bridges, culverts, spillways, dams).
- Estimating **direct runoff volume** for water resource planning.
- Deriving a **Unit Hydrograph**, which is the foundation of rainfall-runoff modeling.
- Performing **base flow separation** to compute groundwater contribution.
- Watershed management and floodplain zoning.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the hydrograph as a **fingerprint** of a watershed. Two watersheds receiving identical rainfall will produce different hydrographs because of differences in slope, area, soil type, vegetation, and land use.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Complete List of Hydrograph Components

A typical **storm hydrograph** (or flood hydrograph) at a gauging station consists of the following key components, analyzed chronologically:

### 2.1.1 The Three Hydrograph Flows (Vertical Decomposition)

| Flow Component | Description | Travel Speed | Pathway |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surface Runoff (Overland Flow)** | Water that flows over the land surface into channels | Fastest | Land surface |
| **Interflow (Throughflow / Subsurface Flow)** | Lateral movement of water through the upper soil horizons, above the water table | Moderate | Unsaturated soil layer |
| **Base Flow (Groundwater Flow)** | Water discharged from the saturated groundwater zone (aquifer) into the stream | Slowest | Groundwater / aquifers |
| **Channel Precipitation** | Direct rainfall onto the stream/water surface | Direct | Water surface |

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH)** = Surface Runoff + Interflow + Channel Precipitation. The DRH is what remains after **base flow separation** from the total storm hydrograph.

### 2.1.2 The Three Hydrograph Segments (Geometric / Horizontal Decomposition)

1. **Rising Limb (Concentration Curve):** The portion from the **point of rise** to the **peak discharge**. Its shape is governed by:
   - Storm duration and intensity
   - Watershed shape (fan-shaped vs. elongated)
   - Slope and land use
   - Channel network density

2. **Crest Segment (Peak):** The maximum discharge point. May be a sharp single peak, a multi-peak (in case of multi-burst storms), or a flat-topped peak.

3. **Falling Limb (Recession Curve / Depletion Curve):** The portion from the **peak** to the **end of direct runoff**. It typically has a slow, exponential decay shape and is governed primarily by:
   - Watershed storage characteristics
   - Base flow contribution
   - Aquifer properties

### 2.1.3 Critical Time Parameters on the Hydrograph

| Parameter | Definition | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Time of Rise ($t_r$)** | Time from start of rising limb to the peak | Indicates watershed response speed |
| **Time of Concentration ($t_c$)** | Time for water from the **hydraulically most distant point** to reach the outlet | Defines the **time base** of rising limb for uniform rainfall |
| **Lag Time ($t_L$)** | Time between the **centroid of effective rainfall hyetograph** and the **peak of the direct runoff hydrograph** | Used in unit hydrograph theory |
| **Time Base ($t_b$)** | Duration of the **direct runoff** (from start of rise to end of DRH) | Defines volume of runoff |
| **Recession Time** | Time from the peak to the end of direct runoff | Indicates watershed storage depletion |

### 2.1.4 Volume Parameters

- **Volume of Direct Runoff (V):** The area under the **DRH curve**, expressed in **m┬│** or **mm depth over the watershed**.

## 2.2 Base Flow Separation ŌĆö Theoretical Foundation

Base flow separation is the **process of partitioning the total storm hydrograph into base flow and direct runoff components**. This is essential for:

- Computing direct runoff volume
- Deriving the unit hydrograph
- Groundwater recharge estimation
- Water balance studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** Base flow separation is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. Master the **three standard methods** with diagrams.

### 2.2.1 Method 1 ŌĆö Straight Line Method (Most Common)

**Procedure:**
1. Identify point **A** = Start of the **rising limb** (initial rise).
2. Identify point **B** = End of the **direct runoff** (where the recession curve flattens and matches the natural base flow trend).
3. Draw a **straight line from A to B**. This line separates base flow (below) from direct runoff (above).

### 2.2.2 Method 2 ŌĆö Fixed Base Method (Constant Base Flow)

**Procedure:**
1. Project the **pre-storm base flow** (horizontal line) forward in time.
2. The line ends at point **B** = end of direct runoff.
3. The volume above the projected base flow line = **Direct Runoff Volume**.

> Used when the **base flow is assumed constant** during the storm event (small to moderate storms).

### 2.2.3 Method 3 ŌĆö Variable Slope Method (Exponential Decay)

**Procedure:**
1. Extend the **pre-storm recession curve** to point C under the peak.
2. From point **C** (under peak), draw a straight line to point **B** at the end of direct runoff.
3. This accounts for the gradual depletion of the aquifer during the storm.

> Used for **large storms** where significant base flow changes occur.

### 2.2.4 Method 4 ŌĆö Concave Method

**Procedure:**
1. From point **A** (start of rise), extend the pre-storm recession curve to **B** (end of direct runoff).
2. The area under this curve = base flow.

> Most accurate for **forested and large watersheds** with deep aquifers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Hydrograph Components

| Formula | Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Runoff Volume** | $V = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} Q_{DR}(t) \, dt$ | Area under the DRH curve | $\text{m}^3$ |
| **Runoff Depth** | $R = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Volume per unit area | $\text{mm}$ |
| **Lag Time Empirical (Kirpich)** | $t_L = 0.00032 \cdot \dfrac{L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$ | Lag time using Kirpich formula | $\text{h}$ |
| **Time of Concentration (Kirpich)** | $t_c = 0.01947 \cdot \dfrac{L^{0.77}}{S^{0.385}}$ | Time of concentration | $\text{min}$ |
| **Rational Method Peak Discharge** | $Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | Peak discharge (rational) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| **Recession Equation (Exponential)** | $Q_t = Q_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | Base flow / groundwater recession | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| **Recession Constant** | $k = \dfrac{\ln(Q_0 / Q_t)}{t}$ | Slope of recession on semi-log plot | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| **Unit Hydrograph Volume** | $V_{UH} = A \cdot 1\text{ mm}$ | Volume under 1 cm UH = A ├Ś 0.001 | $\text{m}^3$ |
| **Time Ratio (Empirical)** | $t_r \approx 0.6 \, t_c$ | Rising limb time vs. conc. time | $\text{h}$ |
| **Recession Relation** | $Q_t = Q_p \cdot e^{-k(t - t_p)}$ | Discharge during recession | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |

> **Notation in the table:**
> - $L$ = maximum length of travel of water (m)
> - $S$ = average slope of watershed (m/m)
> - $C$ = runoff coefficient (dimensionless)
> - $I$ = rainfall intensity (mm/h or m/s)
> - $A$ = drainage area (m┬▓ or km┬▓)
> - $Q_0$ = initial discharge, $Q_t$ = discharge at time $t$
> - $t_p$ = time to peak

## 2.4 Physical Basis for Each Component

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility Beyond the Exam:** Mastery of hydrograph components is the **prerequisite for Module 3 (Unit Hydrograph Theory)** and **Module 4 (Flood Routing)**. You cannot derive a unit hydrograph or apply the Muskingum method without first understanding how to separate base flow and define the DRH.

### Physical Origin of Each Segment

- **Rising Limb (concave up):** Initially gentle (water enters the channel from the nearest sub-areas), then steeper as water from farther parts of the watershed arrives, then flattens near the peak as the entire watershed contributes.
- **Peak Discharge:** Represents the **maximum rate at which the watershed delivers water to the outlet**. It occurs at time $t = t_r$ from the start of rise.
- **Falling Limb (recession, exponential decay):** After the rainfall stops, contributions from near areas cease first, then far areas, and finally only the slow groundwater reservoir continues to feed the stream.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volume of Direct Runoff from Hydrograph

The volume of direct runoff is computed as the **area under the Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH)**. The integration can be done using the **Trapezoidal Rule** for discrete time-discharge data.

### 3.1.1 Continuous Form (Theoretical)

The volume of direct runoff is mathematically defined as:

$$V_{DR} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} Q_{DR}(t) \, dt$$

where:
- $V_{DR}$ = volume of direct runoff, in $\text{m}^3$
- $Q_{DR}(t)$ = direct runoff discharge at time $t$, in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $t_1$ = start time of direct runoff
- $t_2$ = end time of direct runoff (end of recession)

### 3.1.2 Discrete Form (Trapezoidal Rule for Engineering Practice)

For hydrograph data recorded at discrete intervals $\Delta t$, the volume is computed as:

$$V_{DR} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \left( \frac{Q_i + Q_{i+1}}{2} \right) \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

1. The hydrograph between two consecutive observations is approximated as a **trapezoid**.
2. The average of the two discharges ($Q_i$ and $Q_{i+1}$) is multiplied by the time interval.
3. Summing over all intervals gives the **total volume of direct runoff** in $\text{m}^3$.

### 3.1.3 Conversion to Runoff Depth

The runoff depth $R$ (in mm) over the watershed of area $A$ (in $\text{m}^2$) is:

$$R = \frac{V_{DR}}{A} \times 1000$$

The factor **1000** converts metres to millimetres ($1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Recession Curve Equation (Exponential Decay)

After the rainfall stops, the recession limb follows an **exponential decay** governed by the watershed's storage-discharge relationship.

### 3.2.1 Governing Differential Equation

Assume watershed storage $S$ is linearly related to discharge $Q$:

$$S = k \cdot Q$$

The continuity equation (water balance) for the watershed is:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -Q$$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Substituting $S = k Q$ into the continuity equation:

$$\frac{d(kQ)}{dt} = -Q$$

Since $k$ is a constant, it factors out:

$$k \cdot \frac{dQ}{dt} = -Q$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dQ}{Q} = -\frac{dt}{k}$$

Integrating both sides from $(Q_0, 0)$ to $(Q_t, t)$:

$$\int_{Q_0}^{Q_t} \frac{dQ}{Q} = -\frac{1}{k} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{Q_t}{Q_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{k}$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{Q_t}{Q_0} = e^{-t/k}$$

Therefore, the recession equation is:

$$Q_t = Q_0 \cdot e^{-t/k}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The recession constant $k$ has units of time. A large $k$ means the watershed empties slowly (deep aquifer); a small $k$ means rapid depletion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Lag Time from Hyetograph and Hydrograph

Lag time $t_L$ is computed from the **centroid of effective rainfall** to the **peak of the DRH**.

### 3.3.1 Centroid of Effective Rainfall

For an effective rainfall hyetograph with intensities $I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n$ during time intervals centred at $t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n$:

$$t_{\text{centroid}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \cdot t_i \cdot \Delta t}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{\sum I_i \cdot t_i}{\sum I_i}$$

### 3.3.2 Lag Time

$$t_L = t_{\text{peak}} - t_{\text{centroid}}$$

where $t_{\text{peak}}$ is the time of the peak of the DRH on the hydrograph.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix) ŌĆö KTU Style

### Problem Statement

> A 4-hour storm produced the following direct runoff hydrograph ordinates at 1-hour intervals. Compute the **(i) volume of direct runoff** and **(ii) runoff depth** for a watershed of area **A = 4 km┬▓**.

| Time (h) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| DRH Q (m┬│/s) | 0 | 25 | 60 | 80 | 65 | 45 | 25 | 10 | 0 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the Trapezoidal Rule**

Compute the area of each trapezoid between consecutive ordinates:

$$V = \sum \left( \frac{Q_i + Q_{i+1}}{2} \right) \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values** (with $\Delta t = 1$ h $= 3600$ s)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{0-1} &= \frac{0 + 25}{2} \times 3600 = 45{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{1-2} &= \frac{25 + 60}{2} \times 3600 = 153{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{2-3} &= \frac{60 + 80}{2} \times 3600 = 252{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{3-4} &= \frac{80 + 65}{2} \times 3600 = 261{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{4-5} &= \frac{65 + 45}{2} \times 3600 = 198{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{5-6} &= \frac{45 + 25}{2} \times 3600 = 126{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{6-7} &= \frac{25 + 10}{2} \times 3600 = 63{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{7-8} &= \frac{10 + 0}{2} \times 3600 = 18{,}000 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum All Trapezoids**

$$V_{DR} = 45000 + 153000 + 252000 + 261000 + 198000 + 126000 + 63000 + 18000$$

$$V_{DR} = 1{,}116{,}000 \text{ m}^3 = 1.116 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Compute Runoff Depth**

Convert area: $A = 4 \text{ km}^2 = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$

$$R = \frac{V_{DR}}{A} = \frac{1.116 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^6} = 0.279 \text{ m}$$

Converting to mm:

$$R = 0.279 \times 1000 = 279 \text{ mm} \approx 28 \text{ cm}$$

### Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Summary Table for Quick Revision

| Method / Concept | Key Formula | Use Case | Marks Weightage (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Trapezoidal Rule | $V = \sum (Q_i + Q_{i+1})/2 \cdot \Delta t$ | Volume from discrete hydrograph | 3ŌĆō4 |
| Runoff Depth | $R = V/A \times 1000$ | Convert volume to depth in mm | 1ŌĆō2 |
| Recession Equation | $Q_t = Q_0 e^{-t/k}$ | Groundwater depletion analysis | 2ŌĆō3 |
| Lag Time | $t_L = t_p - t_{\text{centroid}}$ | Unit Hydrograph derivation | 3ŌĆō4 |
| Base Flow Separation | Graphical (straight line) | DRH extraction | 7 (frequent) |
| Kirpich $t_c$ | $t_c = 0.01947 L^{0.77} / S^{0.385}$ | Watershed time parameter | 3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
