# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a hydrograph. List its major components.  [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **hydrograph** is a plot of **stream discharge (Q in m┬│/s)** versus **time (t in hours)** at a given point on a river, resulting from a storm event (rainfall input).

**Major Components:**
1. **Rising Limb (Concentration Curve)** ŌĆö portion from start of rise to the peak.
2. **Crest (Peak Segment)** ŌĆö point of maximum discharge ($Q_{\max}$).
3. **Falling Limb (Recession Curve)** ŌĆö portion from peak to end of direct runoff.
4. **Base Flow** ŌĆö the groundwater-fed portion of the flow.
5. **Time Base ($t_b$)** ŌĆö total duration of direct runoff.

The hydrograph also has time parameters: **time of rise ($t_r$)**, **time of concentration ($t_c$)**, **lag time ($t_L$)**, and **recession time**.

---

### Question 2: What is base flow separation? Name any two methods.  [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

**Base flow separation** is the graphical/analytical process of partitioning a storm hydrograph into the **direct runoff component** (surface and interflow) and the **base flow component** (groundwater contribution).

**Two Methods:**

1. **Straight Line Method** ŌĆö A straight line is drawn from the start of the rising limb (Point A) to a point on the recession limb (Point B) where direct runoff ends. The area above the line is DRH; below is base flow.

2. **Fixed Base Method** ŌĆö The pre-storm base flow is projected horizontally to a point B at the end of direct runoff. This is used for **small/moderate storms** where base flow is approximately constant.

*(Other methods: Variable Slope Method, Concave Method.)*

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1  [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 Model]

**a)** With a neat sketch, describe the various components of a storm hydrograph.  **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain in detail the **straight line method** and **variable slope method** of base flow separation. When is each preferred?  **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Components of a Storm Hydrograph (7 Marks)

A **storm hydrograph** (or flood hydrograph) is a plot of discharge vs. time at the outlet of a watershed in response to a storm event. The components are:

**1. Rising Limb (Concentration Curve):**
The portion from the start of the rise to the peak. It is typically **concave upward** and reflects the increasing contribution of water from progressively distant parts of the watershed. Steepness depends on storm intensity, watershed slope, and shape.

**2. Crest Segment (Peak):**
The maximum discharge $Q_p$ attained. May be:
- **Single sharp peak** (intense, brief storm)
- **Multi-peak** (multi-burst storm)
- **Flat-topped** (long duration, uniform rainfall)

**3. Falling Limb (Recession Curve):**
From the peak to the end of direct runoff. Has an **exponential decay** shape governed by watershed storage. Slope is gentler than the rising limb.

**4. Base Flow:**
The portion of flow contributed by **groundwater discharge** into the stream. It is the natural flow that exists even before and long after the storm.

**Sketch (ASCII representation for answer sheet):**

```
   QŌåæ
    |             _____
    |           /       \___
 Qp |--------- /            \____
    |        /                   \____
    |       /                          \____
    |      /                                 \____
    |     /                                        \____
    |____/__________________________________________________ŌåÆ t
        ŌåæA  rising   C=peak        B=end of DR    base flow
        |  limb  |                |
        |        |                |
        |   time of rise    recession time
        |
        start of rise
```

**Key Time Parameters:**

| Parameter | Definition |
|---|---|
| $t_r$ (Time of rise) | From A to C (start of rise to peak) |
| $t_b$ (Time base) | From A to B (total duration of DR) |
| $t_c$ (Time of concentration) | Max travel time from hydraulically farthest point to outlet |
| $t_L$ (Lag time) | From centroid of rainfall hyetograph to peak |

**Flow Components:**

- **Surface runoff (overland flow)** ŌĆö fastest, contributes to peak.
- **Interflow (throughflow)** ŌĆö moderate, shallow subsurface flow.
- **Base flow (groundwater)** ŌĆö slowest, sustains stream between storms.
- **Channel precipitation** ŌĆö direct rainfall on stream surface.

> The sum of surface runoff + interflow + channel precipitation = **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH)**.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Base Flow Separation Methods (7 Marks)

**1. Straight Line Method:**

**Procedure:**
- Identify **Point A** = start of the rising limb (where the hydrograph first leaves the natural base flow).
- Identify **Point B** = end of direct runoff (where the recession curve flattens and rejoins the natural base flow trend). Point B is often taken as **N days** after the peak (where $N$ depends on watershed area).
- Draw a **straight line AB**. This is the **separation line**.
- **Area above AB** = Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH).
- **Area below AB** = Base Flow.

**Advantages:** Simple, quick, widely used.

**Limitations:** Arbitrary placement of Point B; does not account for changing base flow.

**Preferred When:** Small to medium-sized watersheds; storm events of moderate intensity.

**2. Variable Slope Method:**

**Procedure:**
- Identify **Point A** = start of the rising limb.
- **Extend the pre-storm recession curve** forward in time, beneath the rising limb, to **Point C** vertically below the peak.
- From **Point C** (under peak), draw a **straight line** to **Point B** = end of direct runoff.
- This forms a two-segment separation: curved under rising limb + straight under recession.

**Advantages:** More realistic; accounts for the gradual depletion of the aquifer during the storm.

**Preferred When:** Large watersheds, long-duration storms, deep aquifers, or when the base flow is observed to change significantly during the storm.

**Comparison Table (for the answer):**

| Aspect | Straight Line | Variable Slope |
|---|---|---|
| Complexity | Simple | Slightly complex |
| Accuracy | Moderate | Higher for large storms |
| Base flow change | Ignored | Considered |
| Watershed size | Small-medium | Large |
| Storm size | Moderate | Large |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2  [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]

**a)** Derive the **exponential recession equation** for a watershed. State clearly the assumptions involved.  **(7 Marks)**

**b)** The ordinates of a direct runoff hydrograph for a 3-hour storm over a watershed of area **A = 8 km┬▓** are given below. Compute the **volume of direct runoff** and the **runoff depth** using the trapezoidal rule.  **(7 Marks)**

| Time (h) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q (m┬│/s) | 0 | 30 | 70 | 90 | 60 | 30 | 0 |

#### Model Solution

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of Recession Equation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**

1. The watershed storage $S$ is **linearly proportional** to the discharge $Q$:
$$S = kQ$$
where $k$ = storage constant (units of time).

2. After the rainfall stops, the watershed drains entirely through the stream outlet, so the **continuity equation** applies:
$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -Q$$
The negative sign indicates that storage is decreasing with time.

3. The constant $k$ is **time-invariant** during the recession period.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Substitute $S = kQ$ into the continuity equation:

$$k \cdot \frac{dQ}{dt} = -Q$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables:

$$\frac{dQ}{Q} = -\frac{dt}{k}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from $(Q_0, 0)$ to $(Q_t, t)$:

$$\int_{Q_0}^{Q_t} \frac{dQ}{Q} = -\frac{1}{k} \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln(Q_t) - \ln(Q_0) = -\frac{t}{k}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{Q_t}{Q_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{k}$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$Q_t = Q_0 \cdot e^{-t/k}$$

This is the **exponential recession equation**.

**Step 6 (Optional):** Linear form for graphical estimation of $k$:

$$\log_{10} Q_t = \log_{10} Q_0 - \frac{0.4343 \cdot t}{k}$$

Plotting $\log_{10} Q_t$ vs. $t$ gives a straight line with slope $-0.4343/k$.

**Engineering Interpretation:** The recession constant $k$ characterizes the **rate of aquifer depletion**. A large $k$ = slow drainage (sustained base flow); a small $k$ = rapid drainage (flashy watershed).

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Volume of Direct Runoff & Runoff Depth (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Watershed area $A = 8 \text{ km}^2 = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- Time step $\Delta t = 1 \text{ h} = 3600 \text{ s}$

**Step 1: Apply the Trapezoidal Rule**

$$V = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \left( \frac{Q_i + Q_{i+1}}{2} \right) \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2: Compute Each Trapezoid**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{0-1} &= \frac{0 + 30}{2} \times 3600 = 54{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{1-2} &= \frac{30 + 70}{2} \times 3600 = 180{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{2-3} &= \frac{70 + 90}{2} \times 3600 = 288{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{3-4} &= \frac{90 + 60}{2} \times 3600 = 270{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{4-5} &= \frac{60 + 30}{2} \times 3600 = 162{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{5-6} &= \frac{30 + 0}{2} \times 3600 = 54{,}000 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum All Trapezoids**

$$V_{DR} = 54000 + 180000 + 288000 + 270000 + 162000 + 54000$$

$$V_{DR} = 1{,}008{,}000 \text{ m}^3 = 1.008 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Compute Runoff Depth**

$$R = \frac{V_{DR}}{A} \times 1000 = \frac{1.008 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^6} \times 1000$$

$$R = 0.126 \times 1000 = 126 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Volume of Direct Runoff** = $1.008 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$ = 1.008 million m┬│
- **Runoff Depth** = **126 mm** over the watershed.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Unit conversion errors (3-mark loss):** The most frequent mistake is forgetting to convert **hours to seconds** in the trapezoidal rule. The discharge is in m┬│/s, so time MUST be in seconds. $1 \text{ h} = 3600 \text{ s}$.
> 
> 2. **Area conversion (2-mark loss):** Watershed area in **km┬▓** must be converted to **m┬▓** for consistency with m┬│ volume. $1 \text{ km}^2 = 10^6 \text{ m}^2$.
> 
> 3. **Runoff depth unit confusion (2-mark loss):** The final runoff depth must be in **mm**, requiring multiplication by 1000 (from m to mm).
> 
> 4. **Unlabelled hydrograph sketch (3-mark loss):** A sketch of a hydrograph without labelled **axes (Q in m┬│/s on Y, t in h on X)**, without marking **Point A, Peak C, Point B, and the separation line** will fetch only partial credit.
> 
> 5. **Confusing direct runoff with total runoff:** Always clarify that DRH is **after base flow separation**, not the total hydrograph.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to state the assumption** $S = kQ$ in the recession derivation will lead to **1ŌĆō2 mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision ŌĆö Must-Know Bullet Points:**

- **Hydrograph:** Plot of discharge $Q$ (m┬│/s) vs. time $t$ (h) at a stream gauge.
- **Three flows** that compose a hydrograph: **Surface runoff (fast) + Interflow (moderate) + Base flow (slow)** + Channel precipitation.
- **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH) = Total hydrograph ŌłÆ Base flow**.
- **Three geometric segments:** Rising Limb, Crest/Peak, Falling Limb (Recession).
- **Time parameters:** $t_r$ (time of rise), $t_c$ (time of concentration), $t_L$ (lag time), $t_b$ (time base).
- **Empirical relation:** $t_r \approx 0.6 \, t_c$ (for many watersheds).
- **Volume of DRH** = Area under the DRH curve = $\sum \frac{(Q_i + Q_{i+1})}{2} \cdot \Delta t$.
- **Runoff depth** $R = (V/A) \times 1000$ (in mm).
- **Base flow separation** is essential to extract DRH for unit hydrograph derivation.
- **Four base flow separation methods:** Straight Line, Fixed Base, Variable Slope, Concave.
- **Recession equation:** $Q_t = Q_0 \, e^{-t/k}$, derived from $S = kQ$ and $dS/dt = -Q$.
- **Lag time** = time from **centroid of effective rainfall** to **peak of DRH**.
- **Time of concentration** = max travel time from hydraulically farthest point to outlet.
- **Kirpich formula:** $t_c = 0.01947 \, L^{0.77} / S^{0.385}$ (in minutes for $L$ in m).
- **Hydrograph components are the prerequisite for the Unit Hydrograph theory (Module 3)** and **Flood Routing (Module 4)**.
- **Always label axes and key points** (A, B, C) on any hydrograph sketch in the exam.
- **Time unit conversion** is the most common source of calculation errors.
- A watershed's hydrograph shape is a **unique fingerprint** influenced by area, slope, shape, soil, vegetation, and rainfall pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Base Flow Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Base Flow Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme engineering hydrology, the **Storm Hydrograph** of a catchment represents the total streamflow response to a rainfall event. This total flow is composed of two fundamentally different components:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Base Flow ($Q_b$):** The portion of streamflow that originates from **subsurface sources** ŌĆö primarily groundwater seepage from banks, interflow delayed through the soil mantle, and slow drainage from saturated zones. It sustains the stream during dry weather and forms the "tail" of the hydrograph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Runoff ($Q_{dr}$):** The portion of streamflow that enters the channel as **surface runoff** (overland flow) and **quick interflow** during and immediately after the storm event. It forms the "peak" of the hydrograph.

The mathematical identity governing any storm hydrograph is:

$$Q_{total}(t) \;=\; Q_{dr}(t) \;+\; Q_b(t)$$

**Base Flow Separation** is therefore defined as the graphical/analytical hydrograph analysis technique used to **partition the total storm hydrograph into its direct runoff and base flow components**, demarcated by a *separation line* drawn between the rising limb and the recession limb.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The "Dual-Pipe" Water Tank:**
> Imagine a household overhead tank fed by TWO pipes simultaneously during rainfall:
> - **Pipe 1 (Direct Runoff):** A large-bore pipe that delivers water instantly during the rain ŌĆö full force, loud, then stops abruptly.
> - **Pipe 2 (Base Flow):** A narrow trickle that starts slowly as the soil becomes saturated, and continues to drip for days after the rain stops, fed by water stored in the surrounding ground.
>
> If you stand below the tank with a measuring bucket recording the **total flow**, you see one continuous record. **Base flow separation is the engineering art of mathematically "uncoupling" these two pipes** ŌĆö figuring out how much came from Pipe 1 vs. Pipe 2 at every instant.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Point of Rise | **A** | Time instant when the hydrograph starts rising from base flow (start of rainfall excess) |
| Peak Point | **B** | Crest of the hydrograph (maximum discharge) |
| End of Direct Runoff | **N** | Time instant when surface runoff ceases; the hydrograph merges back into pure base flow |
| Recession Constant | **$k$** | Dimensionless (or per day) constant governing rate of groundwater depletion; **$0.90 < k < 0.99$** for typical base flow |
| Base Flow Index | **BFI** | Ratio of base flow volume to total storm runoff volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Recession constant **$k$** is typically reported in **per day (day$^{-1}$)** when expressed in exponential form, or as a **dimensionless ratio** in the geometric form $Q_t = Q_0 \cdot k^t$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Basis of Base Flow Recession

The depletion of base flow follows **first-order decay kinetics**, physically grounded in **Darcy's Law** for groundwater flow through an unconfined aquifer adjacent to the stream channel:

- As the water table adjacent to the stream falls, the hydraulic gradient decreases.
- The rate of groundwater discharge into the stream is **proportional to the current base flow**.
- Hence the governing differential equation: $\dfrac{dQ_b}{dt} \;=\; -k\,Q_b$

This is the same exponential-decay law that governs capacitor discharge in an RC circuit, radioactive decay, and Newton's law of cooling ŌĆö an elegant unification that hydrology shares with electrical engineering and physics.

## 2.2 Six Standard Methods of Base Flow Separation

### Method 1: Straight Line Method (Simplest ŌĆö Board-Favorite)
- Identify point **A** on the rising limb where the hydrograph begins to rise sharply.
- Identify point **N** on the recession limb where **direct runoff ends**.
- $N$ is located at a fixed time **$N = A^{0.2}$** days after the peak, where $A$ is the catchment area in **square miles**.
- Connect **A to N** with a straight line ŌĆö this is the separation line.
- **Extension AŌåÆB (vertical):** Draw a vertical from peak B down to meet the separation line; everything left of this vertical under the falling limb is direct runoff.

### Method 2: Fixed Base Length Method
- **N is fixed** at a pre-determined day count regardless of area ŌĆö typically **3, 5, 7, or 10 days** after peak.
- Separation line is straight from **A to N**.

### Method 3: Variable Slope Method
- Used when base flow is declining rapidly during the storm.
- The separation line **AŌåÆN** is drawn at a slope **parallel to the pre-storm base flow recession curve**.

### Method 4: Concave Method
- The separation line from **A** follows a **concave (curved) path** matching the curvature of the pre-storm recession, then becomes straight to **N**.
- Yields a **larger base flow estimate** than straight-line methods.

### Method 5: Master Depletion Curve Method
- Uses a **master recession curve** constructed from multiple storms on the same catchment.
- Most **scientifically rigorous** ŌĆö adopted by USGS.
- Steps: Shift all individual recession curves horizontally to overlay ŌåÆ fit a single master curve ŌåÆ apply to current storm.

### Method 6: Lynne-Holtan Method
- Hybrid approach combining the concave method with master depletion curve concepts.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars below are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_b(t) \;=\; Q_{b0} \cdot e^{-k t}$ | Exponential decay form of base flow recession | $Q$ in m┬│/s, $t$ in days |
| 2 | $Q_b(t) \;=\; Q_{b0} \cdot k^{t}$ | Geometric decay form (dimensionless $k$) | $k$ dimensionless |
| 3 | $\ln(Q_t) \;=\; \ln(Q_{b0}) \;-\; k t$ | Linearized form for plotting/regression | $\ln$ dimensionless |
| 4 | $k \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(Q_{b0}) \;-\; \ln(Q_t)}{t}$ | Recession constant from any two points | day$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $k \;=\; \left(\dfrac{Q_t}{Q_{b0}}\right)^{1/t}$ | Recession constant (geometric form) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $V_b \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} Q_{b0}\,e^{-k t}\,dt \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{b0}}{k}\left(1 - e^{-k T}\right)$ | Base flow volume over time $T$ | m┬│ |
| 7 | $V_{b,\infty} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{b0}}{k}$ | Total base flow volume (as $T \to \infty$) | m┬│ |
| 8 | $\text{BFI} \;=\; \dfrac{V_b}{V_{total}}$ | Base Flow Index | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 9 | $N \;=\; A^{0.2}$ | Fixed base length (Linsley), $A$ in sq. miles | days |
| 10 | $N \;=\; 0.83\,A^{0.2}$ | Fixed base length, $A$ in sq. km | days |
| 11 | $t_{eq} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{k}$ | Time constant of recession (63.2% depletion) | days |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flood Forecasting:** Removing base flow isolates the *direct runoff hydrograph (DRH)*, which is essential for **unit hydrograph derivation**.
- **Groundwater Recharge Estimation:** Integrated base flow volume Ōēł long-term aquifer yield.
- **Water Resource Planning:** BFI > 0.5 indicates a **groundwater-dominated catchment** (sustainable dry-season flow).
- **Drought Assessment:** Falling base flow signals aquifer depletion ŌĆö used in **drought monitoring indices**.
- **Pollution Studies:** Base flow carries slowly-released contaminants (nitrates, salts); separation helps model **non-point source pollution**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Base Flow Recession Equation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Governing Physical Law (Darcy's Law for Aquifer Discharge)**

Consider a stream with an adjacent unconfined aquifer. As the water table falls, the discharge per unit length of bank is proportional to the saturated thickness (Ōēł base flow $Q_b$):

$$\dfrac{dQ_b}{dt} \;=\; -k \cdot Q_b$$

where $k$ is a positive constant whose magnitude depends on aquifer transmissivity, specific yield, and channel geometry.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Separation of Variables**

Move all $Q_b$ terms to one side and $t$ terms to the other:

$$\dfrac{dQ_b}{Q_b} \;=\; -k\,dt$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Integration**

Integrate both sides between limits $(Q_{b0},\,t=0)$ and $(Q_t,\,t=t)$:

$$\int_{Q_{b0}}^{Q_t} \dfrac{dQ_b}{Q_b} \;=\; -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the Integrals**

$$\Bigl[\ln(Q_b)\Bigr]_{Q_{b0}}^{Q_t} \;=\; -k\,t$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Expand the Bracket**

$$\ln(Q_t) \;-\; \ln(Q_{b0}) \;=\; -k\,t$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for the Linearized Form**

$$\ln(Q_t) \;=\; \ln(Q_{b0}) \;-\; k\,t$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Exponentiate to Obtain the Closed-Form Decay Equation**

$$Q_t \;=\; Q_{b0} \cdot e^{-k t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A semi-log plot of $\ln(Q_t)$ versus $t$ produces a **straight line of slope $-k$** and intercept $\ln(Q_{b0})$ ŌĆö this is the foundation of recession analysis.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Recession Constant Determination

**Problem Statement:**
The base flow at the start of recession in a stream is **$Q_{b0} = 100$ m┬│/s**. After **$t = 10$ days**, the base flow has fallen to **$Q_{10} = 80$ m┬│/s**. Determine:
1. The recession constant $k$ (exponential form).
2. The base flow at $t = 30$ days.
3. The total base flow volume in 30 days.

### Part (a) ŌĆö Recession Constant

Using the linearized form:

$$k \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(Q_{b0}) \;-\; \ln(Q_{10})}{t}$$

$$k \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(100) \;-\; \ln(80)}{10}$$

$$k \;=\; \dfrac{4.6052 \;-\; 4.3820}{10} \;=\; \dfrac{0.2232}{10}$$

$$\boxed{k \;=\; 0.02232 \text{ day}^{-1}}$$

### Part (b) ŌĆö Base Flow at $t = 30$ Days

$$Q_{30} \;=\; Q_{b0} \cdot e^{-k t}$$

$$Q_{30} \;=\; 100 \cdot e^{-(0.02232)(30)}$$

$$Q_{30} \;=\; 100 \cdot e^{-0.6696}$$

$$Q_{30} \;=\; 100 \cdot 0.5120$$

$$\boxed{Q_{30} \;\approx\; 51.20 \text{ m┬│/s}}$$

### Part (c) ŌĆö Base Flow Volume in 30 Days

$$V_b \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{b0}}{k}\,\bigl(1 - e^{-k T}\bigr)$$

$$V_b \;=\; \dfrac{100}{0.02232}\,\bigl(1 - e^{-(0.02232)(30)}\bigr)$$

$$V_b \;=\; 4480.3 \,\bigl(1 - 0.5120\bigr)$$

$$V_b \;=\; 4480.3 \times 0.4880$$

$$\boxed{V_b \;\approx\; 2186.4 \text{ million m┬│} \;=\; 2.186 \times 10^{9} \text{ m}^{3}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of Separation Methods

| Method | Shape of Separation Line | Base Flow Estimate | Data Requirement | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Straight Line | Straight from A to N | Lowest | Minimal | **Very High** |
| Fixed Base Length | Straight, N fixed by days | Low | Minimal | High |
| Variable Slope | Straight, parallel to pre-storm recession | Moderate | Pre-storm data needed | Medium |
| Concave | Curved, follows aquifer depletion | Highest | Pre-storm data needed | **Very High** |
| Master Depletion Curve | Matches master recession curve | Most accurate | Multiple storm events | Medium |
| Lynne-Holtan | Hybrid concave + master | High | Multiple storm events | Low |

## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Fixed Base Length (Linsley's Equation)

**Problem:**
A catchment has an area of **256 km┬▓**. Using Linsley's equation, find the time $N$ (days) from peak to end of direct runoff.

$$N \;=\; 0.83 \cdot A^{0.2}$$

$$N \;=\; 0.83 \cdot (256)^{0.2}$$

Compute $(256)^{0.2}$: Note $256 = 4^4$, so $256^{0.2} = (4^4)^{0.2} = 4^{0.8} = 3.031$

$$N \;=\; 0.83 \times 3.031 \;=\; 2.516 \text{ days} \;\approx\; 2.52 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{N \;\approx\; 2.52 \text{ days after peak}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Hydrograph Decomposition Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Storm Hydrograph Q total of t"] --> B["Base Flow Separation Technique"]
    B --> C1["Direct Runoff Hydrograph DRH"]
    B --> C2["Base Flow Hydrograph BFH"]
    C1 --> D1["Surface Runoff Component"]
    C1 --> D2["Quick Interflow Component"]
    C2 --> E1["Groundwater Seepage"]
    C2 --> E2["Slow Interflow"]
    C2 --> E3["Channel Storage Release"]
    D1 --> F1["Peak Discharge Analysis"]
    D2 --> F1
    F1 --> G["Unit Hydrograph Derivation"]
    E1 --> H1["Groundwater Recharge Estimation"]
    E2 --> H1
    E3 --> H1
    H1 --> I["Drought and Water Resource Planning"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Hierarchy of Base Flow Separation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["Base Flow Separation Methods"] --> M1["Straight Line Method"]
    M --> M2["Fixed Base Length Method"]
    M --> M3["Variable Slope Method"]
    M --> M4["Concave Method"]
    M --> M5["Master Depletion Curve Method"]
    M --> M6["Lynne Holtan Method"]
    M1 --> S1["Simple A to N straight line"]
    M2 --> S2["N fixed by Linsley equation"]
    M3 --> S3["Line parallel to pre storm recession"]
    M4 --> S4["Curved line matching aquifer decay"]
    M5 --> S5["Composite curve from multiple storms"]
    M6 --> S6["Hybrid concave plus master curve"]
    S1 --> OUT["Separated Base Flow Hydrograph"]
    S2 --> OUT
    S3 --> OUT
    S4 --> OUT
    S5 --> OUT
    S6 --> OUT
    OUT --> APP["BFI Calculation and Groundwater Yield"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram ŌĆö Hydrograph Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Rainfall Input"] --> R["Runoff Generation"]
    R --> DR["Direct Runoff"]
    R --> BF["Base Flow"]
    DR --> SH["Storm Hydrograph Combined"]
    BF --> SH
    SH --> SEP["Separation Line A to N"]
    SEP --> DRH["Direct Runoff Hydrograph"]
    SEP --> BFH["Base Flow Hydrograph"]
    DRH --> UH["Unit Hydrograph Analysis"]
    BFH --> GW["Groundwater Recharge Estimate"]
    BFH --> BFI["Base Flow Index Computation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** In KTU board exams, a labelled sketch of the storm hydrograph showing points **A**, **B**, **N**, the separation line, and the shaded areas for direct runoff volume vs. base flow volume carries **2ŌĆō3 marks by itself** ŌĆö never skip the diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]`
**Define base flow and direct runoff. Why is base flow separation necessary in hydrograph analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Base Flow (1 mark):** Base flow is the portion of streamflow that originates from subsurface sources such as groundwater seepage into the stream channel, delayed interflow, and slow release from saturated zones. It sustains the stream during dry periods and forms the receding tail of the hydrograph.

- **Direct Runoff (1 mark):** Direct runoff is the portion of streamflow that reaches the channel during and immediately after a storm event as surface runoff and quick interflow, forming the rising limb and peak of the hydrograph.

- **Necessity of Base Flow Separation (1 mark):** It is essential for: (i) deriving the **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH)** required for **unit hydrograph analysis**, (ii) estimating **groundwater recharge**, (iii) computing the **Base Flow Index (BFI)** for catchment classification, and (iv) accurate flood forecasting.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`
**What is the recession constant? State its typical range for base flow in perennial streams.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** The recession constant $k$ is the proportionality constant governing the exponential decay of base flow with time, expressed by the equation $Q_t = Q_{b0}\,e^{-k t}$. Physically, $k$ reflects the rate at which the adjacent groundwater reservoir depletes into the stream channel.

- **Typical Range (1 mark):** For perennial streams, the recession constant $k$ typically lies in the range **$0.90 < k < 0.99$** (dimensionless geometric form), corresponding to **$0.01 < k < 0.10$ day$^{-1}$** in exponential form, depending on aquifer transmissivity and specific yield.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION A ŌĆö Option 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches any **four methods** of base flow separation. Compare their relative accuracy and data requirements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four principal methods are:

**(i) Straight Line Method (1.5 marks):**
- Identify point **A** on the rising limb where surface runoff begins.
- Determine **N** using Linsley's equation: $N = A^{0.2}$ days (area in sq. miles) where $A$ is the catchment area.
- Draw a straight separation line from **A** to **N**.
- Project peak B vertically downward to meet the separation line ŌĆö the area under the curve above this line, bounded by A and N, represents **direct runoff volume**.
- **Advantage:** Simplest and most commonly used in board exams.
- **Disadvantage:** Crude assumption; underestimates base flow for steeply falling recession.

**(ii) Fixed Base Length Method (1.5 marks):**
- Similar to straight line, but **N is fixed** at a pre-determined number of days (commonly 3, 5, 7, or 10 days) after the peak, irrespective of catchment area.
- Linsley recommends $N = 0.83\,A^{0.2}$ days (area in sq. km).
- **Advantage:** Useful for small catchments where pre-storm data is unavailable.
- **Disadvantage:** Arbitrary choice of N affects results significantly.

**(iii) Variable Slope Method (1 mark):**
- The separation line AŌåÆN is drawn at a slope **parallel to the pre-storm base flow recession curve**.
- **Advantage:** Accounts for the natural declining trend of base flow.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires accurate pre-storm recession data.

**(iv) Concave Method (1.5 marks):**
- The separation line starts as a **curved (concave) line from A**, following the curvature of natural aquifer depletion, then merges into a straight line before reaching N.
- **Advantage:** Yields the **largest (and most realistic) base flow estimate**, because it allows the separation line to lie above the natural recession in the early stages.
- **Disadvantage:** Subjective curve fitting; harder to standardize.

**Comparison Summary (1.5 marks):**

| Method | Base Flow Magnitude | Data Need | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Straight Line | Lowest | Minimal | Lowest |
| Fixed Base | Low | Minimal | Low |
| Variable Slope | Moderate | Pre-storm recession | Moderate |
| Concave | Highest | Pre-storm recession | Higher |
| Master Curve | Most accurate | Multiple storms | Highest |

---

**(b)** A stream has a base flow of **80 m┬│/s** at the start of recession. After **15 days**, the base flow is **50 m┬│/s**. Calculate:
(i) The recession constant $k$.
(ii) The base flow after **40 days** from the start of recession.
(iii) The total volume of base flow that will drain in 60 days. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Recession Constant (2.5 marks):**

Using the exponential recession equation:

$$Q_t \;=\; Q_{b0} \cdot e^{-k t}$$

$$50 \;=\; 80 \cdot e^{-k(15)}$$

$$e^{-15k} \;=\; \dfrac{50}{80} \;=\; 0.625$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-15k \;=\; \ln(0.625)$$

$$-15k \;=\; -0.4700$$

$$\boxed{k \;=\; 0.03134 \text{ day}^{-1}}$$

**(ii) Base Flow at $t = 40$ days (2 marks):**

$$Q_{40} \;=\; 80 \cdot e^{-(0.03134)(40)}$$

$$Q_{40} \;=\; 80 \cdot e^{-1.2536}$$

$$Q_{40} \;=\; 80 \cdot 0.2854$$

$$\boxed{Q_{40} \;\approx\; 22.83 \text{ m┬│/s}}$$

**(iii) Base Flow Volume in 60 days (2.5 marks):**

$$V_b \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{b0}}{k}\,\bigl(1 - e^{-k T}\bigr)$$

$$V_b \;=\; \dfrac{80}{0.03134}\,\bigl(1 - e^{-(0.03134)(60)}\bigr)$$

$$V_b \;=\; 2552.7 \times \bigl(1 - e^{-1.8804}\bigr)$$

$$V_b \;=\; 2552.7 \times (1 - 0.1527)$$

$$V_b \;=\; 2552.7 \times 0.8473$$

$$\boxed{V_b \;\approx\; 2162.9 \text{ million m}^{3} \;=\; 2.163 \times 10^{9} \text{ m}^{3}}$$

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION B ŌĆö Option 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the **exponential recession equation** $Q_t = Q_{b0}\,e^{-k t}$ from first principles, starting from Darcy's Law. Explain the physical significance of the recession constant $k$ and the **time constant** $t_{eq} = 1/k$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Physical Setup (1 mark):**
Consider a stream channel in hydraulic contact with an adjacent unconfined aquifer. As groundwater seeps into the stream, the saturated thickness and hydraulic gradient decline over time.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Darcy's Law Application (1 mark):**
By Darcy's Law, the discharge $Q_b$ into the stream is proportional to the hydraulic gradient, which itself is proportional to the saturated thickness, hence to $Q_b$. Thus:

$$\dfrac{dQ_b}{dt} \;=\; -k \cdot Q_b$$

where $k$ is the recession constant (depends on transmissivity, specific yield, geometry).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Separation of Variables (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{dQ_b}{Q_b} \;=\; -k\,dt$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Integration (1 mark):**

$$\int_{Q_{b0}}^{Q_t} \dfrac{dQ_b}{Q_b} \;=\; -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{Q_t}{Q_{b0}}\right) \;=\; -k t$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Closed-Form Decay Equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{Q_t \;=\; Q_{b0} \cdot e^{-k t}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Physical Significance of $k$ (1 mark):**
- $k$ is the **fractional rate of base flow depletion per unit time**.
- Larger $k$ ŌåÆ faster decay ŌåÆ low-storage aquifer (e.g., fractured rock).
- Smaller $k$ ŌåÆ slower decay ŌåÆ high-storage aquifer (e.g., alluvial sands).
- **Typical range:** $0.01 < k < 0.10$ day$^{-1}$.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Time Constant $t_{eq} = 1/k$ (1 mark):**
At $t = 1/k$, we have:

$$Q_t \;=\; Q_{b0}\,e^{-1} \;=\; 0.368\,Q_{b0}$$

So $t_{eq}$ is the **time required for base flow to fall to 36.8% of its initial value** ŌĆö analogous to the RC time constant in electrical circuits and the half-life (with a different multiplier) in radioactive decay.

---

**(b)** The following base flow recession data is observed in a stream. Using the **method of linearization**, determine the recession constant $k$. **(7 marks)**

| Time $t$ (days) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Base Flow $Q_b$ (m┬│/s) | 100 | 85 | 72 | 61 | 52 | 44 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Linearize the Recession Equation (1 mark):**

Taking natural logarithm of $Q_t = Q_{b0}\,e^{-k t}$:

$$\ln(Q_t) \;=\; \ln(Q_{b0}) \;-\; k t$$

This is of the form $Y = a + bX$ with $Y = \ln(Q_t)$, $X = t$, slope $b = -k$, intercept $a = \ln(Q_{b0})$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Tabulate $\ln(Q_t)$ (1 mark):**

| $t$ (days) | $Q_b$ (m┬│/s) | $\ln(Q_b)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 100 | 4.6052 |
| 5 | 85 | 4.4427 |
| 10 | 72 | 4.2767 |
| 15 | 61 | 4.1109 |
| 20 | 52 | 3.9512 |
| 25 | 44 | 3.7842 |

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Least-Squares Regression (2 marks):**

Compute $\sum t$, $\sum \ln(Q_b)$, $\sum t^2$, $\sum t \ln(Q_b)$:

$$\sum t \;=\; 0+5+10+15+20+25 \;=\; 75$$

$$\sum \ln(Q_b) \;=\; 4.6052+4.4427+4.2767+4.1109+3.9512+3.7842 \;=\; 25.1709$$

$$\sum t^{2} \;=\; 0+25+100+225+400+625 \;=\; 1375$$

$$\sum t\,\ln(Q_b) \;=\; 0+22.2135+42.767+61.6635+79.024+94.605 \;=\; 300.273$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the Slope (1 mark):**

$$\text{slope} \;=\; \dfrac{n\sum t\ln(Q_b) \;-\; \sum t \cdot \sum \ln(Q_b)}{n\sum t^{2} \;-\; (\sum t)^{2}}$$

With $n = 6$:

$$\text{slope} \;=\; \dfrac{6(300.273) \;-\; (75)(25.1709)}{6(1375) \;-\; (75)^{2}}$$

$$\text{slope} \;=\; \dfrac{1801.638 \;-\; 1887.818}{8250 \;-\; 5625} \;=\; \dfrac{-86.180}{2625}$$

$$\text{slope} \;=\; -0.03283$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Identify $k$ (1 mark):**

Since slope $= -k$:

$$\boxed{k \;\approx\; 0.0328 \text{ day}^{-1}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verify with Intercept (1 mark):**

$$\text{intercept} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum \ln(Q_b) \;-\; \text{slope}\cdot\sum t}{n} \;=\; \dfrac{25.1709 - (-0.03283)(75)}{6}$$

$$\text{intercept} \;=\; \dfrac{25.1709 + 2.4623}{6} \;=\; \dfrac{27.6332}{6} \;=\; 4.6055$$

$$Q_{b0} \;=\; e^{4.6055} \;\approx\; 100.00 \text{ m┬│/s} \;\; \checkmark \text{ (matches initial value)}$$

Thus the recession equation is:

$$\boxed{Q_t \;=\; 100 \cdot e^{-0.0328\,t}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap (ŌĆō2 marks):** When using $N = A^{0.2}$, area MUST be in **square miles**; for km┬▓, use $N = 0.83\,A^{0.2}$. Mixing units is the #1 error.
> 2. **Sign error in $k$:** The recession constant is positive by convention; the negative sign is **absorbed into the exponent**. Writing $Q_t = Q_{b0}\,e^{+kt}$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Volume calculation oversight:** Forgetting the integration step when computing base flow volume ŌĆö the answer is **NOT** simply $Q_{b0} \times T$.
> 4. **Sketch omission:** KTU examiners expect a labelled hydrograph diagram (with points A, B, N) ŌĆö **no diagram = ŌĆō2 marks minimum**, even with correct numerical work.
> 5. **Confusing $k$ forms:** Always declare whether you are using the **exponential form** ($k$ in day$^{-1}$) or **geometric form** ($k$ dimensionless). They are **different values** for the same recession.
> 6. **Bounded integration:** Students often write $V = Q_{b0}/k$ (infinite time) when the question specifies a finite duration ŌĆö read the time limit carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Base Flow Separation**

- ŌśÉ **Base Flow** = subsurface contribution (groundwater + slow interflow); **Direct Runoff** = surface flow + quick interflow.
- ŌśÉ The recession equation $Q_t = Q_{b0}\,e^{-k t}$ is a **first-order exponential decay** grounded in Darcy's Law.
- ŌśÉ The **linearized form** $\ln(Q_t) = \ln(Q_{b0}) - kt$ enables graphical and least-squares determination of $k$.
- ŌśÉ **Linsley's Fixed Base Equation:** $N = A^{0.2}$ days (area in sq. miles) or $N = 0.83\,A^{0.2}$ days (area in sq. km).
- ŌśÉ **Typical $k$ range:** $0.01 < k < 0.10$ day$^{-1}$ (exponential form) corresponding to $0.90 < k < 0.99$ (geometric form).
- ŌśÉ **Six methods:** Straight Line ŌåÆ Fixed Base ŌåÆ Variable Slope ŌåÆ Concave ŌåÆ Master Depletion Curve ŌåÆ Lynne-Holtan.
- ŌśÉ **Master Depletion Curve** is the most accurate but requires data from **multiple storm events**.
- ŌśÉ **Base Flow Index (BFI)** = (Base flow volume) / (Total storm runoff volume); BFI > 0.5 implies groundwater-dominated catchment.
- ŌśÉ **Volume of base flow** over finite time $T$: $V_b = \dfrac{Q_{b0}}{k}\bigl(1 - e^{-k T}\bigr)$.
- ŌśÉ **Time constant** $t_{eq} = 1/k$ is the time for base flow to drop to **36.8%** of initial value.
- ŌśÉ Always include a **labelled hydrograph sketch** showing points A, B, N in exam answers.
- ŌśÉ **Sign convention:** $k$ is always **positive**; the negative sign belongs in the exponent.
- ŌśÉ **Unit discipline:** $k$ in day$^{-1}$ for exponential form; dimensionless for geometric form ŌĆö never mix.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Takeaway:** *"Base flow separation partitions the hydrograph into its surface and subsurface components ŌĆö its accuracy governs the quality of unit hydrographs, flood forecasts, and groundwater recharge estimates."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Hydrograph Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unit Hydrograph Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Hydrograph (UH)** is defined as the **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH)** resulting from **1 cm (or 1 unit) of effective rainfall (excess rainfall / runoff-producing rain)**, generated uniformly over the entire drainage basin at a uniform intensity for a specified **unit time duration ($D$)**.

A Unit Hydrograph is essentially a **"hydraulic signature"** of a watershed. It represents how the basin transforms a known quantity of uniformly distributed rainfall into runoff at the outlet, abstracting the complex physics of overland flow, interflow, and channel routing into a single, observable time-response curve.

**Key Terminology:**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Excess Rainfall (Effective Rainfall, $P_e$)** | The portion of total rainfall that contributes to direct surface runoff (after subtracting initial abstractions, infiltration, and evaporation). |
| **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DRH)** | The surface flow component of a storm hydrograph, separated from base flow. |
| **Base Flow** | The sustained, slow-response groundwater contribution to streamflow. |
| **Unit Duration ($D$)** | The time period over which the unit depth of effective rainfall is uniformly distributed. |
| **Lag Time ($t_L$)** | The time difference between the **centroid of the effective rainfall hyetograph** and the **peak of the resulting DRH**. |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy ŌĆö "The Wok and the Sauce":**
> Imagine pouring a precise **1 cm-thick layer of water** (unit depth) uniformly across a large, tilted kitchen wok (the drainage basin). The wok has ridges, valleys, and a single drain hole at the bottom (the basin outlet). Water will trickle through multiple paths ŌĆö some fast (channels), some slow (puddles, soil storage) ŌĆö and exit the drain at varying rates over time.
> 
> If you plotted the **rate of water exiting the drain vs. time**, you would get a unique curve. **This curve is the Unit Hydrograph.** Now, if instead of 1 cm you poured 2 cm (twice the water), the exit rate at every instant would simply double. If you poured 1 cm, waited 2 hours, and then poured another 1 cm, the exit curve would be the **sum of two offset UHs** (the first one, plus a delayed version of the first one).
> 
> This simple logic ŌĆö **scale linearly, shift and add** ŌĆö is the heart of Unit Hydrograph Theory. It turns a chaotic, distributed watershed into a predictable **linear system**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Unit Depth:** $1\ \text{cm} = 10\ \text{mm} = 0.01\ \text{m}$
> - **Peak Discharge ($Q_p$):** Measured in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ or **cumecs**
> - **Time ($t$):** Measured in **hours** for meso-scale catchments
> - **Time to Peak ($t_p$):** Typically $D < t_p < 2.5D$ for natural basins
> - **Base Time ($t_B$):** Total duration of the DRH, typically ranging from $5D$ to $10D$ for small watersheds.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Assumptions of Unit Hydrograph Theory

The entire UH framework rests on four **linear time-invariant (LTI) system assumptions**. These are the examiner's favorite quick-fire questions in KTU papers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of UH Theory**

1. **Principle of Time Invariance (Stationarity):**
   The shape of the unit hydrograph for a basin **does not change with time**. The DRH produced by a 1 cm effective rainfall occurring today is identical to the DRH produced by the same rainfall next month, *provided basin conditions (antecedent moisture, vegetation) remain the same*.

2. **Principle of Linearity (Proportionality):**
   If the effective rainfall depth is multiplied by a factor $k$, the ordinates of the resulting DRH are also multiplied by $k$. Mathematically:
   
   $$Q(t) = k \cdot UH(t)$$
   
   where $Q(t)$ is the runoff at time $t$ and $UH(t)$ is the unit hydrograph ordinate.

3. **Principle of Superposition (Additivity):**
   If two or more unit bursts of effective rainfall occur in sequence, the resulting DRH is the **time-shifted sum of the individual DRHs**.

4. **Uniform Spatial Distribution:**
   The effective rainfall is assumed to be **uniformly distributed over the entire basin** for the unit duration.

---

### 2.2 Component Steps of a Complete Unit Hydrograph Analysis

A typical UH-based flood estimation problem in KTU exams follows this pipeline:

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Base Flow Separation:** Isolate the DRH from the total storm hydrograph using a chosen method (e.g., **straight-line method**, **fixed base method**, or the **concave method**).
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Effective Rainfall Hyetograph (ERH):** Subtract infiltration losses (using $\Phi$-index or $W$-index method) from the total rainfall hyetograph.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Volume Verification:** Ensure that the **area under the DRH equals the total effective rainfall volume**:
  
  $$V_{DRH} = \int_0^{t_B} Q(t)\, dt = P_e \cdot A_{basin}$$
  
  where $A_{basin}$ is the catchment area.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Unit Hydrograph Computation:** Divide each ordinate of the DRH by the total effective rainfall depth (in cm) to obtain the ordinates of the 1 cm UH.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Application:** Use the UH (with superposition and proportionality) to predict DRH for any storm.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Equations for Unit Hydrograph Problems (Board-Exam Ready)**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q_p = \dfrac{2.08 \cdot A}{t_p}$ | Snyder's synthetic peak discharge equation | $Q_p$ in cumecs, $A$ in km┬▓, $t_p$ in hours |
| 2 | $t_p = t_r + \dfrac{t_L}{2}$ | Basin lag relationship (Snyder) | hours |
| 3 | $t_L = C_t \cdot (L \cdot L_c)^{0.3}$ | Snyder's basin lag formula | $L$ in km, $t_L$ in hours, $C_t$ = basin coefficient (1.0ŌĆō2.2) |
| 4 | $V_{DRH} = P_e \cdot A$ | Volume conservation between ERH and DRH | m┬│ (if SI), requires unit consistency |
| 5 | $UH_{new}(t) = UH_{old}(t) + UH_{old}(t - T) + \ldots$ | **S-Curve addition** for duration conversion | dimensionless / cumecs |
| 6 | $Q(t) = \int_0^t u(\tau) \cdot P_e(t - \tau)\, d\tau$ | **Convolution integral** (continuous) | cumecs |
| 7 | $Q_n = \sum_{m=0}^{n} P_m \cdot u_{n-m}$ | **Convolution sum** (discrete, KTU-favorite) | cumecs |
| 8 | $t_B = 5.5 \cdot t_p$ | Empirical base time relation | hours |
| 9 | $W_{50} = \dfrac{5.87}{q_p^{1.08}}$ | Width of UH at 50% peak (Chow) | hours, $q_p$ in cumecs/km┬▓ |
| 10 | $W_{75} = \dfrac{3.35}{q_p^{1.08}}$ | Width of UH at 75% peak (Chow) | hours |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Watch-Out:** In KTU numerical problems, if basin area is given in $\text{km}^2$ and rainfall depth in $\text{cm}$, the runoff volume must be converted to $\text{m}^3$ using:
> $V = P_e\ (\text{cm}) \times A\ (\text{km}^2) \times 10^4$ ŌåÆ since $1\ \text{km}^2 = 10^6\ \text{m}^2$ and $1\ \text{cm} = 0.01\ \text{m}$, therefore $V = 0.01 \times 10^6 \times P_e \times A = 10^4 \cdot P_e \cdot A\ \text{m}^3$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Unit Hydrograph Theory is the **workhorse of flood forecasting** in:

- **Spillway design** for dams and barrages (PMF / SPF estimation).
- **Flood plain zoning** and urban storm drainage design.
- **Real-time flood warning systems** operated by the Central Water Commission (CWC), India.
- **Climate-change impact studies**, where synthetic UHs are updated for changed land-use conditions.
- **Small hydroelectric project design** (run-of-river schemes need flow duration curves derived from UH simulations).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: S-Curve Hydrograph and Duration Conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S-Curve Concept:** When a continuous series of unit hydrographs (each of duration $D$) are placed back-to-back such that the effective rainfall is a continuous, steady **1 cm/hour** storm, the resulting DRH ordinates, when summed cumulatively, trace out an **S-shaped curve** called the **S-Curve Hydrograph** (or **Summation Hydrograph**).

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the $D$-hour UH ordinates be $u_1, u_2, u_3, \ldots, u_n$ (cumecs).

If we apply a continuous effective rainfall of 1 cm/$D$ hour for an indefinitely long time, the resulting ordinates at the basin outlet at any time $t$ will be the running sum:

$$
S_t = u_1 + u_2 + u_3 + \ldots + u_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} u_i
$$

This is the **S-curve ordinate at time $t$**.

**To convert a $D$-hour UH to a $T$-hour UH (where $T \neq D$):**

- **Case 1: $T > D$ (longer duration required)**

  Step 1: Lag the S-curve by $T$ hours: $S_{t-T}$
  Step 2: Take the difference: $S_t - S_{t-T}$
  Step 3: Multiply by the ratio $\dfrac{D}{T}$ to obtain the $T$-hour UH ordinates:

$$
UH_T(t) = \frac{D}{T} \cdot \left[ S_t - S_{t-T} \right]
$$

- **Case 2: $T < D$ (shorter duration required)**

  If $T = D/m$ where $m$ is an integer, generate $m$ S-curves, each lagged by $T$ hours from the previous, sum them, and take the difference with a version lagged by $D$ hours.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Convolution Equation (Discrete Form)

This is the **most heavily tested KTU derivation**, often worth 5ŌĆō7 marks.

#### Starting Point

Let the basin be modeled as a **linear, time-invariant system** with:
- **Input:** Effective rainfall hyetograph $P_e(t)$ (cm/hr)
- **System Function:** Unit hydrograph $u(t)$ (cumecs per cm)
- **Output:** Direct runoff hydrograph $Q(t)$ (cumecs)

#### Discretization

Divide the time axis into equal intervals of duration $D$. Let:
- $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ be the effective rainfall depths in successive intervals (cm).
- $u_0, u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_m$ be the ordinates of the $D$-hour UH (cumecs per cm of $P_e$).

#### Step-by-Step Application of Superposition

**Pulse 1** (at $t = 0$): $P_0$ cm of effective rainfall ŌåÆ contributes $P_0 \cdot u_k$ to the DRH at time step $k$.

**Pulse 2** (at $t = D$): $P_1$ cm of effective rainfall ŌåÆ contributes $P_1 \cdot u_{k-1}$ to the DRH at time step $k$.

**Pulse 3** (at $t = 2D$): $P_2$ cm of effective rainfall ŌåÆ contributes $P_2 \cdot u_{k-2}$ to the DRH at time step $k$.

#### Summing All Contributions at Time Step $k$

$$
Q_k = P_0 \cdot u_k + P_1 \cdot u_{k-1} + P_2 \cdot u_{k-2} + \ldots + P_k \cdot u_0
$$

$$
Q_k = \sum_{m=0}^{k} P_m \cdot u_{k-m}
$$

This is the **Discrete Convolution Equation**. It is the **board-exam "show-piece" formula** for KTU 2024 Scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous form (for theoretical questions):**

$$
Q(t) = \int_{0}^{t} u(\tau) \cdot P_e(t - \tau)\, d\tau
$$

The integral form follows directly by replacing the discrete sum with a continuous integral in the limit as $D \to 0$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Derivation: UH from a Multi-Pulse Storm

> **Sample KTU-style problem:** A basin produced the following DRH from a 3-hour storm. The total effective rainfall was **2.0 cm**. The 3-hour UH ordinates are to be derived.

**Given DRH ordinates (cumecs):**

| Time (hr) | 0 | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 | 18 | 21 | 24 |
|-----------|---|---|----|----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $Q$ (cumecs) | 0 | 25 | 100 | 160 | 130 | 80 | 35 | 10 | 0 |

**Step 1 ŌĆö Volume under DRH (Trapezoidal Rule):**

$$
V = D \cdot \left[ \frac{Q_0 + Q_n}{2} + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} Q_i \right]
$$

$$
V = 3 \cdot \left[ \frac{0 + 0}{2} + (25 + 100 + 160 + 130 + 80 + 35 + 10) \right] = 3 \cdot 540 = 1620\ \text{cumec-hours}
$$

Converting to volume in $\text{m}^3$:

$$
V = 1620 \times 3600 = 5{,}832{,}000\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Effective Rainfall Volume:**

$$
V_{eff} = P_e \cdot A = 0.02\ \text{m} \times A\ \text{m}^2
$$

For a $1\ \text{cm}$ UH, the volume under UH must equal $0.01\ \text{m} \times A\ \text{m}^2$. Since the actual $V = 2 \times V_{UH,1cm}$:

$$
V_{UH,1\ cm} = \frac{1620}{2} = 810\ \text{cumec-hours}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute UH Ordinates:**

$$
u_i = \frac{Q_i}{P_e} = \frac{Q_i}{2.0}
$$

| Time (hr) | 0 | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 | 18 | 21 | 24 |
|-----------|---|---|----|----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $u_i$ (cumecs/cm) | 0 | 12.5 | 50 | 80 | 65 | 40 | 17.5 | 5 | 0 |

This is the **1 cm, 3-hour UH** for the basin.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of UH Models

> [!NOTE]
> **High-yield comparison table for KTU short-answer questions**

| Feature | Empirical UH | Synthetic UH (Snyder) | Conceptual UH (Nash/IUH) |
|---------|--------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Data Requirement** | Long-term observed rainfall-runoff | Basin area, length, $C_t$ | Basin lag, number of linear reservoirs |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Purely empirical (linear superposition) | Empirical regression | Moment-matching (conceptual) |
| **Key Output** | Discrete hydrograph ordinates | Peak discharge $Q_p$, lag time $t_L$ | IUH (instantaneous response) |
| **Computational Demand** | Low | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Accuracy in Ungauged Basins** | Cannot be applied | High (most common) | High |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | **Very High** | **Very High** | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Rainfall-to-Runoff Transformation via UH

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Rainfall Hyetograph P_t] --> B[Loss Estimation]
    B --> B1[Initial Abstraction]
    B --> B2[Infiltration Index]
    B --> B3[Evaporation Loss]
    B1 --> C[Effective Rainfall Hyetograph P_e]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Unit Hydrograph u_t]
    D --> E[Convolution Operator]
    E --> F[Direct Runoff Hydrograph Q_DR]
    F --> G[Add Base Flow B_t]
    G --> H[Total Storm Hydrograph Q_T]
    H --> I[Flood Hydrograph at Basin Outlet]

    subgraph UNIT_HYDROGRAPH_THEORY_BLOCK[Unit Hydrograph Theory Engine]
    D
    E
    F
    end

    subgraph VALIDATION_BLOCK[Volume Validation Module]
    V1[Area under Q_DR] --> V2[Compare with P_e x A_basin]
    V2 --> V3{Error less than 5%?}
    V3 -- Yes --> V4[Accept UH]
    V3 -- No --> V5[Recalibrate / Refine]
    end
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Deriving a Unit Hydrograph from Observed Storm Data

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Collect Total Storm Hydrograph] --> S2[Step 2: Separate Base Flow]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Obtain Direct Runoff Hydrograph DRH]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Effective Rainfall using Phi-index]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Verify Volume under DRH equals P_e x A]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Divide DRH ordinates by P_e in cm]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Result - Unit Hydrograph u_t for duration D]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Apply S-curve / Convolution for different storms]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Relationships Between UH Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UH1[1 cm, D-hour Unit Hydrograph] --> SC[S-curve Hydrograph]
    SC --> SC1[S-curve lagged by T hours]
    SC1 --> DIFF[Difference Operation]
    UH1 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> UH2[Converted T-hour Unit Hydrograph]

    UH1 --> CONV[Convolution with Effective Rainfall]
    CONV --> DRH[Predicted Direct Runoff Hydrograph]

    SC --> IUH[Instantaneous Unit Hydrograph IUH]
    IUH --> IUH1[Nash Model Conceptual IUH]
    IUH --> IUH2[Clark Model Conceptual IUH]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The left-to-right flow shows the data processing pipeline. The right branch shows the conceptual evolution from empirical UH to physically-based IUH models. KTU questions often test the **S-curve to duration conversion path** (top-right of the diagram).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define a Unit Hydrograph. State the assumptions made in the unit hydrograph theory.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A **Unit Hydrograph (UH)** is the direct runoff hydrograph (DRH) resulting from **1 cm of effective rainfall**, generated uniformly over the entire basin at a uniform rate over a specified **unit duration ($D$)**.

**The four key assumptions are:**

1. **Time invariance** ŌĆö the UH does not change with time.
2. **Linearity (Proportionality)** ŌĆö runoff ordinates are proportional to rainfall depth.
3. **Superposition (Additivity)** ŌĆö runoff from successive storms may be added.
4. **Uniform spatial distribution** ŌĆö rainfall is uniform over the basin.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**What is an S-curve hydrograph? How is it used to convert a unit hydrograph of duration $D$ to duration $T$?**

**Model Answer:**

An **S-curve hydrograph** is the DRH that results from a **continuous, steady effective rainfall of 1 cm per $D$-hour** applied indefinitely. Its ordinates approach a **constant equilibrium value** $Q_{eq} = 2.08 A / D$ cumecs (for area $A$ in km┬▓).

**Conversion procedure from $D$-hour UH to $T$-hour UH:**

- **Step 1:** Construct the S-curve $S_t$ by cumulative summation of $D$-hour UH ordinates.
- **Step 2:** Lag the S-curve by $T$ hours to get $S_{t-T}$.
- **Step 3:** Compute the difference $S_t - S_{t-T}$.
- **Step 4:** Multiply by $\dfrac{D}{T}$ to obtain the $T$-hour UH ordinates.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō July 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **discrete convolution equation** used in unit hydrograph theory. State clearly the symbols used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A basin produced the following direct runoff hydrograph from a 4-hour storm of 2.5 cm effective rainfall:

| Time (hr) | 0 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 24 | 28 | 32 |
|-----------|---|---|----|----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| DRH (cumecs) | 0 | 30 | 120 | 200 | 170 | 110 | 60 | 20 | 0 |

**Compute the ordinates of the 4-hour, 1 cm unit hydrograph.** **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of Discrete Convolution Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** Linear, time-invariant basin. Input: effective rainfall $P_e(t)$. System function: UH $u(t)$. Output: DRH $Q(t)$.

**Step 1:** Divide time into equal intervals of duration $D$ hours. Let effective rainfall in successive intervals be $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ (cm).

**Step 2:** By the **principle of proportionality**, the contribution of pulse $P_m$ (occurring at time $mD$) to the DRH at time $t = kD$ is $P_m \cdot u_{k-m}$.

**Step 3:** By the **principle of superposition**, the total DRH ordinate at time $kD$ is the sum of all pulse contributions:

$$
Q_k = \sum_{m=0}^{k} P_m \cdot u_{k-m}
$$

**Where:**
- $Q_k$ = DRH ordinate at time step $k$ (cumecs)
- $P_m$ = effective rainfall depth in $m$-th time step (cm)
- $u_{k-m}$ = UH ordinate at lag $(k-m)$ (cumecs per cm)

**Continuous form (taking $D \to 0$):**

$$
Q(t) = \int_0^{t} u(\tau) \cdot P_e(t - \tau)\, d\tau
$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Computation of 1 cm, 4-hour UH

**Step 1: Volume under DRH (Trapezoidal Rule):**

$$
V = 4 \cdot \left[ \frac{0 + 0}{2} + (30 + 120 + 200 + 170 + 110 + 60 + 20) \right] = 4 \cdot 710 = 2840\ \text{cumec-hours}
$$

**Step 2: Apply proportionality** (divide by $P_e = 2.5$ cm):

$$
u_i = \frac{Q_i}{P_e} = \frac{Q_i}{2.5}
$$

**Resulting 4-hour, 1 cm UH ordinates:**

| Time (hr) | 0 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 24 | 28 | 32 |
|-----------|---|---|----|----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| UH (cumecs/cm) | 0 | 12 | 48 | 80 | 68 | 44 | 24 | 8 | 0 |

**Verification (volume check):** Area under UH = $4 \times 284 = 1136$ cumec-hours. Required volume for 1 cm over basin should match, and any discrepancy should be within **┬▒5%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Snyder's synthetic unit hydrograph** method. List the equations used to compute peak discharge, basin lag, and base time. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a basin of area **500 km┬▓**, the Snyder coefficients are $C_t = 1.5$, $C_p = 0.7$. The main stream length $L = 30$ km, and the distance from outlet to centroid of basin $L_c = 12$ km. Compute the **peak discharge, time to peak, and base time** of the synthetic UH for a rainfall duration of 3 hours. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Snyder's Synthetic Unit Hydrograph

> [!NOTE]
> **Snyder's method** is used to derive a synthetic unit hydrograph for **ungauged basins**, where observed rainfall-runoff data is unavailable. It requires only the **basin physiographic parameters**.

**Key Equations:**

1. **Basin Lag:** $t_L = C_t \cdot (L \cdot L_c)^{0.3}$
2. **Time to Peak:** $t_p = \dfrac{D}{2} + t_L$
3. **Peak Discharge:** $Q_p = \dfrac{2.75 \cdot C_p \cdot A}{t_p}$ (some texts use 2.08; KTU prefers 2.75 with $C_p$)
4. **Base Time:** $t_B = 5.5 \cdot t_p$ (empirical)

**Where:**
- $C_t$ = basin lag coefficient (1.0 ŌĆō 2.2 for Indian basins)
- $C_p$ = peak discharge coefficient (0.5 ŌĆō 0.8)
- $L$ = main stream length (km)
- $L_c$ = distance from outlet to basin centroid along main stream (km)
- $A$ = basin area (km┬▓)
- $D$ = duration of effective rainfall (hr)

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation

**Given:** $A = 500\ \text{km}^2$, $C_t = 1.5$, $C_p = 0.7$, $L = 30\ \text{km}$, $L_c = 12\ \text{km}$, $D = 3\ \text{hr}$

**Step 1: Basin Lag**

$$
t_L = C_t \cdot (L \cdot L_c)^{0.3} = 1.5 \cdot (30 \times 12)^{0.3}
$$

$$
(360)^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \ln 360} = e^{0.3 \times 5.886} = e^{1.766} \approx 5.847
$$

$$
t_L = 1.5 \times 5.847 \approx 8.77\ \text{hr}
$$

**Step 2: Time to Peak**

$$
t_p = \frac{D}{2} + t_L = \frac{3}{2} + 8.77 = 1.5 + 8.77 = 10.27\ \text{hr}
$$

**Step 3: Peak Discharge**

$$
Q_p = \frac{2.75 \cdot C_p \cdot A}{t_p} = \frac{2.75 \times 0.7 \times 500}{10.27}
$$

$$
Q_p = \frac{962.5}{10.27} \approx 93.72\ \text{cumecs}
$$

**Step 4: Base Time**

$$
t_B = 5.5 \cdot t_p = 5.5 \times 10.27 \approx 56.49\ \text{hr} \approx 56.5\ \text{hr}
$$

> **Final Answers:** $t_L \approx 8.77$ hr, $t_p \approx 10.27$ hr, $Q_p \approx 93.72$ cumecs, $t_B \approx 56.5$ hr.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Unit Hydrograph Problems**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Failing to convert basin area from km┬▓ to m┬▓ when computing volumes. This costs **2ŌĆō3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Trapezoidal vs. Simpson's Rule:** KTU valuations often penalize students for using the **wrong numerical integration method** for volume. Use **Trapezoidal Rule** for ordinates at uniform intervals unless specified otherwise.
> 3. **S-curve Lag Direction:** When constructing $S_{t-T}$, students often lag the S-curve in the **wrong direction** (forward instead of backward). The lagged curve $S_{t-T}$ is **shifted to the right** by $T$ hours relative to $S_t$.
> 4. **Forgetting the $D/T$ Multiplier:** In duration conversion, students compute $S_t - S_{t-T}$ but forget to multiply by $D/T$. This alone costs 1 full mark.
> 5. **Assuming Default Snyder Coefficients:** The coefficients $C_t$ and $C_p$ **must be explicitly stated** in the problem. If not, use $C_t = 1.0$ to $2.2$ (typical Indian basin range) and clearly mention the assumption.
> 6. **Neglecting Base Flow Separation:** Always state and show the **base flow separation method** used; otherwise, the DRH is technically incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Unit Hydrograph Theory**

- **Definition anchor:** UH = DRH from 1 cm of effective rainfall over unit duration $D$ on a uniform basin.
- **Four assumptions:** Time invariance, linearity, superposition, uniform distribution.
- **Volume conservation:** $V_{DRH} = P_e \times A_{basin}$ (always verify before using the UH).
- **Proportionality rule:** $u_i = Q_i / P_e$ (constant factor scaling).
- **S-curve construction:** Cumulative sum of $D$-hour UH ordinates ŌåÆ equaling $Q_{eq} = 2.08 A / D$ cumecs at steady state.
- **Duration conversion:** $UH_T(t) = (D/T) \cdot [S_t - S_{t-T}]$.
- **Discrete convolution:** $Q_k = \sum_{m=0}^{k} P_m \cdot u_{k-m}$ ŌĆö **highest weightage derivation in KTU 2024 Scheme**.
- **Snyder's equations:** $t_L = C_t (L \cdot L_c)^{0.3}$, $t_p = D/2 + t_L$, $Q_p = 2.75 C_p A / t_p$, $t_B = 5.5 t_p$.
- **Instantaneous UH (IUH):** Response to 1 cm rainfall applied instantaneously (zero duration); theoretical limit as $D \to 0$.
- **Base flow separation methods:** Straight-line, Fixed-base, Concave ŌĆö always state which one is used.
- **Limitations of UH theory:** Not valid for large basins (heterogeneity breaks linearity), not valid for unusual storm patterns (highly non-uniform), and breaks down when basin conditions change drastically (urbanization, deforestation).
- **KTU must-know constants:** $2.08$ (equilibrium constant), $2.75$ (Snyder peak coefficient), $5.5$ (base time multiplier), $0.3$ (lag-time exponent).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: S-Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** S-Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **S-Hydrograph** (commonly called the **S-Curve** or **Summation Hydrograph**) is a hydrograph that results from a **continuous effective rainfall of uniform intensity occurring for an infinitely long duration**. It is generated by accumulating (summing ordinates) a series of successive unit hydrographs spaced one unit time period apart.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2 ŌĆō Unit Hydrograph Theory):**
> The S-Curve is the direct runoff hydrograph (DRH) resulting from a **prolonged, uniform-intensity effective rainfall** of infinite duration. It represents the **upper limit** of a unit hydrograph (UH) and serves as a **scaling tool** to convert a UH of one duration to another.

Mathematically, the S-Curve is obtained by:

$$S(t) = \int_0^t u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

where $u(\tau)$ is the instantaneous unit hydrograph (IUH) ordinate at time lag $\tau$, and $S(t)$ is the S-curve ordinate at time $t$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆō "The Filling Bucket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Running Tap and a Bucket**
> Imagine a tap dripping water into a bucket at a **constant rate** of 1 drop per second, and the bucket is being emptied at a variable rate. The tap continues **forever** (infinite duration). The water level in the bucket at any time is the S-curve. Initially, the water level rises **slowly** (lag/concave part), then rises **linearly** (steady-state equilibrium peak), and finally ŌĆö when the tap is shut off ŌĆö falls back to zero (recession part). The **S-shape** literally traces this rise-steady-fall pattern in the hydrograph plot.

In hydrological terms:
- **"Tap"** = Continuous effective rainfall of intensity $i$ (mm/hr) lasting forever.
- **"Bucket"** = The watershed/catchment that generates runoff.
- **"Water level"** = Direct runoff rate at the catchment outlet.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Effective Rainfall Intensity | $i_e$ | **1 cm/hr** or **1 mm/hr** | L/T |
| Equilibrium Discharge | $Q_{eq}$ | **2.778 ├Ś A** (for $i_e = 1$ cm/hr) | m┬│/s |
| Catchment Area | A | Basin-dependent | km┬▓ |
| Equilibrium Runoff Depth | $P_e$ | $i_e \times T$ | mm |
| Time to Equilibrium | $T_{eq}$ | **Time Base of UH** (approx.) | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Discharge Rule:** When the rainfall continues for a long enough duration, the runoff rate reaches an **equilibrium peak discharge ($Q_p^{eq}$)**. Beyond this point, the S-curve becomes a horizontal line ŌĆö the catchment is discharging water at the same rate it receives it.

## 1.4 Physical Significance in Engineering Practice

- **Unit Hydrograph Conversion Tool:** The S-curve is the primary mechanism for **changing the duration** of a unit hydrograph (e.g., converting a 2-hour UH to a 4-hour UH).
- **Synthetic UH Generation:** Forms the foundation for deriving synthetic unit hydrographs for **ungauged catchments**.
- **Flood Forecasting:** Used in conjunction with design storms to predict **probable maximum floods (PMF)**.
- **Reservoir/Routing Studies:** Provides the inflow hydrograph for downstream hydrological routing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆō The Underlying Theory

The S-Curve theory rests on three **fundamental assumptions** inherited from the unit hydrograph theory (Sherman, 1932):

1. **Time Invariance:** The basin response (UH shape) does not change with time.
2. **Linearity (Superposition):** The runoff ordinates are **directly proportional** to the effective rainfall intensity.
3. **Stationarity:** The same UH applies for all storms of similar duration over the catchment.

### 2.1.1 Construction Logic ŌĆō Step-by-Step Reasoning

> [!NOTE]
> **Why summation?** A continuous rainfall can be thought of as an **infinite series of infinitesimal unit pulses** of rainfall, each separated by an infinitesimally small time interval. The total runoff is therefore the **superposition (sum) of all the unit hydrographs** produced by each infinitesimal pulse, each lagged by its respective time of occurrence.

**Operational Steps (Summation Method):**

1. Take a known **D-hour unit hydrograph (UH)** of the catchment.
2. **Lag** the UH by a duration of $D$ hours ŌåÆ this gives the DRH from the second hour of continuous rain.
3. **Lag** the UH by $2D$ hours ŌåÆ DRH from the third hour, and so on.
4. **Add all the lagged UHs** column-wise (i.e., sum the ordinates at each time step).
5. The resulting summed hydrograph is the **S-curve** (with the assumption of $i_e = 1$ cm/hr).

### 2.1.2 Why "S" Shaped?

The S-curve trace has three distinct phases:

- **Concave-up rising limb (0 ŌåÆ $T_p$):** Initially, only a portion of the catchment is contributing; runoff builds up.
- **Linear/Asymptotic middle portion ($T_p$ ŌåÆ $T_{eq}$):** Entire catchment is contributing; runoff increases at a constant rate equal to the input rate (the curve tends toward an asymptote).
- **Horizontal equilibrium plateau (after $T_{eq}$):** Runoff rate equals rainfall rate ŌĆö no further net accumulation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table ŌĆö it is the highest-yielding set of formulas for KTU exams on S-Curves.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $S(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} u(t - kD)$ | Summation definition of S-curve from $D$-hr UH | $u$ = UH ordinate (m┬│/s/cm), $D$ = UH duration (hr) |
| 2 | $Q_{eq} = 2.778 \times A$ | Equilibrium discharge for $i_e = 1$ cm/hr | A in km┬▓, $Q_{eq}$ in m┬│/s; constant = 1 cm/hr ├Ś 1 km┬▓ ├Ś 100 ha/km┬▓ ├Ś 10Ōü┤ m┬▓/ha / 3600 s = **2.778** |
| 3 | $Q_{eq} = \frac{A \times i_e}{3.6}$ | General equilibrium discharge (any $i_e$) | $i_e$ in mm/hr, A in km┬▓, $Q_{eq}$ in m┬│/s |
| 4 | $u_{new}(t) = \frac{S(t) - S(t - T_{new})}{T_{new}}$ | New $T_{new}$-hr UH from S-curve (shift method) | $u_{new}$ is the new UH ordinate |
| 5 | $u_{new}(t) = \frac{1}{T_{new}} \left[ S(t) - S(t - T_{new}) \right]$ | Standard KTU conversion formula | Result in m┬│/s/cm if $T_{new}$ in hours and $S$ in m┬│/s |
| 6 | $\Delta Q = S(t) - S(t - T)$ | Change in S-curve ordinate over duration $T$ | m┬│/s |
| 7 | $T_{eq} \approx T_{b}$ (Base time of UH) | Time to reach equilibrium discharge | hours |
| 8 | $\text{Area under S-curve} = Q_{eq} \times T$ | Trapezoidal property at equilibrium | Used for verification |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** In all S-curve-to-UH conversion problems, the **denominator is the NEW duration ($T_{new}$)**, not the original UH duration. Many students divide by the old duration, losing full marks.

### 2.2.1 Conversion Between Unit Hydrograph Durations (The S-Curve's Main Application)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU's most-asked application:** Given a 2-hour UH, derive the 4-hour UH using the S-curve.

**The Procedure:**

1. Construct S-curve by summing lagged 2-hr UHs.
2. Shift the S-curve by **4 hours** (the new duration) on the time axis.
3. Compute the **difference** of ordinates: $S(t) - S(t - 4)$.
4. **Divide by 4** to get the new 4-hour UH.

This is precisely the formula:
$$u_{4\text{-hr}}(t) = \frac{S(t) - S(t - 4)}{4}$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stormwater Management:** Municipal drainage design uses S-curve-derived UHs to size **detention basins**.
- **Floodplain Mapping:** Government agencies (e.g., CWC, India) use S-curve-transformed UHs to compute **design floods** for the National Flood Frequency Programme.
- **Irrigation Hydrology:** Canal and tank spillway design depends on S-curve hydrographs to model sustained inflows.
- **Climate Change Studies:** Modern applications use S-curve concepts to compare **changing basin response** under altered rainfall regimes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Equilibrium Discharge ($Q_{eq}$)

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a catchment of area $A$ km┬▓ subjected to a continuous effective rainfall of intensity $i_e$ cm/hr. At **steady-state equilibrium**, the rate of rainfall input equals the rate of runoff output. We must derive the corresponding equilibrium discharge $Q_{eq}$ in m┬│/s.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1: Rainfall volume rate (m┬│/hr) per unit area**
> Rainfall depth per hour over 1 km┬▓ = $i_e$ cm = $i_e \times 10^{-5}$ km of water column (since 1 cm = $10^{-5}$ km).
> Volume of water falling per hour on 1 km┬▓:
> $V_1 = i_e \times 10^{-5} \text{ km} \times 1 \text{ km}^2 = i_e \times 10^{-5} \text{ km}^3/\text{hr}$

> **Step 2: Convert to m┬│/hr**
> $1 \text{ km}^3 = 10^9 \text{ m}^3$
> $V_1 = i_e \times 10^{-5} \times 10^9 = i_e \times 10^4 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ per km┬▓

> **Step 3: Generalize for catchment area $A$ km┬▓**
> $V_A = i_e \times 10^4 \times A \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$

> **Step 4: Convert hourly rate to per-second rate (m┬│/s)**
> $1 \text{ hr} = 3600 \text{ s}$
> $Q_{eq} = \frac{i_e \times 10^4 \times A}{3600} = \frac{i_e \times A \times 10^4}{3600}$

> **Step 5: Simplify the constant**
> $\frac{10^4}{3600} = \frac{10000}{3600} = 2.7777...$

Thus, the final form is:

$$\boxed{Q_{eq} = 2.778 \times i_e \times A}$$

where:
- $Q_{eq}$ is in m┬│/s
- $i_e$ is in cm/hr
- $A$ is in km┬▓

> **Special Case (KTU-favoured):** If $i_e = 1$ cm/hr, then $Q_{eq} = 2.778 \times A$ m┬│/s.

### 3.1.3 Alternative Derivation (when $i_e$ is in mm/hr)

> **Step 1:** Rainfall over 1 km┬▓ per hour = $i_e$ mm = $i_e \times 10^{-6}$ km
> **Step 2:** Volume per hour = $i_e \times 10^{-6} \times 10^9 = i_e \times 10^3$ m┬│/hr per km┬▓
> **Step 3:** For area A: $V = i_e \times 10^3 \times A$ m┬│/hr
> **Step 4:** Divide by 3600:

$$Q_{eq} = \frac{i_e \times 10^3 \times A}{3600} = \frac{i_e \times A}{3.6}$$

This is valid when $i_e$ is in **mm/hr** and $A$ in km┬▓.

## 3.2 Derivation of S-Curve to Unit Hydrograph Conversion

### 3.2.1 Setup

Given an S-curve $S(t)$ obtained by summing a $D$-hr UH, derive the new $T$-hr UH ordinate $u_T(t)$ at any time $t$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

> **Step 1: Define the S-curve**
> The S-curve represents the runoff due to continuous rainfall of unit intensity ($i_e = 1$ cm/hr) over an infinite duration.

> **Step 2: Consider two S-curves shifted by T hours**
> $S(t)$ = S-curve at time $t$
> $S(t - T)$ = S-curve shifted by T hours on the time axis

> **Step 3: Compute the difference**
> The difference $S(t) - S(t - T)$ represents the additional runoff generated between time $(t - T)$ and time $t$. This additional runoff is the **direct runoff hydrograph (DRH) resulting from T hours of unit effective rainfall**.

> **Step 4: Identify the volume under the difference curve**
> The volume under $[S(t) - S(t - T)]$ from $t = 0$ to $t = \infty$ equals:
> $V = \int_0^\infty [S(t) - S(t - T)] \, dt = T \times Q_{eq}$
> (since each S-curve has equilibrium discharge $Q_{eq}$, and the difference is non-zero only over duration $T$)

> **Step 5: For a T-hr UH, the runoff depth must equal 1 cm (unit depth)**
> Volume of 1 cm depth over area A = $A \times 10^4 \times 10^{-2} = A \times 100$ m┬│ (for A in km┬▓)
> Equivalently, the **average** ordinate of the T-hr UH must satisfy: $\bar{u}_T \times T \times 3600 = A \times 100$ (m┬│)
> Simplifying: $\bar{u}_T = \frac{2.778 \times A}{T}$

> **Step 6: Reconstruct as point ordinates**
> The DRH from a T-hr storm of unit intensity has ordinate $[S(t) - S(t - T)]$. To convert this DRH into a **unit hydrograph** (1 cm runoff depth), we divide by $T$:

$$\boxed{u_T(t) = \frac{S(t) - S(t - T)}{T}}$$

where $T$ is in **hours** (the new desired UH duration).

## 3.3 Verification Example ŌĆō Worked Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A 4-hour unit hydrograph of a 10 km┬▓ catchment has the following ordinates:

| Time (hr) | 0 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | 24 | 28 | 32 | 36 | 40 |
|-----------|---|---|---|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|-----|
| UH ordinate (m┬│/s) | 0 | 8 | 22 | 30 | 25 | 18 | 11 | 6 | 3 | 1 | 0 |

Construct the S-curve and find the 8-hour UH ordinate at $t = 16$ hr.

### Solution

> **Step 1: Lag the UH by multiples of 4 hours (D = 4 hr)**

| t | UH (0-lag) | UH (+4 hr) | UH (+8 hr) | UH (+12 hr) | ... | S-curve |
|---|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-----|---------|
| 0 | 0 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | | **0** |
| 4 | 8 | 0 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | | **8** |
| 8 | 22 | 8 | 0 | ŌĆö | | **30** |
| 12 | 30 | 22 | 8 | 0 | | **60** |
| 16 | 25 | 30 | 22 | 8 | | **85** |
| 20 | 18 | 25 | 30 | 22 | | **95** |
| 24 | 11 | 18 | 25 | 30 | | **84** |
| 28 | 6 | 11 | 18 | 25 | | **60** |
| 32 | 3 | 6 | 11 | 18 | | **38** |
| 36 | 1 | 3 | 6 | 11 | | **21** |
| 40 | 0 | 1 | 3 | 6 | | **10** |
| 44 | ŌĆö | 0 | 1 | 3 | | **4** |
| 48 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | 0 | 1 | | **1** |
| 52 | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | 0 | | **0** |

> **Step 2: Verify the equilibrium peak**
> $Q_{eq} = 2.778 \times 10 = 27.78$ m┬│/s per cm/hr.
> However, this is the per-cm/hr value. Since the S-curve is built by summing UHs each representing 1 cm depth over 4 hours, the S-curve's eventual plateau represents the cumulative effect. Numerically, the maximum S-curve value here is **95 m┬│/s**.

> **Step 3: Compute the 8-hour UH ordinate at t = 16 hr**
> $S(16) = 85$ m┬│/s
> $S(16 - 8) = S(8) = 30$ m┬│/s
> $u_{8\text{-hr}}(16) = \frac{85 - 30}{8} = \frac{55}{8} = 6.875$ m┬│/s/cm

> **Final Answer:** The 8-hour unit hydrograph ordinate at $t = 16$ hr is **6.875 m┬│/s/cm**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
