# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model QP]

**Differentiate between the $\Phi$-index and the W-index methods of estimating infiltration. (3 Marks)**

### Model Answer
| Criterion | $\Phi$-index | W-index |
|---|---|---|
| Basis of calculation | **Rainfall intensity** pattern (hyetograph) | **Volume** of rainfall, runoff, and surface detention |
| Duration used | **Effective duration** $t_e$ (only blocks with $i > \Phi$) | **Total storm duration** $t$ |
| Nature | Capacity-based (assumes constant infiltration capacity) | Volume-based (averages all losses) |
| Sensitivity to storm pattern | High ŌĆö depends on hyetograph shape | Low ŌĆö depends only on totals |
| Practical use | Engineering runoff estimation from storms | Hydrological research and regional loss estimates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners' Note:** Many students confuse the two indices. The key differentiator is that the $\Phi$-index uses the **effective duration** while the W-index uses the **total storm duration**.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model QP]

**Define the term "effective rainfall" and explain its significance in surface water hydrology. (3 Marks)**

### Model Answer
**Effective rainfall** (also called *rainfall excess*) is the portion of storm rainfall that is **neither retained on the land surface nor infiltrated into the soil**, but instead flows over the land as **direct surface runoff**.

**Significance:**
1. It is the **direct input** to the unit hydrograph and runoff hydrograph generation process.
2. It determines the **magnitude and timing of flood peaks** in rivers and drains.
3. It is essential for the design of **culverts, bridges, urban storm drains, and reservoirs**.
4. It is the key variable in the **Rational Method** ($Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$).
5. Computed using $\Phi$-index as $P_e = \sum (i - \Phi)\cdot \Delta t$ for $i > \Phi$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Choose ONE of the two alternatives. Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.]**

---

### Question B1 ŌĆö Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model QP]

**(a) A 9-hour storm with the following intensities produced 25 mm of direct runoff. The total storm precipitation was 105 mm. Compute the $\Phi$-index by the trial method. (7 Marks)**

| Hour | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intensity (mm/hr) | 8 | 12 | 18 | 25 | 30 | 20 | 10 | 6 | 4 |

**(b) Discuss the applications and limitations of the $\Phi$-index method. (7 Marks)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Step 1: Verify total precipitation**

$$
P = 8 + 12 + 18 + 25 + 30 + 20 + 10 + 6 + 4 = 133 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Note:** Question statement says $P = 105$ mm ŌĆö this is a typo in the question (sum is 133 mm). For grading, use $P = 105$ mm as given.

**Step 2: Trial 1 ŌĆö Assume $\Phi = 20$ mm/hr**

Effective hours: 4 (25 mm/hr), 5 (30 mm/hr), 6 (20 mm/hr is **not strictly greater**, so excluded). 

> Wait, correction: 20 > 20 is false, so only Hours 4 and 5 are effective ŌåÆ $t_e = 2$ hours.

$$
P_e = (25 - 20)\cdot 1 + (30 - 20)\cdot 1 = 5 + 10 = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

$15 \neq 25$ ŌåÆ Try higher $\Phi$? No ŌĆö increasing $\Phi$ reduces $P_e$. We need to *decrease* $\Phi$ to include more blocks and increase $P_e$.

**Step 3: Trial 2 ŌĆö Assume $\Phi = 12$ mm/hr**

Effective hours: 2 (12 mm/hr not strictly > 12 ŌåÆ excluded), 3 (18), 4 (25), 5 (30), 6 (20). So $t_e = 4$ hours.

$$
P_e = (18-12) + (25-12) + (30-12) + (20-12) = 6 + 13 + 18 + 8 = 45 \text{ mm}
$$

$45 > 25$ ŌåÆ $\Phi$ must be *higher* (in the range 12ŌĆō20).

**Step 4: Trial 3 ŌĆö Assume $\Phi = 15$ mm/hr**

Effective hours: 3 (18), 4 (25), 5 (30), 6 (20). So $t_e = 4$ hours.

$$
P_e = (18-15) + (25-15) + (30-15) + (20-15) = 3 + 10 + 15 + 5 = 33 \text{ mm}
$$

$33 > 25$ ŌåÆ Increase $\Phi$ further.

**Step 5: Trial 4 ŌĆö Assume $\Phi = 18$ mm/hr**

Effective hours: 4 (25), 5 (30), 6 (20). So $t_e = 3$ hours.

$$
P_e = (25-18) + (30-18) + (20-18) = 7 + 12 + 2 = 21 \text{ mm}
$$

$21 < 25$ ŌåÆ $\Phi$ must be *slightly less* than 18.

**Step 6: Final Answer ŌĆö Interpolate between 15 and 18 mm/hr**

Linear interpolation to match $P_e = 25$:

At $\Phi = 15$: $P_e = 33$ mm
At $\Phi = 18$: $P_e = 21$ mm
Difference in $P_e$: $33 - 21 = 12$ mm
Difference in $\Phi$: $18 - 15 = 3$ mm/hr
Excess $P_e$ to remove: $33 - 25 = 8$ mm

$$
\Phi = 15 + 3 \cdot \frac{8}{12} = 15 + 2 = 17 \text{ mm/hr}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Phi \approx 17 \text{ mm/hr}, \quad t_e = 3 \text{ hours}\;}
$$

**Verification at $\Phi = 17$:**
Effective hours: 4 (25), 5 (30), 6 (20). $t_e = 3$ hr.
$P_e = (25-17) + (30-17) + (20-17) = 8 + 13 + 3 = 24$ mm (Ōēł 25 mm Ō£ō)

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) ŌĆö Applications and Limitations

**Applications of $\Phi$-index:**

1. **Computation of effective rainfall** for unit hydrograph derivation ŌĆö the most common KTU application.
2. **Rational Method design** of urban storm drains where $I = \Phi$ defines the critical intensity threshold.
3. **Flood estimation** in ungauged catchments using regional $\Phi$ maps.
4. **Runoff forecasting** for reservoir operation and flood warning.
5. **Design of erosion control structures** (e.g., terracing) by estimating rainfall excess volumes.
6. **Calibration input** for distributed hydrological models (HEC-HMS, MIKE 11).

**Limitations of $\Phi$-index:**

1. **Assumes uniform infiltration capacity** over the entire watershed ŌĆö not realistic for heterogeneous catchments.
2. **Constant with time** during the storm ŌĆö ignores the natural decrease of infiltration capacity as soil saturates.
3. **Depends on storm intensity pattern** ŌĆö two storms with same total $P$ and $R$ can give different $\Phi$ values, making it non-unique.
4. **Ignores initial abstraction and depression storage** separately ŌĆö they are bundled into infiltration losses.
5. **Effective duration $t_e$ is sensitive to small changes in $\Phi$** ŌĆö small errors in observed runoff can lead to large errors in $\Phi$.
6. **Not applicable to frozen or snow-covered watersheds**.
7. **Trial-and-error solution** is tedious and sometimes non-convergent for multi-peak storms.
8. **Cannot capture spatial variability** of soil, vegetation, and antecedent moisture conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read Carefully!)**
> In Part (b) of this question, students commonly lose **3ŌĆō4 marks** by:
> - Listing only 2ŌĆō3 applications/limitations (need at least 4ŌĆō5 of each for full 7 marks).
> - Confusing $\Phi$-index limitations with W-index limitations.
> - Writing vague statements like "it is not accurate" without engineering justification.
> - Forgetting to mention the **non-uniqueness** of $\Phi$ (depends on storm pattern) ŌĆö this is a favourite KTU marking point.

---

### Question B1 ŌĆö Choice B (Alternative 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model QP]

**(a) The total rainfall during a 4-hour storm is 60 mm and the direct runoff is 14 mm. The hourly rainfall intensities are 10, 18, 20, and 12 mm/hr respectively. Determine the $\Phi$-index of the watershed. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Compare and contrast the $\Phi$-index, W-index, and average infiltration rate. When would you prefer one over the other in engineering practice? (7 Marks)**

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Step 1: Total Precipitation $P$**

$$
P = 10 + 18 + 20 + 12 = 60 \text{ mm} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 2: Trial 1 ŌĆö Assume $\Phi = 12$ mm/hr**

Effective hours: 2 (18), 3 (20). Hour 1 (10) and Hour 4 (12) are not strictly greater. So $t_e = 2$ hours.

$$
P_e = (18-12) + (20-12) = 6 + 8 = 14 \text{ mm} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Conclude**

Since the computed $P_e = 14$ mm exactly matches the observed runoff $R = 14$ mm, the trial is successful.

$$
\boxed{\;\Phi = 12 \text{ mm/hr}, \quad t_e = 2 \text{ hours}\;}
$$

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) ŌĆö Comparison Table

| Property | $\Phi$-index | W-index | Average Infiltration $f_{avg}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Constant intensity above which rainfall = runoff | Average loss rate over entire storm | Mean of actual $f(t)$ curve |
| **Formula** | $\Phi = (P - R) / t_e$ | $W = (P - R - S)/t$ | $f_{avg} = F_{total} / T$ |
| **Duration used** | Effective $t_e$ | Total $t$ | Total $T$ |
| **Storm pattern dependence** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Practical preference** | For runoff estimation from hyetographs | For regional loss studies, water balance | For laboratory/field infiltration tests |

**Engineering Preference Criteria:**

- **Use $\Phi$-index** when you have a **hyetograph** and need to compute effective rainfall for a specific storm event (e.g., unit hydrograph derivation).
- **Use W-index** when you only have **daily or monthly totals** of rainfall and runoff (long-term water balance).
- **Use $f_{avg}$** when you have **measured infiltration data** from double-ring infiltrometer tests and want a representative single value for design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Note:** In a 7-mark question, the examiner allocates ~3 marks for the comparison table and ~4 marks for the practical preference criteria. Writing only a paragraph without a table attracts 0.5ŌĆō1 mark penalty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 ŌĆö Infiltration Indices**

### Key Formulas (Memorize These)

- **$\Phi$-index**: $\Phi = (P - R)/t_e$ ŌĆö *but always verify by trial-and-error* since the formula assumes $t_e$ corresponds to the correct $\Phi$.
- **W-index**: $W = (P - R - S)/t$ ŌĆö uses *total* duration.
- **Effective rainfall**: $P_e = \sum (i - \Phi) \cdot \Delta t$ for $i > \Phi$ ŌĆö equals observed runoff $R$.
- **Average infiltration**: $f_{avg} = F_{total}/T$.

### Critical Concepts

1. The $\Phi$-index is **not a physical property** of the soil ŌĆö it is a *mathematical construct* derived from observed rainfall and runoff data.
2. The $\Phi$-index is **storm-specific** ŌĆö same watershed can yield different $\Phi$ for different storms.
3. Effective duration $t_e$ includes **only those time blocks** where rainfall intensity is *strictly greater* than $\Phi$ ŌĆö equality does not count.
4. The W-index is always **less than or equal to** the $\Phi$-index (since it includes periods of low intensity).
5. For a single-peak hyetograph, the trial-and-error method is the **only reliable approach** in KTU exams.

### Common Exam Pitfalls

- Using $\Phi = (P-R)/t$ instead of $t_e$ ŌåÆ **wrong** (must be effective duration).
- Including blocks with $i = \Phi$ in effective duration ŌåÆ **wrong** (strict inequality).
- Reporting $\Phi$ without verifying that $P_e = R$ ŌåÆ **loses 1 mark**.
- Confusing infiltration indices with infiltration **capacity curves** (Horton, Philip, Kostiakov) ŌåÆ they are different concepts.
- Forgetting to state **units** in the final answer ŌåÆ loses 0.5 mark in KTU valuation.

### Applications Snapshot (For Theory Questions)

- Unit hydrograph derivation
- Rational method for urban drainage
- Flood estimation in ungauged basins
- SCS Curve Number calibration
- Irrigation water requirement

### Limitations Snapshot (For Theory Questions)

- Spatial uniformity assumption
- Temporal constancy assumption
- Storm-pattern dependence
- Bundles all losses into infiltration
- Trial-and-error tediousness
- Non-uniqueness

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floods - Terms and Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Floods - Terms and Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Floods

## 1.1 What is a Flood? ŌĆö Formal KTU Definition

A **flood** is an abnormally high stage of a river or water body, occurring when the discharge of water exceeds the **bank-full capacity** of the channel, causing water to overflow onto the adjoining lands known as the **floodplain**.

In KTU engineering hydrology terminology, a flood is a hydrological event characterized by a peak flow rate (or stage) that has a quantifiable probability of being equalled or exceeded in any given year. It is a stochastic, extreme-value hydrological phenomenon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** "A flood is a relatively high flow of water, which overtops the natural or artificial banks in any reach of a stream, and which may be a threat to life and property." ŌĆö This is the standard answer framing expected in 3-mark and 7-mark questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "Flood = Bathtub Overflow"

Imagine pouring water into a **bathtub** (the river channel) at a fixed rate. The bathtub has a defined edge (the **bank-full level**). If you pour water fast enough, or for long enough, the water level rises and eventually spills over the edge onto the bathroom floor (the **floodplain**). 

- **Steady pouring = Normal flow** (within channel capacity)
- **Sudden gush from the shower = Heavy storm runoff** (rapid inflow)
- **Water reaching the brim = Bank-full stage**
- **Water spilling over the edge = Flooding**
- **Bathroom floor = Floodplain**
- **Maximum water depth on the floor = Flood depth/severity**

This analogy helps visualize that a flood is essentially a **mismatch between inflow and channel carrying capacity**.

## 1.3 Standardized Terms, Thresholds & Quantities

| Parameter | Standard Engineering Meaning |
|-----------|-----------------------------|
| **Bank-full Discharge ($Q_{bf}$)** | Maximum flow that a channel can carry without overflow, in **m┬│/s** |
| **Bank-full Stage** | Water surface elevation corresponding to $Q_{bf}$, in **metres (m)** above a datum |
| **Flood Stage** | Water level at which overflow onto floodplain begins, generally slightly above bank-full |
| **Peak Discharge ($Q_p$)** | Maximum instantaneous flow during a flood event, in **m┬│/s** |
| **Flood Hydrograph** | Graph of discharge vs. time at a given river cross-section |
| **Floodplain** | Land adjacent to the river that is susceptible to inundation during floods |
| **Base Flow** | Sustained low flow in a river fed by groundwater, in **m┬│/s** |
| **Direct Runoff (DRO)** | Portion of rainfall that reaches the stream as overland flow, in **mm** or **m┬│** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** All flood discharge values in India are conventionally expressed in **cubic metres per second (cumecs or m┬│/s)**. Older Indian texts use **cusecs (ft┬│/s)** ŌĆö a 1-mark conversion question is common!

## 1.4 Why Engineers Study Floods ŌĆö Real-World Engineering Importance

Flood studies are fundamental to:

1. **Design of hydraulic structures** ŌĆö dams, barrages, weirs, bridges, culverts.
2. **Spillway design** ŌĆö sizing based on **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** for major dams.
3. **Floodplain zoning** and urban planning to minimize loss of life and property.
4. **Flood forecasting and warning systems** using real-time hydro-meteorological data.
5. **Insurance and risk assessment** in actuarial and disaster management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Every hydraulic structure has a "design return period" ŌĆö e.g., a minor bridge may be designed for a **50-year flood**, while a major dam's spillway is designed for the **PMF (Ōēł 10,000+ year equivalent)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Floods ŌĆö Structured Logic Breakdown

Floods are classified on the basis of **origin, duration, location, and severity**. Understanding this taxonomy is critical for KTU questions worth 3ŌĆō7 marks.

### A. Based on Origin / Cause
- **Flash Floods** ŌĆö Sudden, short-duration floods (few hours) due to intense localized storms, common in arid/semi-arid regions and **Kerala's Western Ghats catchments**.
- **Riverine (Fluvial) Floods** ŌĆö Slow-onset floods from prolonged rainfall or snowmelt in large river basins (e.g., Brahmaputra floods).
- **Coastal Floods** ŌĆö Due to storm surge, tsunamis, or sea-level rise.
- **Urban Floods** ŌĆö Caused by high-intensity rainfall exceeding the capacity of urban drainage systems (e.g., Kerala 2018, Chennai 2015).
- **Ice-Jam Floods** ŌĆö Caused by ice-blockage in rivers of cold regions.

### B. Based on Frequency / Return Period
- **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** ŌĆö Theoretically the largest flood possible from the most severe combination of critical meteorological and hydrological conditions.
- **Standard Project Flood (SPF)** ŌĆö Adopted by Indian standards (CWC/IS 7784) for major dams = **~ 67% of PMF** (commonly used figure in KTU).
- **Design Flood** ŌĆö The flood magnitude adopted for the design of a structure, corresponding to an acceptable level of risk.
- **Mean Annual Flood** ŌĆö The arithmetic mean of the annual maximum floods (recurrence interval Ōēł **2.33 years**).

### C. Based on Severity (FEMA / NDMA Standards)
- **Minor Flood** ŌĆö Causes minimal damage, low-lying area inundation.
- **Moderate Flood** ŌĆö Significant inundation of structures and roads.
- **Major Flood** ŌĆö Extensive damage, evacuations, threat to life.
- **Catastrophic Flood** ŌĆö Disastrous, with potential for mass casualties.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Term / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Recurrence Interval (T)** | $T = \dfrac{N+1}{m}$ (Weibull) | years | $N$ = total years of record, $m$ = rank (highest = 1) |
| 2 | **Exceedance Probability (p)** | $p = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | dimensionless (or %) | Probability of being equalled/exceeded in any year |
| 3 | **Risk of Failure (R)** | $R = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ | dimensionless | Probability of at least one exceedance in $n$ years |
| 4 | **Reliability** | $\text{Reliability} = 1 - R = (1-p)^{n}$ | dimensionless | Probability of no exceedance in $n$ years |
| 5 | **Return Period for Given Risk** | $T = \dfrac{1}{1 - (1-R)^{1/n}}$ | years | Used for design flood selection |
| 6 | **Plotting Position (Hazen)** | $P = \dfrac{2m - 1}{2N}$ | fraction | Useful for $N < 50$ |
| 7 | **Plotting Position (California)** | $P = \dfrac{m}{N}$ | fraction | Simple but biased |
| 8 | **Plotting Position (Weibull)** | $P = \dfrac{m}{N+1}$ | fraction | Most commonly used |
| 9 | **Bank-full Discharge (Rational)** | $Q_{bf} = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | m┬│/s | $C$ = runoff coefficient, $I$ = intensity, $A$ = area |
| 10 | **Flood Volume (Triangular H'graph)** | $V = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot Q_p \cdot t_b$ | m┬│ | $t_b$ = base time of hydrograph |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When writing absolute values or vertical bars in tabular cells, use `\vert` or `\mid` ŌĆö never the raw pipe symbol `|`, as it will break the markdown table renderer.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Recurrence Interval (T)** is the cornerstone of all design flood studies ŌĆö engineers select a structure's design flood based on its acceptable risk over its design life (e.g., 100-year flood for a bridge, 1000-year flood for a small dam, PMF for a major dam).
- **Risk equation** allows planners to convert "return period" into "probability of failure during project life" ŌĆö a more intuitive metric for stakeholders.
- **Plotting position formulas** are used to construct empirical flood frequency curves from observed annual maxima ŌĆö the very first step in frequency analysis (Gumbel, Log-Pearson Type III).

## 2.4 Flood Hydrograph ŌĆö Key Definitions

A **flood hydrograph** is the graphical representation of stream discharge versus time at a gauging station. Its essential components are:

- **Rising Limb (Concentration Curve):** Increasing flow from direct runoff.
- **Crest (Peak Segment):** Contains the **peak discharge ($Q_p$)** and **peak time ($t_p$)**.
- **Falling Limb (Recession Curve):** Declining flow as direct runoff ends and base flow dominates.
- **Base Flow:** The portion supplied by groundwater, separated as a line below the hydrograph.
- **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DROH):** The portion above the base flow line, representing surface and interflow contributions.
- **Time of Concentration ($t_c$):** Time from end of rainfall to peak of hydrograph.
- **Lag Time ($t_l$):** Time from centre of mass of rainfall hyetograph to peak of hydrograph.
- **Base Time ($t_b$):** Total duration of the direct runoff hydrograph.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Formula $R = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Setup**
Let $p$ = probability that a flood of magnitude Ōēź design value occurs in **any single year** (annual exceedance probability).
For a T-year return period: $p = 1/T$.

Let $q$ = probability that the design flood is **NOT** exceeded in any single year.
Then, by the complement rule of probability:
$$q = 1 - p$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply to a Project Life of n years**
Assuming each year is **statistically independent** (a standard assumption in hydrology), the probability that the design flood is NOT exceeded in **all $n$ years** is the product of yearly non-exceedance probabilities:
$$P(\text{no exceedance in } n \text{ years}) = (1-p) \times (1-p) \times \ldots \times (1-p)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply n times**
$$P(\text{no exceedance in } n \text{ years}) = (1-p)^{n}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Complement Rule to Obtain Risk**
The **risk $R$** is the probability that the design flood IS exceeded at least once during the $n$-year life:
$$R = 1 - P(\text{no exceedance in } n \text{ years})$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final Risk Equation**
$$R = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The risk of failure **always increases** with project life $n$, even for a fixed return period $T$. A 100-year flood has only a **1% chance** of being exceeded in 1 year, but a **~63% chance** of being exceeded in 100 years!

**Step 6 ŌĆö Worked Numerical Demonstration**
Suppose $T = 100$ years, $n = 50$ years (project life of a small bridge). Calculate risk.

$p = 1/T = 1/100 = 0.01$

$$R = 1 - (1 - 0.01)^{50}$$

$$R = 1 - (0.99)^{50}$$

Computing $(0.99)^{50}$ using logarithms:
$$\ln(0.99) = -0.01005$$
$$50 \times \ln(0.99) = -0.5025$$
$$(0.99)^{50} = e^{-0.5025} = 0.6050$$

$$R = 1 - 0.6050 = 0.3950 \approx 39.5\%$$

**Engineering interpretation:** There is a **~40% chance** that the 100-year flood will be exceeded during the 50-year bridge life. This level of risk is **too high for critical infrastructure** ŌĆö designers should select a larger return period.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Design Return Period for a Given Risk

We invert the risk equation. Given acceptable risk $R$ and project life $n$, find $T$.

**Starting from:**
$$R = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$$

**Rearrange:**
$$1 - R = (1-p)^{n}$$

**Take the nth root:**
$$(1-R)^{1/n} = 1 - p$$

**Solve for p:**
$$p = 1 - (1-R)^{1/n}$$

**Substitute $p = 1/T$:**
$$\frac{1}{T} = 1 - (1-R)^{1/n}$$

**Invert to get the design return period:**
$$T = \frac{1}{1 - (1-R)^{1/n}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **inverse risk formula** and is heavily tested in KTU numerical problems. Memorize both the forward and inverse forms.

## 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Selection of Design Return Period

**Problem:** A culvert has a design life of $n = 25$ years. The owner tolerates a maximum risk of $R = 10\%$. Find the design return period $T$.

**Solution:**
$$T = \frac{1}{1 - (1 - 0.10)^{1/25}}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{1 - (0.90)^{0.04}}$$

Compute $(0.90)^{0.04}$:
$$\ln(0.90) = -0.10536$$
$$0.04 \times (-0.10536) = -0.004214$$
$$(0.90)^{0.04} = e^{-0.004214} = 0.9958$$

$$T = \frac{1}{1 - 0.9958} = \frac{1}{0.0042} \approx 238 \text{ years}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A **~240-year return period** flood must be adopted to keep the failure risk below 10% over the 25-year culvert life.

## 3.4 Derivation of Weibull's Plotting Position

Given $N$ years of annual maximum flood records, ranked in descending order of magnitude ($m = 1$ is the largest, $m = N$ is the smallest).

**Assumption (Weibull, 1939):** Each observation has an equal chance of being the $m$-th largest out of $N+1$ possible ranks (since the "true" maximum could be higher than observed).

**Probability of being the $m$-th ranked event:**
$$P_m = \frac{m}{N+1}$$

This is the **exceedance probability** of the $m$-th ranked event. The corresponding **return period** is:
$$T = \frac{1}{P_m} = \frac{N+1}{m}$$

This yields the standard Weibull plotting position formula used universally in Indian hydrology practice (CWC guidelines).

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Key Input | Key Output | Used For |
|---------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Recurrence Interval | Annual maxima series | T (years) | Frequency classification of floods |
| Exceedance Probability | T | p (fraction) | Risk communication |
| Risk Equation | p, n | R (fraction) | Safety evaluation |
| Inverse Risk Equation | R, n | T (years) | Design flood selection |
| Plotting Position | N, m | p | Constructing frequency curves |
| Flood Hydrograph | Discharge vs. time | $Q_p$, $t_p$, $V$ | Hydrologic design of reservoirs & spillways |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Flood Hydrograph Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["PRECIPITATION AND WATERSHED INPUTS"]
        P1[Rainfall Hyetograph]
        S1[Soil Moisture and Antecedent Conditions]
        G1[Groundwater Contribution]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["RUNOFF GENERATION PROCESS"]
        R1[Interception and Depression Storage]
        R2[Infiltration Excess Hortonian Flow]

    end

    subgraph CHANNEL["CHANNEL RESPONSE HYDROLOGY"]
        C1[Overland Flow Routing]
        C2[Channel Flow Routing via Muskingum]
        C3[Lag and Attenuation in Channel]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["FLOOD HYDROGRAPH OUTPUT"]
        O1[Base Flow Component]
        O2[Rising Limb Concentration Curve]
        O3[Peak Discharge Qp at Time tp]
        O4[Recession Falling Limb]
        O5[Total Direct Runoff Volume V]
    end

    P1 --> R1
    S1 --> R2
    S1 --> R3
    G1 --> O1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R4

```

```mermaid

```

## 4.3 Flood Frequency Curve ŌĆö Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Annual Maximum Flood Series N years] --> B[Rank in Descending Order m equals 1 to N]
    B --> C[Apply Weibull Plotting Position P equals m over N plus 1]
    C --> D[Plot Discharge Q versus Exceedance Probability P on Gumbel or Log Pearson Paper]
    D --> E[Fit Theoretical Distribution Gumbel or Log Pearson III]
```

## 4.4 Risk versus Return Period Schematic (for $n = 50$ years)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[T equals 10 years] --> Y1[Risk R approximately 99 percent]

    X4[T equals 500 years] --> Y4[Risk R approximately 10 percent]
    X5[T equals 1000 years] --> Y5[Risk R approximately 5 percent]
    X6[T equals PMF 10000 years] --> Y6[Risk R approximately 0.5 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Notice how doubling the return period roughly halves the risk for typical values. For major dams, PMF is adopted to keep risk well below 1% over the project's life.

## 4.5 Textual Sketch ŌĆö Typical Flood Hydrograph Shape

```
Discharge Q
(m^3/s)
   |
Q_p|          _________
   |         /         \____
   |        /               \____
   |       /                     \____
   |______/___________________________\________
   |     /                            \____   Base Flow
   |    /                                  
   |___/________________________________________> Time t
        t_r      t_p           t_f        t_b
        Rising    Peak         Falling     Base
        Limb      Time         Limb        Time
   <--DROH (Direct Runoff Hydrograph)------->
```

Labels:
- **$t_r$** = time to peak (rising limb duration)
- **$t_p$** = time of peak
- **$t_f$** = falling limb duration
- **$t_b$** = total base time of DROH
- **$Q_p$** = peak discharge
- **Area under DROH** = direct runoff volume

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] Define a flood and explain the difference between **bank-full stage** and **flood stage**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **flood** is a high flow of water in a stream that overtops the natural or artificial banks and poses a threat to life and property in the adjacent floodplain.

- **Bank-full Stage** ŌåÆ The water surface elevation at which the channel is just full; any higher level results in overflow onto the floodplain. (1.5 marks)
- **Flood Stage** ŌåÆ The water level at which damage to life, property, or infrastructure begins; in practice it is set slightly **above** the bank-full stage by the concerned agency (e.g., CWC/State Water Resources Dept.) to allow early warning. (1.5 marks)

The distinction is critical because bank-full is a **hydraulic/geomorphic** threshold, while flood stage is an **administrative/warning** threshold.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] List and briefly explain the three standard **design flood** terminologies used in Indian dam engineering practice.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** ŌĆö Theoretically the largest flood that can occur from the most severe combination of critical meteorological and hydrological conditions that are reasonably possible in the region. Used for design of **major and large dams** (1 mark).

2. **Standard Project Flood (SPF)** ŌĆö Estimated from the Standard Project Storm (SPS); generally adopted as **~ 67% of PMF** for medium dams or as per IS 7784. Used for **intermediate dams** (1 mark).

3. **Design Flood** ŌĆö The flood magnitude adopted for the design of a specific hydraulic structure, corresponding to an acceptable level of risk over its design life. Selected based on structure importance, economic life, and consequence of failure (1 mark).

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]

**a)** Derive the **risk equation** $R = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ used in flood frequency analysis. Define all terms clearly. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A bridge is to be designed for a service life of 60 years. The acceptable risk of the design flood being exceeded is 20%. Determine the **design return period** to be used. Also compute the **annual exceedance probability**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) (7 marks)

**Definition of terms (1 mark):**
- $p$ = annual exceedance probability
- $n$ = project design life (years)
- $R$ = risk (probability that the design flood is exceeded at least once in $n$ years)
- $q = 1 - p$ = annual non-exceedance probability

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Probability of non-exceedance in a single year:
$$q = 1 - p$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Assuming yearly events are statistically independent, probability of non-exceedance in all $n$ years:
$$P(\text{no exceedance in } n \text{ years}) = (1-p)^{n}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Apply the complement rule:
$$R = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitution $p = 1/T$ gives the form most used in design:
$$R = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{T}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Engineering significance** ŌĆö The risk of failure rises sharply with project life; therefore, longer-life or higher-consequence structures must adopt larger return periods. A 100-year flood is not "safe for 100 years" ŌĆö its exceedance probability over 100 years is Ōēł 63%.

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $n = 60$ years
- Acceptable risk $R = 0.20$

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Use the inverse risk formula:
$$T = \frac{1}{1 - (1-R)^{1/n}}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute values:
$$T = \frac{1}{1 - (1 - 0.20)^{1/60}} = \frac{1}{1 - (0.80)^{0.01667}}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute $(0.80)^{0.01667}$:
$$\ln(0.80) = -0.22314$$
$$0.01667 \times (-0.22314) = -0.003720$$
$$(0.80)^{0.01667} = e^{-0.003720} = 0.99629$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Calculate $T$:
$$T = \frac{1}{1 - 0.99629} = \frac{1}{0.00371} \approx 269.5 \text{ years}$$

**Step 5 (0.5 marks):** Annual exceedance probability:
$$p = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{269.5} \approx 3.71 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.371\%$$

**Final Answer:** Design return period Ōēł **270 years**, annual exceedance probability Ōēł **0.37%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**a)** Define the following terms with a labelled sketch of a flood hydrograph: **peak discharge, time of peak, rising limb, falling limb, base flow, and direct runoff hydrograph**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** For a small catchment, the design return period was fixed as $T = 250$ years and the project life is $n = 30$ years. Calculate the **risk of failure** and the **reliability** of the structure. Comment on whether the design is acceptable if the tolerable risk is 10%. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) (7 marks)

**Sketch (3 marks):**
```
     Q(m^3/s)
       |
   Q_p |        _____
       |       /     \____
       |      /            \____  <- Falling Limb
       |     /                  \____
       |____/__________________________\______
       |   /    Ōåæ                      \____
       |  /   t_p                          \____  Base Flow
       |_/________________________________________> t
        ŌåÉt_rŌåÆ
        Rising Limb

       <-- Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DROH) -------->
```

**Definitions (4 marks = 0.5 + 0.5 + 1 + 1 + 1):**

- **Peak Discharge ($Q_p$)** ŌĆö The maximum instantaneous discharge occurring during the flood event.
- **Time of Peak ($t_p$)** ŌĆö The time from the start of the rising limb to the occurrence of peak discharge.
- **Rising Limb (Concentration Curve)** ŌĆö The portion of the hydrograph from the start of direct runoff to the peak; reflects the rate at which runoff enters the channel.
- **Falling Limb (Recession Curve)** ŌĆö The portion after the peak where discharge declines; governed by storage depletion and base flow.
- **Base Flow** ŌĆö The portion of stream flow contributed by groundwater, represented by the line below the DROH.
- **Direct Runoff Hydrograph (DROH)** ŌĆö The portion of the hydrograph above the base flow separation line, representing surface and interflow contributions.

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $T = 250$ years, $n = 30$ years.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Annual exceedance probability:
$$p = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{250} = 0.004$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the risk equation:
$$R = 1 - (1 - p)^{n} = 1 - (1 - 0.004)^{30}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $(0.996)^{30}$:
$$\ln(0.996) = -0.004008$$
$$30 \times \ln(0.996) = -0.12024$$
$$(0.996)^{30} = e^{-0.12024} = 0.8867$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Calculate risk:
$$R = 1 - 0.8867 = 0.1133 \approx 11.33\%$$

**Step 5 (0.5 marks):** Calculate reliability:
$$\text{Reliability} = 1 - R = 1 - 0.1133 = 0.8867 \approx 88.67\%$$

**Step 6 (0.5 marks):** Comment ŌĆö The computed risk (11.33%) **slightly exceeds** the tolerable risk of 10%. The design is **marginally inadequate**; a higher return period (e.g., ~340 years) should be adopted to bring the risk below 10%.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Floods Module:**
> 1. **Confusing $T$ and $R$:** Many students swap return period and risk. Remember: $T$ is the **return period**, $R$ is the **probability of failure** ŌĆö they are reciprocally related but not equal.
> 2. **Forgetting the complement rule:** Writing $R = (1-p)^{n}$ (incorrect ŌĆö this is reliability) instead of $R = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$.
> 3. **Unit Errors:** Always express discharge in **m┬│/s** (cumecs) in KTU papers unless the question explicitly uses cusecs.
> 4. **Sketch labels:** In flood hydrograph questions, **forgetting to mark the base flow separation line** results in loss of 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 5. **Independent-years assumption:** Students often forget to state the assumption of statistical independence between years. Examiners award 0.5ŌĆō1 mark for it.
> 6. **Plotting position formula:** If asked, write **Weibull's formula** with the formula $T = (N+1)/m$, not $T = N/m$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Flood** = flow exceeding bank-full capacity, spilling onto the floodplain.
- **PMF** is the most severe; **SPF Ōēł 67% of PMF**; **Design Flood** is the engineer-selected magnitude.
- **Mean Annual Flood** Ōēł 2.33-year return period.
- **Recurrence Interval** $T = (N+1)/m$ (Weibull's plotting position).
- **Exceedance Probability** $p = 1/T$.
- **Risk** $R = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$; **Reliability** $= (1-p)^{n}$.
- **Inverse Risk Formula** $T = 1/\left[1 - (1-R)^{1/n}\right]$.
- **Flood Hydrograph** components: rising limb, peak ($Q_p$, $t_p$), falling limb, base flow, DROH.
- **Time of Concentration ($t_c$)** = rainfall-end to peak; **Lag Time ($t_l$)** = rainfall-centroid to peak.
- **Assumption of independent years** is essential to apply the risk equation.
- **Indian design standards:** IS 7784 (dams), CWC guidelines for return period selection, BIS 11223 (spillway design floods).
- **Flood frequency analysis** next steps: Gumbel, Log-Pearson Type III, and GEV distributions (covered in higher modules).
- For **Kerala catchments**, the CWC recommends SPF for medium dams and PMF for large dams.
- A **100-year flood** is NOT safe for 100 years ŌĆö its exceedance probability over 100 years is **~63%**.
- The **larger the consequence of failure**, the larger the return period adopted (and hence the lower the risk).
- A neat **labelled hydrograph sketch** is worth 2ŌĆō3 marks in any 7/14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Head Water Flood Control - Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Head Water Flood Control - Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Head Water Flood Control** refers to a set of engineering, agronomic, and watershed-management techniques applied in the **upper catchment (headwater region)** of a river basin to intercept, store, retard, and regulate surface runoff *at its origin* ŌĆö long before flood waters can accumulate in the lower reaches and cause damage.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, head water flood control is the **"upstream" or "catchment-based" flood mitigation strategy** that operates on the principle:

$$\boxed{\text{Reduce Peak Discharge }(Q_p) \text{ by increasing lag time }(t_L) \text{ and infiltration}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ŌĆö Module 3):**
> Head water flood control is one of the **two pillars of flood management**, the other being *channel improvement* (downstream method). The KTU examiner expects students to clearly distinguish **upstream (headwater) methods** from **downstream (channel) methods** and to justify the preference for head water control in small to medium catchments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Tap-and-Bucket Story

Imagine a giant bucket (the river) being filled by ten taps (tributaries) in the upper hills. When all ten taps are opened at once, the bucket overflows. **Head water flood control is equivalent to:**

- **Throttling each tap** (terracing, contour bunds, check dams slow the water),
- **Installing small overflow trays under each tap** (detention basins),
- **Sponging up the floor** (afforestation & vegetative cover increases infiltration),
- **Redirecting some taps to a garden** (diversion channels).

The opposite (downstream) method would be making the bucket *bigger* ŌĆö but you can never make it big enough for a cloudburst. **Always work on the taps first.**

## 1.3 Categories of Head Water Flood Control Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Classification Used by KTU Board Examiners**

| Category | Principle | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Agrotechnical / Agronomic** | Increase infiltration, reduce raindrop impact | Contour farming, terracing, mulching, strip cropping |
| **B. Afforestation & Vegetative** | Increase interception & roughness | Forest cover, grassed waterways, shelter belts |
| **C. Small Structural (Minor)** | Retard & store runoff in upper catchment | Check dams, farm ponds, gully plugs, percolation tanks |
| **D. Major Structural** | Large-scale storage in upper reaches | Flood control reservoirs, retarding basins, debris dams |
| **E. Land-Use Regulation** | Avoid flood-prone misuse | Zoning, building codes, floodplain regulation |

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics and Constants

- **Design return period for headwater works:** $T = 25 \text{ to } 100 \text{ years}$ (smaller than downstream reservoirs).
- **Catchment area considered:** $A < 250 \text{ km}^2$ (typical headwater range).
- **Manning's roughness coefficient ($n$):** increases from **0.02** (concrete) to **0.10ŌĆō0.20** (dense forest) ŌĆö directly raises lag time.
- **Time of concentration ($t_c$):** lengthens from **0.5 h** (barren) to **2ŌĆō4 h** (forested), reducing peak discharge by **40ŌĆō70%**.
- **Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)** constant units, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Mechanisms of Head Water Control

A headwater flood control measure works through one or more of the following physical mechanisms:

1. **Reduction of runoff volume** ŌĆö by promoting infiltration, interception, and evapotranspiration.
2. **Attenuation of peak discharge** ŌĆö by increasing the **time of concentration ($t_c$)** and **lag time ($t_L$)**.
3. **Trapping of sediment & debris** ŌĆö protecting downstream reservoirs and channels from siltation.

## 2.2 Why Head Water Control is Preferred (KTU-Favourite 2-Mark Question)

- **Economical for small catchments** (low cost per unit volume of flood storage).
- **Erosion control is a co-benefit** ŌĆö reduces siltation of downstream reservoirs.
- **Recharges groundwater** through percolation tanks and farm ponds.
- **Environmentally sustainable** ŌĆö uses vegetation and land-use rather than heavy concrete.
- **Reduces flood peak** rather than merely *conveying* the flood downstream.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Method

### A. Agrotechnical Methods
1. **Contour farming** ŌĆö ploughing along contours slows overland flow.
2. **Terracing (bench, broad-base)** ŌĆö converts long slopes into a series of steps.
3. **Strip cropping** ŌĆö alternating strips of crop and grass.
4. **Mulching** ŌĆö covers soil, reduces raindrop splash and evaporation loss from soil.

### B. Afforestation & Vegetative Cover
- **Root systems** increase infiltration capacity of soil ($f_c$ rises).
- **Canopy** intercepts rainfall, releasing it slowly via stem-flow.
- **Leaf litter** increases surface roughness, raising Manning's $n$.

### C. Minor Structural Methods
1. **Check dams** ŌĆö small masonry/earthen dams across gullies, $H = 1 \text{ŌĆō}3 \text{ m}$.
2. **Farm ponds** ŌĆö dug-out structures storing $500 \text{ŌĆō}5000 \text{ m}^3$.
3. **Percolation tanks** ŌĆö built on first-order streams, designed to *recharge* not store.
4. **Gully plugs / Gabion structures** ŌĆö control ephemeral gully erosion.

### D. Major Structural Methods
1. **Flood control reservoirs** ŌĆö large storages with dedicated flood cushions.
2. **Detention basins** ŌĆö *temporary* storage with controlled release.
3. **Retention basins** ŌĆö *permanent* storage; water may not be released except via spillway.
4. **Debris dams** ŌĆö trap coarse sediment upstream of main dam.

### E. Land-Use Regulation
- Restricting construction in floodplains, maintaining natural watercourses, zoning.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Scheme Board-Exam Approved**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_p = C \cdot i \cdot A$ | Rational Method peak discharge | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $t_L = 0.6 \cdot t_c$ | Lag time $\approx 0.6$ of time of concentration | h |
| 3 | $t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$ | Kirpich formula (small upland catchments) | min |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | SCS-CN potential maximum retention | mm |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2 S)^2}{P + 0.8 S}$ | SCS runoff depth | mm |
| 6 | $V_s = (Q_i - Q_o) \cdot t$ | Required storage volume (simplified) | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 7 | $R_o = R_i \cdot (1 - k_v)$ | Runoff after vegetative cover (reduction factor $k_v$) | mm |
| 8 | $n_e = n_o + n_v$ | Equivalent Manning's $n$ (Pavlovskii additive) | ŌĆö |
| 9 | $V = A_{spillway} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 g H}$ | Spillway discharge (broad-crested) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 10 | $f_c = k \cdot \tau_c \cdot S_a$ | Horton / Green-Ampt infiltration (conceptual) | mm/h |

> **Symbols:** $Q_p$ = peak discharge; $C$ = rational runoff coefficient; $i$ = rainfall intensity (mm/h); $A$ = area (km┬▓ or ha); $L$ = longest watercourse length (m); $S$ = slope; $CN$ = Curve Number; $P$ = rainfall depth; $Q$ = runoff depth; $V_s$ = storage volume; $Q_i, Q_o$ = inflow/outflow; $C_d$ = discharge coefficient ($\approx 0.6\text{ŌĆō}0.7$); $H$ = head over spillway.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Kerala Case:** Periyar basin uses percolation tanks (Chalakudy river tributaries) to mitigate Wayanad flash floods.
- **Karnataka Case:** Check dams across Arkavathy catchment for Bangalore water security.
- **Bihar Kosi Headwater:** Embankment + afforestation combination in Nepal headwater.
- **Maharashtra:** Jal Yukt Shivir programme uses farm ponds and CCTs (continuous contour trenches).

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rational Method (For Peak Discharge)

The Rational Method is the **foundational formula** of all headwater flood estimation.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Assumption.** Rainfall intensity $i$ is **uniform** over catchment $A$ for a duration equal to (or greater than) the time of concentration $t_c$. The whole catchment is therefore contributing runoff simultaneously.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Mass balance.** The peak runoff rate equals the rainfall rate minus losses, integrated over the contributing area:

$$Q_p = (i - f) \cdot A$$

where $f$ is the *average* loss rate (infiltration + depression storage + interception). Convert units from mm/h to m/s: multiply by $\dfrac{1}{3.6 \times 10^6}$ if $A$ is in m┬▓, or by $\dfrac{1}{3.6}$ if $A$ is in km┬▓.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define runoff coefficient.** Let $C$ be the fraction of rainfall that appears as direct runoff:

$$C = \dfrac{i - f}{i} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; i - f = C \cdot i$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute.** Replace $i - f$ in Step 2:

$$Q_p = C \cdot i \cdot A$$

This is the **Rational Method equation**, valid for small catchments ($A \le 50 \text{ km}^2$) per KTU conventions.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Boundary / applicability check.**
- Valid when $t_{rainfall} \ge t_c$.
- If $t_{rainfall} < t_c$, only a *partial area* contributes.
- $C$ ranges from **0.05** (sandy flat, dense vegetation) to **0.95** (urban paved).

## 3.2 Derivation of SCS Curve Number Runoff Equation

The Soil Conservation Service (SCS-CN) method is a unitless parametric method. The underlying hypothesis is:

$$\dfrac{F}{S} = \dfrac{Q}{P - I_a}$$

where $F$ = actual retention after runoff begins, $S$ = potential maximum retention, $I_a$ = initial abstraction, $P$ = total rainfall, $Q$ = runoff depth.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Continuity.** $P = I_a + F + Q$, so $F = P - I_a - Q$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute** into the hypothesis:

$$\dfrac{P - I_a - Q}{S} = \dfrac{Q}{P - I_a}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cross multiply:**

$$(P - I_a - Q)(P - I_a) = Q \cdot S$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expand the left side:**

$$(P - I_a)^2 - Q(P - I_a) = Q \cdot S$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Collect $Q$ terms on one side:**

$$(P - I_a)^2 = Q \cdot S + Q(P - I_a) = Q \bigl[S + (P - I_a)\bigr]$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for $Q$:**

$$Q = \dfrac{(P - I_a)^2}{S + (P - I_a)}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Standard SCS empirical simplification:** $I_a = 0.2 S$ (from decades of experimental data on US watersheds).

**Step 8 ŌĆö Substitute $I_a$:**

$$Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2 S)^2}{P - 0.2 S + S} = \dfrac{(P - 0.2 S)^2}{P + 0.8 S}$$

This is the **canonical SCS-CN equation** used universally.

**Step 9 ŌĆö Express $S$ via Curve Number:**

$$S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254 \quad [\text{mm}]$$

For $S$ in inches, $S = \dfrac{1000}{CN} - 10$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Kirpich's Time of Concentration

For a small upland catchment, $t_c$ is derived empirically (1940) by dimensional analysis of overland + channel flow. Kirpich fitted:

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$$

where $L$ is the longest watercourse length in **metres**, $S$ is the dimensionless slope, and $t_c$ is in **minutes**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Note the physics.** A longer channel ($L \uparrow$) gives a longer $t_c$. A steeper channel ($S \uparrow$) gives a shorter $t_c$ (water moves faster). Hence exponents are **positive on $L$** and **negative on $S$**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Physical interpretation in head water control.** A headwater measure that increases surface roughness raises $t_c$, hence reduces the design rainfall intensity $i$ (since $i$ falls as duration rises in an IDF curve), hence reduces $Q_p$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Worked sensitivity check.** Suppose $L = 1000 \text{ m}$, $S = 0.01$. Then:

$$t_c = 0.01947 \cdot (1000)^{0.77} \cdot (0.01)^{-0.385}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute exponents:**

$$(1000)^{0.77} = 10^{0.77 \times 3} = 10^{2.31} \approx 204.2$$

$$(0.01)^{-0.385} = 10^{-0.385 \times (-2)} = 10^{0.77} \approx 5.888$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Multiply:**

$$t_c = 0.01947 \times 204.2 \times 5.888 \approx 23.4 \text{ min}$$

This is the value used in Rational Method for that catchment.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Choosing the Right Head Water Method

| Catchment Characteristic | Best Head Water Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Small ($< 5 \text{ km}^2$), steep, agricultural | **Contour bunds + farm ponds** | Direct interception; high cost-effectiveness |
| Medium ($5 \text{ŌĆō}50 \text{ km}^2$), mixed land use | **Check dams + percolation tanks** | Moderate storage, groundwater recharge |
| Large ($> 50 \text{ km}^2$), forested | **Flood control reservoir + afforestation** | Bulk storage; vegetation sustains over decades |
| Degraded / high erosion | **Terracing + gully plugs + afforestation** | Erosion control is the priority |
| Urbanising peri-urban | **Detention basins + zoning regulations** | Stops imperviousness growth |
| High debris yield (e.g., Western Ghats) | **Debris dams + vegetative cover** | Coarse sediment trapping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "design a head water flood control scheme," a complete answer must list **(i) hydrological analysis, (ii) structural measures, (iii) vegetative measures, (iv) maintenance plan, and (v) cost-benefit justification**. Forgetting even one of these typically costs **2 marks**.

## 3.5 Design of a Detention Basin ŌĆö Step Template

**Step 1.** Compute pre-development peak $Q_{p,1}$ using Rational or SCS-CN method.
**Step 2.** Compute post-development peak $Q_{p,2}$ (with new $C$ values).
**Step 3.** Target release rate $Q_o = Q_{p,1}$ (no increase downstream).
**Step 4.** Required storage $V_s = (Q_{p,2} - Q_o) \cdot t_d$ where $t_d$ is the design drawdown time.
**Step 5.** Choose basin shape; check side slopes and freeboard.
**Step 6.** Size outlet works (orifice + spillway) using:

$$Q_{orifice} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 g h}$$

**Step 7.** Spillway capacity $Q_{spillway} = C_d B H^{3/2}$ for a broad-crested weir.

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Question]
**"Distinguish between Head Water Flood Control and Channel Improvement methods. Give two advantages of the former."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Head Water (Upstream) | Channel Improvement (Downstream) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Upper catchment, sub-basin scale | Along main river / downstream reach |
| **Principle** | Reduce peak by storage & infiltration | Increase conveyance / enlarge channel |
| **Examples** | Check dams, afforestation, percolation tanks | Levees, dredging, channel straightening |
| **Cost trend** | Distributed, many small works | Few large, capital-intensive works |

**Two advantages of Head Water Control (any 2 ├Ś 1 mark):**
1. Reduces both **water volume and peak** (channel improvement only increases conveyance).
2. Provides **co-benefits**: groundwater recharge, sediment trapping, ecological improvement.
3. **More economical** in small to medium catchments.
4. **Sustainable** as it works with vegetation and soil, not against them.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Question]
**"List any six head water flood control methods and state the principle on which each works."**

**Model Answer (1 mark for correct list, 2 marks for principles):**

| # | Method | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Afforestation | Increases interception & infiltration |
| 2 | Contour bunding | Slows overland flow velocity |
| 3 | Terracing | Converts long slope into stepped profile, reducing $S$ effective |
| 4 | Check dam | Temporarily stores water & sediment |
| 5 | Percolation tank | Recharges groundwater, reduces surface flow |
| 6 | Farm pond | Stores runoff for reuse, attenuates peak |
| 7 | Detention basin | Temporarily stores excess runoff, releases slowly |
| 8 | Debris dam | Traps coarse sediment & boulders |

*(Any six with correct principle = full 3 marks.)*

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** *Explain the Rational Method for estimating peak runoff. A 4 km┬▓ catchment near Thrissur has a rational coefficient $C = 0.45$ and a time of concentration of 30 minutes. Using the IDF relation $i = 600 / (t + 20)$ mm/h (where $t$ is in minutes), compute the design peak discharge. Assume a 25-year return period.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *With the help of a neat sketch, describe the design of a check dam. State four design criteria. A gully has a catchment area of 0.8 km┬▓ and a 50-year peak discharge of 18 m┬│/s. Size the spillway length for a permissible head of 1.5 m. Take $C_d = 1.7$ (SI broad-crested).* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Rational Method Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Theory (3 marks):** The Rational Method assumes rainfall intensity is uniform over the catchment for a duration $\ge t_c$. The peak discharge is:

$$Q_p = C \cdot i \cdot A$$

This is valid for $A \le 50 \text{ km}^2$. The runoff coefficient $C$ lumps all losses (infiltration, depression storage, interception).

**Calculation (4 marks):**

**Step 1.** Given $t_c = 30$ min, IDF relation $i = \dfrac{600}{t + 20}$ mm/h.

**Step 2.** Substitute $t = 30$ min:

$$i = \dfrac{600}{30 + 20} = \dfrac{600}{50} = 12 \text{ mm/h}$$

**Step 3.** Convert units. We need $A$ in consistent units. For $A$ in **km┬▓**, the Rational formula in SI is:

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \cdot C \cdot i \cdot A$$

where $i$ is in mm/h and $A$ in km┬▓, and 0.00278 = $\dfrac{1}{3.6} \times \dfrac{1}{1000}$ unit conversion.

**Step 4.** Substitute:

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \times 0.45 \times 12 \times 4$$

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \times 21.6 = 0.0600 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5.** Convert to litres/second for engineering intuition:

$$Q_p = 0.060 \times 1000 = 60 \text{ L/s}$$

**Answer:** $Q_p \approx 0.06 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (60 L/s).

---

**(b) Check Dam Design (7 marks):**

**Sketch (2 marks):** Refer ASCII schematic in Section 4.3 ŌĆö show crest, scoured silt, apron, cut-off wall.

**Design Criteria (any 4 ├Ś 0.5 = 2 marks):**
1. H Ōēż 3 m (minor structure classification).
2. Spillway designed for $T = 50$ŌĆō$100$ year flood.
3. Stable on foundation (no piping, no sliding).
4. Provision for silt-flushing during dry season.
5. Side slopes 1:1.5 to 1:2 (earthen) or vertical (masonry).
6. Freeboard Ōēź 0.5 m above HFL.

**Spillway Sizing (3 marks):**

**Step 1.** Broad-crested weir formula:

$$Q = C_d \cdot L \cdot H^{3/2}$$

where $L$ = spillway length, $H$ = head over crest.

**Step 2.** Solve for $L$:

$$L = \dfrac{Q}{C_d \cdot H^{3/2}}$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $Q = 18 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $C_d = 1.7$, $H = 1.5 \text{ m}$:

$$H^{3/2} = (1.5)^{1.5} = 1.5 \times \sqrt{1.5} = 1.5 \times 1.2247 = 1.837$$

**Step 4.** Compute denominator:

$$C_d \cdot H^{3/2} = 1.7 \times 1.837 = 3.123$$

**Step 5.** Solve:

$$L = \dfrac{18}{3.123} = 5.76 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6.** Provide safe length $L_{design} = 6.0 \text{ m}$ (rounded up, plus 0.5 m freeboard wall).

**Answer:** Spillway length $\approx 6 \text{ m}$.

---

### QUESTION B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** *What is the SCS-CN method? Derive the SCS runoff depth equation $Q = (P - 0.2S)^2 / (P + 0.8S)$. For a watershed with $CN = 75$, compute the runoff depth for a storm of $P = 100$ mm. Comment on the effect of afforestation on $CN$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Design a small farm pond to control runoff from a 2-hectare agricultural plot. Assume $C = 0.4$, design rainfall intensity = 40 mm/h, drawdown time = 6 hours, and an allowable release rate equal to $30\%$ of peak inflow. Use the Rational Method. Comment on the suitability of the pond for a Kerala monsoonal climate.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) SCS-CN Method (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** The Soil Conservation Service Curve Number method is a parametric rainfall-runoff model that estimates direct runoff depth from total rainfall using an empirical dimensionless parameter $CN$ (0ŌĆō100) representing **land use, soil type, and antecedent moisture condition**.

**Derivation (3 marks):** See Section 3.2 above. Key intermediate result:

$$Q = \dfrac{(P - I_a)^2}{S + (P - I_a)}, \quad I_a = 0.2 S$$

$$\Rightarrow\;\; Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$$

**Numerical (1 mark):**

**Step 1.** Compute $S$:

$$S = \dfrac{25400}{75} - 254 = 338.67 - 254 = 84.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2.** Compute $Q$ with $P = 100$ mm:

$$Q = \dfrac{(100 - 0.2 \times 84.67)^2}{100 + 0.8 \times 84.67}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{(100 - 16.93)^2}{100 + 67.73} = \dfrac{(83.07)^2}{167.73}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{6900.6}{167.73} \approx 41.14 \text{ mm}$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Afforestation **reduces CN** (e.g., from 85 to 55) because of higher infiltration and interception. Since $Q \propto (P - 0.2S)^2$ and $S$ increases as $CN$ decreases, runoff drops sharply. This is the scientific basis of **headwater afforestation as flood control**.

---

**(b) Farm Pond Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Peak inflow (Rational Method):** $Q_p = C \cdot i \cdot A$ in SI units:

$$Q_p = 0.00278 \times 0.4 \times 40 \times 2 = 0.089 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Allowable release:**

$$Q_o = 0.30 \times Q_p = 0.0267 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Required storage volume.** Assume a triangular hydrograph for simplicity (linearly rising to peak, linearly falling). Average excess over time = $\dfrac{Q_p - Q_o}{2}$. Drawdown time $t_d = 6$ h = 21600 s.

$$V_s = \dfrac{(Q_p - Q_o)}{2} \times t_d = \dfrac{0.089 - 0.0267}{2} \times 21600$$

$$V_s = 0.0311 \times 21600 = 672.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Pond geometry.** Assume square pond with 1:1.5 side slopes and effective depth 1.5 m. Surface area:

$$A_s = \dfrac{V_s}{d} = \dfrac{672.5}{1.5} \approx 448 \text{ m}^2$$

So a pond of $\approx 21 \text{ m} \times 21 \text{ m}$ at top, $\approx 18 \text{ m} \times 18 \text{ m}$ at bottom.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Outlet design.** Orifice area using $Q_o = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 g h}$ with $C_d = 0.6$, $h = 1.5$ m:

$$A_o = \dfrac{0.0267}{0.6 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.5}} = \dfrac{0.0267}{0.6 \times 5.42} = 0.00821 \text{ m}^2$$

Diameter $d_o = \sqrt{4 A_o / \pi} = \sqrt{4 \times 0.00821 / 3.14} = 0.102 \text{ m} = 102 \text{ mm}$. Use a **100 mm dia. PVC outlet pipe**.

**Comment on Kerala (1 mark):** Suitable ŌĆö Kerala receives **3000+ mm/yr** rainfall concentrated in 6 monsoon months; farm ponds of this size capture first-flush runoff, recharge open wells, and reduce siltation of downstream paddy fields. **Best when combined with percolation pits** for groundwater augmentation in laterite terrain.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit error in Rational Method:** Forgetting the **0.00278** factor when $A$ is in km┬▓. Loses **full 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $S$ in SCS-CN** with slope $S$ in Kirpich. Always state units.
> 3. **Treating farm pond as retention vs detention** incorrectly. A farm pond is *detention* (with outlet) unless specifically designed to hold water year-round.
> 4. **Spillway design:** Students often use the **sharp-crested formula** ($Q = 1.84 L H^{3/2}$) instead of broad-crested. The coefficient $C_d$ depends on crest geometry ŌĆö always specify.
> 5. **Omitting "co-benefits"** in long answers (groundwater recharge, silt trapping) loses 1 mark in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Head Water Flood Control (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Flood control applied in the **upper catchment** to reduce peak at source.
- **Core Principle:** $\uparrow t_c$, $\uparrow$ infiltration, $\downarrow Q_p$, $\uparrow$ lag time.
- **Five categories:** Agrotechnical, Vegetative, Minor Structural, Major Structural, Land-Use.
- **Rational Method:** $Q_p = 0.00278 \, C \, i \, A$ (with $A$ in km┬▓, $i$ in mm/h).
- **SCS-CN:** $Q = (P - 0.2S)^2 / (P + 0.8S)$; $S = 25400/CN - 254$ (mm).
- **Kirpich:** $t_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$ (min, m, dimensionless).
- **Check Dam:** $H \le 3$ m, $L_{apron} = 2H$ to $4H$, freeboard $\ge 0.5$ m.
- **Spillway:** $Q = C_d \, L \, H^{3/2}$ (broad-crested, $C_d \approx 1.7$ SI).
- **Farm Pond:** Volume $\approx$ (Peak excess) ├Ś (Drawdown time) / 2.
- **Afforestation** is the **single most effective long-term** headwater measure ŌĆö reduces $CN$ from ~85 to ~55, cutting runoff by **40ŌĆō60%**.
- **Kerala context:** Percolation tanks + farm ponds = standard approach due to laterite terrain and high monsoon intensity.
- **Comparison reminder:** Head water = *source control*; Channel improvement = *conveyance control*; both used together in integrated flood management.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Gauging - Discharge Rating Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stream Gauging - Discharge Rating Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Stream Gauging (Streamflow Measurement):**
> Stream gauging is the quantitative determination of the volume rate of flow (discharge, **Q**, in **m┬│/s** or **cumecs**) past a given cross-section of a natural or artificial open channel per unit time. It integrates two fundamental field measurements: **stage (H)** ŌĆö the height of the water surface above a fixed datum ŌĆö and **mean velocity (V)** ŌĆö the cross-sectional average of flow velocity vectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discharge Rating Curve (StageŌĆōDischarge Relationship):**
> A Discharge Rating Curve is an empirical, monotonic graphical/analytical relationship established between the **stage (water level)** observed at a gauging station and the corresponding **discharge (Q)** at that section. It is mathematically expressed in the standard power form:
> $$Q = C(H - H_0)^n$$
> where $H_0$ is the gauge height at which flow ceases (zero-flow datum), and $C$ and $n$ are station-specific constants determined by regression of concurrent stage and discharge observations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Fuel Gauge" of a River

Imagine a **car's fuel tank**. You cannot continuously measure the exact volume of petrol being consumed by the engine, but you *can* read the float-level (the "stage") on the dashboard gauge and, from a pre-calibrated curve supplied by the manufacturer, instantly know how many litres are left.

A **discharge rating curve** does exactly this for a river:

- The **fuel gauge needle** ŌåÆ the **stage recorder / staff gauge** (water level in metres).
- The **manufacturer's calibration chart** ŌåÆ the **rating curve** ($Q$ vs. $H$).
- The **remaining litres** ŌåÆ the **discharge in m┬│/s**.

Once calibrated, the hydrologist simply reads the water level and the rating curve instantly gives the discharge ŌĆö no need to put instruments in the river every single day.

> [!TIP]
> **Why we need rating curves:** Continuous 24├Ś7 measurement of velocity is impossible, but a simple **stage recorder** (float, pressure transducer, or bubble gauge) can record water level every 15 minutes for years. The rating curve converts this cheap, continuous stage record into a continuous discharge record.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Practical Field Unit |
|----------|--------|---------|----------------------|
| Discharge | $Q$ | **m┬│/s** (cumec) | cusec (ft┬│/s) |
| Stage / Gauge Height | $H$ or $G$ | **metre (m)** | ft |
| Cross-sectional Area | $A$ | **m┬▓** | ft┬▓ |
| Velocity | $V$ | **m/s** | ft/s |
| Width of sub-section | $b$ | **m** | ft |
| Density of water (at 20 ┬░C) | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m┬│** | ŌĆō |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s┬▓** | ŌĆō |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown ŌĆö Methods of Stream Gauging

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies stream gauging into **three principal families**:

#### (A) AreaŌĆōVelocity Method (Direct, Most Common in Practice)
- Measure cross-sectional area $A$ by sounding depths at regular horizontal intervals.
- Measure velocity $V$ at each vertical using a **current meter** (Price AA, pygmy, or electromagnetic) or **floats**.
- Compute discharge as $Q = A \cdot V$.

#### (B) Dilution (Chemical Tracing) Method
- A tracer of known concentration is injected upstream.
- Downstream sampling gives the diluted concentration.
- Used where turbulent mixing is sufficient but direct velocity measurement is impractical (mountain streams, flood flows).

$$Q = \frac{q \cdot C_1}{C_1 - C_2}$$

where $q$ = injection rate of tracer, $C_1$ = concentration of injected solution, $C_2$ = concentration at sampling station.

#### (C) SlopeŌĆōArea Method (Indirect, Post-Flood)
- Uses Manning's/Ch├®zy equation with high-water marks after a flood.
- Vital for extending rating curves to **un-gauged high stages**.

### 2.2 Mid-Section Method (KTU Favourite)

The stream cross-section is divided into **n vertical strips**, each of width $b$. The mid-section method assigns the velocity at a vertical to a strip extending $b/2$ on either side of that vertical:

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i = b \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot v_i$$

where $d_i$ = depth at $i$-th vertical, $v_i$ = mean velocity in $i$-th vertical.

### 2.3 Mean-Section Method

The mean-section method averages the properties of two adjacent verticals:

$$Q = \frac{b}{2}\left[d_0 v_0 + 2(d_1 v_1 + d_2 v_2 + \cdots + d_{n-1} v_{n-1}) + d_n v_n\right]$$

### 2.4 Mean Vertical Velocity ŌĆö The 0.6 Depth Rule

For a logarithmic velocity profile, the **mean velocity in a vertical** equals the velocity measured at **0.6 of the depth below the water surface**:

$$v_{mean} = v_{0.6d}$$

If two-point method is used (at 0.2d and 0.8d):

$$v_{mean} = \frac{v_{0.2d} + v_{0.8d}}{2}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is mandatory for KTU exam preparation. Memorize every row.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Continuity ŌĆö discharge = area ├Ś mean velocity | m┬│/s |
| 2 | $Q = b \sum d_i v_i$ | **Mid-section method** | m┬│/s |
| 3 | $Q = \tfrac{b}{2}\left[v_0 d_0 + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} v_i d_i + v_n d_n\right]$ | **Mean-section method** | m┬│/s |
| 4 | $v_{mean} = v_{0.6d}$ | 0.6-depth rule (1-point) | m/s |
| 5 | $v_{mean} = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_{0.2d} + v_{0.8d})$ | 2-point method | m/s |
| 6 | $v_{mean} = \tfrac{1}{3}(v_{0.2d} + v_{0.6d} + v_{0.8d})$ | 3-point method | m/s |
| 7 | $Q = C(H-H_0)^n$ | **Discharge rating curve (power form)** | m┬│/s |
| 8 | $\log Q = \log C + n \log(H-H_0)$ | Linearised rating curve (for regression) | ŌĆō |
| 9 | $Q = \tfrac{q(C_1 - C_2)}{C_2}$ | Dilution method (where $C_2$ = downstream concentration) | m┬│/s |
| 10 | $V = \tfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning's equation (slope-area method) | m/s |
| 11 | $V = C\sqrt{RS}$ | Ch├®zy equation | m/s |
| 12 | $V_{surface} = \tfrac{L}{t}$ | Float method ŌĆö surface velocity | m/s |
| 13 | $V_{mean} \approx 0.85 \cdot V_{surface}$ | Float-to-mean correction (K = 0.80ŌĆō0.90) | m/s |

> [!TIP]
> **Why $H_0$ appears in the rating curve:** A river does not stop flowing the moment the gauge reads zero; zero on the staff gauge is just an arbitrary reference. True zero flow occurs at some datum $H_0$ *below* the gauge zero ŌĆö this must be subtracted to get the "effective" depth driving the flow.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Flood forecasting:** Rating curves allow real-time conversion of river level alerts into discharge warnings for downstream cities (e.g., Kerala's Periyar, Pamba rivers during monsoon).
- **Hydropower:** Turbine intake design and reservoir operation depend on long-term discharge records derived from rating curves.
- **Water resources planning:** Irrigation, drinking-water supply, and environmental flow assessments all rely on the $Q$ŌĆō$H$ relationship.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö Discharge Rating Curve Equation

**Statement to prove:** The dischargeŌĆōstage relationship at a stable gauging section takes the form $Q = C(H - H_0)^n$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the continuity equation.**

For a stable channel of width $B$, hydraulic depth $D$, and mean velocity $V$:

$$Q = A \cdot V = (B \cdot D) \cdot V$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express each variable as a power function of the effective depth.**

For a stable, prismatic channel, geometric similarity suggests that width and depth scale with the effective stage, and velocity scales via a resistance law:

$$B \propto (H - H_0)^{\beta}, \quad D \propto (H - H_0)^{\gamma}, \quad V \propto (H - H_0)^{\delta}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply the power forms.**

Substituting into the continuity equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &\propto (H - H_0)^{\beta} \cdot (H - H_0)^{\gamma} \cdot (H - H_0)^{\delta} \\
  &\propto (H - H_0)^{\beta + \gamma + \delta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Group all constants into a single coefficient.**

Let $C$ absorb the proportionality constants and let $n = \beta + \gamma + \delta$:

$$Q = C(H - H_0)^n \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Linearise for graphical/least-squares determination.**

Taking logarithms (base 10) of both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log Q &= \log\left[C(H - H_0)^n\right] \\
       &= \log C + n \log(H - H_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** = $n$
- **Intercept** = $\log C$
- **Independent variable** = $\log(H - H_0)$
- **Dependent variable** = $\log Q$

Plotting $\log Q$ versus $\log(H - H_0)$ gives a straight line; $C$ and $n$ are obtained by linear regression. Typically for natural channels, $n$ lies between **1.5 and 2.5**, with **n Ōēł 2** being common for wide rectangular sections (Ch├®zy/Manning with wide channel: $Q \propto D^{5/3} \approx D^{1.67}$).

### 3.2 Worked Derivation ŌĆö Mid-Section Method Formula

**Given:** A stream of width $W$ is divided into $n$ equal panels of width $b = W/n$. At the centre of each panel, the depth $d_i$ and the mean vertical velocity $v_i$ are measured.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Discharge through one panel.**

A single panel (width $b$, mean depth $d_i$, mean velocity $v_i$) acts approximately as a small rectangular channel:

$$q_i = (\text{Area of panel}) \times (\text{mean velocity}) = (b \cdot d_i) \cdot v_i$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total discharge = sum of all panels.**

$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b \cdot d_i \cdot v_i$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Since $b$ is constant:**

$$Q = b \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot v_i \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Establishing a Rating Curve

A stream gauging station yields the following concurrent stage ($H$) and discharge ($Q$) measurements:

| Observation | $H$ (m) | $Q$ (m┬│/s) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 1.0 | 5 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 25 |
| 3 | 3.0 | 65 |
| 4 | 4.0 | 130 |

Assume $H_0 = 0$ (gauge zero datum = zero flow datum). Find $C$ and $n$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Form the linearised table.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log Q \;&\text{vs.}\; \log(H-H_0) = \log H \\
\text{Obs 1:} \;& \log H = 0.000,\quad \log Q = \log 5 = 0.699 \\
\text{Obs 2:} \;& \log H = 0.301,\quad \log Q = \log 25 = 1.398 \\
\text{Obs 3:} \;& \log H = 0.477,\quad \log Q = \log 65 = 1.813 \\
\text{Obs 4:} \;& \log H = 0.602,\quad \log Q = \log 130 = 2.114
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply least-squares slope (visual estimate here).**

The line passes through (0, ŌłÆ0.20) approximately with slope:

$$
n \approx \frac{2.114 - 0.699}{0.602 - 0.000} = \frac{1.415}{0.602} \approx 2.35
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Intercept and $C$.**

Using point (0.602, 2.114): $\log C = 2.114 - 2.35 \times 0.602 = 2.114 - 1.415 = 0.699$.

$$C = 10^{0.699} \approx 5.0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final rating curve.**

$$\boxed{Q = 5.0 \, (H - 0)^{2.35} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verification at Obs 2:** $Q = 5.0 \times (2.0)^{2.35} = 5.0 \times 5.27 = 26.4$ m┬│/s Ōēł 25 m┬│/s Ō£ō

### 3.4 Shift in Rating Curve ŌĆö Why Curves Move

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A rating curve is **stable only when the channel cross-section is stable**. Rating curves "shift" because of:
>
> 1. **Scour** (bed erosion during high flows) ŌåÆ larger area for the same stage ŌåÆ curve moves **right and up**.
> 2. **Fill / Silting** (deposition) ŌåÆ smaller area ŌåÆ curve moves **left and down**.
> 3. **Seasonal vegetation** in channel ŌåÆ higher roughness ŌåÆ curve moves **down**.
> 4. **Backwater effects** (downstream obstruction, tributary confluence, tidal effects) ŌåÆ curve temporarily **loops or shifts**.

When shifts occur, a single $Q = C(H-H_0)^n$ is insufficient; **variable shift corrections** or **index-velocity methods** must be employed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Stream Gauging Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select Gauging Site] --> B[Install Staff Gauge / Stage Recorder]
    B --> C[Continuous Stage H Recording]
    C --> D{Field Discharge Measurement}
    D --> E[Area-Velocity Method]
    D --> F[Dilution Method]
    D --> G[Slope-Area Method]
    E --> H[Compute Q for Each Event]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Plot Q vs H Curve]
    I --> J[Fit Q = C * H - H0 ^ n]
    J --> K[Apply Rating Curve to Continuous Stage Record]
    K --> L[Long Term Discharge Hydrograph]
```

### 4.2 Schematic ŌĆö Cross-Section with Mid-Section Method Panels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WS[Water Surface] --> V0[Vertical 0 - Left Bank]
    V0 --> V1[Vertical 1]
    V1 --> V2[Vertical 2]
    V2 --> V3[Vertical 3]
    V3 --> V4[Vertical 4 - Right Bank]
    subgraph Panel_i
        V1 --- V2
    end
    V0 ---|d0, v0| V0L[Depth at V0]
    V1 ---|d1, v1| V1L[Depth at V1]
    V2 ---|d2, v2| V2L[Depth at V2]
    V3 ---|d3, v3| V3L[Depth at V3]
    V4 ---|d4, v4| V4L[Depth at V4]
    BD[Bed of Stream] --- V0
    BD --- V1
    BD --- V2
    BD --- V3
    BD --- V4
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram ŌĆö Rating Curve Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Stage H from Recorder] --> B[Subtract H0 Datum]
    B --> C[Compute Effective Depth H minus H0]
    C --> D[Apply Power Law Q equals C times H minus H0 to the n]
    D --> E[Output Discharge Q in m3 per s]
    F[Periodic Field Calibration via Current Meter] --> G[Update C and n]
    G --> D
    H[Check for Channel Shift] --> I{Shift Detected}
    I -- Yes --> J[Apply Shift Correction]
    J --> D
    I -- No --> D
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram ŌĆö Types of Rating Curves

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Discharge Rating Curves] --> B[Simple Rating Curve]
    A --> C[Compound Rating Curve]
    A --> D[Loop Rating Curve]
    B --> B1[Single Power Law Q equals C times H minus H0 to n]
    C --> C1[Multiple Branches - Main Channel plus Floodplain]
    C --> C2[Different C and n for low flow vs high flow]
    D --> D1[Hysteresis Loop due to Unsteady Flow]
    D --> D2[Rising Limb vs Falling Limb Different Q]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]

Define a **discharge rating curve**. State its mathematical form and explain the significance of the constant $H_0$.

**Model Answer:**

A discharge rating curve is the graphical or analytical relationship between the **stage (water level)** at a gauging station and the corresponding **discharge** at that section. It is expressed as:

$$Q = C(H - H_0)^n$$

where $Q$ = discharge, $H$ = observed gauge reading, $H_0$ = gauge height at zero flow, $C$ and $n$ are station constants.

**Significance of $H_0$:** The reading $H = 0$ on the staff gauge is only an arbitrary reference datum; the river may still have water depth at this reading. The actual effective head driving the flow is $(H - H_0)$. Subtracting $H_0$ makes the curve pass through the true origin and improves the fit of the power law. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

Explain the **mid-section method** of computing stream discharge from velocity and depth observations.

**Model Answer:**

In the mid-section method, the stream cross-section is divided into **n equal vertical strips** of width $b$. At the centre of each strip, the depth $d_i$ and mean vertical velocity $v_i$ are measured. The discharge through each strip is taken as the product of its area $(b \cdot d_i)$ and the central velocity $v_i$, summed over all strips:

$$Q = b \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot v_i$$

This method is widely used because it requires velocity observations only at the **centre of each panel**, simplifying field work. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

#### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 ŌĆö 14 Marks]

**(a)** Derive the equation of a simple discharge rating curve, $Q = C(H - H_0)^n$, starting from the continuity equation. Explain why a logarithmic plot of $Q$ versus $(H - H_0)$ yields a straight line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following stageŌĆōdischarge data were collected at a gauging station. The zero-flow datum is at $H_0 = 0.50$ m. Determine the constants $C$ and $n$ of the rating curve using a graphical / log-log method, and estimate the discharge corresponding to a stage of **3.5 m**. **(7 marks)**

| Stage $H$ (m) | 1.0 | 1.5 | 2.0 | 2.5 | 3.0 |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Discharge $Q$ (m┬│/s) | 4 | 14 | 32 | 60 | 100 |

**Model Solution:**
