<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
Define drought. Distinguish between meteorological drought and hydrological drought.

**Model Answer:**

**Drought:** A sustained, regionally extensive deficiency of precipitation that adversely affects water resources, agriculture, ecosystems, and human society.

| Aspect | Meteorological Drought | Hydrological Drought |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Precipitation deficiency | Deficiency in runoff, reservoir, groundwater |
| **Detection** | Rainfall data (immediate) | Streamflow / lake level (delayed) |
| **Lag** | None | Weeks to months after met. drought |
| **Impact** | Rainfall deficit | Water supply, navigation, hydropower |

**Marking Hint:** Definition (1 mark) + Tabular distinction (2 marks) = 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
Explain the Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) and list any four categories with SPI values.

**Model Answer:**

**SPI** is a drought index developed by McKee et al. (1993) that transforms precipitation data over a chosen time scale (e.g., 3 months, 6 months) into a standard normal distribution.

**Four Categories:**
1. **Near normal:** $-0.99 \leq SPI \leq 0.99$
2. **Moderately dry:** $-1.49 \leq SPI \leq -1.00$
3. **Severely dry:** $-1.99 \leq SPI \leq -1.50$
4. **Extremely dry:** $SPI \leq -2.00$

**Marking Hint:** Definition (1) + 4 categories (2) = 3 marks.

---

### PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the four major types of drought with suitable examples and a neat sketch of the drought propagation cascade.

**Model Solution:**

The four major types of drought recognized by the **National Drought Mitigation Center (NDMC), USA** and the **WMO** are:

**1. Meteorological Drought:**
Defined as a significant departure from the long-term normal precipitation over an extended period. Example: Failure of the Indian Summer Monsoon in 2002 caused a meteorological drought over India.

**2. Agricultural Drought:**
Refers to a situation where soil moisture is insufficient to support crop growth, leading to reduced yields. Depends on crop type, growth stage, and soil characteristics. Example: The 2015ŌĆō2016 drought in Tamil Nadu and Karnataka caused widespread crop failure.

**3. Hydrological Drought:**
Occurs when runoff, streamflow, reservoir storage, and groundwater levels fall below long-term averages. Lag time from meteorological drought can be weeks to months. Example: Reduced water levels in the Mullaperiyar Reservoir during Kerala droughts.

**4. Socioeconomic Drought:**
Arises when the demand for water exceeds supply, causing social and economic disruption. Includes impacts on food security, employment, public health, and industry. Example: 2016 Maharashtra drought where tanker supply became the norm.

**Drought Propagation:** A cascade effect from meteorological ŌåÆ agricultural ŌåÆ hydrological ŌåÆ socioeconomic, with increasing lag and increasing social impact.

**Sketch:** Refer to Mermaid Diagram 1 in Section 4.

**Marking:** 1 mark each type definition + 1 mark example + 1 mark cascade explanation + 2 marks sketch = 7 marks.

---

**(b)** [7 marks] A 12-year annual streamflow record of a river is given below (in m┬│/s):

$Q = \{120, 85, 95, 60, 70, 110, 100, 55, 90, 75, 105, 65\}$

Using run theory, identify the drought events and calculate their magnitudes, intensities, and durations. Also determine the design storage of a reservoir required to sustain the worst drought.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute truncation level (mean).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_0 &= \frac{1}{12}\sum Q_i \\
    &= \frac{120+85+95+60+70+110+100+55+90+75+105+65}{12} \\
    &= \frac{1030}{12} \\
    &= 85.83 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute deficits $d_i = Q_0 - Q_i$ for $Q_i < Q_0$.**

| Year | $Q_i$ (m┬│/s) | $d_i$ (m┬│/s) | Run |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | ŌĆö | Positive |
| 2 | 85 | 0.83 | Negligible (Ōēł0) |
| 3 | 95 | ŌĆö | Positive |
| 4 | 60 | 25.83 | **Run 1** |
| 5 | 70 | 15.83 | Run 1 |
| 6 | 110 | ŌĆö | Positive |
| 7 | 100 | ŌĆö | Positive |
| 8 | 55 | 30.83 | **Run 2** |
| 9 | 90 | ŌĆö | Positive |
| 10 | 75 | 10.83 | **Run 3** |
| 11 | 105 | ŌĆö | Positive |
| 12 | 65 | 20.83 | **Run 4** |

(Year 2 deficit is 0.83 m┬│/s, treated as a small run with D=1, M=0.83.)

**Step 3: Tabulate drought parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Run 1: } &D_1=2, \quad M_1 = 25.83+15.83 = 41.66, \quad I_1 = 20.83 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
\text{Run 2: } &D_2=1, \quad M_2 = 30.83, \quad I_2 = 30.83 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
\text{Run 3: } &D_3=1, \quad M_3 = 10.83, \quad I_3 = 10.83 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
\text{Run 4: } &D_4=1, \quad M_4 = 20.83, \quad I_4 = 20.83 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Identify worst drought and design storage.**

Worst drought = **Run 1** (longest duration with maximum cumulative deficit).

$$
\boxed{V_{design} = M_{max} = 41.66 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \cdot \text{year}}
$$

Converting to volume:
$$
V = 41.66 \times 365 \times 86400 = 1.314 \times 10^9 \text{ m}^3 = 1314 \text{ Mm}^3
$$

**Marking:** Mean (1) + Deficits (2) + Parameters (2) + Worst drought + storage (2) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** [7 marks] Describe the structural and non-structural measures for drought management. Give two examples for each.

**Model Solution:**

**Structural Measures (Engineering Interventions):**

1. **Reservoirs and Dams:** Surface storage for multi-year carryover to bridge drought periods. *Example:* Idukki Dam in Kerala, Bhakra Dam in Punjab.
2. **Check Dams / Percolation Tanks:** Small barriers to enhance groundwater recharge. *Example:* Jalyukt Shivar Abhiyan structures in Maharashtra.
3. **Recharge Wells / Injection Wells:** Artificial recharge of aquifers. *Example:* Check dam-cum-recharge wells in arid Rajasthan.
4. **Desalination Plants:** Convert saline water to fresh water. *Example:* Minjur Plant near Chennai.
5. **Watershed Treatment:** Contour bunding, terracing, afforestation. *Example:* Integrated Watershed Management Programme (IWMP).

**Non-Structural Measures (Policy and Management):**

1. **Drought Codes and Policies:** Early warning and declaration. *Example:* Manual for Drought Management, Ministry of Agriculture, GoI.
2. **Crop Insurance:** Financial protection to farmers. *Example:* Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY).
3. **Water Pricing and Demand Management:** Tariff structures to reduce wastage. *Example:* Tiered water pricing in Bengaluru.
4. **Public Awareness:** Behavioural change campaigns. *Example:* "Jal Shakti Abhiyan" of Government of India.
5. **Crop Planning:** Shift to drought-tolerant varieties. *Example:* Promoting millets in rainfed regions.

**Marking:** Structural: 1+1 marks (definitions + 2 examples) = 2 + 2 = 4 marks; Non-structural: 1+1+1 = 3 marks; Conclusion 1 mark = 7 marks.

---

**(b)** [7 marks] Define Palmer Drought Severity Index (PDSI). Explain its calculation procedure and classify the drought categories.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The **Palmer Drought Severity Index (PDSI)**, developed by **Wayne Palmer in 1965**, is a soil moisture balance-based drought index that incorporates precipitation, temperature (for evapotranspiration), and antecedent soil moisture conditions.

**Calculation Procedure (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1: Compute the climatic water balance.**
$$
P = \overline{P} + \Delta S = ET + R + RO
$$
where $P$ = precipitation, $ET$ = evapotranspiration, $R$ = recharge, $RO$ = runoff, $\Delta S$ = soil moisture change.

**Step 2: Compute the moisture departure (anomaly).**
$$
d = P - \hat{P}
$$
where $\hat{P}$ is the **climatically appropriate precipitation** for the existing conditions.

**Step 3: Compute the Z-index (Standardized Anomaly).**
$$
Z = \frac{d}{\sigma}
$$
where $\sigma$ = standard deviation of $d$ for the location and month.

**Step 4: Apply the recursive PDSI equation.**
$$
X_i = 0.897 X_{i-1} + \frac{Z_i}{3}
$$
where $X_i$ is the PDSI at time $i$.

**PDSI Drought Categories:**

| PDSI Range | Category |
|---|---|
| $\geq 4.00$ | Extremely wet |
| $3.00$ to $3.99$ | Very wet |
| $2.00$ to $2.99$ | Moderately wet |
| $1.00$ to $1.99$ | Slightly wet |
| $0.50$ to $0.99$ | Incipient wet spell |
| $-0.49$ to $0.49$ | Near normal |
| $-0.50$ to $-0.99$ | Incipient drought |
| $-1.00$ to $-1.99$ | Mild drought |
| $-2.00$ to $-2.99$ | Moderate drought |
| $-3.00$ to $-3.99$ | Severe drought |
| $\leq -4.00$ | Extreme drought |

**Marking:** Definition (1) + 4 steps of calculation (4) + Categories (2) = 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Drought Severity with Drought Magnitude.** Severity = M/D, while Magnitude = ╬ŻdßĄó. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Truncation level** is not always the mean ŌĆö it can also be a fixed demand or a percentile. Mention the basis clearly.
> 3. **SPI vs PDSI:** SPI is precipitation-based, PDSI is water-balance-based. Mixing these confusions costs 2 marks in long answers.
> 4. **Reservoir design from drought analysis:** Always specify the **return period** of the design drought. A reservoir sized for a 5-year drought is different from a 50-year drought.
> 5. **Skip the units** and you lose half a mark. Always write $\text{m}^3/\text{s}\cdot\text{year}$ for drought magnitude in streamflow problems.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- Ō£ģ Drought is a **recurrent, regional, prolonged deficiency of precipitation** ŌĆö not a single-day event.
- Ō£ģ Four types: **Meteorological ŌåÆ Agricultural ŌåÆ Hydrological ŌåÆ Socioeconomic**, with **increasing lag** and **increasing social impact**.
- Ō£ģ **Run Theory (Yevjevich)** uses a **truncation level** (usually the mean) to identify negative runs and computes **Duration, Magnitude, Intensity**.
- Ō£ģ Key identity: $\boxed{M = I \times D = S \times D}$
- Ō£ģ **SPI** is purely precipitation-based; **PDSI** uses a water balance; **SPEI** includes evapotranspiration.
- Ō£ģ **Structural measures** = engineering (reservoirs, recharge, desalination). **Non-structural measures** = policy, awareness, demand management.
- Ō£ģ **Reservoir design storage** = maximum historical drought magnitude (or magnitude of design return period).
- Ō£ģ **Severity-Area-Duration (SAD) curves** integrate drought severity across space and time.
- Ō£ģ Drought management has **3 phases**: **Preparedness (before), Mitigation (during), Recovery (after)**.
- Ō£ģ India-specific: **PMFBY, Jal Shakti Abhiyan, MGNREGA-linked watershed works, Drought Manual (GoI 2016)** are favorite exam points.
- Ō£ģ **Return Period** $T = 1/P$ where $P$ is the probability of exceedance of the drought event.
- Ō£ģ Always **state units** (mm, m┬│/s, m┬│/s┬Ęyear, Mm┬│) in numerical problems.
- Ō£ģ Distinguish **drought** (temporary) from **aridity** (permanent climatic state).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geologic Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Geologic Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Geologic Methods** in hydrogeology refer to the systematic set of surface and subsurface investigative techniques used to identify, characterize, and delineate the geometry, lithology, structure, and hydraulic properties of geological formations that govern the occurrence, movement, storage, and yield of groundwater.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, geologic methods are classified into two broad categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification of Geologic Methods**
> 1. **Direct (Surface/Sub-surface) Geologic Methods** ŌĆö Geologic mapping, geomorphological analysis, borehole logging, and stratigraphic correlation.
> 2. **Indirect (Geophysical) Methods** ŌĆö Electrical, seismic, gravity, magnetic, and electromagnetic surveys that infer subsurface geology from physical property contrasts.

The objective is to construct a **Hydrogeological Map** that integrates lithology, structure, aquifer geometry, water table depth, and groundwater quality to support exploration, exploitation, and management of groundwater resources.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "Detective Work" Beneath Our Feet**
>
> Imagine a detective trying to find a hidden underground water tank in a vast estate. The detective cannot dig everywhere (too expensive). Instead, the detective uses:
>
> - **Surface clues** ŌĆö cracks on the road, plant type, soil dampness ŌåÆ *Geologic Mapping*
> - **Knocking on the ground** to hear hollow sounds ŌåÆ *Seismic Methods*
> - **Measuring electrical resistance** of soil (wet soil conducts better) ŌåÆ *Electrical Resistivity*
> - **Drilling selective test holes** to confirm findings ŌåÆ *Borehole Logging*
>
> Geologic methods are exactly this: a layered, evidence-based approach to "see" the invisible aquifer below the surface without (and with) drilling.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The following standard physical properties govern the choice of a geologic method:

- **Electrical Resistivity** of saturated rock/soil (typically **10 ŌĆō 1000 ╬®┬Ęm** for most sedimentary aquifers).
- **Seismic P-wave Velocity** in unconsolidated sediments (Ōēł **300 ŌĆō 2000 m/s**).
- **Density contrast** between aquifer and confining layers (Ōēł **1.8 ŌĆō 2.7 g/cm┬│**).
- **Magnetic susceptibility** contrast (used in basement terrain studies).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 4 places strong emphasis on *electrical resistivity* and *seismic refraction* methods because they are most frequently asked in Part B (14-mark) questions. Mastery of these two is non-negotiable for the ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Tree of Geologic Methods

Geologic methods are operationally divided as follows:

### A. Surface Geologic Methods
- **Geologic Mapping**: Outcrop identification, lithologic logging, structural mapping (folds, faults, joints, lineaments).
- **Geomorphological Analysis**: Landform identification ŌĆö alluvial plains, floodplains, terraces, buried channels, karst features.
- **Lineament Analysis**: Using satellite/Aerial photos to identify fractures, faults, and joints that act as preferential groundwater conduits.
- **Hydrogeological Mapping**: Integration of geology, water table depth, spring locations, well yields, and water quality.

### B. Subsurface Geologic Methods
- **Borehole Drilling & Litholog Preparation**: Direct sampling through rotary, percussion, or auger drilling.
- **Stratigraphic Correlation**: Linking borehole lithologs across an area to construct cross-sections.
- **Well Logging**: Downhole measurement of physical properties (spontaneous potential, resistivity, gamma, neutron, etc.).

### C. Geophysical (Indirect) Methods
- **Electrical Resistivity Method** (most common in Kerala laterite/sedimentary terrain).
- **Seismic Refraction / Reflection Method**.
- **Gravity Method**.
- **Magnetic Method**.
- **Electromagnetic (EM) and Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR)**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Each Method

### 2.2.1 Electrical Resistivity Method (ERM)

- **Underlying Principle:** Electrical current flow in the ground depends on the resistivity contrast between formations. Water-saturated porous rocks conduct better (lower resistivity) than dry crystalline rocks.
- **Why it works for groundwater:** Porosity + salinity of pore fluid ŌåÆ controls bulk resistivity.
- **How it is deployed:** Four electrodes (two current A, B; two potential M, N) inserted into the ground. Current $I$ is injected, potential difference $\Delta V$ is measured.

### 2.2.2 ArchieŌĆÖs Law (Bridge Between Resistivity and Aquifer Parameters)

Archie's Law relates formation resistivity to porosity and water saturation ŌĆö a high-yield concept for the KTU ESE.

$$R_t = a \cdot R_w \cdot \phi^{-m} \cdot S^{-n}$$

where:
- $R_t$ = true formation resistivity (╬®┬Ęm)
- $R_w$ = pore-water resistivity (╬®┬Ęm)
- $\phi$ = porosity (fraction)
- $S$ = water saturation (fraction)
- $a$ = tortuosity factor (Ōēł 0.6 ŌĆō 1.5)
- $m$ = cementation exponent (Ōēł 1.3 ŌĆō 2.5)
- $n$ = saturation exponent (Ōēł 2)

### 2.2.3 Seismic Refraction Method

- **Principle:** Seismic waves travel faster in compact/dense rocks. A critical-angle refraction at layer interfaces produces head waves that can be timed at the surface.
- **Use:** To identify the depth to the water table and bedrock, and to map weathered/fractured zones.

### 2.2.4 Magnetic and Gravity Methods

- **Magnetic:** Used in hard-rock (crystalline basement) terrain to map lineaments, fractures, and intrusive bodies.
- **Gravity:** Used to detect subsurface density variations such as buried channels, intrusive bodies, or karst cavities.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 4. Replace any absolute value notation using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Method** | **Key Equation** | **Variables / Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Apparent Resistivity (Wenner) | $\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$ | $a$ = electrode spacing (m), $\Delta V$ in V, $I$ in A, $\rho_a$ in ╬®┬Ęm | Depth sounding in sedimentary aquifers |
| Apparent Resistivity (Schlumberger) | $\rho_a = \pi \cdot \dfrac{(L/2)^2 - (l/2)^2}{l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$ | $L$ = current electrode separation, $l$ = potential electrode separation (m) | Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES) |
| Formation Factor | $F = \dfrac{R_o}{R_w} = a \cdot \phi^{-m}$ | $R_o$ = 100% saturated rock resistivity | Porosity estimation |
| ArchieŌĆÖs Law | $R_t = a \cdot R_w \cdot \phi^{-m} \cdot S^{-n}$ | $R_t, R_w$ in ╬®┬Ęm, $\phi, S$ dimensionless | Aquifer characterization |
| Seismic Refraction (Two-layer) | $t = \dfrac{x}{V_1} + \dfrac{2h\cos\theta_c}{V_1}$ | $t$ = travel time, $V_1$ = upper layer velocity, $h$ = layer depth, $\theta_c$ = critical angle | Depth to bedrock |
| Intercept Time | $t_i = \dfrac{2h\cos\theta_c}{V_1}$ | $t_i$ in seconds | Computes $h$ |
| Gravity Anomaly (Bouguer) | $\Delta g = 2\pi G \rho h$ | $G$ = gravitational constant, $\rho$ = density contrast, $h$ = thickness | Buried structure detection |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Engineers Care**
> - In **KeralaŌĆÖs laterite terrain**, ERM is the primary tool for identifying weathered-fractured aquifers.
> - In **alluvial Indo-Gangetic plains**, seismic refraction maps the thickness of the alluvium over bedrock.
> - In **coastal Kerala**, geologic mapping combined with resistivity identifies saltwater-freshwater interfaces.
> - In **siting borewells** for municipal water supply or irrigation, geologic methods reduce dry-well failures from typical **40% to less than 10%**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Apparent Resistivity for the Wenner Array

### Given Setup
- Four collinear, equally-spaced electrodes on the ground surface.
- Outer two (A, B) inject current $I$.
- Inner two (M, N) measure potential difference $\Delta V$.
- Electrode spacing = $a$.

### Step 1: Potential at Electrode M due to current electrode A

The electric potential at a distance $r$ from a point current source on the surface of a homogeneous, isotropic half-space is:

$$V = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$$

Therefore, the contribution from current electrode A (located at distance $a$ from M) is:

$$V_M^{(A)} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a}$$

### Step 2: Potential at M due to current electrode B (located at distance $2a$ from M, on the opposite side)

$$V_M^{(B)} = -\dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi (2a)} = -\dfrac{\rho I}{4\pi a}$$

### Step 3: Net potential at M

$$V_M = V_M^{(A)} + V_M^{(B)} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a} - \dfrac{\rho I}{4\pi a} = \dfrac{\rho I}{4\pi a}$$

### Step 4: Similarly, potential at N

By symmetry, N is at distance $a$ from B and $2a$ from A:

$$V_N = -\dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a} + \dfrac{\rho I}{4\pi a} = -\dfrac{\rho I}{4\pi a}$$

### Step 5: Potential difference between M and N

$$\Delta V = V_M - V_N = \dfrac{\rho I}{4\pi a} - \left(-\dfrac{\rho I}{4\pi a}\right) = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a}$$

### Step 6: Rearranging for apparent resistivity

$$\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

This is the standard Wenner formula and is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on ŌĆ£ApparentŌĆØ vs ŌĆ£TrueŌĆØ Resistivity**
> In a layered earth (which is the real case), the measured $\rho_a$ is an integrated, weighted average of the true resistivities of all layers penetrated by the current. The true layered resistivity is then extracted by **inversion** of the field curve.

## 3.2 Derivation: Depth to Interface in Two-Layer Seismic Refraction

### Given Setup
- Two horizontal layers.
- Upper layer P-wave velocity = $V_1$, lower layer velocity = $V_2$ (with $V_2 > V_1$).
- Thickness of upper layer = $h$.

### Step 1: Critical Angle (SnellŌĆÖs Law at Interface)

$$\sin \theta_c = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$$

$$\cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_c} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^2}$$

### Step 2: Critical Distance

The refracted wave from the interface first arrives at a horizontal distance:

$$x_c = 2h \tan \theta_c$$

### Step 3: Total Travel Time of the Refracted Wave to a Geophone at Offset $x > x_c$

$$t = \underbrace{\dfrac{x}{V_2}}_{\text{along interface}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{2h \cos \theta_c}{V_1}}_{\text{down and up}}$$

### Step 4: Time-Intercept ($t_i$)

Setting $x = 0$ in the refracted-wave equation gives the time intercept (an extrapolated quantity):

$$t_i = \dfrac{2h \cos \theta_c}{V_1}$$

### Step 5: Solve for $h$ (Depth to Interface)

$$h = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot t_i}{2 \cos \theta_c}$$

Substituting $\cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - (V_1/V_2)^2}$:

$$h = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot t_i}{2 \sqrt{1 - (V_1/V_2)^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Numerical problems on seismic refraction typically give you $V_1, V_2,$ and the time-intercept $t_i$. Substitute directly into the above equation. Always compute $\theta_c$ first for clarity in the answer script.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A Wenner array survey in a coastal alluvial aquifer used an electrode spacing of $a = 10$ m. The injected current was $I = 200$ mA and the measured potential difference was $\Delta V = 47.7$ mV. Determine the apparent resistivity.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Convert units.
$I = 200 \times 10^{-3}$ A
$\Delta V = 47.7 \times 10^{-3}$ V

**Step 2:** Apply the Wenner formula.

$$\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$\rho_a = 2\pi (10) \cdot \dfrac{47.7 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\rho_a = 62.832 \cdot 0.2385$$

$$\rho_a \approx 14.99 \ \Omega \cdot \text{m} \approx 15 \ \Omega \cdot \text{m}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

A resistivity of **15 ╬®┬Ęm** suggests a **saturated, clayey-sandy formation**, typical of coastal aquifers. Such low values (under 20 ╬®┬Ęm) often correlate with brackish-water influence in KeralaŌĆÖs coastal belt ŌĆö a high-yield KTU insight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Define apparent resistivity and explain why we measure ŌĆ£apparentŌĆØ rather than ŌĆ£trueŌĆØ resistivity in the field.**

**Model Answer:**
Apparent resistivity is the resistivity of a hypothetical homogeneous, isotropic half-space that would produce the same voltage-to-current ratio as measured in the field. The formula for the Wenner array is:

$$\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

We measure *apparent* resistivity because the subsurface is heterogeneous and layered; the current flows through multiple formations simultaneously. Hence the measured value is an *integrated response*, not a single layerŌĆÖs true resistivity. True layer resistivities are obtained later by **inversion** of the field VES curve.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**List any four geologic indicators of groundwater occurrence.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Presence of **alluvial deposits, floodplains, and river terraces** ŌĆö high porosity and recharge potential.
2. **Lineaments, fractures, and joints** in hard rock ŌĆö act as conduits.
3. **Laterite and weathered residuum** in humid tropical regions like Kerala ŌĆö porous and stores water.
4. **Karst features (sinkholes, springs)** in limestone terrain ŌĆö direct evidence of dissolution channels holding water.
5. **Spring locations and seepage zones** ŌĆö confirm shallow water table.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two-part questions. Each part carries 7 marks. Choice is given between two full 14-mark questions (A or B).

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **Electrical Resistivity Method** for groundwater exploration. With a neat sketch, describe the **Wenner array** and derive the formula for apparent resistivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Schlumberger VES was conducted in a laterite terrain. Current electrode separation $L/2 = 50$ m, potential electrode separation $l/2 = 2.5$ m, current $I = 250$ mA, and measured voltage $\Delta V = 0.318$ V. Compute the apparent resistivity. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

1. **Principle** (1 mark): The resistivity of a rock/soil formation depends on porosity, pore-fluid salinity, and saturation. Aquifer materials saturated with water show distinctly lower resistivity than dry, dense crystalline rocks.

2. **Wenner Array** (2 marks): Four collinear, equally-spaced electrodes A, M, N, B on the surface. Outer two are current electrodes; inner two are potential electrodes. Spacing between adjacent electrodes = $a$.

3. **Derivation** (3 marks): As shown in Section 3.1 of these notes, the apparent resistivity is:

$$\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

4. **Procedure** (1 mark): Field measurement involves progressively increasing $a$ to obtain a sounding curve (VES) of $\rho_a$ vs $a/2$, which is then interpreted by curve matching to infer layer resistivities and thicknesses.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Schlumberger formula:**

$$\rho_a = \pi \cdot \dfrac{(L/2)^2 - (l/2)^2}{l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute values:**

$L/2 = 50$ m ŌåÆ $(L/2)^2 = 2500$
$l/2 = 2.5$ m ŌåÆ $l = 5$ m ŌåÆ $(l/2)^2 = 6.25$
$I = 250 \times 10^{-3}$ A
$\Delta V = 0.318$ V

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute:**

$$\rho_a = \pi \cdot \dfrac{2500 - 6.25}{5} \cdot \dfrac{0.318}{0.250}$$

$$\rho_a = \pi \cdot 498.75 \cdot 1.272$$

$$\rho_a \approx 3.1416 \cdot 634.41$$

$$\rho_a \approx 1992.7 \ \Omega \cdot \text{m}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Interpretation:** A resistivity near **2000 ╬®┬Ęm** indicates a **massive, dry crystalline bedrock** with no significant weathered/fractured aquifer. The site is **unsuitable** for productive borewell drilling. A better site would show values below ~100 ╬®┬Ęm in deeper VES levels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to convert $I$ and $\Delta V$ to consistent units (A and V) and lose 1ŌĆō2 marks. Also, do NOT confuse the Wenner and Schlumberger formulas; mis-substitution of $a$ vs $L$ and $l$ leads to wrong answers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **Seismic Refraction Method** for groundwater exploration. Derive the equation for depth to a refracting interface in a two-layer case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a two-layer seismic refraction survey, the velocity of the upper layer is $V_1 = 800$ m/s and the lower layer is $V_2 = 2000$ m/s. The time-intercept of the refracted wave is $t_i = 0.04$ s. Determine the depth to the interface. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

1. **Principle** (1 mark): Seismic waves refract at the interface between two layers, with the refracted head wave traveling along the top of the faster lower layer.

2. **Field setup** (2 marks): A source generates a sharp impulse; multiple geophones record arrival times at various offsets. A *time-distance* graph is plotted.

3. **Derivation** (4 marks): As shown in Section 3.2:

$$h = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot t_i}{2 \cos \theta_c}, \quad \text{where} \quad \cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^2}$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $\sin \theta_c$ and $\cos \theta_c$:**

$$\sin \theta_c = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{800}{2000} = 0.4$$

$$\cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - 0.16} = \sqrt{0.84} \approx 0.9165$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the depth formula:**

$$h = \dfrac{800 \times 0.04}{2 \times 0.9165}$$

$$h = \dfrac{32}{1.833}$$

$$h \approx 17.46 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Interpretation:** The bedrock/water-bearing interface lies at a depth of approximately **17.5 m**, suitable for shallow dug-cum-bored wells in this terrain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to use $h = V_1 \cdot t_i / 2$ (forgetting the $\cos \theta_c$ term). This is wrong and costs full marks. Always show the calculation of $\theta_c$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE ŌĆö Module 4: Geologic Methods**

- Geologic methods are **direct** (mapping, drilling) and **indirect** (geophysical).
- **Electrical Resistivity** is the most-used method; memorize both **Wenner** and **Schlumberger** formulas.
- **Apparent resistivity** is field-measured; **true resistivity** requires inversion of the VES curve.
- **ArchieŌĆÖs Law** links formation resistivity, porosity, and water saturation ŌĆö a must-know for hydrogeology problems.
- **Seismic refraction** gives depth to a faster refracting layer using:
  $$h = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot t_i}{2 \cos \theta_c}$$
- **Geologic indicators** of groundwater: alluvium, lineaments, weathered rock, karst, springs, and vegetation anomalies.
- **Kerala-specific insight:** Laterite and weathered crystalline aquifers are best identified using **electrical resistivity** (low values, 20ŌĆō100 ╬®┬Ęm) and **lineament analysis** on satellite imagery.
- Always carry out **multiple methods** (e.g., resistivity + geologic mapping) for site confirmation.
- Remember unit conversions: **$I$ in A**, **$\Delta V$ in V**, **lengths in m**, **resistivity in ╬®┬Ęm**.
- For 14-mark questions, expect either a derivation (Wenner, Archie, or seismic depth) or a numerical problem.
- A **VES curve** is $\rho_a$ vs half-current-electrode-separation on a log-log plot; curve-type matching reveals aquifer geometry.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Geophysical Exploration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 ŌĆö GEOPHYSICAL EXPLORATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Geophysical Exploration?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geophysical Exploration** is the systematic application of quantitative physical methods (electrical, seismic, magnetic, gravitational, and radiometric) to investigate the **subsurface geological conditions** without physically drilling or excavating. In groundwater hydrology, it is used to delineate **aquifer geometry, depth, thickness, lateral extent, and quality** before committing to expensive borehole drilling.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The course *PBAGT304* emphasizes (a) **Surface Geophysical Methods** ŌĆö primarily the **Electrical Resistivity Method** and the **Seismic Refraction Method**, and (b) an introduction to **Borehole (Subsurface) Geophysical Methods** used in well logging.

### 1.2 Classification of Geophysical Methods

Geophysical methods used in groundwater exploration fall into two broad families:

| Family | Where Measurements are Made | Typical Use in KTU Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Geophysical Methods** | At ground level (no borehole needed) | Reconnaissance ŌĆö locating aquifers, fractures, basement depth |
| **Subsurface (Borehole) Methods** | Inside a drilled well | Refining aquifer parameters, water quality indicators, casing design |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Doctor's Stethoscope"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a geophysicist as a **doctor** and the Earth as the **patient**.
> The doctor does not cut the patient open (drill) on the first visit. Instead, she uses **non-invasive tools** ŌĆö a stethoscope, an X-ray, an MRI scan ŌĆö to "see" inside.
> - The **stethoscope** = **Seismic method** (listens to vibrations travelling through the body)
> - The **X-ray** = **Electrical Resistivity method** (sends a current through the body and reads the "shadow")
> - The **MRI / ultrasound** = **Electromagnetic & Magnetic methods** (senses different tissue responses)
> Each tool reveals a different "tissue contrast" (rock contrast) underground.

### 1.4 Physical Properties Exploited

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every geophysical method relies on the **contrast in some physical property** between the target (aquifer) and surrounding rock. The standard property mapping for KTU is:

| Physical Property | Method Using It | Unit (SI) | Rock Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Resistivity (Žü)** | Electrical Resistivity | Ohm-metre (╬®┬Ęm) | Clay ~ 1ŌĆō100; Sand aquifer ~ 30ŌĆō500; Massive granite > 1000 |
| **Seismic Velocity (V)** | Seismic Refraction / Reflection | m/s | Sand ~ 300ŌĆō800; Limestone ~ 2500ŌĆō6000; Granite ~ 4000ŌĆō6000 |
| **Density (Žā)** | Gravity | kg/m┬│ | Sand ~ 1800; Basement ~ 2700ŌĆō3100 |
| **Magnetic Susceptibility (Žć)** | Magnetic | dimensionless | Basement rocks show high contrast |
| **Natural Gamma (╬│)** | Gamma-ray logging | API units | Shale high; Sandstone/clean aquifer low |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ELECTRICAL RESISTIVITY METHOD (Most Important for KTU)

### 2.1.1 Governing Principle

When two current electrodes (**CŌéü, CŌéé**) are driven into the ground and a known current **I** is passed, an electric field spreads through the subsurface. Two potential electrodes (**PŌéü, PŌéé**) measure the resulting potential difference **╬öV**.

> [!NOTE]
> The Earth's response is quantified by its **resistivity Žü (Ohm-metre)**. Because the ground is **inhomogeneous and anisotropic**, the value computed from a surface measurement is an integrated response ŌĆö called the **Apparent Resistivity (ŽüŌéÉ)**.

### 2.1.2 The General Apparent Resistivity Formula

For *any* four-electrode collinear array with the geometry shown:

$$\rho_{a} \;=\; K \,\cdot\, \frac{\Delta V}{I}$$

where **K** is the **Geometric Factor (Array Constant)** that depends purely on the electrode spacings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of electrode array (Wenner, Schlumberger, Dipole-Dipole) changes only the value of **K**. The physics ŌĆö Ohm's law in a heterogeneous half-space ŌĆö is the same.

### 2.1.3 KTU Array Configurations & Their Geometric Factors

| Array Name | Electrode Layout (Left ŌåÆ Right) | Geometric Factor **K** | Penetration Depth (rule of thumb) | Best For (KTU context) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wenner** | CŌéü PŌéü PŌéé CŌéé (equal spacing **a**) | $K = 2\pi a$ | $\approx a$ | Vertical sounding, layered terrain, quick reconnaissance |
| **Schlumberger** | CŌéü PŌéü PŌéé CŌéé (with C-spacing $2L$ and P-spacing $2l$, $L \gg l$) | $K = \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\pi L^{2}}{l}$ | $\approx L$ | Deep sounding, sharper vertical resolution |
| **Dipole-Dipole** | CŌéüCŌéé ŌĆö gap ŌĆö PŌéüPŌéé (spacing **a**, separation **n┬Ęa**) | $K = \pi n (n+1)(n+2) a$ | $\approx n a$ | Lateral profiling, fracture / dyke mapping |
| **Pole-Dipole** | One C at infinity, PŌéüPŌéé local | $K = 2\pi a$ | $\approx 2a$ | Strong lateral variations |

> [!NOTE]
> For all arrays, the **rule of penetration Ōēł ┬Į to Ōģō of the largest current-electrode separation** is the KTU-acceptable thumb rule.

### 2.1.4 Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES) vs Horizontal Profiling

- **VES (Depth Sounding):** The centre of the array is fixed; the current-electrode spacing is **progressively increased**. The data obtained is a plot of **ŽüŌéÉ vs half-current-spacing (AB/2)** ŌĆö this reveals **vertical layering** (number of aquifers, their depths and thicknesses).
- **Horizontal Profiling:** The array is moved laterally at a **fixed spacing** along a traverse. The data obtained is **ŽüŌéÉ vs horizontal distance** ŌĆö this reveals **lateral variations** such as fractures, dykes, and buried channels.

### 2.1.5 Master KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Resistivity Method

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apparent Resistivity (general) | $\rho_{a} = K \,\dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$ | ╬®┬Ęm |
| 2 | Geometric Factor (Wenner) | $K_{W} = 2\pi a$ | m |
| 3 | Geometric Factor (Schlumberger) | $K_{S} = \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l}$ | m |
| 4 | Geometric Factor (Dipole-Dipole) | $K_{DD} = \pi n(n+1)(n+2) a$ | m |
| 5 | True Resistivity (homogeneous case) | $\rho = \rho_{a}$ | ╬®┬Ęm |
| 6 | Approx. depth of investigation | $d \approx 0.5 \,(\text{AB}/2)$ | m |
| 7 | Layer parameters from VES curve | $\rho_{1}, h_{1}, \rho_{2}, h_{2}, \ldots$ | ╬®┬Ęm, m |
| 8 | Transverse resistance (T) | $T = \sum \rho_{i} h_{i}$ | ╬®┬Ęm┬▓ |
| 9 | Longitudinal conductance (S) | $S = \sum h_{i}/\rho_{i}$ | mho (╬®Ōü╗┬╣) |
| 10 | Dar-Zarrouk variable (Total) | $T \cdot S$ | ŌĆö |

### 2.1.6 Engineering Utility in Groundwater

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world use:** Before drilling an irrigation borewell in a Kerala laterite terrain, a VES survey with the Schlumberger array is conducted. The resulting **ŽüŌéÉ curve** is interpreted (manually with master curves, or using software) to find:
> - **Layer 1 (Top soil / laterite)** ŌĆö Žü Ōēł 50ŌĆō150 ╬®┬Ęm, h Ōēł 2ŌĆō5 m
> - **Layer 2 (Weathered/fractured aquifer)** ŌĆö Žü Ōēł 30ŌĆō80 ╬®┬Ęm, h Ōēł 10ŌĆō40 m  ŌåÉ **target zone**
> - **Layer 3 (Massive basement / compact rock)** ŌĆö Žü > 500 ╬®┬Ęm
> The driller then sets the borehole depth inside Layer 2 to maximize sustainable yield.

---

## 2.2 SEISMIC REFRACTION METHOD

### 2.2.1 Governing Principle

A seismic wave is generated at the surface (by a hammer strike or small explosive charge). The wave travels outward as a **direct wave** through the top layer and as a **refracted (head wave)** along the top of a deeper, faster layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sneider's Rule (KTU Exam Favourite):** Seismic refraction succeeds only when the **deeper layer is FASTER (higher velocity)** than the overlying layer. It cannot detect a low-velocity layer sandwiched between two faster layers ŌĆö a **"hidden layer" problem**.

### 2.2.2 Wave Types Encountered in KTU Problems

| Wave | Symbol | Particle Motion | Medium Requirement | Velocity Range (m/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P-wave (Primary / Compressional)** | Vp | Parallel to propagation | Solid, liquid, gas | 300 ŌĆō 6000 |
| **S-wave (Secondary / Shear)** | Vs | Perpendicular to propagation | Solid only | Vs < Vp |

> [!NOTE]
> The **critical angle of incidence** for total refraction along the interface is given by Snell's law:
>
> $$\sin \theta_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}, \qquad V_{2} > V_{1}$$
>
> This is the angle at which the refracted wave travels **along** the top of Layer 2 and re-emerges as a **head wave** at the surface.

### 2.2.3 Travel-Time Equations for the Two-Layer Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **single horizontal interface** at depth **h**, with the source and a geophone at horizontal separation **x** (both on the surface), the travel times are:

**Direct wave** (travels entirely in Layer 1):

$$t_{d} \;=\; \dfrac{x}{V_{1}}$$

**Refracted (head) wave** (goes down at ╬Ėc, travels along top of Layer 2, comes up at ╬Ėc):

$$t_{r} \;=\; \dfrac{x}{V_{2}} \;+\; T_{i}$$

where the **Intercept Time** is:

$$T_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{2h \cos \theta_{c}}{V_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{2h \sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}{V_{1} V_{2}}$$

**Critical (crossover) distance** $x_{c}$ ŌĆö the point where the two travel-time curves intersect (the refracted wave becomes the first arrival):

$$x_{c} \;=\; 2h \,\tan \theta_{c} \;=\; 2h \,\dfrac{V_{1}}{\sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Depth-to-refractor** solved from the intercept time ŌĆö the standard KTU working formula:
>
> $$h \;=\; \dfrac{T_{i} \, V_{1} V_{2}}{2 \sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}$$

### 2.2.4 Generalised Master Formula Sheet ŌĆö Seismic Method

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct wave time | $t_{d} = x / V_{1}$ | seconds |
| 2 | Refracted wave time | $t_{r} = x / V_{2} + T_{i}$ | seconds |
| 3 | Slope ŌåÆ Layer velocity | $V = 1 / (\text{slope of time-distance graph})$ | m/s |
| 4 | Intercept time (2-layer) | $T_{i} = t_{r} - x/V_{2}$ (from graph) | seconds |
| 5 | Critical angle | $\sin \theta_{c} = V_{1} / V_{2}$ | radians |
| 6 | Depth (from intercept time) | $h = T_{i} V_{1} V_{2} \,/\, 2\sqrt{V_{2}^{2}-V_{1}^{2}}$ | metres |
| 7 | Critical (crossover) distance | $x_{c} = 2h \tan \theta_{c}$ | metres |
| 8 | Depth (from crossover dist.) | $h = \dfrac{x_{c}}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{2} - V_{1}}{V_{2} + V_{1}}}$ | metres |
| 9 | Multi-layer (n-layer) intercept | $T_{i,n} = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \dfrac{2 h_{k} \cos \theta_{kn}}{V_{k}}$ | seconds |
| 10 | Average velocity (multi-layer) | $\bar{V}_{n} = \dfrac{\sum h_{k}}{\sum (h_{k}/V_{k})}$ | m/s |

### 2.2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical deployment:** The seismic refraction method is used for:
> 1. **Depth-to-bedrock studies** (e.g., dam foundation, tunnel alignment, hard-rock aquifer base).
> 2. **Mapping buried channels** and **paleo-valleys** in alluvial groundwater exploration.
> 3. **Elastic moduli of rock masses** through the Vp / Vs relationship.
> 4. **Limestone cavity detection** (cavities create anomalously low Vp zones).

---

## 2.3 OTHER METHODS (Conceptual Coverage for KTU)

### 2.3.1 Gravity Method

- Measures minute variations in the **gravitational field** caused by subsurface density contrasts.
- Instrument: **Gravimeter / LaCosteŌĆōRomberg**.
- Useful for: **Basement topography** mapping, large basin studies, **salt domes**, **cavities**.
- Standard gravity on Earth: $g \approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$; the field variations measured are of the order of **0.1 mGal** ($1 \text{ mGal} = 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}^{2}$).

### 2.3.2 Magnetic Method

- Detects contrasts in **magnetic susceptibility**.
- Instrument: **Proton-precession or fluxgate magnetometer**.
- Useful for: locating **igneous intrusions, dykes, and the basement surface** (basement rocks contain magnetite).

### 2.3.3 Electromagnetic (EM) Methods

- Use **time-varying magnetic fields** to induce currents in the ground.
- Useful for: **rapid profiling** of conductive zones (e.g., clay-filled fractures, contamination plumes).

### 2.3.4 Borehole (Well-Logging) Methods

After a well is drilled, a probe is lowered to log the formations in situ:

| Log Type | Measures | Aquifer Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| **Spontaneous Potential (SP)** | Natural electrical potential | Permeable beds (sand) vs shale |
| **Resistivity Log** | Žü of formations | Distinguishes fresh-water-bearing sand from clay |
| **Gamma (Natural Gamma)** | Natural radioactivity | Shale (high ╬│) vs clean sand (low ╬│) |
| **Neutron Log** | Hydrogen content (water) | Porosity of aquifer |
| **Density Log** | Bulk density | Porosity via $\phi = (\rho_{ma}-\rho_{b})/(\rho_{ma}-\rho_{f})$ |
| **Sonic Log** | Interval transit time ╬öt | Porosity via **Wyllie Time-Average** |
| **Caliper Log** | Borehole diameter | Cavity detection, casing design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wyllie Time-Average Equation** (KTU high-yield):
>
> $$\Delta t \;=\; \phi \cdot \Delta t_{f} \;+\; (1-\phi) \cdot \Delta t_{ma}$$
>
> Solving for porosity:
>
> $$\phi \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta t - \Delta t_{ma}}{\Delta t_{f} - \Delta t_{ma}}$$

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Derivations Aligned to KTU Valuation Style.** Each step is shown explicitly so the student can write it as a model answer in the ESE.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 ŌĆö Geometric Factor for the Wenner Array

### Setup

Four collinear electrodes on a homogeneous, isotropic half-space of resistivity Žü. From left to right: CŌéü, PŌéü, PŌéé, CŌéé, with **equal spacing a** between successive electrodes. Total spread = **3a**.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Potential at any point from a single current electrode

For a point current source of strength I embedded at the surface of a homogeneous half-space (the image of a buried electrode effectively doubles the field), the potential at radial distance r is:

$$V(r) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi r}$$

This is the **fundamental point-source formula** for current flow into a uniform half-space. It comes from Ohm's law in cylindrical coordinates: current density $J = I/(2\pi r^{2})$ and field $E = \rho J = \rho I/(2\pi r^{2})$, so $V = \int E\, dr = \rho I/(2\pi r)$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Superposition for CŌéü (current +I) and CŌéé (current ŌłÆI)

The total potential at any point P on the surface is the sum of contributions from CŌéü and CŌéé.

Let the distances from P to CŌéü and CŌéé be $r_{C1}$ and $r_{C2}$:

$$V(P) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{r_{C1}} - \dfrac{1}{r_{C2}} \right)$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the potential at PŌéü and at PŌéé separately

For the **Wenner array**, with the origin placed at the centre of the array, the positions are:

- $C_{1}$ is at $x = -3a/2$
- $P_{1}$ is at $x = -a/2$
- $P_{2}$ is at $x = +a/2$
- $C_{2}$ is at $x = +3a/2$

**Potential at PŌéü** (distance to CŌéü = a, distance to CŌéé = 2a):

$$V(P_{1}) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{a} - \dfrac{1}{2a} \right) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2a}$$

**Potential at PŌéé** (distance to CŌéü = 2a, distance to CŌéé = a):

$$V(P_{2}) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{2a} - \dfrac{1}{a} \right) \;=\; -\dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2a}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the measured potential difference

$$\Delta V \;=\; V(P_{1}) - V(P_{2}) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2a} - \left(-\dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2a}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi a}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for resistivity

Rearranging:

$$\rho \;=\; \dfrac{2 \pi a \cdot \Delta V}{I}$$

Comparing with the general form $\rho_{a} = K \cdot \Delta V / I$:

$$\boxed{\,K_{\text{Wenner}} \;=\; 2 \pi a\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** The factor 2ŽĆ comes from the half-space geometry; if the medium were a full-space (electrode at the centre of an infinite rock), the factor would be 4ŽĆ.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 ŌĆö Geometric Factor for the Schlumberger Array

### Setup

CŌéü and CŌéé are current electrodes with half-spacing **L** (so AB = 2L). PŌéü and PŌéé are potential electrodes with half-spacing **l** (so MN = 2l). The condition **L >> l** keeps the measured ╬öV a sharp indicator of the centre.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Use the same point-source formula

For a point P on the surface at distances $r_{C1}$ and $r_{C2}$ from the current electrodes:

$$V(P) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{r_{C1}} - \dfrac{1}{r_{C2}} \right)$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate at PŌéü (distance to CŌéü = LŌłÆl, to CŌéé = L+l)

$$V(P_{1}) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{L-l} - \dfrac{1}{L+l} \right) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2} - l^{2}}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate at PŌéé (distance to CŌéü = L+l, to CŌéé = LŌłÆl)

$$V(P_{2}) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{L+l} - \dfrac{1}{L-l} \right) \;=\; -\dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2} - l^{2}}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Potential difference

$$\Delta V \;=\; V(P_{1}) - V(P_{2}) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{4l}{L^{2} - l^{2}}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for resistivity

$$\rho \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,K_{\text{Schlumberger}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When **L >> l**, the standard KTU approximation $K_{S} \approx \pi L^{2} / l$ is acceptable. Always quote the **exact form** in derivations and the approximate form in numerical substitution.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 ŌĆö Depth to Refractor from Seismic Refraction Data

### Setup

A two-layer model: Layer 1 (thickness h, velocity VŌéü) overlying Layer 2 (velocity VŌéé, with VŌéé > VŌéü). A shot is fired at the source point S; a geophone G is at horizontal distance **x** on the surface.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Geometry of the head-wave path

The ray from S travels **downward at the critical angle ╬Ėc** through Layer 1, runs along the **top of Layer 2** from the refraction point to the emergence point, then travels **upward at ╬Ėc** to G.

By Snell's law at the interface:

$$\sin \theta_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}, \qquad \cos \theta_{c} \;=\; \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{V_{1}^{2}}{V_{2}^{2}}} \;=\; \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}{V_{2}}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Total travel time along the refracted path

$$t_{r} \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{h}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c}}}_{\text{down-leg}} \;+\; \underbrace{\dfrac{x - 2h \tan \theta_{c}}{V_{2}}}_{\text{horizontal run}} \;+\; \underbrace{\dfrac{h}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c}}}_{\text{up-leg}}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify

Group the two identical vertical legs:

$$t_{r} \;=\; \dfrac{2h}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c}} \;+\; \dfrac{x - 2h \tan \theta_{c}}{V_{2}}$$

$$t_{r} \;=\; \dfrac{x}{V_{2}} \;+\; \dfrac{2h}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c}} \;-\; \dfrac{2h \tan \theta_{c}}{V_{2}}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Combine the constant terms to form the intercept time

$$T_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{2h}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c}} \;-\; \dfrac{2h \tan \theta_{c}}{V_{2}}$$

$$T_{i} \;=\; 2h \left( \dfrac{1}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c}} - \dfrac{\sin \theta_{c}}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c}} \right) \;=\; 2h \cdot \dfrac{1 - \sin \theta_{c}}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1 + \sin \theta_{c})$:

$$T_{i} \;=\; 2h \cdot \dfrac{(1 - \sin \theta_{c})(1 + \sin \theta_{c})}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c} (1 + \sin \theta_{c})} \;=\; 2h \cdot \dfrac{\cos^{2} \theta_{c}}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c} (1 + \sin \theta_{c})}$$

$$T_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{2h \cos \theta_{c}}{V_{1} (1 + \sin \theta_{c})}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Use the more standard form

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1 - \sin \theta_{c})$ and using $\cos^{2} = 1 - \sin^{2}$:

$$T_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{2h \cos \theta_{c} (1 - \sin \theta_{c})}{V_{1} (1 - \sin^{2} \theta_{c})} \;=\; \dfrac{2h \cos \theta_{c} (1 - \sin \theta_{c})}{V_{1} \cos^{2} \theta_{c}}$$

$$T_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{2h (1 - \sin \theta_{c})}{V_{1} \cos \theta_{c}}$$

Re-expressing with VŌéü and VŌéé only (using $\sin \theta_{c} = V_{1}/V_{2}$ and $\cos \theta_{c} = \sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}} \,/\, V_{2}$):

$$T_{i} \;=\; 2h \,\dfrac{1 - V_{1}/V_{2}}{V_{1} \cdot \sqrt{V_{2}^{2}-V_{1}^{2}}/V_{2}} \;=\; 2h \,\dfrac{V_{2} - V_{1}}{V_{1} \sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}$$

Rearranging for **h**:

$$\boxed{\,h \;=\; \dfrac{T_{i} \, V_{1} \sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}{2 (V_{2} - V_{1})}\,} \;\; \equiv \;\; \dfrac{T_{i} V_{1} V_{2}}{2 \sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The two boxed forms are algebraically equivalent.** The KTU board generally accepts either. The second form $h = T_{i} V_{1} V_{2} / (2 \sqrt{V_{2}^{2}-V_{1}^{2}})$ is the one most often printed in standard texts, but the first form is the "textbook-clean" simplification derived here.

---

## 3.4 NUMERICAL EXAMPLE (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> A Schlumberger VES is conducted with half-current spacing $L = 50$ m and half-potential spacing $l = 2$ m. The measured $\Delta V = 120$ mV for $I = 200$ mA. Compute (a) the apparent resistivity, (b) the depth of investigation, and (c) the value of geometric factor if the same AB/2 were achieved with a Wenner array (equal spacing a).

### Part (a) ŌĆö Apparent Resistivity

**Geometric factor (Schlumberger):**

$$K_{S} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (50^{2} - 2^{2})}{2 \times 2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (2500 - 4)}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{2496 \pi}{4} \;=\; 624 \pi \;\approx\; 1960.4 \text{ m}$$

**Apparent resistivity:**

$$\rho_{a} \;=\; K_{S} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I} \;=\; 1960.4 \times \dfrac{0.120}{0.200} \;=\; 1960.4 \times 0.6 \;=\; 1176.2 \text{ ╬®┬Ęm}$$

### Part (b) ŌĆö Depth of Investigation (rule of thumb)

$$d \;\approx\; 0.5 \times (AB/2) \;=\; 0.5 \times 50 \;=\; 25 \text{ m}$$

### Part (c) ŌĆö Equivalent Wenner spacing

For Wenner, $AB/2 = 3a/2 = 50$ m, so $a = 33.33$ m:

$$K_{W} \;=\; 2 \pi a \;=\; 2 \pi (33.33) \;\approx\; 209.4 \text{ m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **huge drop in K (1960 ŌåÆ 209)** when switching to Wenner. The instrument sensitivity setting must be changed accordingly. This is a common source of error in field practice and a **favourite 2-mark sub-question** in KTU exams.

---

## 3.5 KTU VALUATION ANALYTICAL MATRIX ŌĆö Common Sub-Questions and Their Method

| Sub-Question Pattern | First-Step Response Strategy | KTU Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| "Define apparent resistivity" | Define as the resistivity of a fictitious homogeneous ground that would give the same ╬öV/I for the same electrode array. | 2 |
| "Why is ŽüŌéÉ ŌēĀ Žü for layered ground?" | State that current lines bend towards more conductive layers, biasing the measured ╬öV. | 2 |
| "Why Schlumberger over Wenner for deep sounding?" | Better vertical resolution, fewer P-electrode moves (only C expands), smoother field curve. | 3 |
| "Why does seismic refraction fail to detect hidden layers?" | Snell's law requires the lower layer to be faster; a low-velocity layer cannot be a refractor. | 3 |
| "How is depth obtained from a time-distance graph?" | Read slope ŌåÆ V, read intercept ŌåÆ $T_i$, then apply $h = T_i V_1 V_2 / (2 \sqrt{V_2^2 - V_1^2})$. | 7 |
| "Why is natural gamma log useful for sand/shale discrimination?" | Shales contain K-40 and are radioactive; clean sands are ╬│-low. | 2 |

## 4.4 Block Diagram ŌĆö Workflow of a Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    G5 -- Yes --> G6[Plot rhoa vs AB2 on Log Log Paper]
    G6 --> G7[Match Curve with Master Curves or Inversion]
    G7 --> G8([Obtain Layer Resistivities and Thicknesses])
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Define apparent resistivity. Why does the value measured in a field survey not represent the true resistivity of a homogeneous ground?**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparent Resistivity (ŽüŌéÉ):** The apparent resistivity is the resistivity of a fictitious, electrically homogeneous and isotropic half-space that would produce the same potential difference $\Delta V$ for the same current $I$ and the same electrode configuration as the actual, heterogeneous ground.

$$\rho_{a} \;=\; K \,\dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

**Why ŽüŌéÉ ŌēĀ Žü (true):** In nature, the subsurface is **inhomogeneous** and often **anisotropic** ŌĆö the resistivity varies from layer to layer and even within a single layer. Current lines therefore bend preferentially through the more conductive paths, biasing the measured $\Delta V$ towards those layers. The value computed via $\rho_{a} = K \Delta V / I$ is a **weighted average** of the resistivities of all layers contributing to the current flow, and is meaningful only for the chosen electrode spacing. As spacing increases, deeper layers contribute more, and $\rho_{a}$ changes ŌĆö which is the very principle that allows depth sounding.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`
**State Snell's law for seismic waves at a horizontal interface. Why must the lower layer be faster for refraction surveying to detect it?**

**Model Answer:**

**Snell's Law (for a wave incident at angle ╬ĖŌéü in Layer 1 of velocity VŌéü onto a Layer 2 of velocity VŌéé):**

$$\dfrac{\sin \theta_{1}}{V_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{\sin \theta_{2}}{V_{2}}$$

**Critical angle condition:** When the refracted ray grazes along the interface, $\theta_{2} = 90┬░$, so $\sin \theta_{2} = 1$. The corresponding **critical angle of incidence** is:

$$\sin \theta_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}$$

For $\theta_{c}$ to be a **real angle** (i.e. $\sin \theta_{c} \le 1$), it is **mandatory that VŌéé Ōēź VŌéü**. Equivalently, refraction surveying can only detect an interface where the lower layer is **faster** (higher seismic velocity) than the overlying layer. If the lower layer is slower, no real critical angle exists, no head wave propagates, and the interface becomes a "hidden layer" that cannot be detected by surface refraction ŌĆö a fundamental limitation of the method.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION A (Choice 1) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the geometric factor of the Schlumberger electrode array, starting from the point-source potential formula for a homogeneous half-space.** ŌĆö **7 Marks**

**(b) A Schlumberger VES at a site gives the following data. Compute the apparent resistivity and the depth of investigation for each spread, and comment on the suitability of the site for a borewell.**

| $L$ (m) | $l$ (m) | $\Delta V$ (mV) | $I$ (mA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 1 | 85 | 50 |
| 25 | 1 | 60 | 50 |
| 50 | 1 | 42 | 50 |
| 100 | 1 | 28 | 50 |

ŌĆö **7 Marks**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (a)

**Starting formula** (potential at radial distance r from a current source I on a homogeneous half-space):

$$V(r) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi r}$$

**Total potential at any surface point P** (superposition of CŌéü at +I and CŌéé at ŌłÆI):

$$V(P) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{r_{C1}} - \dfrac{1}{r_{C2}} \right)$$

**Geometry of Schlumberger array** (CŌéü at $x = -L$, CŌéé at $x = +L$, PŌéü at $x = -l$, PŌéé at $x = +l$):

**Potential at PŌéü** (distances to CŌéü = LŌłÆl, to CŌéé = L+l):

$$V(P_{1}) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{L-l} - \dfrac{1}{L+l} \right) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{(L+l)-(L-l)}{L^{2}-l^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2}-l^{2}}$$

**Potential at PŌéé** (by symmetry, opposite sign):

$$V(P_{2}) \;=\; -\dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2}-l^{2}}$$

**Measured potential difference:**

$$\Delta V \;=\; V(P_{1}) - V(P_{2}) \;=\; \dfrac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{4l}{L^{2}-l^{2}}$$

**Solving for Žü:**

$$\rho \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I} \;=\; K_{S} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

**Geometric factor:**

$$\boxed{\,K_{S} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l}\,} \quad \xrightarrow{L \gg l} \quad K_{S} \approx \dfrac{\pi L^{2}}{l}$$

(Each derivation step: 1 mark ├Ś 7 lines = 7 marks. **Show the algebra line-by-line.**)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (b)

**General formula:**

$$\rho_{a} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}, \qquad d \approx 0.5 L$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $l = 1$ m and $L$ ranges from 10 to 100 m, the **exact** $K_S$ formula is used. The approximation $K_S \approx \pi L^2 / l$ would give a negligible error ($\sim 0.01$%).

**Row 1: $L = 10$ m**

$$K_{S,1} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (100 - 1)}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{99 \pi}{2} \;\approx\; 155.5 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho_{a,1} \;=\; 155.5 \times \dfrac{0.085}{0.050} \;=\; 155.5 \times 1.7 \;=\; 264.4 \text{ ╬®┬Ęm}, \quad d_{1} \approx 5 \text{ m}$$

**Row 2: $L = 25$ m**

$$K_{S,2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (625 - 1)}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{624 \pi}{2} \;\approx\; 980.2 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho_{a,2} \;=\; 980.2 \times \dfrac{0.060}{0.050} \;=\; 980.2 \times 1.2 \;=\; 1176.2 \text{ ╬®┬Ęm}, \quad d_{2} \approx 12.5 \text{ m}$$

**Row 3: $L = 50$ m**

$$K_{S,3} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (2500 - 1)}{2} \;\approx\; 3926.7 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho_{a,3} \;=\; 3926.7 \times \dfrac{0.042}{0.050} \;=\; 3926.7 \times 0.84 \;=\; 3298.4 \text{ ╬®┬Ęm}, \quad d_{3} \approx 25 \text{ m}$$

**Row 4: $L = 100$ m**

$$K_{S,4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (10000 - 1)}{2} \;\approx\; 15707.2 \text{ m}$$

$$\rho_{a,4} \;=\; 15707.2 \times \dfrac{0.028}{0.050} \;=\; 15707.2 \times 0.56 \;=\; 8796.0 \text{ ╬®┬Ęm}, \quad d_{4} \approx 50 \text{ m}$$

**Summary table (final answer):**

| $L$ (m) | $K_S$ (m) | $\rho_a$ (╬®┬Ęm) | Depth $d$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 155.5 | 264.4 | 5 |
| 25 | 980.2 | 1176.2 | 12.5 |
| 50 | 3926.7 | 3298.4 | 25 |
| 100 | 15707.2 | 8796.0 | 50 |

**Comment on suitability:** The ŽüŌéÉ values **rise sharply with depth** (264 ŌåÆ 8796 ╬®┬Ęm), which is the classic signature of a **massive, unfractured basement rock** at depth. There is **no low-resistivity weathered/fractured layer** at this location that would constitute a productive aquifer. The site is **not recommended** for drilling a high-yield borewell. A more promising site would show ŽüŌéÉ **decreasing then increasing** (K-shaped or H-shaped VES curve) ŌĆö typical of weathered-over-fractured-over-basement.

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION B (Choice 2) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat timeŌĆōdistance sketch, derive the formula for the depth of a single refractor from seismic refraction data. State clearly the assumption about layer velocities.** ŌĆö **7 Marks**

**(b) The first arrivals at a seismic refraction profile give the following times. Determine the velocities VŌéü and VŌéé, the intercept time T_i, and the depth to the refractor.** ŌĆö **7 Marks**

| Geophone Position $x$ (m) | 5 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 50 | 70 | 100 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| First Arrival Time $t$ (ms) | 14 | 25 | 43 | 57 | 81 | 95 | 117 |

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (a)

**Sketch (description for the answer script):**

> A horizontal surface with source S at the origin and geophones at distance x to the right. Draw a horizontal interface at depth h. From S, draw a ray going down to the interface at critical angle ╬Ėc, a horizontal segment along the interface, and a ray coming up at ╬Ėc to the geophone G. Label the time-distance graph on the right side: a straight line of slope $1/V_1$ from the origin (direct wave) and a second straight line of slope $1/V_2$ that crosses the y-axis at $T_i$ (refracted wave). The two lines intersect at the **crossover point** $(x_c, t_c)$.

**Assumption (mandatory to state):** $V_2 > V_1$ (lower layer is seismically faster).

**Derivation (use the derivation from Section 3.3 of these notes):** The head-wave travel time is:

$$t_{r} \;=\; \dfrac{x}{V_{2}} \;+\; T_{i}$$

where the **intercept time** (from the head wave's path through Layer 1 down and up, minus the horizontal run) is:

$$T_{i} \;=\; \dfrac{2 h \cos \theta_{c}}{V_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{2 h \sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}{V_{1} V_{2}}$$

Solving for h:

$$\boxed{\,h \;=\; \dfrac{T_{i} \, V_{1} V_{2}}{2 \sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}\,}$$

**Alternate depth formula** using crossover distance $x_c$:

$$h \;=\; \dfrac{x_{c}}{2}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{V_{2} - V_{1}}{V_{2} + V_{1}}}$$

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Plot and identify two straight-line segments.**

From the data, the points fall into two clear groups:

- **Early points (5, 10, 20, 30 m):** slope is small ŌåÆ direct wave in Layer 1.
- **Late points (50, 70, 100 m):** slope is larger ŌåÆ refracted wave from Layer 2.

**Step 2 ŌĆö VŌéü from first segment (use 5 m and 30 m).**

$$V_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} \;=\; \dfrac{30 - 5}{(57 - 14) \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \dfrac{25}{0.043} \;\approx\; 581.4 \text{ m/s}$$

(Sanity check with 10 m and 20 m: $V_1 = 10/0.018 = 555.6$ m/s. The two estimates are close. Use the **two-point method on widely separated points** for best accuracy, then average.) **Adopt VŌéü Ōēł 580 m/s.**

**Step 3 ŌĆö VŌéé from second segment (use 50 m and 100 m).**

$$V_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{100 - 50}{(117 - 81) \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \dfrac{50}{0.036} \;\approx\; 1388.9 \text{ m/s}$$

**Adopt VŌéé Ōēł 1390 m/s.**

> [!NOTE]
> Since $V_2 = 1390 > V_1 = 580$, the **Sneider's rule is satisfied** and refraction detection is valid.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Intercept time $T_i$ (extrapolate refracted line to x = 0).**

The refracted line equation is $t_r = x / V_2 + T_i$. Use one point on it, say $x = 50$ m, $t = 0.081$ s:

$$T_{i} \;=\; t_{r} - \dfrac{x}{V_{2}} \;=\; 0.081 - \dfrac{50}{1388.9} \;=\; 0.081 - 0.0360 \;=\; 0.0450 \text{ s} \;=\; 45.0 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Depth to refractor.**

$$h \;=\; \dfrac{T_{i} \, V_{1} V_{2}}{2 \sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}}}$$

Compute denominator:

$$\sqrt{V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{1390^{2} - 580^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{1,932,100 - 336,400} \;=\; \sqrt{1,595,700} \;\approx\; 1263.2 \text{ m/s}$$

Compute h:

$$h \;=\; \dfrac{0.0450 \times 580 \times 1390}{2 \times 1263.2} \;=\; \dfrac{36,279}{2526.4} \;\approx\; 14.36 \text{ m}$$

**Final answer for part (b):**

$$V_{1} \approx 580 \text{ m/s}, \quad V_{2} \approx 1390 \text{ m/s}, \quad T_{i} \approx 45.0 \text{ ms}, \quad h \approx 14.4 \text{ m}$$

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Geophysical Exploration questions (real KTU trends):**
> 1. **Units Mismatch:** Writing $l = 1$ instead of $l = 1$ m, or mixing seconds and milliseconds. **Always carry units through.** Full mark only if final answer carries correct units (m, m/s, ╬®┬Ęm, s, ms).
> 2. **Confusing VŌéü and VŌéé:** In Schlumberger VES tables, $L$ is **half** the current-electrode separation. Students often plug in the full separation $2L$ instead of $L$, leading to a factor-of-4 error in $K_S$. **Always draw the electrode layout and label $L$.**
> 3. **Forgetting the Snell prerequisite:** Stating the seismic refraction depth formula without verifying $V_2 > V_1$ loses a mark.
> 4. **Schlumberger approximation misuse:** Writing $K_S = \pi L^2 / l$ when $L$ is NOT much greater than $l$ is a 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **Seismic graph: missing the intercept time clearly:** Examiner cannot award the depth-formula mark if the value of $T_i$ is not extracted from the graph (or computed from the refracted line).
> 6. **Wrong direction for "deeper" resistivity:** A student who concludes the site is "good for drilling" when ŽüŌéÉ rises sharply with depth will lose 1 mark on interpretation.
> 7. **Averaging slopes incorrectly:** A common error is to take the slope of the refracted line by averaging the first and last time values, rather than using $\Delta t / \Delta x$ over a **wide separation**.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as a **last-night revision sheet** before the KTU ESE.

- [x] **Apparent resistivity** is **NOT** the true resistivity of any single layer ŌĆö it is an integrated response of the entire current-flow volume.
- [x] **General formula:** $\rho_{a} = K \cdot \Delta V / I$. The array dictates **K**, not the physics.
- [x] **Wenner K = 2ŽĆa**, **Schlumberger K = ŽĆ(L┬▓ ŌłÆ l┬▓)/(2l)**, **Dipole-Dipole K = ŽĆn(n+1)(n+2)a**.
- [x] **VES** = centre fixed, spread expands ŌåÆ **vertical** layering. **Profiling** = spread fixed, centre moves ŌåÆ **lateral** variation.
- [x] **Penetration depth** Ōēł ┬Į ├Ś (AB/2). Use this rule of thumb to plan survey.
- [x] **Seismic refraction requires VŌéé > VŌéü**. Hidden low-velocity layer cannot be detected ŌĆö Snell's law limit.
- [x] **Slope of tŌĆōx graph** ŌåÆ **inverse of velocity**. The flatter line is the **faster** layer.
- [x] **Intercept time $T_i$** is read at $x = 0$ on the refracted line. **Crossover distance $x_c$** is read at the intersection.
- [x] **Depth from seismic:** $h = T_i V_1 V_2 / (2 \sqrt{V_2^2 - V_1^2}) = (x_c/2) \sqrt{(V_2 - V_1)/(V_2 + V_1)}$.
- [x] **Dar-Zarrouk variables:** $T = \sum \rho_i h_i$ (transverse resistance), $S = \sum h_i / \rho_i$ (longitudinal conductance) ŌĆö used in **aquifer transmissivity** estimation.
- [x] **Wyllie time-average** for sonic log porosity: $\phi = (\Delta t - \Delta t_{ma})/(\Delta t_f - \Delta t_{ma})$.
- [x] **Gamma log:** Shale = high ╬│; clean sand aquifer = low ╬│. **Single most diagnostic log for lithology.**
- [x] **Kerala laterite terrain (exam-oriented):** Typical 3-layer VES ŌĆö laterite top (Žü ~ 50ŌĆō150), weathered aquifer (Žü ~ 30ŌĆō80), basement (>500).
- [x] **Always show the electrode layout sketch** in derivations ŌĆö it earns the first mark in KTU part-(a) questions.
- [x] **Always state assumptions** explicitly ŌĆö homogeneity, isotropy, infinite half-space, VŌéé > VŌéü, etc.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES ŌĆö GEOPHYSICAL EXPLORATION**
