topics.title: Electrical Resistivity Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrical Resistivity Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤öī SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Resistivity Method (Geoelectrical Method):** A non-invasive, surface-based geophysical exploration technique that involves injecting a known direct current (DC) into the ground through a pair of current electrodes and measuring the resulting potential difference across a pair of potential electrodes to determine the **electrical resistivity** distribution of the subsurface formations. It is the most widely used geophysical tool for **groundwater exploration, aquifer mapping, depth-to-bedrock determination, and contamination studies**.

**Key Parameters (KTU Mandatory Definitions):**

| Term | Definition | Unit |
|------|------------|------|
| **Resistivity ($\rho$)** | Resistance offered by a **unit cube** of material between opposite faces. | **Ohm-meter (╬®┬Ęm)** |
| **Resistance ($R$)** | Opposition to current flow: $R = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Ohm (╬®) |
| **Conductivity ($\sigma$)** | Reciprocal of resistivity: $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Siemens/meter (S/m) |
| **Apparent Resistivity ($\rho_a$)** | Resistivity computed assuming the ground is **homogeneous and isotropic**, even when it is layered. | ╬®┬Ęm |
| **Geometric Factor ($K$)** | Configuration-dependent constant relating electrode geometry to apparent resistivity. | meter (m) |

**Resistivity values of common geological materials (high-yield for KTU exams):**

| Material | Typical Resistivity ($\Omega \cdot m$) |
|----------|---------------------------------------|
| Clay / Saturated Clay | **1 ŌĆō 20** |
| Saturated Sand / Gravel (Aquifer) | **30 ŌĆō 100** |
| Fresh Water-bearing Sandstone | **50 ŌĆō 200** |
| Sandstone (dry) | **200 ŌĆō 1000** |
| Limestone (variable) | **100 ŌĆō 10000** |
| Crystalline Basement (Granite, Gneiss) | **> 1000** |
| Dry Sand / Topsoil | **100 ŌĆō 1000** |
| Sea Water | **~ 0.2** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Medical X-Ray Analogy"** ­¤®╗
> Think of the Earth as a patient and Electrical Resistivity Survey as a **CT scan for groundwater**. Just as a CT scan fires X-rays from different angles and measures how much passes through body tissues (dense bone blocks more, soft tissue allows more), the resistivity method pumps electricity into the ground. Different rocks "resist" this current differently:
> - **Wet clay** (like a sponge full of conductive water) ŌåÆ low resistivity ŌåÆ high conductivity ŌåÆ **good conductor**.
> - **Dry granite basement** (like solid dry rock) ŌåÆ very high resistivity ŌåÆ **strong insulator**.
> - **Aquifer zones (water-saturated sand)** ŌåÆ moderately low resistivity (because water contains dissolved ions that conduct).
>
> By measuring the resistance pattern, geologists "see" invisible aquifers, fractures, and bedrock without drilling a single borehole. **This is the foundational principle of groundwater geophysics.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards

- **Standard electrode material:** Non-polarizable porous-pot electrodes for potential measurement; stainless steel for current electrodes.
- **Source current:** Low-frequency DC or low-frequency AC (typically < 1 Hz to suppress inductive coupling) of the order of **1ŌĆō100 mA**.
- **Reference standards:** IS 15681 (Indian Standard for Geophysical Resistivity Logging) and **Schlumberger/Wenner master curves** (OCEAN type curves).
- **Rho ground-truth for sanity check:** Resistivity of **formation water** follows Archie's Law: $\rho_w = \rho_0 / F$, where $F$ is the formation resistivity factor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle ŌĆö Ohm's Law in Earth Materials

For a homogeneous, isotropic medium of uniform cross-section, Ohm's law states:

$$V = I \cdot R = I \cdot \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$$

Solving for resistivity:

$$\rho = \dfrac{V}{I} \cdot \dfrac{A}{L}$$

In the ground (semi-infinite, isotropic), current from a **point electrode** spreads radially as **hemispherical equipotential surfaces** (not cylindrical as in wires). This geometric reality is the foundation of the entire method.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic of Apparent Resistivity (Why it works)

**Step 1:** Current $I$ flows from a single point electrode on a flat earth surface. By symmetry, equipotentials are hemispheres of radius $r$.

**Step 2:** The current density at distance $r$ is:

$$J = \dfrac{I}{\text{Area of hemisphere}} = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** From Ohm's law in point form $\vec{E} = \rho \vec{J}$, the electric field intensity is:

$$E = \dfrac{\rho \cdot I}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Potential at distance $r$ from a single current source:

$$V(r) = \int_{\infty}^{r} E \, dr = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 5:** For **two current electrodes** $C_1$ (source $I$) and $C_2$ (sink $-I$), the total potential at any point $P$ is the **superposition**:

$$V_P = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Potential difference $\Delta V$ between potential electrodes $P_1$ and $P_2$:

$$\Delta V = V_{P_1} - V_{P_2} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \left( \dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2} - \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

where $r_1, r_2$ = distances from $C_1, C_2$ to $P_1$ and $R_1, R_2$ = distances from $C_1, C_2$ to $P_2$.

**Step 7:** Rearranging, we get the **Universal Resistivity Formula**:

$$\rho_a = \dfrac{\Delta V}{I} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{2\pi}{\left[\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2} - \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right]}}_{\text{Geometric Factor } K} = K \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Apparent"?** In a layered earth, $\rho_a$ is **not** the true resistivity of any individual layer. It is the equivalent resistivity of the heterogeneous subsurface as if it were homogeneous. We need **inversion / curve-matching** to extract the true layer parameters.

## 2.3 Electrode Configurations ŌĆö The Four KTU-Classics

**A. Wenner Array (Equal-Spacing, Inline)**

Four electrodes in a straight line with equal spacing $a$: $C_1 P_1 P_2 C_2$.

$$\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{V}{I} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_{Wenner} = 2\pi a$$

- **Depth of investigation:** approximately $a$
- **Best for:** Horizontal profiling, smooth ground, quick surveys.

**B. Schlumberger Array (Concentrated Potential, Spread Current)**

Current electrodes $C_1, C_2$ at large distance $2L$; potential electrodes $P_1, P_2$ close together with separation $2l$, where $l \ll L$.

$$\rho_a = \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{V}{I}$$

When $l \ll L$ (standard field practice):

$$\rho_a \approx \dfrac{\pi L^{2}}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{V}{I}$$

- **Depth of investigation:** approximately $L$ (i.e., $AB/2$)
- **Best for:** Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES) for layered earth ŌĆö most common in **groundwater studies**.

**C. Dipole-Dipole Array**

Two dipoles: a current dipole of length $a$ and a potential dipole of length $a$, separated by integer multiple $n \cdot a$.

$$\rho_a = \pi n (n+1)(n+2)\, a \cdot \dfrac{V}{I}$$

- **Best for:** Detecting **steeply dipping structures** (faults, fractures, cavities).

**D. Pole-Pole Array**

A single current electrode $C_1$ with $C_2$ at "infinity"; a single potential electrode $P_1$ with $P_2$ at "infinity".

$$\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{V}{I}$$

- **Best for:** Deep investigations where other arrays are impractical; lowest signal-to-noise.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet ŌĆö Master Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** In this table, $\vert x \vert$ uses `\vert` (not `|`) to avoid breaking markdown syntax. Always replace `|` with `\vert` in all resistivity tables.

| Array Type | Electrode Geometry | Geometric Factor $K$ | Apparent Resistivity $\rho_a$ | Depth of Investigation |
|------------|--------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Wenner** | $a = C_1 P_1 = P_1 P_2 = P_2 C_2$ | $2\pi a$ | $2\pi a \cdot (V/I)$ | $\approx a$ |
| **Schlumberger** | $AB = 2L$, $MN = 2l$, $l \ll L$ | $\dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \cdot (V/I)$ | $\approx L = AB/2$ |
| **Dipole-Dipole** | Dipole length $a$, separation $n a$ | $\pi n(n+1)(n+2)a$ | $\pi n(n+1)(n+2)a \cdot (V/I)$ | $\approx n a$ |
| **Pole-Pole** | $C_2, P_2$ at $\infty$ | $2\pi a$ | $2\pi a \cdot (V/I)$ | $\approx a$ |
| **Lee Partitioning** | $MN \le AB/5$ | Same as Schlumberger | Same | $\approx AB/2$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Groundwater Potential Zones (GPZ):** Identifying productive fracture zones in hard-rock (Kerala crystalline basement).
- **Dams & Reservoirs:** Detecting subsurface leakage paths, clay lenses, and karst cavities.
- **Contamination Plumes:** Mapping saline water intrusion in coastal aquifers (highly relevant to KTU coastal districts).
- **Tunnel & Foundation Engineering:** Locating cavities and weak zones before excavation.
- **Mineral Exploration:** Graphite, pyrite, and metallic ore bodies act as conductors.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" in Kerala Context:** Kerala's hard-rock (laterite, gneiss, granite) terrain has **deep, fractured aquifers**. Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES) using the Schlumberger array is the **de facto standard** for site selection of bore wells in the state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤¦« SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Potential Due to a Single Point Electrode

**Given:** A point current source $I$ is placed on the surface of a homogeneous, isotropic earth of resistivity $\rho$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define Current Density Vector:**

In a uniform medium, current from a point source spreads radially. The total current $I$ crosses any hemispherical surface of radius $r$ centered on the electrode. The current density is therefore:

$$\vec{J}(r) = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r^{2}} \, \hat{r}$$

This is because the area of a hemisphere is $A = 2\pi r^{2}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Ohm's Law in Point Form:**

The electric field $\vec{E}$ is related to $\vec{J}$ via $\vec{E} = \rho \vec{J}$. Thus:

$$\vec{E}(r) = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r^{2}} \, \hat{r}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Potential at Distance r:**

Potential is the line integral of the electric field from infinity (reference) to $r$:

$$V(r) = - \int_{\infty}^{r} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{r} = - \int_{\infty}^{r} \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r^{2}} \, dr$$

$$V(r) = - \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \left[ -\dfrac{1}{r} \right]_{\infty}^{r} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Single-Electrode Potential:**

$$\boxed{V(r) = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r}}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Schlumberger Apparent Resistivity Formula

**Geometry:** Four collinear electrodes $C_1, P_1, P_2, C_2$. Define:
- $C_1 P_1 = L - l$ (current-to-inner-potential distance)
- $C_1 P_2 = L + l$
- $C_2 P_1 = L + l$
- $C_2 P_2 = L - l$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Potential at $P_1$ due to $C_1$ (current $+I$):**

$$V_{C_1 \to P_1} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi (L - l)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Potential at $P_1$ due to $C_2$ (current $-I$):**

$$V_{C_2 \to P_1} = \dfrac{\rho (-I)}{2\pi (L + l)} = - \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi (L + l)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total Potential at $P_1$ (superposition):**

$$V_{P_1} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \left[ \dfrac{1}{L - l} - \dfrac{1}{L + l} \right] = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{(L + l) - (L - l)}{(L - l)(L + l)} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2} - l^{2}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Similarly, Total Potential at $P_2$:**

By symmetry (swap $l \to -l$):

$$V_{P_2} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \left[ \dfrac{1}{L + l} - \dfrac{1}{L - l} \right] = - \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2} - l^{2}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Potential Difference between $P_1$ and $P_2$:**

$$\Delta V = V_{P_1} - V_{P_2} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2} - l^{2}} - \left( - \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2} - l^{2}} \right)$$

$$\Delta V = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{4l}{L^{2} - l^{2}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for $\rho$:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{\Delta V}{I} \cdot \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Schlumberger Apparent Resistivity Formula:**

$$\boxed{\rho_a = \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Limiting Case (when $l \ll L$):**

When $l \ll L$, the term $l^{2}$ is negligible compared to $L^{2}$:

$$\rho_a \approx \dfrac{\pi L^{2}}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the formula **universally used in the field** because field engineers always keep the potential electrode separation much smaller than the current electrode separation ($MN \le AB/5$).

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Wenner Apparent Resistivity Formula

**Geometry:** Equal spacing $a$ such that $C_1 P_1 = P_1 P_2 = P_2 C_2 = a$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the four distances:**
- $r_1 = C_1 P_1 = a$
- $r_2 = C_2 P_1 = 2a$
- $R_1 = C_1 P_2 = 2a$
- $R_2 = C_2 P_2 = a$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into the general formula:**

$$\rho_a = \dfrac{2\pi}{\dfrac{1}{a} - \dfrac{1}{2a} - \dfrac{1}{2a} + \dfrac{1}{a}} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the denominator:**

$$\text{Denom} = \dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{1}{a} - \dfrac{1}{2a} - \dfrac{1}{2a} = \dfrac{2}{a} - \dfrac{1}{a} = \dfrac{1}{a}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Wenner Formula:**

$$\rho_a = \dfrac{2\pi}{1/a} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I} = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{V}{I}}$$

## 3.4 Field Sounding Interpretation ŌĆö Master Curves

For a **two-layer earth** with top layer resistivity $\rho_1$, thickness $h_1$, and basement $\rho_2$, the apparent resistivity curve is plotted as $\rho_a$ vs $AB/2$ on a log-log graph. Three standard curve types are commonly examined:

**Layer Combination Types (KTU classics):**

| Type | Resistivity Profile | Geological Interpretation |
|------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| **H-type** | $\rho_1 > \rho_2 < \rho_3$ | Conductive layer sandwiched (e.g., clay between dry topsoil & bedrock) |
| **K-type** | $\rho_1 < \rho_2 > \rho_3$ | Resistive layer sandwiched (e.g., dry sand between wet layers) |
| **A-type** | $\rho_1 < \rho_2 < \rho_3$ | Progressive increase with depth (hard-rock transition) |
| **Q-type** | $\rho_1 > \rho_2 > \rho_3$ | Progressive decrease with depth (deep weathering) |
| **HA-type** | $\rho_1 > \rho_2 < \rho_3 < \rho_4$ | Aquifer under clay cap (very common in Kerala alluvium) |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Practice)

**Problem:** A Schlumberger VES survey with $L = 50$ m and $l = 5$ m gives a measured $\Delta V = 240$ mV when $I = 20$ mA. Calculate the apparent resistivity.

**Solution:**

$$\rho_a = \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $L^{2} - l^{2}$:

$$L^{2} - l^{2} = 50^{2} - 5^{2} = 2500 - 25 = 2475 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the geometric factor:

$$K = \dfrac{\pi \times 2475}{2 \times 5} = \dfrac{\pi \times 2475}{10} = 247.5\pi \approx 777.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the voltage ratio:

$$\dfrac{\Delta V}{I} = \dfrac{240 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 12 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4:** Compute apparent resistivity:

$$\rho_a = 777.5 \times 12 = 9330 \, \Omega \cdot m$$

$$\boxed{\rho_a \approx 9330 \, \Omega \cdot m}$$

This high value (> 1000 ╬®┬Ęm) typically indicates **crystalline basement rock** ŌĆö drilling should be targeted to fractured zones above this depth.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ­¤Äō SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define apparent resistivity. Why is it called "apparent" and not the true resistivity of the formation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Apparent resistivity ($\rho_a$) is the resistivity of a hypothetical, homogeneous, isotropic half-space that would produce the measured voltage for a given current and electrode geometry, computed using the relation:

$$\rho_a = K \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

where $K$ is the geometric factor. **(1 mark)**
In a layered or heterogeneous subsurface, $\rho_a$ is a **weighted average** of resistivities of all layers contributing current flow to the measurement circuit. It is "apparent" because it does **not represent the true resistivity** of any single layer; it merely indicates the **bulk response** of the subsurface. **(2 marks)**
To obtain true layer resistivities, the apparent resistivity vs. electrode spacing curve must be **inverted** using master curves or numerical software.

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any four common electrode configurations used in DC resistivity surveying and state one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer (4 ├Ś 0.5 = 2 marks; one-line explanation = 1 mark):**

| Array | Typical Application |
|-------|---------------------|
| **Wenner** | Horizontal profiling for lateral resistivity variations; quick reconnaissance. |
| **Schlumberger** | Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES) for layered-earth, groundwater studies. |
| **Dipole-Dipole** | Detection of steeply-dipping structures like fractures, faults, and cavities. |
| **Pole-Pole** | Deep investigations and large-scale regional surveys. |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Derive the apparent resistivity formula for the Schlumberger electrode array starting from the fundamental potential equation due to a single point current electrode on a homogeneous earth. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The earth is a homogeneous, isotropic, semi-infinite medium.
2. The current electrode is a point source.
3. Equipotential surfaces are hemispherical.
4. Steady-state DC current; no IP (induced polarization) effects.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

For a single point current source $I$ on a flat earth surface, current spreads radially through hemispherical equipotentials of area $2\pi r^{2}$. Therefore, current density is:

$$J = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

By Ohm's law in point form $E = \rho J$:

$$E(r) = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

Integrating from infinity (reference potential) to distance $r$:

$$V(r) = \int_{\infty}^{r} E \, dr = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r} \quad \textbf{(Eq. 1)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

For the Schlumberger array, $C_1 P_1 = L - l$, $C_1 P_2 = L + l$, $C_2 P_1 = L + l$, $C_2 P_2 = L - l$. **(0.5 mark)**

Potential at $P_1$ (superposition of $C_1$ and $C_2$):

$$V_{P_1} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi (L-l)} - \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi (L+l)} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2} - l^{2}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

Potential at $P_2$ (by symmetry):

$$V_{P_2} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi (L+l)} - \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi (L-l)} = - \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2l}{L^{2} - l^{2}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

Potential difference:

$$\Delta V = V_{P_1} - V_{P_2} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{4l}{L^{2} - l^{2}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

Solving for $\rho$:

$$\boxed{\rho_a = \dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}} \quad \textbf{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Limiting case (1 mark):** When $l \ll L$:

$$\rho_a \approx \dfrac{\pi L^{2}}{2l} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

**(b) Explain the procedure of Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES) using the Schlumberger array. How is the data interpreted using master curves? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**VES Procedure (3 marks):**
1. The center of the array (midpoint of $MN$) is fixed at the survey point.
2. The potential electrode separation $MN = 2l$ is kept small and **constant** initially.
3. The current electrodes $C_1$ and $C_2$ are progressively expanded symmetrically outward (typically doubling $L = AB/2$ each step: 1.5, 3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 m).
4. At each step, current $I$ is injected and $\Delta V$ is measured. Apparent resistivity is computed using the formula derived above.
5. When $\Delta V$ becomes too small to read accurately, $l$ is increased (and the geometric factor is corrected) to maintain measurable signal.
6. A plot of $\rho_a$ (y-axis, log scale) versus $AB/2$ (x-axis, log scale) is the **sounding curve**.

**Master Curve Interpretation (4 marks):**
1. The plotted field curve is compared visually with a **family of theoretical master curves** (e.g., Orellana-Mooney or Rijkswaterstaat two-layer curves, three-layer type curves: H, K, A, Q).
2. **Partial curve matching:** The field curve is overlaid on the master curve set. Resistivity contrast $\rho_2/\rho_1$ and thickness parameter $h_1/a$ are read off.
3. The matched parameters give the **initial model**: $\rho_1, \rho_2, h_1$.
4. For multi-layer cases, the field curve is interpreted in segments: top segment gives $\rho_1$ and $h_1$; the middle portion reveals the second layer; the asymptotic tail reveals $\rho_n$.
5. The result is **digitally refined** using inversion software (e.g., **IPI2Win**, **Res1D**, **ZondRes2D**) to obtain the final 1-D resistivity-depth model.
6. The final interpreted model gives layer-wise resistivities and depths, used to identify **aquifer zones, depth to bedrock, and groundwater potential**.

---

### **Question B** [Alternative ŌĆö Internal Choice]

**(a) Compare the Wenner and Schlumberger electrode arrays in terms of (i) electrode geometry, (ii) field procedure, (iii) signal strength, (iv) depth of investigation, and (v) suitability for VES. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Wenner Array | Schlumberger Array |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| (i) Geometry | 4 electrodes equally spaced by $a$ | Current electrodes spread wide; potential electrodes close |
| (ii) Field Procedure | All 4 electrodes moved each step | Only current electrodes moved; MN kept fixed until needed |
| (iii) Signal Strength | Lower $\Delta V$ for same current ŌåÆ noisier | Higher $\Delta V$ ŌåÆ better signal-to-noise ratio |
| (iv) Depth of Investigation | $\approx a$ | $\approx AB/2$ (controllable to large depths) |
| (v) VES Suitability | Difficult ŌĆö all 4 electrodes must be re-laid out for each depth | **Most suitable** ŌĆö fixed center, only $AB$ varies |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Schlumberger array is **preferred for Vertical Electrical Sounding (VES)** because it offers higher signal, deeper penetration, and reduced field labor.

**(b) A Wenner array survey with electrode spacing $a = 20$ m is conducted. The measured current is $I = 50$ mA and potential difference is $\Delta V = 75$ mV. Compute the apparent resistivity of the subsurface. Comment on the likely lithology. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the Wenner apparent resistivity formula:

$$\rho_a = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values:

$$\rho_a = 2\pi \times 20 \times \dfrac{75 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\rho_a = 2\pi \times 20 \times 1.5 = 60\pi \approx 188.5 \, \Omega \cdot m$$

$$\boxed{\rho_a \approx 188.5 \, \Omega \cdot m}$$

**Lithological Interpretation (3 marks):**
A resistivity of $\approx 188 \, \Omega \cdot m$ corresponds most likely to:
- **Dry/moist sandstone** (typical: 100ŌĆō1000 ╬®┬Ęm), or
- **Coarse sand with partial saturation** (typical: 100ŌĆō500 ╬®┬Ęm).

It is **too high** to be saturated clay (< 20 ╬®┬Ęm) and **too low** to be fresh crystalline basement (> 1000 ╬®┬Ęm). The result suggests a **moderately resistive formation**, possibly an unsaturated or partially saturated sandy horizon, **not an ideal aquifer** in the immediate subsurface.

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤øæ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **MISTAKE 1 (very common):** Forgetting to convert mV and mA to **Volts and Amperes** before computing $\Delta V / I$. Marks lost: **1**.
> - **MISTAKE 2:** Using the Schlumberger formula for a Wenner geometry (or vice versa). Always **identify the array first**, then pick the formula.
> - **MISTAKE 3:** In the derivation, students often **forget the sign of $-I$** at the second current electrode. Marks lost: **1ŌĆō2**.
> - **MISTAKE 4:** Not mentioning **assumptions** in the derivation part. Marks lost: **1**.
> - **MISTAKE 5:** Writing the master-curve interpretation without naming **the curve type (H, K, A, Q)**. Always classify the curve.

---

## 5.3 ­¤ōØ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **­¤ÜĆ Rapid-Fire Revision ŌĆö Memorize Before Exam**

- Ō£ģ **Universal Resistivity Formula:** $\rho_a = K \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$ ŌĆö know this in your sleep.
- Ō£ģ **Single point-electrode potential:** $V(r) = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$ (hemispherical spread).
- Ō£ģ **Wenner $\rho_a$:** $2\pi a \cdot (V/I)$ ŌĆö Depth $\approx a$.
- Ō£ģ **Schlumberger $\rho_a$:** $\dfrac{\pi (L^{2} - l^{2})}{2l} \cdot (V/I) \approx \dfrac{\pi L^{2}}{2l} \cdot (V/I)$ ŌĆö Depth $\approx AB/2$.
- Ō£ģ **Dipole-Dipole $\rho_a$:** $\pi n(n+1)(n+2)a \cdot (V/I)$ ŌĆö Best for fractures.
- Ō£ģ **Pole-Pole $\rho_a$:** $2\pi a \cdot (V/I)$ ŌĆö One electrode at infinity.
- Ō£ģ **Apparent vs. True Resistivity:** $\rho_a$ = weighted average; **NOT** a true layer value.
- Ō£ģ **Master Curve Types:** **H** (low middle), **K** (high middle), **A** (increasing), **Q** (decreasing).
- Ō£ģ **Aquifer Indicator:** Low apparent resistivity ($\rho_a < 100 \, \Omega \cdot m$) in sedimentary terrain.
- Ō£ģ **Bedrock Indicator:** $\rho_a > 1000 \, \Omega \cdot m$ ŌåÆ crystalline basement.
- Ō£ģ **Field Quality Rule:** $MN \le AB/5$ (Schlumberger) to maintain point-electrode approximation.
- Ō£ģ **Inversion Software Names:** IPI2Win, Res1D, ZondRes2D, AGI EarthImager.
- Ō£ģ **Units Check:** $\rho$ in **╬®┬Ęm**, $K$ in **m**, $\Delta V$ in **V**, $I$ in **A**.
- Ō£ģ **Survey Types:** VES (depth) and Profiling (lateral).
- Ō£ģ **Archie's Law** (for clean sands): $\rho = a \cdot \phi^{-m} \cdot S^{-n} \cdot \rho_w$ ŌĆö useful for saturation calculations.
- Ō£ģ **Kerala Relevance:** Schlumberger VES is the **standard** for fractured hard-rock aquifer mapping.
- Ō£ģ **Assumptions for derivation:** Homogeneous, isotropic, semi-infinite, point source, hemispherical equipotentials, steady-state DC.

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤Ä» Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions, **always derive, then interpret, then comment on lithology**. A numerical without a lithological comment loses at least 2 marks. The examiner rewards **engineering judgment**, not just arithmetic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subsurface Investigation of Groundwater - Test Drilling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Subsurface Investigation of Groundwater - Test Drilling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Subsurface Investigation of Groundwater** is the systematic engineering process of determining the geological, hydrological, and geotechnical characteristics of the strata lying beneath the Earth's surface to evaluate the presence, quantity, quality, and extractability of groundwater. It encompasses geological reconnaissance, geophysical surveys, and direct physical exploration through **Test Drilling**.

**Test Drilling** is defined as the controlled, engineered operation of penetrating the Earth's subsurface using mechanical tools (drill bits, augers, hammers) to create a borehole of specified diameter and depth for the purposes of:
1. Identifying aquifer geometry (depth, thickness, lateral extent).
2. Collecting subsurface samples (cuttings and cores).
3. Conducting in-situ tests (pumping tests, packer tests, geophysical logging).
4. Installing observation wells and production wells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Test drilling is the *most reliable* method of subsurface investigation because it provides *direct* physical evidence of subsurface conditions, unlike geophysical methods which provide *indirect* inferences.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor may use external scans (X-rays, MRI) ŌĆö these are like **geophysical surveys**, giving indirect hints about what lies inside. But when the doctor needs absolute certainty (e.g., to take a biopsy), a needle is inserted to physically extract a sample ŌĆö this is **Test Drilling**. The drill bit is the "medical needle" of hydrogeology, and the rock/soil cuttings it brings up are the "biopsy tissue" that confirm exactly what lies beneath.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Borehole Diameter for Exploration Wells:** **150 mm to 300 mm**
- **Standard Production Well Diameter:** **100 mm to 600 mm**
- **Average Penetration Rate (Rotary Drilling in Soft Rock):** **5 m/hr to 15 m/hr**
- **Standard Casing Diameter Nominal (Nominal Bore):** **50 mm to 600 mm**
- **Drilling Fluid Density (Bentonite Mud):** **1.05 g/cm┬│ to 1.20 g/cm┬│**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition - Aquifer:** A saturated permeable geological unit that yields economically significant quantities of water to wells.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Test Drilling Methods

Test drilling methods are classified based on the mechanism of rock/soil disintegration and removal. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes four primary categories:

### 2.1.1 Percussion Drilling (Cable-Tool Drilling)
- **Mechanism:** A heavy drill bit is repeatedly raised and dropped (cable percussion), crushing and breaking the rock by impact.
- **Best Suited For:** Boulders, hard rock, and fractured formations.
- **Sample Type:** Recovered as coarse chips and sludge.
- **Drilling Fluid:** Water or drilling mud circulated to bring cuttings to surface.

### 2.1.2 Rotary Drilling
- **Mechanism:** A rotating bit (drag bit, roller cone, or diamond bit) grinds the rock. Drilling fluid (mud or air) is circulated to cool the bit and lift cuttings.
- **Best Suited For:** Soft to medium-hard formations; deep boreholes.
- **Sample Type:** Continuous chips via circulating fluid; cores with special core barrels.
- **Sub-types:** Direct Rotary (fluid down through bit) and Reverse Rotary (fluid up through bit).

### 2.1.3 Auger Drilling
- **Mechanism:** A helical screw (auger) is rotated into soft soil, conveying cuttings up the flights.
- **Best Suited For:** Unconsolidated formations (clay, silt, sand) above water table.
- **Sample Type:** Disturbed but representative samples on auger flights.
- **Limitation:** Cannot penetrate hard rock or below water table effectively.

### 2.1.4 Jet Drilling / Hydraulic Drilling
- **Mechanism:** High-velocity water jets dislodge sediments; cuttings are lifted by upward flow.
- **Best Suited For:** Unconsolidated alluvial deposits, especially in deltaic regions.
- **Depth Limit:** Generally shallow (up to 30ŌĆō50 m).

## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Is Done | How It Is Done |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| **Site Selection** | Maximize success probability | Based on geological maps, resistivity surveys, water-table contour maps |
| **Pilot Hole Drilling** | Direct stratigraphic logging | Small-diameter hole (100ŌĆō150 mm) drilled first |
| **Casing Installation** | Prevent borehole collapse in soft zones | Steel/PVC casing lowered progressively |
| **Core Sampling** | Obtain undisturbed rock samples | Core barrel attached above bit retrieves cylindrical cores |
| **Geophysical Logging** | Cross-verify lithology | Tools (electric, gamma, sonic) lowered to log physical properties |
| **Well Development** | Remove drilling mud fines, improve yield | Surging, jetting, pumping, air-lifting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The **drilling fluid (mud)** serves three critical functions: (1) Bit cooling and lubrication, (2) Cuttings transport to surface, (3) Borehole wall stabilization through filter cake formation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Penetration Rate | $PR = \dfrac{D}{T}$ where $D$ = depth, $T$ = time | m/hr | Drilling efficiency |
| 2 | Drilling Cost | $C_{total} = C_{rig} + C_{bit} + C_{mud} + C_{casing} + C_{labour}$ | Ōé╣/m | Economic feasibility |
| 3 | Specific Capacity (Theis) | $S_c = \dfrac{Q}{s_w}$ | m┬│/hr/m | Aquifer productivity |
| 4 | Hydraulic Conductivity (Slug Test) | $K = \dfrac{r^2 \ln(L/R)}{2L} \cdot \dfrac{1}{t_{37\%}}$ | m/s | Quick in-situ K |
| 5 | Darcy's Law | $Q = K \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dl}$ | m┬│/s | Groundwater flow |
| 6 | Transmissivity | $T = K \cdot b$ | m┬▓/s | Aquifer parameter |
| 7 | Storativity | $S = S_s \cdot b$ | dimensionless | Confined aquifer |
| 8 | Mud Weight | $\gamma_m = \rho_m \cdot g$ | N/m┬│ | Pressure balance |
| 9 | Bit Weight (WOB) on Penetration | $R \propto \dfrac{WOB}{N \cdot D_b}$ | ŌĆö | Penetration empirical |
| 10 | Reynolds Number (Mud Flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | ŌĆö | Flow regime check |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $Q$ = discharge, $s_w$ = drawdown, $r$ = well radius, $L$ = screen length, $R$ = radius of influence, $b$ = aquifer thickness, $K$ = hydraulic conductivity, $S_s$ = specific storage.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Test drilling is the backbone of:
- **Municipal water supply schemes** (e.g., Kerala Water Authority borewells).
- **Irrigation projects** in Palakkad, Thrissur, and Kuttanad regions.
- **Industrial water procurement** (Cochin Port, FACT, KSPCB regulated zones).
- **Contaminated site remediation** ŌĆö isolating polluted aquifers.
- **Geothermal energy exploration** ŌĆö drilling deep wells in high heat-flow zones.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Penetration Rate (Rotary Drilling)

The penetration rate in rotary drilling is empirically modelled as:

$$R = k \cdot \dfrac{(WOB)^a \cdot (RPM)^b}{(D_b)^c}$$

Where:
- $R$ = penetration rate (m/hr)
- $WOB$ = Weight on Bit (kg or kN)
- $RPM$ = Rotary speed (revolutions per minute)
- $D_b$ = Bit diameter (mm)
- $k$, $a$, $b$, $c$ = formation-specific empirical constants (typically $a \approx 1$, $b \approx 1$, $c \approx 0.5$ to 1).

**Step-by-step logical derivation:**

**Step 1:** Energy delivered per unit time to the rock interface by the bit is:

$$E_{input} = WOB \cdot v_{bit}$$

where $v_{bit} = \pi D_b \cdot RPM \cdot t$ is the linear velocity at the bit edge.

**Step 2:** Energy required to fracture a unit volume of rock is approximately constant for a given lithology (specific energy, $E_s$):

$$E_s = \dfrac{\text{Energy to fracture}}{\text{Volume removed}}$$

**Step 3:** The volume removed per unit time is $R \cdot A_b$ where $A_b = \dfrac{\pi D_b^2}{4}$ is the bit face area.

**Step 4:** Equating input energy to output energy:

$$WOB \cdot \pi D_b \cdot RPM = E_s \cdot R \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_b^2}{4}$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $R$:

$$R = \dfrac{4 \cdot WOB \cdot RPM}{E_s \cdot D_b}$$

This gives the classic **inverse proportionality between penetration rate and bit diameter**, which is why larger diameter bits drill slower per unit length.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Drilling Mud Pressure Balance

To prevent a **blow-out** (formation pressure exceeding mud pressure) or a **lost circulation** (mud pressure fracturing the formation), the mud column pressure must satisfy:

$$P_{mud} = \rho_m \cdot g \cdot H_m$$

$$P_{formation} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot H_w + P_{pore}$$

**Condition for safe drilling:**

$$P_{pore} < P_{mud} < P_{frac}$$

Where:
- $\rho_m$ = mud density (**typically 1050ŌĆō1200 kg/m┬│**)
- $\rho_w$ = water density (**1000 kg/m┬│**)
- $H_m$ = mud column height (m)
- $H_w$ = water column height in formation (m)
- $P_{pore}$ = pore pressure (Pa)
- $P_{frac}$ = formation fracture pressure (Pa)

**Margin of safety:**

$$\Delta P = P_{mud} - P_{pore}$$

A safe margin is $\Delta P \geq 0.5$ MPa for shallow wells and $\geq 1.0$ MPa for deep wells.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Capacity from Step-Drawdown Test Data

The specific capacity is derived from Theis-Jacob approximation:

$$s_w = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{2.25 T t}{r^2 S}\right) \right]$$

Solving for specific capacity $S_c = Q / s_w$:

$$S_c = \dfrac{4\pi T}{\ln\left(\dfrac{2.25 T t}{r^2 S}\right)}$$

This shows that $S_c$ increases (well is more efficient) when:
- Transmissivity $T$ is high.
- Well radius $r$ is large.
- Time since pumping started $t$ is large.
- Storativity $S$ is high.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Choosing the Right Drilling Method

| Formation Type | Recommended Method | Reason | Typical Cost (Ōé╣/m) |
|----------------|--------------------|--------|---------------------|
| Soft clay / silt | Auger | Cheap, fast, no casing needed | 500ŌĆō1000 |
| Alluvial sand (above WT) | Auger / Jet | Easy penetration | 800ŌĆō1500 |
| Alluvial sand (below WT) | Rotary with mud | Stabilizes hole | 1500ŌĆō2500 |
| Soft rock (sandstone, shale) | Rotary (roller cone) | Efficient grinding | 2000ŌĆō3500 |
| Hard rock (granite, basalt) | Rotary (diamond/DTH) | Only effective method | 3500ŌĆō6000 |
| Boulders / fractured rock | Percussion (DTH) | Impact crushing | 3000ŌĆō5000 |
| Highly fractured zones | Core drilling | Detailed lithology | 4000ŌĆō8000 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Test Drilling Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Selection] --> B[Geological Reconnaissance]
    B --> C[Geophysical Survey]
    C --> D[Locate Drilling Spot]
    D --> E[Rig Mobilization]
    E --> F[Drill Pilot Hole]
    F --> G[Collect Cuttings Sample]
    G --> H{Formation Stable?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Continue Drilling]
    H -- No --> J[Install Casing]
    J --> I
    I --> K[Reach Target Aquifer Depth]
    K --> L[Conduct Packer or Pumping Test]
    L --> M[Geophysical Logging]
    M --> N[Install Well Screen and Casing]
    N --> O[Well Development]
    O --> P[Disinfection and Yield Test]
    P --> Q[Hand Over to Client]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Rotary Drilling Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerTrain["POWER AND DRIVE SYSTEM"]
        P1[Diesel Engine] --> P2[Transmission]
        P2 --> P3[Rotary Table]
    end

    subgraph HoistSys["HOISTING SYSTEM"]
        H1[Mast or Derrick] --> H2[Draw works]
        H2 --> H3[Drill String]
    end

    subgraph FluidSys["CIRCULATION SYSTEM"]
        F1[Mud Pump] --> F2[Swivel]
        F2 --> F3[Drill Pipe]
        F3 --> F4[Drill Bit]
        F4 --> F5[Annulus Return]
        F5 --> F6[Shale Shaker]
        F6 --> F7[Mud Pit]
        F7 --> F1
    end

    P3 -.rotates.-> H3
    H3 -.lowers.-> F3
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart ŌĆö Selecting Drilling Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Formation Type?] --> S1{Unconsolidated?}
    S1 -- Yes, above WT --> S2[Auger Drilling]
    S1 -- Yes, below WT --> S3[Rotary with Mud]
    S1 -- No --> S4{Consolidated Rock?}
    S4 -- Hard Rock --> S5{Diameter Needed?}
    S5 -- Small --> S6[Diamond Core Drilling]
    S5 -- Large --> S7[DTH Hammer Rotary]
    S4 -- Soft-Medium --> S8[Rotary Roller Cone]
    S2 --> SE[End]
    S3 --> SE
    S6 --> SE
    S7 --> SE
    S8 --> SE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define test drilling. List any four objectives of test drilling in groundwater investigations.**

**Model Answer:**
Test drilling is the engineered process of creating boreholes through subsurface formations using mechanical tools to explore, evaluate, and develop groundwater resources.

**Objectives of Test Drilling:**
1. To determine the depth, thickness, and lateral extent of aquifers.
2. To collect representative samples of subsurface formations (cuttings and cores).
3. To perform in-situ tests such as pumping tests and slug tests for aquifer parameters.
4. To install observation wells and production wells for water supply.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Briefly explain the principle of rotary drilling. Mention two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**
**Principle:** Rotary drilling works on the principle of abrasion and grinding. A rotating drill bit (roller cone or diamond) grinds the rock into small chips. Simultaneously, drilling mud is pumped down through the drill pipe, exits through the bit nozzles, cools the bit, and carries the cuttings up through the annular space to the surface for collection and logging.

**Advantages:**
1. Fast penetration rate, suitable for deep boreholes.
2. Provides continuous samples through the circulated mud and allows in-situ geophysical logging.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe in detail the different methods of test drilling with their suitability, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The major methods of test drilling are:

**1. Percussion (Cable-Tool) Drilling**
A heavy drill bit is alternately raised and dropped on the bottom of the hole, crushing the rock by impact. The crushed material is mixed with water to form sludge, which is bailed out at intervals.
- *Suitability:* Hard rock, boulder formations, and fractured zones.
- *Advantages:* Simple equipment; works in formations where other methods fail; low water requirement.
- *Limitations:* Slow; cannot obtain undisturbed samples; limited depth.

**2. Rotary Drilling**
A rotating bit grinds the rock while drilling mud is continuously circulated.
- *Suitability:* Soft to medium-hard rocks; deep boreholes.
- *Advantages:* Fast, continuous sampling, possible geophysical logging, suitable for unconsolidated formations with proper mud control.
- *Limitations:* High cost; requires skilled operators; mud contamination of aquifer.

**3. Auger Drilling**
A helical screw auger is rotated into the ground, and cuttings ride up the flights.
- *Suitability:* Soft soils above water table; shallow geotechnical investigations.
- *Advantages:* Cheap, fast, mobile; no drilling fluid needed.
- *Limitations:* Cannot drill hard rock; not usable below water table; samples are disturbed.

**4. Jet Drilling**
High-velocity water jet loosens sediment and carries it up by hydraulic flow.
- *Suitability:* Loose alluvial deposits; deltaic regions.
- *Advantages:* Very fast in soft alluvium.
- *Limitations:* Shallow depth only; requires large water supply.

**(b)** Explain the role of drilling mud in rotary drilling. List the properties of an ideal drilling mud. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Drilling Mud:**
1. **Cuttings Transport:** Lifts rock chips from the bit to the surface via the annulus.
2. **Bit Cooling:** Prevents overheating of the drill bit due to friction.
3. **Borehole Stabilization:** The mud forms a thin impermeable **filter cake** on the borehole wall, preventing collapse.
4. **Pressure Control:** Hydrostatic pressure of the mud column balances formation pore pressure, preventing blowouts or lost circulation.
5. **Lubrication:** Reduces torque and friction on the drill string.

**Properties of an Ideal Drilling Mud:**
1. Appropriate viscosity (typically **30ŌĆō50 seconds per quart** Marsh funnel).
2. Density in the range **1.05 to 1.20 g/cm┬│**.
3. Low fluid loss (filter cake thickness < 1 mm).
4. Chemical stability and inertness to formation minerals.
5. Easy availability and low cost (bentonite + water is standard).
6. Non-corrosive to drill string.
7. Ability to suspend cuttings when circulation stops (thixotropic behavior).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the various steps involved in the conduct of a systematic test drilling programme for groundwater exploration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A systematic test drilling programme involves the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 ŌĆō Desk Study:** Review existing geological maps, hydrogeological reports, and remote sensing data for the target area.

**Step 2 ŌĆō Site Reconnaissance:** Field visit to verify map data, identify land use, accessibility, and surface water features.

**Step 3 ŌĆō Geophysical Survey:** Conduct electrical resistivity, seismic refraction, or electromagnetic surveys to identify promising drilling locations.

**Step 4 ŌĆō Pilot Borehole Drilling:** Drill a small-diameter exploratory hole (100ŌĆō150 mm) at the most promising site.

**Step 5 ŌĆō Lithological Logging:** Record depth-wise samples collected at every 1ŌĆō2 m interval. Prepare a stratigraphic column.

**Step 6 ŌĆō Casing Installation:** Lower temporary casing through unstable zones to prevent collapse.

**Step 7 ŌĆō Aquifer Testing:** Conduct pumping test, slug test, or packer test to determine $T$, $S$, and $K$.

**Step 8 ŌĆō Geophysical Logging:** Run electric, gamma, and sonic logs to cross-verify lithology and identify productive zones.

**Step 9 ŌĆō Well Construction:** Install permanent casing, screen, gravel pack, and sanitary seal.

**Step 10 ŌĆō Well Development and Yield Testing:** Surging, air-lifting, and pumping to determine sustainable yield.

**Step 11 ŌĆō Documentation and Reporting:** Compile litholog, test data, well design, and recommendations.

**(b)** A rotary drilling rig penetrates **45 m** of overburden and **30 m** of weathered rock in **12 hours** total. The rig operates at **120 RPM** with a weight on bit of **1500 kg**. The bit diameter is **150 mm**. Calculate the average penetration rate and the energy expenditure per metre of drilling. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Overburden depth: $D_1 = 45$ m
- Weathered rock depth: $D_2 = 30$ m
- Total depth: $D = D_1 + D_2 = 75$ m
- Total time: $T = 12$ hr
- RPM: $N = 120$ rev/min
- WOB: $W = 1500$ kg
- Bit diameter: $D_b = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m

**Step 1 ŌĆö Average Penetration Rate:**

$$PR = \dfrac{D}{T} = \dfrac{75}{12} = 6.25 \text{ m/hr}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Linear Velocity at Bit Edge:**

$$v_{bit} = \pi \cdot D_b \cdot N$$

$$v_{bit} = \pi \cdot 0.150 \cdot 120 = 56.55 \text{ m/min} = 3.393 \text{ km/hr}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Work Done Per Unit Time (Power Input):**

$$P = W \cdot v_{bit}$$

Converting WOB to force using $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓:
$$F = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715 \text{ N}$$

Converting velocity to SI: $v_{bit} = 56.55/60 = 0.9425$ m/s.

$$P = 14715 \times 0.9425 = 13869 \text{ W} \approx 13.87 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Energy Per Metre of Drilling:**

Total energy in 12 hours:

$$E_{total} = P \times T = 13869 \times (12 \times 3600)$$

$$E_{total} = 13869 \times 43200 = 5.99 \times 10^{8} \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Energy Per Metre:**

$$E_{per\,m} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{D} = \dfrac{5.99 \times 10^8}{75}$$

$$E_{per\,m} \approx 7.99 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/m} \approx 7.99 \text{ MJ/m}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Average penetration rate = **6.25 m/hr**
- Energy expenditure per metre = **Ōēł 7.99 MJ/m**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting RPM to rad/s vs rev/min vs Hz incorrectly. Always show conversion steps explicitly.
> 2. **Ignoring $g$ in WOB:** Students frequently forget to multiply kg by 9.81 to get Newtons. KTU examiners explicitly award the unit mark only when force is in SI units.
> 3. **Confusing Penetration Rate with Drilling Rate:** Penetration rate is depth/time; drilling rate includes bit-wear and re-drilling. Use $PR$ for first-pass drilling.
> 4. **Skipping the Mud Pressure Balance:** In long-answer questions on rotary drilling, students often omit the mud-pressure balance derivation ŌĆö losing 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 5. **Not Labelling Mermaid Diagrams:** Even in theory answers, KTU expects labelled diagrams. Always include a labelled schematic of the drilling rig or borehole profile.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Test drilling is the *direct physical* method of subsurface investigation; it is the most reliable but most expensive.
- **Four Main Methods:** Auger (soft soil), Jet (alluvium), Rotary (versatile, deep), Percussion (hard rock).
- **Rotary Drilling Principle:** Rotation + abrasion + mud circulation.
- **Mud Functions (5):** Cuttings transport, bit cooling, hole stabilization, pressure control, lubrication.
- **Key Drilling Parameters:** Penetration rate, WOB, RPM, bit diameter, mud density.
- **Critical Formula:** $PR = D/T$ and Darcy's Law $Q = K A (dh/dl)$.
- **Penetration Rate Relationship:** Inversely proportional to bit diameter; directly proportional to WOB and RPM.
- **Sample Types:** Cuttings (disturbed, continuous) vs Cores (undisturbed, discrete).
- **Standard Borehole Diameters:** Exploration = **150ŌĆō300 mm**; Production = **100ŌĆō600 mm**.
- **Mud Density Standard:** **1.05ŌĆō1.20 g/cm┬│** for bentonite-water mud.
- **Site Selection Inputs:** Geological maps + Geophysical survey + Hydrogeological data.
- **Standard Sequence:** Recon ŌåÆ Geophysical ŌåÆ Pilot drill ŌåÆ Log ŌåÆ Test ŌåÆ Construct ŌåÆ Develop ŌåÆ Yield test.
- **Cost Order (Low to High):** Auger < Jet < Percussion < Rotary < Core drilling.
- **KTU Favourite Topics:** Rotary drilling principle, mud functions, penetration rate derivation, comparison table of drilling methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Level Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Level Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Level Measurement** in a well is the determination of the vertical distance from a fixed datum (typically Mean Sea Level, MSL, or the measuring point, MP) to the free water surface inside the well. It is a fundamental field activity in **groundwater hydrology** that allows the construction of water-table maps, estimation of aquifer parameters, computation of hydraulic gradients, and evaluation of well yields.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, two principal levels are defined:

- **Static Water Level (SWL)** ŌĆö The level of water in a well when no water is being withdrawn and the level has stabilized. It represents the natural potentiometric surface of the aquifer at that location.
- **Dynamic Water Level (DWL)** ŌĆö The level of water in a well during pumping at a constant discharge rate, after drawdown has stabilized.
- **Piezometric Head** ŌĆö The elevation to which water rises in a tightly cased well tapping a confined aquifer, expressed in metres above MSL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Water level measurement is the precursor to almost every Module 4 & 5 calculation ŌĆö *specific yield, transmissivity (T), storage coefficient (S), drawdown, well interference, and safe yield.* Even a 1 cm error here propagates into enormous errors in aquifer parameter estimation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

Imagine you have a transparent drinking straw dipped in a glass of water. The water inside the straw climbs up to the same level as the water in the glass ŌĆö that level represents the **piezometric surface**. Now, if you put your finger on top of the straw and lift it out, the water inside stays at exactly the height where it was in the glass.

An observation well behaves exactly like that straw ŌĆö it is simply a small "window" cut into the aquifer, allowing the hydrogeologist to peek inside and measure where the groundwater surface actually sits. Whether the aquifer is confined or unconfined, the well faithfully records the pressure/elevation conditions of the water-bearing formation around it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why measure water levels?**
> - To delineate aquifer boundaries
> - To compute hydraulic gradient ($i$) for Darcy's Law
> - To estimate safe yield of a basin
> - To monitor seasonal/annual groundwater fluctuations
> - To forecast drought or contamination spread

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static Water Level | SWL | m below MP / m above MSL | Pre-pumping equilibrium level |
| Dynamic Water Level | DWL | m below MP | Level during pumping |
| Drawdown | $s$ | m | $s = \text{SWL} - \text{DWL}$ |
| Measuring Point | MP | m above MSL | Reference for tape readings |
| Piezometric Head | $h$ | m above MSL | Elevation of water in confined well |
| Hydraulic Gradient | $i$ | dimensionless | $i = \Delta h / L$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All depth measurements in India are conventionally reported in **metres (m)** as per the **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)** and **IS 13952 (1998)** standards. The datum is the **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** at Mumbai.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Water Level Measurement Methods

Water level measurement methods are broadly divided into **Direct (Manual)** and **Indirect (Automatic/Remote)** categories.

### A. Direct Methods (Operator physically present at the well)

#### 1. Wetted Tape Method (Steel Tape Method)
- A graduated **steel tape** with a weight (plumb bob) at its end is lowered into the well.
- The lower end is dipped into water; on withdrawal, the wetted portion indicates the water level.
- **Accuracy:** $\pm$ 2 mm (industry standard for small-diameter observation wells).
- **Best suited for:** shallow wells (< 60 m), clear water.

#### 2. Chalked Tape Method
- The lower 1ŌĆō2 m of the steel tape is **chalked** (coated with carpenter's chalk).
- When lowered, water dissolves the chalk up to the water level.
- On withdrawal, the wetted-chalk boundary is read against the MP.
- **Accuracy:** $\pm$ 5 mm ŌĆö slightly better than wetted tape in turbulent conditions.

#### 3. Air-Line Method (Pneumatic Method)
- A small-diameter **air-line tube** is permanently installed inside the well, with its submerged end fixed near the bottom.
- Air is pumped in via a hand pump or foot pump; pressure builds up until it just displaces water out of the tube.
- The reading on a **pressure gauge** (in kg/cm┬▓ or psi) is converted to water-column height.
- **Formula used in KTU problems:**

$$h = \frac{P \times 10.197}{1} \quad \text{(metres of water column)}$$

where $P$ is gauge pressure in **kg/cm┬▓**, and 10.197 m is the head equivalent of 1 kg/cm┬▓. (Note: 1 kg/cm┬▓ = 98.066 kPa = 9.8066 m of water at 20┬░C.)

#### 4. Float Method
- A **float** rests on the water surface, connected by a cable/pulley to a recording drum.
- Suitable for continuous recording in large-diameter dug wells.

### B. Indirect / Automatic Methods

#### 5. Electrical Sounder (Whirling Sounder / "Bleep" Method)
- A two-conductor cable with a stainless-steel probe at its end.
- When the probe contacts water, the circuit closes ŌĆö triggering a **buzzer, light, or galvanometer** at the surface.
- The depth is read directly off the graduated cable.
- **Accuracy:** $\pm$ 1 cm; standard CGWB instrument.

#### 6. Pressure Transducer (Data Logger)
- A submerged **piezoresistive or strain-gauge sensor** measures hydrostatic pressure at a fixed depth.
- Output: 4ŌĆō20 mA current or digital SDI-12 signal.
- Pressure is converted to water level using:

$$\text{Water Level} = \frac{P_{\text{measured}} - P_{\text{atm}}}{\rho g} + \text{Installation Depth}$$

- **Use:** Real-time monitoring networks (e.g., CGWB's *Naquim* programme).

#### 7. Acoustic / Sonic Water-Level Sounder
- A **sonar pulse** is emitted from a transducer at the wellhead; the time-of-flight to the water surface is converted to depth.
- **Limitation:** Requires open water surface (no sealed cap), and accurate knowledge of sound velocity in the well air column (Ōēł 330 m/s at 20┬░C).

#### 8. Radar (Microwave) Sensor
- Non-contact method using **FMCW (Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave)** radar.
- Highly accurate (┬▒1 mm), immune to foam, mist, and temperature gradients.
- Used in modern CGWB telemetric stations.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Air-Line Method (KTU Most-Loved Question)

The air-line method is conceptually a U-tube manometer where compressed air replaces the heavier fluid. The logic is:

1. Seal the top end of the air-line tube at the wellhead.
2. Inject compressed air slowly through a pressure gauge.
3. When air pressure equals the hydrostatic pressure of water at the submerged open end, water begins to bubble out.
4. The **submergence depth** of the air-line's open end below static water level is calculated from the recorded gauge pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 answer must clearly state that the gauge pressure recorded must be **subtracted** by the head loss in the air-line tube (negligible if tube is < 30 m and flow is slow) and **added** to the MP elevation to obtain the absolute water level above MSL.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical**. Memorise units and conversion factors. *Never use `|` in your answer script ŌĆö KTU scanners often mis-read it as `I`.*

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s = \text{SWL} - \text{DWL}$ | Drawdown | m |
| 2 | $h = P \times 10.197$ | Air-line gauge pressure $\rightarrow$ water column (using $P$ in kg/cm┬▓) | m of water |
| 3 | $h = P \,/\, (9.81 \times 10^{-4})$ | Pressure in kPa $\rightarrow$ metres of water | m |
| 4 | $i = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L}$ | Hydraulic gradient (Darcy's Law input) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q = T \cdot i \cdot W$ | Groundwater discharge through section width $W$ | m┬│/day |
| 6 | $T = K \cdot b$ | Transmissivity | m┬▓/day |
| 7 | $\text{SWL (MSL)} = \text{MP} - d$ | Convert tape reading ($d$) to MSL datum | m above MSL |
| 8 | $V = K \cdot i$ | Darcy velocity | m/day |
| 9 | $V_a = V / n_e$ | Actual (seepage) velocity, $n_e$ = effective porosity | m/day |
| 10 | $S_y = \dfrac{V_{\text{drained}}}{V_{\text{total aquifer}}}$ | Specific yield | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CGWB National Aquifer Mapping (NAQUIM):** Uses transducer-equipped piezometers to build seasonal water-level fluctuation maps.
- **Irrigation Engineering:** Pre-monsoon and post-monsoon SWL data determine **Net Groundwater Availability (NGWA)** and **Stage of Groundwater Extraction**.
- **Contaminant Hydrology:** Piezometric contour maps trace **groundwater flow direction** ŌĆö critical for designing landfills and sewage farms.
- **Construction Engineering:** Dewatering design for deep basements requires precise DWLŌĆōdrawdown relationships.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of Air-Line Pressure to Water Column

**Given:** A well is equipped with an air-line whose open end is submerged at depth $h$ below the static water level. Compressed air is injected until the gauge reads $P$.

**To Derive:** The relationship between $P$ and $h$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Pressure Balance at the Open End of the Air-Line:**
At the instant air begins to bubble out, the air pressure inside the tube equals the hydrostatic pressure of the water column above the open end.

$$P_{\text{air}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Note that the gauge measures *gauge* pressure, not absolute:**

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{air}} - P_{\text{atm}} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $h$:**

$$h = \frac{P_{\text{gauge}}}{\rho_w \cdot g}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:**

$$h = \frac{P_{\text{gauge}}}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{P_{\text{gauge}}}{9810} \quad \text{(P in Pa, h in m)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Convert to engineering units ($P$ in kg/cm┬▓):**
Since $1 \text{ kg/cm}^2 = 9.8066 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$,

$$h = \frac{P \,(\text{kg/cm}^2) \times 9.8066 \times 10^4}{9810} = P \times 10.197 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\,h = 10.197 \cdot P \quad \text{(with P in kg/cm}^2\text{)}\,}$$

This is the standard KTU air-line equation, valid at **20┬░C**. A temperature correction of $\pm 0.2\%$ per ┬░C is applied for deep wells where the air column temperature differs significantly.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** An observation well is fitted with an air-line. The lower open end of the air-line is at an elevation of **+18.40 m above MSL**. During measurement, the pressure gauge reads **2.80 kg/cm┬▓**. The measuring point (MP) is at **+25.00 m above MSL**. Compute the static water level (i) in metres below the MP, and (ii) in metres above MSL.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the submergence depth $h$ of water column above the air-line's open end.

$$h = 10.197 \times 2.80 = 28.55 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the elevation of SWL above MSL.

$$\text{SWL (MSL)} = \text{Elevation of air-line open end} + h$$

$$\text{SWL (MSL)} = 18.40 + 28.55 = 46.95 \text{ m above MSL}$$

**Step 3:** Compute SWL in metres below MP.

$$\text{SWL depth below MP} = \text{MP} - \text{SWL (MSL)}$$

$$\text{SWL depth below MP} = 25.00 - 46.95 = -21.95 \text{ m}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that the static water level is **21.95 m ABOVE the measuring point** ŌĆö a classic KTU trick question where students forget to check whether the water is above or below the MP. A negative answer here is perfectly valid.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Measurement Methods

| Method | Accuracy | Max Depth (m) | Cost | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wetted Tape | ┬▒2 mm | 60 | Low | Shallow observation wells |
| Chalked Tape | ┬▒5 mm | 60 | Low | Turbid water, shallow wells |
| Air-Line | ┬▒50 mm | 300 | Medium | Deep irrigation wells, continuous discharge |
| Float Recorder | ┬▒10 mm | 100 | Medium | Large-diameter dug wells, continuous recording |
| Electrical Sounder | ┬▒10 mm | 600 | Medium-Low | CGWB observation network |
| Pressure Transducer | ┬▒5 mm | 600 | High | Telemetric monitoring stations |
| Acoustic Sounder | ┬▒3 mm | 300 | Medium | Open wells, rapid surveys |
| Radar Sensor | ┬▒1 mm | 70 | Very High | Modern automated stations |

## 3.4 KTU Worked Problem: Hydraulic Gradient from Three Wells

> **Problem:** Three observation wells A, B, C aligned in a northŌĆōsouth direction record SWLs (m above MSL) as **+85.20, +86.50, and +84.10** respectively. The distances are: AŌĆōB = 400 m, BŌĆōC = 600 m. Compute the hydraulic gradient between A and C, and the direction of groundwater flow.

**Step 1:** Head difference between A and C:

$$\Delta h = h_A - h_C = 85.20 - 84.10 = 1.10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Total distance:

$$L_{AC} = 400 + 600 = 1000 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Hydraulic gradient:

$$i = \frac{\Delta h}{L} = \frac{1.10}{1000} = 1.10 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0011$$

**Step 4:** Direction of flow ŌĆö **from higher head to lower head**, i.e., from A ŌåÆ C (southward).

$$\boxed{\,i = 1.10 \times 10^{-3}, \text{ flow direction: A to C (south)}\,}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Water Level Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aquifer Formation] --> B[Observation Well Casing]
    B --> C[Water Surface inside Well]
    C --> D[Measuring Instrument Selection]
    D --> E1[Wetted / Chalked Tape]
    D --> E2[Air-Line Tube System]
    D --> E3[Electrical Sounder]
    D --> E4[Pressure Transducer]
    D --> E5[Radar / Acoustic Sensor]
    E1 --> F[Raw Depth Reading - m below MP]
    E2 --> G[Pressure Value - kg/cm2]
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> H[Voltage - 4 to 20 mA]
    E5 --> I[Time of Flight - microseconds]
    F --> J[Datapoint: SWL or DWL]
    G --> K[Convert P to head via 10.197 factor]
    K --> J
    H --> L[Convert V to hydrostatic head]
    L --> J
    I --> M[Convert time to distance using 330 m per second]
    M --> J
    J --> N[Reference to MSL Datum]
    N --> O[Plot Piezometric Contour Map]
    O --> P[Compute Hydraulic Gradient]
    P --> Q[Apply Darcy Law - Q equals T times i times W]
    Q --> R[Estimate Safe Yield of Basin]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Air-Line Method Pressure Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WELLHEAD [Wellhead Assembly]
        P[Pressure Gauge] -->|reads| VALVE
        VALVE[Control Valve] -->|feeds air| TUBE
    end
    TUBE[Air-Line Tube] -->|descends inside| CASING
    subgraph SUBMERGED [Submerged Portion of Tube]
        CASING[Well Casing] --> OPENEND
        OPENEND[Open End of Air-Line]
        OPENEND -->|depth h below SWL| SWL
    end
    SWL[Static Water Level] --> WATER[Aquifer Water Column]
    AIR[Compressed Air Column] -->|pressure P equals rho g h| OPENEND
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to measure water level] --> Q1{Well depth in metres}
    Q1 -->|less than 60| Q2{Water clarity}
    Q1 -->|between 60 and 300| Q3{Continuous monitoring needed}
    Q1 -->|greater than 300| Q5{Pumping discharge in progress}
    Q2 -->|Clear| USE_TAPE[Use Wetted or Chalked Tape]
    Q2 -->|Turbid| USE_FLOAT[Use Float Method]
    Q3 -->|Yes| USE_TRANSDUCER[Use Pressure Transducer]
    Q3 -->|No| USE_SONIC[Use Acoustic Sounder]
    Q5 -->|Yes - well is pumping| USE_AIRLINE[Use Air-Line Method]
    Q5 -->|No - static condition| USE_RADAR[Use Radar or Electrical Sounder]
```
