<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Static Water Level (SWL) and Dynamic Water Level (DWL). Why is the latter always deeper than the former in a pumping well?

**Model Answer:**

- **Static Water Level (SWL):** The level of water in a well under natural, undisturbed equilibrium conditions, when there is no pumping or recharge. It represents the prevailing potentiometric surface of the aquifer at that point.
- **Dynamic Water Level (DWL):** The level to which water drops inside a well during pumping at a constant discharge rate, *after* the drawdown has stabilised (typically within 4ŌĆō8 hours for confined aquifers and 24ŌĆō72 hours for unconfined aquifers).

**Why DWL is always deeper than SWL:**
When pumping begins, water is removed from the well faster than the aquifer can transmit water laterally to refill it. This creates a **cone of depression** around the well, lowering the water level locally. The DWL represents the apex of this cone, while SWL represents the regional undisturbed level.

$$\text{Drawdown } s = \text{SWL} - \text{DWL}$$

Since $s > 0$ during pumping, DWL < SWL (DWL is deeper below the measuring point).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]** Why is the air-line method preferred for measuring water levels in **deep irrigation wells** during continuous pumping?

**Model Answer:**

The air-line method is preferred for the following reasons:

1. **No need to stop pumping** ŌĆö the measurement is taken while the pump is operating, which is the practical scenario in irrigation wells.
2. **Unaffected by turbulence** ŌĆö unlike float or chalked-tape methods, air-line readings are not disturbed by water surging or air bubbles from the pump.
3. **Long-term installation** ŌĆö the air-line tube is permanently fixed inside the well, allowing **repeated measurements** at any time.
4. **Suitable for deep wells** ŌĆö works reliably up to depths of 300 m or more, well beyond the practical limit of steel tapes.
5. **Compatible with automatic recording** ŌĆö modern pressure transducers can be connected to the air-line for telemetry.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]** Describe in detail the various methods of water level measurement in observation wells. Compare their accuracy and field of application. *(Part-a: 7 marks for description; Part-b: 7 marks for comparison table and field applicability.)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Description of Methods (7 marks):**

Refer to the detailed descriptions in **Section 2.1** of these notes. Briefly cover:

1. **Wetted Tape** ŌĆö graduated steel tape with plumb bob, dipped and withdrawn; wetted length gives SWL. Used in shallow observation wells (< 60 m) with clear water. CGWB standard for routine monitoring.
2. **Chalked Tape** ŌĆö chalk-coated tape; water dissolves chalk up to water level; cleaner boundary in turbid water. Accuracy ┬▒ 5 mm.
3. **Air-Line Method** ŌĆö pressure-based; compressed air injected through a tube until water is displaced; gauge pressure converted to water column via $h = 10.197 \times P$. Ideal for **pumping wells** up to 300 m deep.
4. **Float Method** ŌĆö float on water surface connected to a recording drum; provides continuous water-level chart over weeks/months.
5. **Electrical Sounder** ŌĆö probe closes an electrical circuit on contact with water; buzzer/galvanometer indication. Accuracy ┬▒ 10 mm; standard for CGWB observation networks.
6. **Pressure Transducer** ŌĆö submerged hydrostatic sensor; converts pressure to 4ŌĆō20 mA signal for digital data logging. Used in modern telemetric stations.
7. **Acoustic/Radar Sensor** ŌĆö non-contact measurement using sound or microwave pulses; high accuracy (┬▒1ŌĆō3 mm) but expensive.

**(b) Comparison Table & Field Applicability (7 marks):**

Use the **Analytical Matrix** in Section 3.3 of these notes. Key points to highlight:

- For **static, shallow wells** ŌåÆ wetted/chalked tape (low cost, high accuracy).
- For **pumping, deep wells** ŌåÆ air-line method (only practical option).
- For **continuous monitoring** ŌåÆ pressure transducer or float recorder.
- For **modern automated stations** ŌåÆ radar or acoustic sensor.
- For **CGWB observation network** ŌåÆ electrical sounder + transducer hybrid.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023]** An observation well is fitted with an air-line whose lower open end is at an elevation of **+24.50 m above MSL**. The measuring point (MP) is at **+30.00 m above MSL**. During measurement, the pressure gauge reads **1.85 kg/cm┬▓**. Determine: **(a)** the static water level elevation above MSL; **(b)** the depth of water below the MP; **(c)** if pumping lowers the DWL to 6.50 m below MP, compute the drawdown.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SWL Elevation Above MSL:**

Submergence depth of water above the air-line's open end:

$$h = 10.197 \times P = 10.197 \times 1.85 = 18.86 \text{ m}$$

Elevation of SWL:

$$\text{SWL (MSL)} = \text{Elevation of air-line open end} + h$$

$$\text{SWL (MSL)} = 24.50 + 18.86 = \mathbf{+43.36 \text{ m above MSL}}$$

**(b) Depth of Water Below MP:**

$$\text{SWL below MP} = \text{MP} - \text{SWL (MSL)} = 30.00 - 43.36 = -13.36 \text{ m}$$

The negative sign indicates the static water level is **13.36 m ABOVE the MP** (artesian condition).

**(c) Drawdown Calculation:**

$$\text{SWL depth} = -13.36 \text{ m (i.e., 13.36 m above MP)}$$
$$\text{DWL depth} = +6.50 \text{ m (below MP)}$$

$$\text{Drawdown } s = \text{SWL} - \text{DWL} = (-13.36) - (+6.50) = -19.86 \text{ m}$$

Taking magnitudes (since drawdown is conventionally reported as a positive quantity):

$$\boxed{\,s = 19.86 \text{ m}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Sign convention confusion:** Many students write the final drawdown as $-19.86$ m. KTU expects a **positive** drawdown value, since drawdown is defined as the *magnitude* of water-level decline.
> - **Unit slip:** Forgetting to convert gauge pressure from psi or kPa to kg/cm┬▓ before applying the $10.197$ factor is a **2-mark deduction**.
> - **Datum mismatch:** Reporting the SWL as "m below MP" without also stating "m above MSL" loses **1 mark** ŌĆö KTU 2024 explicitly demands both forms.
> - **Air-line submergence:** Some students forget that $h$ is the depth *above* the air-line's open end ŌĆö they mistakenly subtract it. Always add it to the air-line elevation.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before entering the exam hall, ensure you can confidently tick off every item below:**

- ŌśÉ Define **SWL**, **DWL**, **drawdown ($s$)**, **piezometric head**, and **measuring point (MP)** with correct units.
- ŌśÉ Recall the air-line formula $h = 10.197 \times P$ (with $P$ in kg/cm┬▓) and its derivation from $\rho g h$.
- ŌśÉ List **at least 5 methods** of water level measurement with their typical accuracies and suitable well-depth ranges.
- ŌśÉ Convert a tape-depth reading (m below MP) into elevation (m above MSL) and vice-versa without sign errors.
- ŌśÉ Explain why DWL is always **deeper** than SWL in a pumping well ŌĆö reference the **cone of depression**.
- ŌśÉ Compute the **hydraulic gradient** $i = \Delta h / L$ from at least three observation wells.
- ŌśÉ Identify the **most suitable method** for: (i) a CGWB observation well, (ii) a deep irrigation well under pumping, (iii) a telemetric real-time monitoring station.
- ŌśÉ Correctly handle **artesian (negative drawdown)** conditions where SWL lies above MP.
- ŌśÉ Mention the **CGWB / IS 13952** standard datum (MSL at Mumbai) when answering theory questions.
- ŌśÉ In numerical answers, write the formula first, substitute values next, then box the final answer ŌĆö **exactly the KTU valuation pattern**.

---

### ­¤ōī Final Note to Students

Water level measurement is one of the **highest-weightage, easiest-to-score** topics in Module 4 of PBAGT304. A solid grasp of the air-line derivation, sign conventions, and method-selection logic will fetch you **full marks** in both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Practise at least three numerical problems on the air-line method before the exam ŌĆö they appear almost every semester.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geophysical Logging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Geophysical Logging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geophysical Logging (Borehole Geophysics / Well Logging):** A continuous in-situ measurement technique in which a specialized sensor probe (sonde) is lowered into a borehole to record the physical, electrical, acoustic, and radioactive properties of the surrounding geological formations and contained fluids as a function of depth. The recorded trace is called a **geophysical log** or **well log**, which serves as the primary subsurface diagnostic tool for aquifer identification, correlation, and evaluation in groundwater hydrology.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PBAGT304), geophysical logging is positioned as an **indirect subsurface investigation method** that complements surface-based geophysical surveys (like resistivity and seismic) by providing continuous, depth-specific, quantitative data about the strata penetrated by a borehole.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Medical X-Ray for the Earth" Analogy**
> Imagine a doctor sliding an ultrasound or endoscope probe down a patient's throat to "see" the internal organs without cutting the body open. **Geophysical logging does the exact same thing for a borehole.** A cylindrical instrument (the *sonde*) is lowered down a drilled well on a cable. As it descends, it "listens," "shines," "vibrates," and "listens again" to the surrounding rock and water, continuously translating those signals into a printed curve (the log). Each type of log answers a specific question ŌĆö *Is the layer porous? Is it water-bearing? Is it clay? How thick is it?*

The hydrogeologist then reads these curves like a doctor reads an ECG, identifying **aquifers**, **aquicludes**, **aquitards**, and **confining layers**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The fundamental measurements rely on standard physical constants:

- **Formation water resistivity ($R_w$):** typically **10 ŌĆō 100 ╬®┬Ęm** in freshwater aquifers
- **Mud filtrate resistivity ($R_{mf}$):** $\approx$ **1 ŌĆō 10 ╬®┬Ęm** (depends on drilling mud chemistry)
- **Gamma ray intensity:** measured in **API units (American Petroleum Institute gamma units)**
- **Standard logging speed:** **3 ŌĆō 6 m/min** for most qualitative logs; **~3 m/min** for quantitative nuclear logs
- **Tool diameter:** **3.75 cm ŌĆō 10 cm** (matched to borehole diameter)
- **Formation temperature gradient:** **~25 ŌĆō 30 ┬░C/km** (geothermal, used for temperature logs)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes *application* of geophysical logs to identify **aquifer zones, formation boundaries, water quality indicators, and well construction assessment** ŌĆö not the underlying nuclear/electrical physics. Focus on **log shapes, interpretation patterns, and hydrogeological meaning**.

## 1.4 Classification of Geophysical Logs (Overview)

| Category | Physical Principle | Typical Logs |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Logs** | Resistivity / Conductivity of formation | SP, Normal / Lateral Resistivity, Focused Resistivity, Induction |
| **Nuclear / Radioactive Logs** | Natural and induced radioactivity | Gamma Ray (GR), Neutron, Density (Gamma-Gamma) |
| **Acoustic Logs** | Sound wave propagation | Sonic / Acoustic Velocity |
| **Miscellaneous Logs** | Mechanical / Thermal | Caliper, Temperature, Fluid Conductivity, Flowmeter (Impeller / Heat-pulse) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Logging (Step-by-Step)

1. **Borehole Preparation:** The borehole is drilled (rotary or cable-tool) and kept filled with drilling mud to prevent collapse. The mud creates a **mud cake** on the borehole wall and an **invaded zone** ($R_{xo}$) in the adjacent formation where mud filtrate displaces native formation water.

2. **Tool Insertion:** A logging sonde containing sensors is attached to an **armored multi-conductor cable** wound on a winch at the surface. The cable also carries power and signal lines.

3. **Continuous Measurement:** As the tool is hoisted up at a constant speed, sensors measure a physical property of the formation surrounding the borehole. Depth is recorded simultaneously via a **measuring sheave (depth encoder)** at the surface.

4. **Signal Transmission:** Analog/digital signals are transmitted up the cable to a **surface panel** (analog) or **computerized acquisition system** (digital) where they are plotted as a continuous curve vs. depth.

5. **Interpretation:** The geologist/hydrogeologist correlates log responses to lithology, porosity, water content, and permeability.

## 2.2 The Archie's Equation (Foundational to Resistive Log Interpretation)

The **Archie Equation** is the single most important formula in petrophysics and forms the basis for quantitative log interpretation in water-saturated formations:

$$
F = \frac{R_o}{R_w} = \frac{a}{\phi^m}
$$

where:

- $F$ = **Formation Resistivity Factor** (dimensionless)
- $R_o$ = resistivity of the **100% water-saturated** formation (╬®┬Ęm)
- $R_w$ = resistivity of formation water (╬®┬Ęm)
- $a$ = **tortuosity constant** (typically **0.6 ŌĆō 1.0**, often taken as **1.0** for clean sands)
- $\phi$ = **porosity** (fraction)
- $m$ = **cementation exponent** (typically **1.3** for unconsolidated sand, **1.8 ŌĆō 2.0** for consolidated sandstone, **~2.0** for limestone)

The **Hydrocarbon (or water) Saturation Equation** (Archie's second equation):

$$
S_w^n = \frac{F \cdot R_w}{R_t} = \frac{R_o}{R_t}
$$

where:

- $S_w$ = **water saturation** (fraction, 0 to 1)
- $n$ = **saturation exponent** (typically **~2.0**)
- $R_t$ = **true formation resistivity** (╬®┬Ęm) ŌĆö read directly from deep resistivity log

> [!NOTE]
> In freshwater aquifers, the salinity is low, so $R_w$ is **high** and the formation is **resistive**. In saline / coastal aquifers, $R_w$ is **low** and the formation is **conductive**. This is the diagnostic criterion for water-quality zoning.

## 2.3 The Spontaneous Potential (SP) Log ŌĆö Physics

The SP log measures the **natural electrical potential (millivolts)** that develops between a movable electrode in the borehole and a reference electrode at the surface. Two main electrochemical effects generate the SP:

**1. Liquid-Junction Potential ($E_{lj}$):**
$$
E_{lj} = -K \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{a_w}{a_{mf}}\right)
$$

**2. Membrane Potential ($E_m$):** (only across shale, where $a_w \to 0$)
$$
E_m = -K \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{a_w}{a_{mf}}\right)
$$

where:

- $K$ = **Nernst constant** $\approx$ **ŌłÆ71 mV at 25 ┬░C** (for NaCl equivalent solutions)
- $a_w$ = chemical activity of formation water
- $a_{mf}$ = chemical activity of mud filtrate

The **Static SP (SSP)** in a thick, clean, permeable bed is:

$$
SSP = -K \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_{mf}}{R_w}\right)
$$

## 2.4 Gamma Ray (GR) Log ŌĆö Physics

Natural radioactive isotopes of **Potassium-40 ($^{40}$K)**, **Thorium-232 ($^{232}$Th)**, and **Uranium-238 ($^{238}$U)** occur in rocks, predominantly concentrated in **clay/shale** minerals. The GR log uses a **scintillation detector** (NaI crystal + photomultiplier) to count gamma photons per unit time. **API units** are the standard scale (calibrated at the API test pit in Houston, Texas).

> [!TIP]
> **Deflection Rule:** **High GR = Shale (clay-rich, low permeability).** **Low GR = Clean sand / carbonate (clay-poor, potentially water-bearing).**

The **Gamma Ray Index ($I_{GR}$)** for shale volume estimation:

$$
I_{GR} = \frac{GR_{log} - GR_{clean}}{GR_{shale} - GR_{clean}}
$$

$$
V_{sh} = 0.083 \cdot (2^{3.7 \cdot I_{GR}} - 1) \quad \text{(Steiber, 1973 ŌĆö Larionov tertiary)}
$$

where $V_{sh}$ is the **shale (clay) volume fraction**.

## 2.5 Neutron Log ŌĆö Physics

A neutron source (Am-Be, ~4 ŌĆō 16 Ci) emits **fast neutrons** that collide elastically with hydrogen nuclei (which have nearly equal mass), losing energy rapidly. The detected **epithermal or thermal neutron flux** is inversely proportional to **hydrogen index (HI)**. In water-saturated formations, hydrogen is in the **pore water**, so the neutron log effectively reads **porosity (water-filled)**.

**Apparent Neutron Porosity ($\phi_N$):**

$$
\phi_N \approx \phi \cdot S_w \quad \text{(in clean, water-saturated formations)}
$$

The neutron log is presented in **limestone-equivalent porosity units (p.u.)** on the log track.

## 2.6 Density (Gamma-Gamma) Log ŌĆö Physics

A **Cesium-137 source** emits medium-energy gamma rays (**0.662 MeV**) which Compton-scatter off formation electrons. Scattering intensity depends on **electron density ($n_e$)**, which is proportional to **bulk density ($\rho_b$)**.

**Mass absorption equation (Beer-Lambert form):**

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho_b x}
$$

**Bulk density to porosity conversion:**

$$
\phi_D = \frac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f}
$$

where:

- $\rho_{ma}$ = matrix (grain) density ŌĆö **~2.65 g/cm┬│** for sand, **~2.71 g/cm┬│** for limestone, **~2.87 g/cm┬│** for dolomite
- $\rho_f$ = fluid density ŌĆö **~1.00 g/cm┬│** for freshwater, **~1.10 g/cm┬│** for saline
- $\rho_b$ = bulk density read from log (g/cm┬│)

## 2.7 Sonic / Acoustic Log ŌĆö Physics

A transmitter emits an acoustic pulse (~10 ŌĆō 30 kHz). Two receivers, spaced 0.6 m apart, detect the first-arrival **P-wave (compressional wave)**. The **interval transit time ($\Delta t$)** is the time for the wave to traverse 1 ft (0.3048 m) of formation, in **┬Ąs/ft**.

**Wyllie Time-Average Equation (1958):**

$$
\Delta t_{log} = \phi \cdot \Delta t_f + (1 - \phi) \cdot \Delta t_{ma}
$$

Solving for porosity:

$$
\phi_S = \frac{\Delta t_{log} - \Delta t_{ma}}{\Delta t_f - \Delta t_{ma}}
$$

Standard values:

- $\Delta t_{ma}$ (quartz sand) = **55.5 ┬Ąs/ft**
- $\Delta t_{ma}$ (limestone) = **47.5 ┬Ąs/ft**
- $\Delta t_{ma}$ (dolomite) = **43.5 ┬Ąs/ft**
- $\Delta t_f$ (water) = **189 ┬Ąs/ft**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In gas-bearing zones, $\Delta t$ "cycleskip"** appears ŌĆö the slow compressional wave is masked, producing anomalously high apparent porosity on the sonic log but low on the density log. This is a classic **gas-effect diagnostic** in cross-plot analysis.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Range / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = a \,/\, \phi^m$ | Archie's Formation Factor | $F = 4 - 20$ (typical) |
| 2 | $S_w^n = R_o \,/\, R_t$ | Water Saturation | $0 < S_w \le 1$ |
| 3 | $SSP = -K \log_{10}(R_{mf}/R_w)$ | Static SP | K Ōēł ŌłÆ71 mV @ 25 ┬░C |
| 4 | $I_{GR} = (GR - GR_{min})/(GR_{max} - GR_{min})$ | Gamma Ray Index | 0 ŌĆō 1 |
| 5 | $\phi_D = (\rho_{ma} - \rho_b)/(\rho_{ma} - \rho_f)$ | Density Porosity | Fraction |
| 6 | $\phi_S = (\Delta t - \Delta t_{ma})/(\Delta t_f - \Delta t_{ma})$ | Sonic Porosity (Wyllie) | Fraction |
| 7 | $\phi_N \approx \phi \cdot S_w$ | Neutron Porosity (clean) | p.u. (%) |
| 8 | $R_{xo} = F \cdot R_{mf}$ | Flushed Zone Resistivity | ╬®┬Ęm |
| 9 | $K = (a \phi^m S_w^n) / \text{(calibration)}$ | Permeability (Kozeny-Carman, conceptual) | mD |
| 10 | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho_b x}$ | Gamma attenuation (Compton) | counts/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In hydrogeology, these formulas are the *quantitative bridge* between a log trace and a decision ŌĆö *where to place the screen? at what depth? is the water saline? is the aquifer confined?* Civil engineers use this in designing water supply wells, dewatering systems, and contamination studies.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Archie's Law (Conceptual from Conductivity)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with parallel resistor model of a porous rock.**

Treat the rock as a **bundle of capillary tubes** carrying water of resistivity $R_w$. For a unit cube of rock with porosity $\phi$ and tortuous flow paths of length $L_t$ in a cube of length $L$:

$$
\text{Conductance of water in rock} = \frac{1}{R_o} = \frac{\phi}{R_w} \cdot \frac{L^2}{L_t^2}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define tortuosity and formation factor.**

Tortuosity $\tau = L_t / L$. Define $F = R_o / R_w$, so:

$$
F = \frac{\tau}{\phi} = \frac{1}{\phi} \cdot \left(\frac{L_t}{L}\right)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Empirical extension to real consolidated rocks.**

Winsauer and others (1952) found empirically that:

$$
F = \frac{a}{\phi^m}
$$

Setting $a = 1$ and $m = 1$ recovers the simple tube model. Real cemented, compacted, or shaly rocks require $a \neq 1$ and $m > 1$.

$$
\therefore \quad \boxed{F = \frac{a}{\phi^m}} \quad \text{(Archie's First Equation)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Introduce hydrocarbon/water saturation.**

If only a fraction $S_w$ of the pore space is filled with conductive water:

$$
\frac{1}{R_t} = \frac{\phi^m \cdot S_w^n}{a \cdot R_w}
$$

Solving for $S_w$:

$$
S_w^n = \frac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot R_t} = \frac{R_o}{R_t}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_w = \left(\frac{R_o}{R_t}\right)^{1/n}} \quad \text{(Archie's Second Equation)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Determining Porosity from Resistivity Log

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A clean sandstone aquifer (no shale) is logged with a deep resistivity tool yielding $R_t = 50\ \Omega\cdot$m. The formation water resistivity measured on a water sample is $R_w = 12.5\ \Omega\cdot$m. Laboratory core analysis on a nearby sample gives $a = 1.0$, $m = 1.8$, and $n = 2.0$. Calculate the **porosity** and **water saturation** of the formation. Comment on the hydrogeological implication.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $R_o$ from $R_w$ and assumed 100% saturation.** Since $R_o$ is the resistivity when $S_w = 1$:

$$
R_o = F \cdot R_w = \frac{R_w \cdot a}{\phi^m}
$$

But we don't know $\phi$ yet, so we rearrange using $S_w$:

$$
S_w^2 = \frac{R_o}{R_t} = \frac{R_w \cdot a}{\phi^m \cdot R_t}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Recognize a missing piece: we need an independent porosity estimate.**

Suppose a **neutron-density cross-plot** gives $\phi_{N-D} = 0.20$ for the clean sand. Then $m = 1.8$ yields:

$$
F = \frac{1}{(0.20)^{1.8}}
$$

Let us compute $(0.20)^{1.8}$ step by step:

$$
\ln(0.20) = -1.6094
$$

$$
1.8 \times (-1.6094) = -2.8970
$$

$$
e^{-2.8970} = 0.0551
$$

Therefore:

$$
F = \frac{1}{0.0551} = 18.15
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $R_o$:**

$$
R_o = F \cdot R_w = 18.15 \times 12.5 = 226.9\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $S_w$:**

$$
S_w^2 = \frac{R_o}{R_t} = \frac{226.9}{50} = 4.538
$$

$$
S_w = \sqrt{4.538} = 2.13
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$S_w > 1$ is physically impossible!** This indicates that the assumed $\phi$ is too low, **OR** the formation contains **clay/shale** (which violates the "clean" assumption and lowers $R_t$ artificially). Re-interpret with a Waxman-Smits or shaly-sand model.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Re-interpretation using a corrected $\phi$:** Try $\phi = 0.30$:

$$
F = \frac{1}{(0.30)^{1.8}}
$$

$$
\ln(0.30) = -1.2040
$$

$$
1.8 \times (-1.2040) = -2.1672
$$

$$
e^{-2.1672} = 0.1145
$$

$$
F = 8.73
$$

$$
R_o = 8.73 \times 12.5 = 109.1\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}
$$

$$
S_w^2 = \frac{109.1}{50} = 2.182
$$

$$
S_w = 1.48
$$

Still too high. Try $\phi = 0.35$:

$$
F = \frac{1}{(0.35)^{1.8}} = \frac{1}{0.1487} = 6.725
$$

$$
R_o = 6.725 \times 12.5 = 84.06
$$

$$
S_w^2 = 84.06 / 50 = 1.681
$$

$$
S_w = 1.30
$$

Try $\phi = 0.40$:

$$
F = \frac{1}{(0.40)^{1.8}} = \frac{1}{0.1918} = 5.214
$$

$$
R_o = 5.214 \times 12.5 = 65.17
$$

$$
S_w^2 = 65.17 / 50 = 1.303
$$

$$
S_w = 1.142
$$

Try $\phi = 0.45$:

$$
F = \frac{1}{(0.45)^{1.8}} = \frac{1}{0.2455} = 4.073
$$

$$
R_o = 4.073 \times 12.5 = 50.92
$$

$$
S_w^2 = 50.92 / 50 = 1.018
$$

$$
S_w = 1.009
$$

> **[Verification: $\phi = 0.45$, $S_w \approx 1.0$** ŌåÆ formation is **fully water-saturated, very porous**. This is consistent with a productive freshwater sandstone aquifer.]

**Engineering Implication:** Such a high-porosity, fully water-saturated zone is an **excellent aquifer candidate** ŌĆö a screen should be set across this interval. The high $S_w$ also confirms **freshwater** ($R_w$ is large), so water quality is acceptable.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Shale Volume from Gamma Ray

> **Problem:** In a logged section, $GR_{log} = 75$ API, $GR_{clean} = 25$ API, $GR_{shale} = 110$ API. Find the shale volume using the **Larionov (Tertiary) equation**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $I_{GR}$:**

$$
I_{GR} = \frac{75 - 25}{110 - 25} = \frac{50}{85} = 0.588
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Larionov equation:**

$$
V_{sh} = 0.083 \left( 2^{3.7 \times 0.588} - 1 \right)
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
3.7 \times 0.588 = 2.176
$$

Compute $2^{2.176}$:

$$
2^{2.176} = e^{2.176 \ln 2} = e^{2.176 \times 0.6931} = e^{1.508} = 4.516
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{sh} = 0.083 (4.516 - 1) = 0.083 \times 3.516 = 0.292
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{sh} \approx 29.2\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The bed contains about **29% clay minerals**. It is a **"shaly sand"** ŌĆö moderately clayey, with permeability degraded by clay. May still produce water but at a reduced yield. For "clean" potable aquifer targeting, zones with $V_{sh} > 40\%$ are usually rejected.

---

## 3.4 Cross-Plot Analysis: NeutronŌĆōDensity Log for Lithology & Fluid Identification

A **NeutronŌĆōDensity cross-plot** has $\phi_N$ (x-axis) and $\rho_b$ (y-axis). Three standard lithology lines are pre-plotted:

- **Sandstone line:** $\rho_{ma} = 2.65$ g/cm┬│
- **Limestone line:** $\rho_{ma} = 2.71$ g/cm┬│
- **Dolomite line:** $\rho_{ma} = 2.87$ g/cm┬│

A point falling **on a line** = that lithology with water in pores.
A point falling **left of the limestone line (low $\phi_N$, low $\rho_b$)** = **gas effect** (gas reduces both neutron and density apparent porosity).
A point falling **right of the line (high $\phi_N$)** = **shale or clay** (clay-bound water increases $\phi_N$).

> **Conceptual Result Table:**

| Log Combination | Water-Saturated Sand | Gas Zone | Shale/Clay |
|---|---|---|---|
| GR | Low | Low | **High** |
| Resistivity | ModerateŌĆōHigh | Very High | **Very Low** |
| $\phi_N$ | Matches $\phi$ | **Low** | **High** (HI of bound water) |
| $\phi_D$ | Matches $\phi$ | **Low** | Moderate |

This **triangulation** between logs is the heart of log interpretation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question]** Define geophysical logging. List any four common types of geophysical logs used in groundwater studies and state the physical principle measured by each.

**Model Answer:**

**Geophysical logging** is a borehole-based subsurface investigation technique in which a sensor probe (sonde) is lowered into a well to continuously measure physical properties of geological formations as a function of depth, producing a graphical record called a log.

Four common log types:

1. **Gamma Ray (GR) Log** ŌĆö Measures **natural gamma radiation** emitted by $K$, $U$, $Th$ isotopes; high reading = clay/shale, low reading = clean sand.
2. **Spontaneous Potential (SP) Log** ŌĆö Measures **natural electrical potential** caused by electrochemical differences between mud filtrate and formation water; identifies permeable beds and formation water resistivity.
3. **Resistivity Log** ŌĆö Measures the **electrical resistivity** of the formation; clean freshwater aquifers are resistive, saline aquifers and clays are conductive.
4. **Neutron Log** ŌĆö Measures **hydrogen index** by detecting thermalized neutrons; estimates water-filled porosity.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question]** Explain the Archie's equation and state any two assumptions on which it is based.

**Model Answer:**

Archie's equation relates the resistivity of a porous, water-saturated rock ($R_o$) to the resistivity of the formation water ($R_w$):

$$
F = \frac{R_o}{R_w} = \frac{a}{\phi^m}
$$

where $F$ is the **formation resistivity factor**, $\phi$ is porosity, $a$ is a constant (Ōēł1), and $m$ is the cementation exponent (1.3 ŌĆō 2.0).

**Two key assumptions:**
1. The rock is **clean** (no significant clay or shale content), so all conductivity comes from formation water.
2. The rock is **100% water-saturated** (i.e., $S_w = 1$). For partial saturation, the second Archie equation with exponent $n$ is used.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Geophysical Logging Principles & Resistivity Interpretation

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Describe with neat sketches the principle, tool configuration, and typical log response of:
**(i) Spontaneous Potential (SP) Log**  
**(ii) Gamma Ray (GR) Log**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Spontaneous Potential (SP) Log:**

**Principle:** A natural electrical potential difference develops between the conductive drilling mud column inside the borehole and the formation water in permeable beds. This is recorded in **millivolts (mV)** as the **SP curve**.

**Tool Configuration:** A simple downhole **lead electrode** is mounted on an insulated cable and lowered into the hole. A **reference electrode** is placed at the surface in a shallow mud pit. The millivolt difference between the two is measured continuously.

**Log Response:**
- **Shale baseline (constant):** SP is flat and defines the "shale line."
- **Permeable clean sand (fresh water formation):** SP deflects **negatively** (to the left in standard display).
- **Impermeable tight zones:** SP returns to the shale baseline.
- **Magnitude of deflection** = $SSP \approx -71 \log_{10}(R_{mf}/R_w)$ mV.

**Diagnostic Value:** Identifies permeable zones, estimates $R_w$, detects formation boundaries.

---

**(ii) Gamma Ray (GR) Log:**

**Principle:** Detects natural **gamma photon emissions** from radioactive isotopes ($^{40}K$, $^{232}Th$, $^{238}U$) in minerals, especially in **clay minerals** (illite, montmorillonite, kaolinite).

**Tool Configuration:** A **scintillation counter** (NaI crystal + photomultiplier tube) shielded in a pressure housing is mounted on the cable. The count rate is recorded in **API units**.

**Log Response:**
- **Shale / clay:** **High GR** (50 ŌĆō 150+ API).
- **Clean sand, limestone, dolomite, coal:** **Low GR** (< 30 API).
- **Limestone and dolomite** cannot be distinguished from sandstone purely by GR ŌĆö use densityŌĆōneutron for this.

**Diagnostic Value:** Lithology determination, shale volume calculation, correlation between wells, clay identification.

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A clean sandstone aquifer is logged using a deep resistivity tool. The following data are obtained:

- $R_t = 80\ \Omega\cdot$m
- $R_w = 20\ \Omega\cdot$m
- $a = 1.0$, $m = 1.9$, $n = 2.0$
- Porosity ($\phi$) from neutron log = **0.25**

**Compute the formation resistivity factor $F$, the resistivity of 100% water-saturated rock $R_o$, and the water saturation $S_w$. Comment on the hydrogeological implication.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $F$:**

$$
F = \frac{a}{\phi^m} = \frac{1.0}{(0.25)^{1.9}}
$$

Compute $(0.25)^{1.9}$:

$$
\ln(0.25) = -1.3863
$$

$$
1.9 \times (-1.3863) = -2.6340
$$

$$
e^{-2.6340} = 0.07179
$$

$$
F = \frac{1}{0.07179} = 13.93
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $R_o$:**

$$
R_o = F \cdot R_w = 13.93 \times 20 = 278.6\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $S_w$:**

$$
S_w^2 = \frac{R_o}{R_t} = \frac{278.6}{80} = 3.4825
$$

$$
S_w = \sqrt{3.4825} = 1.866
$$

**$S_w > 1$** is physically impossible.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Hydrogeological Implication:**

The physically impossible $S_w$ indicates that one of the assumptions is violated:

1. The **porosity may be underestimated** (the neutron log reads only water-bound hydrogen; in fine-grained rock some hydrogen is bound to clay ŌĆö but the problem states "clean sandstone," so this is less likely).
2. The **formation may not be fully water-saturated** (e.g., contains **gas or air** in the vadose zone) ŌĆö but the deep resistivity reading should rise in this case, lowering $S_w$ further, not the other way.
3. **Most likely:** the **deep resistivity reading $R_t$ is artificially low** due to **invasion**, **bed thickness effects**, or **shale contamination** (not actually "clean").
4. **The Archie parameters may be inappropriate** ŌĆö typical sandstone $m = 1.8$ ŌĆō $2.0$, but if this is an unconsolidated sand, $m$ might be lower (1.3).

> Re-compute assuming $m = 1.5$:

$$
F = \frac{1}{(0.25)^{1.5}} = \frac{1}{0.125} = 8.0
$$

$$
R_o = 8.0 \times 20 = 160\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}
$$

$$
S_w^2 = 160 / 80 = 2.0
$$

$$
S_w = 1.41
$$

Still > 1. The data as stated are inconsistent; the **recommended action** is to verify the porosity estimate, $R_w$ (via a water sample), and check for shale laminations on the GR log.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Nuclear & Acoustic Logs

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** With a clear labelled diagram, explain the working principle of the **Neutron Log**. State the assumptions made and the corrections applied during interpretation.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** A chemical neutron source (e.g., **Americium-Beryllium**, emitting fast neutrons at ~4 MeV) is mounted in the tool alongside a **detector** (either He-3 proportional counter for thermal neutrons or a $^{3}$He/Li-glass for epithermal). Fast neutrons undergo **elastic scattering** with atomic nuclei; in formation pores filled with water, the abundant **hydrogen nuclei** (H, mass Ōēł 1) efficiently thermalize the neutrons. The detector counts the returning **thermal or epithermal neutrons**.

**Cross-section diagram of neutron tool in borehole:**

```
        ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
        Ōöé   Steel Pressure HousingŌöé
        Ōöé  ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ  ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ  Ōöé
        Ōöé  Ōöé NeutronŌöé  ŌöéDetectorŌöé Ōöé
        Ōöé  Ōöé Source Ōöé  ŌöéHe-3 or Ōöé Ōöé
        Ōöé  ŌöéAm-Be   Ōöé  ŌöéLi Glass Ōöé Ōöé

                Ōöé          Ōöé
   Fast neutronsŌöé         ŌöéCount rate (cps)
   emitted      Ō¢╝         Ō¢╝
       ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ

       Ōöé   (water molecules = H source)   Ōöé

```

**Log Response:**

- **High count rate** = low hydrogen index = low water content (gas, tight rock, dry zone).
- **Low count rate** = high hydrogen index = high water-filled porosity.

Calibrated in **limestone-equivalent porosity units (p.u.)**.

**Assumptions:**
1. All hydrogen detected is in **pore water** (not in clay-bound water, hydrocarbons with low H/C ratio, or hydrated minerals).
2. **No gas effect** in the formation (or it is corrected).
3. The borehole is **filled with liquid mud** (gas-filled holes require a sidewall neutron pad tool).

**Corrections Applied:**
1. **Stand-off correction** (tool not centered).
2. **Mud-weight and mud-salinity correction** (chlorine absorbs neutrons).
3. **Formation salinity correction.**
4. **Lithology correction** (sandstone, limestone, dolomite have different matrix absorption).
5. **Gas-effect correction** using a **NeutronŌĆōDensity cross-plot**.

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A density log in a limestone aquifer reads $\rho_b = 2.30\ \text{g/cm}^3$. Given $\rho_{ma}$ (limestone) = 2.71 g/cm┬│ and $\rho_f$ (freshwater) = 1.00 g/cm┬│, calculate the **density porosity** $\phi_D$. If the same interval shows a sonic log reading of $\Delta t = 65\ \mu\text{s/ft}$, compute the **sonic porosity** $\phi_S$ using Wyllie's equation (with $\Delta t_{ma} = 47.5\ \mu\text{s/ft}$, $\Delta t_f = 189\ \mu\text{s/ft}$). Comment on the **agreement (or disagreement)** between the two porosity values.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Density Porosity $\phi_D$:**

$$
\phi_D = \frac{\rho_{ma} - \rho_b}{\rho_{ma} - \rho_f} = \frac{2.71 - 2.30}{2.71 - 1.00} = \frac{0.41}{1.71} = 0.2398
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi_D \approx 24.0\%}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Sonic Porosity $\phi_S$ (Wyllie):**

$$
\phi_S = \frac{\Delta t_{log} - \Delta t_{ma}}{\Delta t_f - \Delta t_{ma}} = \frac{65 - 47.5}{189 - 47.5} = \frac{17.5}{141.5} = 0.1237
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi_S \approx 12.4\%}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Comment on Disagreement:**

The two porosities disagree significantly: $\phi_D = 24\%$ is roughly **double** $\phi_S = 12.4\%$.

**Possible Reasons:**

1. **Gas effect:** A gas-bearing zone reduces the density reading ($\rho_b$ drops) more than it affects the sonic log. The **density porosity reads too high**, while the sonic porosity may also read too high (cycle skipping), but typically density is more gas-sensitive.
2. **Clay content:** Clays contain bound water that increases $\Delta t$ (high apparent sonic porosity) ŌĆö but here $\phi_S$ is *low*, so this is unlikely.
3. **Secondary porosity / vugs:** Limestone aquifers often have **vugular or fracture porosity** that the sonic log (which measures only **intergranular / primary porosity** along the borehole wall) does not detect, while the density log integrates a larger volume and "sees" the vugs.
4. **Compaction / overpressure correction missing** in the Wyllie equation (Wyllie assumes a compaction factor $C_p = 1$ for unconsolidated, 1.5 for compacted, but this is for sandstones ŌĆö limestone behaves differently).

**Engineering Conclusion:** The **density log is more representative of the total porosity** in this vuggy limestone. Aquifer yield from fracture/vug flow may be **much higher** than sonic porosity would suggest. Recommend cross-checking with a caliper log (to rule out washouts) and a flowmeter log (under pumping) to confirm productive fractures.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Geophysical Logging Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the SP sign convention.** In standard display, **freshwater (low $R_w$) permeable zones deflect to the LEFT (negative)**. Many students reverse this and lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units.** GR is in **API**, not counts/second. Density is in **g/cm┬│**, not kg/m┬│. Resistivity is in **╬®┬Ęm**, not ╬®/cm. Mixing these loses marks in numericals.
>
> 3. **Applying Archie's equation to shaly sands** without correction. The "clean" assumption fails for $V_{sh} > 10\%$. Use **Waxman-Smits, Simandoux, or Indonesia** equation. Even naming them earns a partial mark.
>
> 4. **Reporting $S_w > 1$** without flagging it as physically impossible and re-interpreting.
>
> 5. **Confusing $\phi_N$ and $\phi_D$ cross-plot interpretation.** Gas zones cause a **"crossover"** in the neutronŌĆōdensity log tracks ŌĆö students often mix this up with shale crossover. Remember: **Gas ŌåÆ density > neutron (separation to the right of limestone line on a neutronŌĆōdensity plot).**
>
> 6. **Skipping the depth-of-investigation difference** between shallow logs (SP, micro-resistivity) and deep logs (deep resistivity). The shallow tools see $R_{xo}$; the deep tools see $R_t$. Mention this for full marks.
>
> 7. **In the Wyllie equation, students often write $189$ ┬Ąs/ft for $\Delta t_f$ (water)** but then incorrectly use it for **oil**. Oil: $\Delta t_{oil} \approx 238$ ┬Ąs/ft; gas: $\Delta t_{gas} \approx 600$+ ┬Ąs/ft (at low pressure).
>
> 8. **Forgetting to mention tool speed, calibration, and borehole environment corrections** in descriptive answers ŌĆö these are **"process" marks** worth 1 ŌĆō 2 points per question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Geophysical Logging**

- [x] Geophysical logging is a **continuous, in-situ borehole measurement** of physical properties vs. depth.
- [x] Major log families: **Electrical, Nuclear, Acoustic, Miscellaneous** (caliper, temperature, flowmeter).
- [x] **SP Log** ŌåÆ permeable beds, $R_w$ estimation. Shale baseline = flat. Fresh water = negative deflection.
- [x] **Resistivity Log** ŌåÆ clean freshwater sand = high R; saline water = low R; shale = very low R.
- [x] **Gamma Ray Log** ŌåÆ clay/shale = high API; clean sand/carbonate = low API.
- [x] **Neutron Log** ŌåÆ reads **water-filled porosity**; suppressed by gas.
- [x] **Density Log** ŌåÆ reads **bulk density** $\rho_b$; integrated via Compton scattering of $^{137}$Cs gamma rays.
- [x] **Sonic Log** ŌåÆ reads **interval transit time** $\Delta t$; estimates intergranular porosity via Wyllie equation.
- [x] **Archie's equation** $F = a/\phi^m$ is the foundation of quantitative log interpretation; requires **clean, water-saturated** formation.
- [x] **Shale volume** computed from GR via $I_{GR}$ and Larionov / Steiber equation.
- [x] **Density porosity formula:** $\phi_D = (\rho_{ma} - \rho_b)/(\rho_{ma} - \rho_f)$.
- [x] **Sonic porosity formula:** $\phi_S = (\Delta t - \Delta t_{ma})/(\Delta t_f - \Delta t_{ma})$ (Wyllie).
- [x] **Invaded zone geometry:** Borehole ŌåÆ Mud Cake ŌåÆ Flushed Zone ($R_{xo}$) ŌåÆ Transition ŌåÆ Uninvaded ($R_t$).
- [x] **Gas effect** is detected by **neutronŌĆōdensity crossover** (density porosity < neutron porosity, or low values on both).
- [x] **Caliper log** is essential for **hole-diameter correction** of all other logs.
- [x] **Flowmeter logs** (impeller or heat-pulse) identify **producing fractures and aquifer inflows** under pumping.
- [x] **Hydrogeological use:** aquifer identification, screen placement, water quality zoning, well construction QC, aquifer correlation across wells.
- [x] **Standard matrix parameters** to memorize: $\rho_{ma, sand} = 2.65$, $\rho_{ma, lime} = 2.71$, $\rho_{ma, dol} = 2.87$ (g/cm┬│); $\Delta t_{ma, sand} = 55.5$, $\Delta t_{ma, lime} = 47.5$, $\Delta t_{ma, dol} = 43.5$ (┬Ąs/ft); $\Delta t_{water} = 189$ ┬Ąs/ft.
- [x] **Cementation exponent $m$:** unconsolidated sand Ōēł 1.3, consolidated sandstone Ōēł 1.8 ŌĆō 2.0, carbonate Ōēł 2.0.
- [x] **Saturation exponent $n$** Ōēł 2.0 (default).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Resistivity Logging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAGT304 - SURFACE AND GROUNDWATER HYDROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrical Resistivity Logging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrical Resistivity Logging (ERL)** is a geophysical borehole logging technique that records the electrical resistivity (or its inverse, conductivity) of subsurface formations and the fluids saturating them, by passing an electric current through the rock mass via electrodes and measuring the resulting potential difference.

It is one of the oldest and most widely used **well logging methods** in both hydrocarbon exploration and groundwater investigations. The method operates on **Ohm's Law** principles and provides continuous in-situ measurements of the electrical properties of geological formations surrounding a borehole.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Resistivity logging is grouped under "Subsurface Investigation Methods" and is critical for aquifer characterization, identifying permeable zones, and estimating groundwater quality.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Watermelon Tester

Imagine you have a large watermelon and want to know if it is sweet *without cutting it open*. You could:

- Pass a small **electric current** through it using two needles (electrodes)
- Measure the **resistance** to that current
- A sweeter (more conductive) watermelon allows current to pass easily; a less sweet one resists the current

**Electrical Resistivity Logging works exactly the same way**, but applied to rock formations in a borehole:

- **Needles** ŌåÆ Electrodes (current + potential)
- **Watermelon flesh** ŌåÆ The rock/soil matrix and pore fluid
- **Sweetness** ŌåÆ Presence of conductive pore water (saline = conductive; fresh = resistive)
- **Tester reading** ŌåÆ Voltage difference, which we convert to resistivity

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Resistivity (Žü):** Measured in **ohm-meters (╬®┬Ęm)**
- **Conductivity (Žā):** Inverse of resistivity, measured in **siemens per meter (S/m)** or **milli-siemens per meter (mS/m)**
- **Formation Resistivity Factor (F):** Dimensionless ratio
- **Archie's Law constant (a, m, n):** Dimensionless empirical constants

> [!TIP]
> Typical resistivity ranges in nature:
> - Clay/Shale: 1 ŌĆō 100 ╬®┬Ęm
> - Sand aquifers (fresh water): 30 ŌĆō 500 ╬®┬Ęm
> - Sand aquifers (saline water): 1 ŌĆō 10 ╬®┬Ęm
> - Crystalline bedrock: > 1000 ╬®┬Ęm

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The principle is rooted in **Ohm's Law**:

$$V = I \times R$$

Where:
- $V$ = Potential difference (Volts)
- $I$ = Current (Amperes)
- $R$ = Resistance (Ohms)

Resistivity ($\rho$) is a material property independent of geometry:

$$\rho = R \times \frac{A}{L}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area perpendicular to current flow
- $L$ = Length of conductor

## 2.2 General Workflow of Resistivity Logging (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Lower the sonde** (a cylindrical probe housing electrodes) into the borehole on a multi-conductor cable.
2. **Inject current (I)** into the formation through two **current electrodes (A and B)**.
3. **Measure potential difference (╬öV)** between two **potential electrodes (M and N)**.
4. **Compute apparent resistivity** using the geometric configuration of the electrodes.
5. **Record continuously** as the sonde is pulled up the hole, generating a log of resistivity versus depth.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"apparent resistivity"** ($\rho_a$) is used because the measured value is influenced by the surrounding rock, borehole fluid (mud), invaded zone, and tool geometry ŌĆö not just the undisturbed formation.

## 2.3 Electrode Configurations (The Heart of Resistivity Logging)

### A. Normal (Potential) Electrode Arrangement
- One **current electrode (A)** is on the surface; the other current electrode (B) is placed far away.
- Two **potential electrodes (M and N)** are on the sonde, with M closer to A.
- Notation: **AM** spacing (e.g., 16" Normal, 64" Normal).
- **Penetrates shallow** into the formation (deeper with longer AM spacing).

### B. Lateral Electrode Arrangement
- A central potential electrode (O) with current electrodes (A and B) symmetrically placed.
- Notation: **AO = OB** (e.g., 18'8" Lateral).
- **Penetrates deeper** than normal arrangement at the same depth.

### C. Spontaneous Potential (SP) Log
- **No current is injected** ŌĆö measures the natural electrochemical potential difference between a movable electrode in the borehole and a fixed surface electrode.
- Useful for identifying **permeable beds** and **shale boundaries**.

### D. Single Point Resistance (SPR)
- A downhole current electrode with a surface return electrode.
- **Simplest** form; qualitatively indicates resistivity changes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Symbol Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ohm's Law | $V = I \times R$ | Voltage, Current, Resistance | V, A, ╬® |
| Resistivity | $\rho = R \times A / L$ | Material property | ╬®┬Ęm |
| Apparent Resistivity | $\rho_a = K \times (\Delta V / I)$ | $K$ = geometric factor | ╬®┬Ęm |
| Geometric Factor (Normal) | $K = 4\pi \times AM \times AN / MN$ | For pole-pole / normal | meters |
| Geometric Factor (Lateral) | $K = 4\pi \times AM \times AN / MN$ | Symmetric lateral config | meters |
| Formation Factor | $F = R_o / R_w$ | $R_o$ = 100% water saturated rock | dimensionless |
| Archie's Law | $F = a / \phi^m$ | $a, m$ = empirical constants | dimensionless |
| Resistivity Index | $I = R_t / R_o$ | $R_t$ = true formation resistivity | dimensionless |
| Water Saturation | $S_w^n = R_o / R_t$ | $n$ = saturation exponent | dimensionless |
| Total Porosity | $\phi = (R_{w} / R_t)^{1/m}$ | From resistivity logs | fraction |

> [!WARNING]
> In the formula sheet, **avoid** writing $\vert x \vert$ using the vertical bar inside tables ŌĆö it breaks markdown table syntax. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ notation when needed.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Groundwater Exploration:** Identifying aquifers, distinguishing fresh from saline zones.
- **Hydrocarbon Exploration:** Differentiating oil-bearing from water-bearing formations.
- **Civil Engineering:** Site characterization for dams, tunnels, and foundations.
- **Environmental Studies:** Mapping contaminant plumes (e.g., saline intrusion in coastal aquifers).
- **Mineral Exploration:** Locating ore bodies and conductive mineral veins.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Apparent Resistivity for a Normal Electrode Arrangement

### Setup
Consider a single current electrode **A** (point source on the surface) injecting current $I$ into a homogeneous, isotropic medium of resistivity $\rho$. The other current electrode **B** is far away (at infinity for simplicity). The current flows radially outward from A in a hemispherical pattern.

### Step 1: Current Density at Distance r
The current $I$ spreads over a hemisphere of radius $r$:

$$J = \frac{I}{2\pi r^2}$$

The factor $2\pi r^2$ is the surface area of a hemisphere (current flows in all directions in the lower half-space).

### Step 2: Electric Field at Distance r
Using the constitutive relation $\vec{E} = \rho \vec{J}$:

$$E = \rho \times \frac{I}{2\pi r^2}$$

### Step 3: Potential at Distance r
The electric potential at distance $r$ from A is obtained by integrating the electric field:

$$V(r) = \int_{\infty}^{r} E \, dr' = \int_{\infty}^{r} \frac{\rho I}{2\pi (r')^2} \, dr'$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$V(r) = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi r}$$

### Step 4: Potential Difference Between M and N
The potential at M (distance $AM$ from A) and at N (distance $AN$ from A) are:

$$V_M = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi \times AM}$$
$$V_N = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi \times AN}$$

The measured potential difference is:

$$\Delta V = V_M - V_N = \frac{\rho I}{2\pi} \left( \frac{1}{AM} - \frac{1}{AN} \right)$$

### Step 5: Solve for Resistivity
Rearranging to isolate $\rho$:

$$\rho = \frac{2\pi \times \Delta V}{I} \times \frac{1}{\left( \frac{1}{AM} - \frac{1}{AN} \right)}$$

### Step 6: Express as Apparent Resistivity
The geometric factor $K$ is defined as:

$$K = \frac{2\pi}{\left( \frac{1}{AM} - \frac{1}{AN} \right)} = \frac{2\pi \times AM \times AN}{AN - AM}$$

If $MN$ is small compared to $AM$ and $AN$, then $AN - AM \approx MN$, giving:

$$K = \frac{2\pi \times AM \times AN}{MN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **pole-pole** arrangement (B and N at infinity), the geometric factor simplifies to $K = 4\pi \times AM$ (using the full sphere symmetry).

The final expression for apparent resistivity:

$$\boxed{\rho_a = K \times \frac{\Delta V}{I}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Geometric Factor (Generalized)

For a **four-electrode configuration** with current electrodes A and B and potential electrodes M and N, the geometric factor is derived by superposition:

$$K = \frac{4\pi}{\left( \frac{1}{AM} - \frac{1}{AN} - \frac{1}{BM} + \frac{1}{BN} \right)}$$

### Step-by-Step Justification

**Step A:** Potential at M due to current $I$ at A: $V_{M,A} = +\rho I / (4\pi \times AM)$

**Step B:** Potential at M due to current return $-I$ at B: $V_{M,B} = -\rho I / (4\pi \times BM)$

**Step C:** Total potential at M:
$$V_M = \frac{\rho I}{4\pi} \left( \frac{1}{AM} - \frac{1}{BM} \right)$$

**Step D:** Similarly for N:
$$V_N = \frac{\rho I}{4\pi} \left( \frac{1}{AN} - \frac{1}{BN} \right)$$

**Step E:** Potential difference:
$$\Delta V = V_M - V_N = \frac{\rho I}{4\pi} \left( \frac{1}{AM} - \frac{1}{BM} - \frac{1}{AN} + \frac{1}{BN} \right)$$

**Step F:** Solve for $\rho$:
$$\rho = \frac{4\pi \times \Delta V}{I \times \left( \frac{1}{AM} - \frac{1}{BM} - \frac{1}{AN} + \frac{1}{BN} \right)}$$

$$\rho = K \times \frac{\Delta V}{I}$$

Where:

$$K = \frac{4\pi}{\left( \frac{1}{AM} - \frac{1}{BM} - \frac{1}{AN} + \frac{1}{BN} \right)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Style Problem (Dec 2023 Pattern)**

**Problem:** A normal resistivity log is run with electrode spacing $AM = 0.5$ m, $AN = 2.0$ m. The measured current $I = 100$ mA and potential difference $\Delta V = 250$ mV. Compute the apparent resistivity of the formation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the geometric factor $K$:

$$K = \frac{2\pi \times AM \times AN}{AN - AM} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.5 \times 2.0}{2.0 - 0.5}$$

$$K = \frac{2\pi \times 1.0}{1.5} = \frac{2\pi}{1.5} \approx 4.189 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $\Delta V / I$:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{I} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}}{100 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} = 2.5 \text{ ╬®}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate apparent resistivity:

$$\rho_a = K \times \frac{\Delta V}{I} = 4.189 \times 2.5 = 10.47 \text{ ╬®┬Ęm}$$

**Interpretation:** This low resistivity value suggests a **clay-rich or saline-water-saturated formation**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Borehole Resistivity Logging ŌĆö System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    surfaceCtrl[Surface Recording Unit] --> cable[Armored Multi Conductor Cable]
    cable --> winch[Winch and Depth Encoder]
    winch --> borehole[Borehole Filled with Drilling Mud]
    borehole --> formationA[Formation Zone A Shallow]
    borehole --> formationB[Formation Zone B Aquifer]
    borehole --> formationC[Formation Zone C Bedrock]
    
    surfaceCtrl --> currentSrc[Current Source Generator]
    currentSrc --> elecA[Current Electrode A]
    elecA --> formationA
    formationA --> returnB[Return Electrode B at Surface]
    returnB --> currentSrc
    
    surfaceCtrl --> voltMeter[High Impedance Voltmeter]
    voltMeter --> elecM[Potential Electrode M]
    elecM --> formationA
    formationA --> elecN[Potential Electrode N]
    elecN --> voltMeter
    
    surfaceCtrl --> logPlot[Continuous Log Plot Resistivity vs Depth]
    voltMeter --> logPlot
    currentSrc --> logPlot
```

## 4.2 Electrode Configuration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NormalConfig[Normal Arrangement]
        A1[Current Electrode A] -->|AM spacing| M1[Potential Electrode M]
        M1 -->|MN spacing| N1[Potential Electrode N]
        B1[Return Electrode B far away] -.-> A1
    end
    
    subgraph LateralConfig[Lateral Arrangement]
        A2[Current Electrode A] -->|AO spacing| O2[Central Potential O]
        O2 -->|OB spacing| B2[Return Current Electrode B]
        M2[Potential Electrode M] -.-> O2
    end
    
    subgraph SPConfig[Spontaneous Potential]
        M3[Movable Electrode in Borehole] -->|natural voltage| N3[Reference Electrode at Surface]
        N3 --> spRec[SP Recorder mV vs Depth]
    end
```

## 4.3 Data Interpretation Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Raw Resistivity Log Data] --> corr[Apply Borehole and Mud Corrections]
    corr --> invaded[Determine Invaded Zone Resistivity]
    invaded --> trueRes[Compute True Formation Resistivity Rt]
    trueRes --> archie[Apply Archie Equation]
    archie --> porosity[Estimate Porosity phi]
    archie --> saturation[Estimate Water Saturation Sw]
    porosity --> aquifer[Identify Aquifer Zones]
    saturation --> quality[Classify Water Quality Fresh or Saline]
    aquifer --> output[Final Hydrogeological Report]
    quality --> output
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Define Electrical Resistivity Logging. State any two limitations of the method.**

**Model Answer:**
Electrical resistivity logging is a borehole geophysical technique that measures the electrical resistivity of subsurface formations and their pore fluids by injecting current through electrodes and recording the resulting potential difference.

**Limitations:**
1. Affected by **borehole mud invasion** ŌĆö requires correction for accurate $R_t$.
2. Cannot be used effectively in **non-conductive (dry) boreholes** without special fluid.
3. Provides **apparent** resistivity that needs interpretation, not direct true formation values.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**Distinguish between the Normal and Lateral electrode arrangements in resistivity logging.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Normal Arrangement | Lateral Arrangement |
|---|---|---|
| Configuration | One current + one potential close | Two current electrodes symmetric about a central potential |
| Notation | $AM$ spacing (e.g., 16" N) | $AO = OB$ (e.g., 18'8" Lateral) |
| Depth of investigation | Shallow to medium | Deeper than normal at same depth |
| Resolution | Good for thick beds | Better for thin bed resolution |
| Typical use | General lithology | Deep investigation |

## Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice Option) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **principle, procedure, and electrode configurations** of electrical resistivity logging with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **applications of resistivity logging in groundwater exploration**, including identification of aquifers, salinity mapping, and correlation of subsurface formations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Principle (2 marks):**
The method is based on **Ohm's Law**. A known current $I$ is passed through the formation via current electrodes (A, B), and the resulting potential difference $\Delta V$ is measured between potential electrodes (M, N). The apparent resistivity is:

$$\rho_a = K \times \frac{\Delta V}{I}$$

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Lower the sonde into the borehole on a cable.
2. Inject constant current through A and B.
3. Record potential difference between M and N.
4. Pull the sonde up at constant speed; record $\rho_a$ continuously vs depth.
5. Apply environmental corrections (borehole, mud cake, invasion).

**Electrode Configurations (3 marks):**
- **Normal:** Point current source A on surface, M and N on sonde ŌĆö shallow penetration.
- **Lateral:** Symmetric arrangement ŌĆö deeper penetration.
- **SP Log:** Natural potential, no current injection ŌĆö marks permeable zones.
- **Induction Log:** Uses electromagnetic coupling, works in oil-based mud.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Applications in Groundwater Exploration (7 marks):**

1. **Aquifer Identification (2 marks):** Permeable formations saturated with water show distinct resistivity contrasts with impermeable clays and shales. Sand aquifers (30ŌĆō500 ╬®┬Ęm) stand out from clays (1ŌĆō100 ╬®┬Ęm).

2. **Salinity Mapping (2 marks):** Higher dissolved solids = lower resistivity. Saltwater intrusion in coastal aquifers is detected as low-resistivity zones.

3. **Formation Correlation (1.5 marks):** Resistivity signatures (peaks, troughs) are matched across multiple boreholes to trace stratigraphic units.

4. **Porosity and Saturation Estimation (1.5 marks):** Using **Archie's Law**:

$$S_w = \left( \frac{a \times R_w}{\phi^m \times R_t} \right)^{1/n}$$

Where $R_w$ is water resistivity, $R_t$ is true formation resistivity, and $\phi$ is porosity.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for **apparent resistivity** in a four-electrode resistivity logging system. Define the geometric factor and explain its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Spontaneous Potential (SP) log** in detail. Discuss its generation, components, and hydrogeological applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Derivation (5 marks):** Refer to the derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes. The final expression is:

$$\rho_a = K \times \frac{\Delta V}{I}$$

Where the geometric factor:

$$K = \frac{4\pi}{\left( \frac{1}{AM} - \frac{1}{BM} - \frac{1}{AN} + \frac{1}{BN} \right)}$$

**Significance of Geometric Factor (2 marks):**
- $K$ depends **only on electrode spacings**, not on resistivity.
- It converts the measured $V/I$ (a resistance) into a formation property ($\rho$).
- Different $K$ values mean different depths of investigation ŌĆö larger $K$ = deeper penetration.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**SP Log Generation (3 marks):**
The SP log measures the **natural electrical potential** between an electrode in the borehole and a reference at the surface, without injecting any current. Two main components generate the SP:

1. **Electrochemical Component (2 marks):**
   - **Liquid Junction Potential:** Difference in ion mobility between mud filtrate and formation water across a permeable bed.
   - **Membrane Potential:** Across shales (acting as ion-selective membranes).

2. **Electrokinetic Component (1 mark):**
   - Streaming potential due to mud filtrate flowing through mud cake.

**Hydrogeological Applications (1 mark):**
- Identifying permeable beds (clay-free sands show SP deflections).
- Determining formation water resistivity $R_w$.
- Correlating formations between wells.
- Locating shale boundaries.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert**
> 1. **Do not confuse** $R_o$ (resistivity of 100% water-saturated rock) with $R_t$ (true resistivity of partially saturated rock). Many students interchange them in Archie's Law ŌĆö this costs 1ŌĆō2 marks easily.
> 2. **Units matter:** Resistivity is in **╬®┬Ęm**, NOT ╬®. A common slip-up in numerical problems.
> 3. In the apparent resistivity formula $\rho_a = K \times (\Delta V / I)$, the geometric factor $K$ is **often given in tables** for standard electrode configurations ŌĆö you should NOT recompute it from scratch every time unless asked.
> 4. The SP log is a **voltage** log (mV), NOT a resistivity log. Writing "SP resistivity" will be marked wrong.
> 5. Always show the **derivation with both current electrodes (A and B) considered** for full marks. Treating B as "at infinity" is a simplification that may lose marks in rigorous grading.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ Electrical resistivity logging measures **formation resistivity** by passing current and recording voltage ŌĆö based on **Ohm's Law**.
- Ō£ģ Apparent resistivity: $\rho_a = K \times (\Delta V / I)$ where $K$ = geometric factor.
- Ō£ģ **Normal arrangement** ŌåÆ shallow to medium depth of investigation; **Lateral** ŌåÆ deeper penetration.
- Ō£ģ **SP log** is a natural potential log ŌĆö no current injected ŌĆö useful for permeable bed detection.
- Ō£ģ **Archie's Law** connects resistivity to porosity and water saturation:

$$F = \frac{a}{\phi^m}, \quad S_w^n = \frac{F \times R_w}{R_t}$$

- Ō£ģ **Resistivity increases** as: clay ŌåÆ sand (fresh water) ŌåÆ crystalline rock; decreases as salinity increases.
- Ō£ģ **Mud invasion** creates a shallow "flushed zone" that affects log reading ŌĆö correction is required.
- Ō£ģ **Multi-spacing logs** (e.g., 16" and 64" Normal) allow estimation of **invasion profile** and **true $R_t$**.
- Ō£ģ Borehole corrections include: borehole diameter effect, mud resistivity, bed thickness, and adjacent bed influence.
- Ō£ģ For KTU 2024 scheme: focus on **definitions, derivations of $\rho_a$ and $K$, electrode arrangements, and groundwater applications**.
- Ō£ģ Remember: high resistivity ŌēĀ always good aquifer ŌĆö could mean dry rock. Always combine with porosity/SP logs.
- Ō£ģ Units: **╬®┬Ęm** for resistivity, **mV** for SP, **m** for electrode spacings.
